topics.title: Basic Structure of Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Structure of Computers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **computer system** is an integrated assembly of interconnected electronic and electromechanical components that accept data in the form of input, process it according to a programmed set of instructions, store the intermediate and final results, and produce meaningful output. From the architectural standpoint, every digital computer — regardless of size or capability — is organized around **five functionally distinct units** working in concert via control and data pathways. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > A computer is a programmable electronic device that can **store, retrieve, and process data** under the control of a stored program of instructions, transforming input data into useful output information through the coordinated operation of Input, Memory, Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), Control Unit (CU), and Output subsystems. ## 1.2 The Five Functional Units (Hamacher / Stallings / Hayes Model) The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates understanding of the classical **five-unit model** derived from the IAS (Institute for Advanced Study) architecture of John von Neumann: | # | Functional Unit | Primary Role | Physical Realization | |---|----------------|--------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Input Unit** | Accepts raw data and instructions from the external environment | Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, MICR, OMR, Barcode Reader, Touchscreen, Sensors | | 2 | **Memory (Storage) Unit** | Stores data, instructions, intermediate results, and final output | RAM (primary), Hard Disk/SSD (secondary), Cache, ROM, Registers | | 3 | **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** | Performs all arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shift) operations | Arithmetic logic circuitry, adders, multipliers, shifters | | 4 | **Control Unit (CU)** | Directs and coordinates all other units; fetches, decodes, executes instructions | Hardwired control (finite state machine) or microprogrammed control (microcode store) | | 5 | **Output Unit** | Communicates processed results to the external world in human/peripheral-readable form | Monitor, Printer, Speaker, Plotter, LED indicators, Actuators | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Office" Model > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: The Modern Office** > Imagine a well-run office: > - The **mailroom** is the **Input Unit** — it receives letters (data) from outside. > - The **filing cabinet** is the **Memory Unit** — letters and replies are stored until needed. > - The **calculator + decision desk** is the **ALU** — it does sums and makes logical judgments. > - The **supervisor** is the **Control Unit** — telling the mailroom when to collect, the cabinet when to retrieve, the calculator what to compute, and the post office when to dispatch. > - The **post office / office notice board** is the **Output Unit** — sending results back to the world. This analogy works because, like an office, a computer's strength lies in **coordinated flow of information** rather than any single component's power. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Architectural Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU High-Yield Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall):** > - **Word Length:** typically **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** — the natural data unit size a CPU processes per instruction. > - **Clock Frequency (f):** measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **GHz** in modern systems (1 GHz = 10⁹ cycles/sec). > - **Clock Period (T):** **T = 1 / f** (seconds per cycle). > - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** average number of clock cycles per instruction, typically **1 ≤ CPI ≤ 10+**. > - **MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second):** **MIPS = f / (CPI × 10⁶)**. > - **Throughput:** tasks completed per unit time (instructions/sec, requests/sec, FLOPS). > - **Latency:** time to complete **one** task from start to finish (seconds or cycles). > - **1 Byte = 8 bits; 1 KB = 2¹⁰ Bytes = 1024 Bytes; 1 MB = 2²⁰ Bytes; 1 GB = 2³⁰ Bytes.** ## 1.5 The Stored Program Concept (von Neumann Architecture) A defining principle of modern general-purpose computers is the **stored-program concept**: instructions and data of a program reside in the **same memory** and are indistinguishable at the storage level — both are bit patterns addressable by the CPU. This was first physically realized in the **EDVAC (1949)** and the **IAS machine at Princeton (1946–1952)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** > A computer in which program and data share the same memory is called a **von Neumann machine**. The "bottleneck" caused by this shared pathway is known as the **von Neumann bottleneck** — a frequently-asked 3-mark question. ## 1.6 System Interconnection — The Concept of a Bus For the five units to communicate, they need **shared pathways**. The dominant solution is the **bus** — a set of common communication lines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Bus Types (Mandatory KTU Learning):** > - **Data Bus:** carries the actual data being transferred. Its **width** (in bits) determines the largest data item moved in one transaction. > - **Address Bus:** carries the memory/I/O location being accessed. Its **width** determines the maximum addressable memory: **Maximum Memory = 2^(address bus width)**. > - **Control Bus:** carries timing and command signals (Read/Write, Memory/IO, Interrupt Acknowledge, Bus Request, Bus Grant, Clock, Reset). ## 1.7 Basic Operational Concept — Instruction Execution Cycle Every instruction the CPU executes follows the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle (often written as **FDE** cycle or **instruction cycle**): 1. **Fetch** the next instruction from memory (address held in **PC — Program Counter**). 2. **Decode** the instruction to determine the operation and operands. 3. **Evaluate Address** (compute effective address if needed — e.g., in indirect or indexed modes). 4. **Fetch Operands** from memory or registers. 5. **Execute** the operation in the ALU. 6. **Store Result** back to a register or memory location. 7. Update **PC** to point to the next instruction (PC ← PC + instruction size, or branch target). This cycle repeats for every instruction of every program, typically billions of times per second on modern CPUs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Units — Deep Dive ### 2.1.1 Input Unit - **Role:** Acts as the transducer between human/peripheral world and the binary world of the computer. - **Internal Structure:** `Input Device → Interface Logic (buffer, decoder) → System Bus → CPU/Memory`. - **Why Buffering Matters:** Peripherals operate at **orders of magnitude slower** than the CPU. An intermediate buffer (e.g., keyboard's 16-byte FIFO) prevents the CPU from stalling on every keystroke. - **Decoded Signals:** Each input device has a unique **port address**; the interface decodes address bus bits to activate the right device. ### 2.1.2 Memory Unit - **Two-Tier Architecture:** - **Primary Memory (Main Memory):** directly accessible by the CPU. Volatile (RAM: SRAM + DRAM) and non-volatile (ROM: PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash). - **Secondary Memory:** not directly addressable by the CPU; loaded into main memory first. Non-volatile, high-capacity, low-cost: magnetic disks, SSDs, optical media. - **Access Time Hierarchy (Fastest → Slowest, cost decreases):** `Registers ≪ L1 Cache ≪ L2 Cache ≪ L3 Cache ≪ Main DRAM ≪ SSD ≪ HDD ≪ Tape ≪ Cloud` - **Memory Address Register (MAR)** holds the address to access. **Memory Data Register (MDR)** holds the data being read/written. ### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) - **Arithmetic Operations:** integer +, −, ×, ÷; in modern CPUs also floating-point operations (handled by a separate **FPU**). - **Logic Operations:** bitwise AND, OR, NOT, XOR, compare (set flags), shift left/right (logical and arithmetic), rotate. - **Operands Source:** registers (fastest), immediate values, memory locations. - **Flags Output:** most ALUs output condition codes (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) into a **Status Register / Program Status Word (PSW)** used for branching. ### 2.1.4 Control Unit - **Hardwired Control:** combinational logic (AND/OR/NOT gates + decoders + flip-flops) producing fixed control sequences. **Fast**, but inflexible. - **Microprogrammed Control:** each machine instruction triggers a microprogram stored in **Control Memory (CM)**; each microinstruction produces one set of control signals. **Flexible**, **slower** by a few cycles. - **Registers inside CU:** PC (Program Counter), IR (Instruction Register), Decoder output register, Timing generator. ### 2.1.5 Output Unit - Performs the reverse of the input unit — binary data → human/peripheral-recognizable form. - **Examples:** Monitor (VGA, HDMI, DisplayPort), Printer (laser/inkjet/dot-matrix), Speaker, Plotter, LED displays, robotic actuators. ## 2.2 Bus Organization — Three Detailed Variants | Topology | Description | Cost | Speed | Used In | |----------|-------------|------|-------|---------| | **Single Bus** | All units share one set of lines | **Low** | **Low** (bus contention) | Simple embedded systems, early PCs | | **Multiple Buses (2 or 3)** | Dedicated buses for I/O, memory, system; bridged | **Medium** | **Higher** | Modern PCs (FSB, Northbridge/Southbridge) | | **Hierarchy / Point-to-Point** | CPU directly connected to memory via dedicated links; peripherals on shared bus | **High** | **Highest** | Modern desktops/laptops (QPI, HyperTransport, PCIe, Infinity Fabric) | **Bus Arbitration Methods (KTU 3-Mark Favorite):** 1. **Daisy Chaining** — priority fixed by physical chain order; simple but not flexible. 2. **Polling (Software)** — CPU queries each device in turn; slow. 3. **Independent Request & Grant** — separate request and grant lines per device; fast and fair. 4. **Centralized / Distributed Arbitration** — a dedicated bus arbiter (e.g., in chipset) decides. ## 2.3 Performance Metrics — The "Why" Behind the Numbers > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Performance Formula Sheet** | Symbol | Metric | Formula | Unit | Notes | |--------|--------|---------|------|-------| | $T$ | Clock Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds (s) | Reciprocal of clock frequency | | $f$ | Clock Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Often GHz in modern CPUs | | $CPI$ | Cycles per Instruction | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} CPI_i \times IC_i}{IC}$ | cycles/instr | Weighted average over instruction mix | | $CPU_{time}$ | CPU Execution Time | $CPU_{time} = IC \times CPI \times T$ | seconds | **THE** central performance equation | | $MIPS$ | Million Instr. per sec | $MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | millions | Higher = faster, but instruction set matters | | $MFLOPS$ | Million FP ops/sec | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{FP_{ops}}{Exec_{time} \times 10^{6}}$ | millions | For scientific workloads | | $Speedup$ | Amdahl's Speedup | $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | dimensionless | $f$ = parallel fraction, $n$ = speedup of parallel part | | $M_{max}$ | Max Addressable Memory | $M_{max} = 2^{a}$ | bytes | $a$ = address bus width in bits | | $Throughput$ | Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{Data\ transferred}{Time}$ | bytes/sec | Equals **width × frequency** for buses | | $P$ | Dynamic Power | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | watts | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = capacitance | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **CPU designers** minimize $CPU_{time}$ by attacking all three factors: reduce $IC$ (better ISA/compiler), reduce $CPI$ (pipelining, caches), reduce $T$ (smaller transistors, higher $f$). - **Embedded engineers** pick MCUs by balancing $MIPS$ per milliwatt — the "energy efficiency" frontier. - **Data-centre architects** use **Amdahl's Law** to decide how many cores to add before diminishing returns dominate. - **Memory and bus designers** ensure $BW \geq$ worst-case demand to avoid stalls. ## 2.4 System Performance — A Worked Conceptual View Consider a program with: - $IC = 100 \times 10^{6}$ instructions - $CPI = 2$ cycles/instruction - $f = 3$ GHz Then: $$CPU_{time} = IC \times CPI \times T = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 2 \times \dfrac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.0667 \text{ seconds} \approx 66.7 \text{ ms}$$ And its **MIPS rating**: $$MIPS = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 1500 \text{ MIPS}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why MIPS Alone is Misleading:** A RISC CPU doing 1500 MIPS may complete a benchmark *slower* than a CISC CPU doing 800 MIPS — because each "instruction" does less work. Hence KTU examiners prefer $CPU_{time}$ as the honest metric. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Execution Time Equation The most important single equation in computer architecture. We derive it from first principles. ### Step 1 — Define the *number of cycles* used The CPU executes $IC$ instructions. On average, each instruction takes $CPI$ clock cycles. $$\text{Total clock cycles} = IC \times CPI$$ ### Step 2 — Convert cycles to seconds Each clock cycle has period $T$ seconds (one tick of the system clock). So the total wall-clock time the CPU spends on this program is cycles × period: $$CPU_{time} = (\text{Total cycles}) \times T = IC \times CPI \times T$$ ### Step 3 — Express T as the reciprocal of frequency Since $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$: $$CPU_{time} = IC \times CPI \times \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$$ This is the **golden equation** of single-CPU performance. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** You can be given any *three* of the five variables ($IC$, $CPI$, $T$ or $f$, $CPU_{time}$, instruction count, or MIPS) and asked to find the other *two*. The examiner expects you to write the equation and rearrange — not plug in numbers mentally. ### Step 4 — Derive MIPS from the same equation Starting from $CPU_{time} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$, isolate the instruction count: $$IC = \dfrac{CPU_{time} \times f}{CPI}$$ Now *per second* the CPU completes $IC / CPU_{time}$ instructions. Multiplying by $10^{-6}$ to get millions: $$MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{CPU_{time} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$$ ### Step 5 — Effective CPI for mixed instruction classes For a program using $n$ classes of instructions, with $IC_i$ instructions of class $i$ and $CPI_i$ cycles per class-$i$ instruction: $$CPI_{effective} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i}{IC_{total}}$$ This is a **weighted average** of per-class CPIs, weighted by instruction count. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problems ### Problem 1 — Basic CPU Time **Given:** $IC = 500 \times 10^{6}$ instructions, $CPI = 3.2$, $f = 2.5$ GHz. **Find:** $CPU_{time}$, $MIPS$. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} T &= \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ ns} \\[6pt] CPU_{time} &= IC \times CPI \times T \\[4pt] &= 500 \times 10^{6} \times 3.2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt] &= 500 \times 3.2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt] &= 640 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.64 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ $$MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{3.2 \times 10^{6}} = 781.25 \text{ MIPS}$$ ### Problem 2 — Effective CPI and Total Time for a Mixed Workload **Given:** A program uses three instruction classes with: - Class A (Arithmetic): $IC_A = 200 \times 10^{6}$, $CPI_A = 1$ - Class B (Load/Store): $IC_B = 150 \times 10^{6}$, $CPI_B = 5$ - Class C (Branch): $IC_C = 50 \times 10^{6}$, $CPI_C = 2$ Clock frequency $f = 2$ GHz. Find $CPI_{effective}$, $CPU_{time}$, and $MIPS$. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} CPI_{eff} &= \dfrac{(200 \times 10^{6})(1) + (150 \times 10^{6})(5) + (50 \times 10^{6})(2)}{(200 + 150 + 50) \times 10^{6}} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{200 + 750 + 100}{400} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1050}{400} = 2.625 \text{ cycles/instruction} \end{aligned}$$ $$CPU_{time} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{eff}}{f} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6} \times 2.625}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.525 \text{ s}$$ $$MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI_{eff} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{9}}{2.625 \times 10^{6}} \approx 761.9 \text{ MIPS}$$ ### Problem 3 — Amdahl's Law Application **Given:** A program spends 80% of its time in a parallelizable part. With 4 processors, the parallel part runs 4× faster. Find overall speedup. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} Speedup &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{n}} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{4}} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} = \dfrac{1}{0.4} = 2.5 \end{aligned}$$ Even with 4 CPUs, the program only runs **2.5× faster** because the serial 20% dominates. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Always quote Amdahl's Law with the serial fraction explicitly — students who write only the formula but skip the substitution lose half marks. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed Control | Feature | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control | |---------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Implementation | Combinational logic gates | Microcode in ROM/Control Memory | | Speed | **Faster** (1 cycle per signal) | Slower (microinstruction fetch adds delay) | | Flexibility | **Low** (rewire logic to change) | **High** (rewrite microcode) | | Cost of design | **High** initial | Lower initial, easier to debug | | Used in | RISC processors, simple controllers | CISC processors (e.g., classic x86) | | Fault tolerance | Poor — single design error | Good — patchable microcode | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five Functional Units of a Computer This is the **single most important diagram** for KTU Module 1. You should be able to redraw it from memory in under 90 seconds. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INPUT UNIT
Keyboard Mouse Scanner"] --> B["MEMORY UNIT
RAM ROM Cache"] B --> C["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT
ALU and FPU"] C --> B B --> D["OUTPUT UNIT
Monitor Printer Speaker"] E["CONTROL UNIT
PC IR Decoder Timing"] -.->|Control Signals| A E -.->|Read Write Signals| B E -.->|Operation Select| C E -.->|Ready Strobe| D style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000 style D fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000 style E fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000 ``` **How to interpret for a 7-mark answer:** - **Solid arrows** = data flow (operands, results, instructions). - **Dotted arrows** = control flow (signals from CU directing the other units). - The **Memory Unit sits at the center** because the stored-program concept makes it the hub through which both instructions and data flow. ## 4.2 Single Shared-Bus Organization The simplest, cheapest, and slowest bus architecture — all units connect to **one common bus**. ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU
ALU CU Registers"] --> BUS["SYSTEM BUS
Data Address Control Lines"] MEM["MAIN MEMORY
RAM ROM"] --> BUS IO1["INPUT DEVICE
Keyboard"] --> BUS IO2["OUTPUT DEVICE
Monitor"] --> BUS BUS --> CPU BUS --> MEM BUS --> IO1 BUS --> IO2 style BUS fill:#ffe599,stroke:#806000,color:#000,stroke-width:3px style CPU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style MEM fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Only **one** unit can drive the bus at a time → the **bus arbitrator** (often inside the chipset/CPU) grants bus access. This is why high-performance systems use **multiple buses**. ## 4.3 Three-Bus Architecture (Modern Variant) Used in classic Northbridge/Southbridge and similar designs. ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU2["CPU"] -->|Local Bus| L1["L1 LOCAL BUS
High Speed"] L1 --> NB["NORTHBRIDGE
Memory Controller Hub"] NB -->|Memory Bus| MEM2["MAIN MEMORY DRAM"] NB -->|Graphics Port| GPU["GPU"] NB --> SB["SOUTHBRIDGE
IO Controller Hub"] SB -->|Peripheral Bus| USB["USB SATA AUDIO ETHERNET"] style L1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style NB fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 style SB fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4d2a6b,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle Flowchart ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 s1["START"] --> s2["FETCH
IR to Memory Address
via MAR and PC"] s2 --> s3["DECODE
Decoder examines Opcode
CU generates control signals"] s3 --> s4{"Operands
in memory?"} s4 -->|Yes| s5["EVALUATE ADDRESS
Compute effective address"] s4 -->|No| s6["FETCH OPERANDS
From registers"] s5 --> s7["FETCH OPERANDS
From memory via MDR"] s6 --> s8["EXECUTE
ALU performs operation"] s7 --> s8 s8 --> s9{"Result to
memory?"} s9 -->|Yes| s10["STORE RESULT
Write back to memory"] s9 -->|No| s11["STORE RESULT
Write back to register"] s10 --> s12["UPDATE PC
PC to next instruction"] s11 --> s12 s12 --> s2 ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the instruction cycle", write the **fetch phase** (PC → MAR, Memory → MDR → IR, PC+1) and the **execute phase** (Decode → Operands → ALU → Store) as two clear numbered lists. Diagrams earn 1–2 extra marks even on theory questions. ## 4.5 Processor–Memory Speed Gap (Memory Wall) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CPU
Cycle ~ 0.3 ns"] ---|Gap of 100x to 1000x| B["DRAM Main Memory
Access ~ 50 to 100 ns"] C["L1 Cache
~ 1 to 2 ns"] -->|Bridge| A C --> B D["L2 L3 Cache
~ 5 to 20 ns"] -->|Bridge| A D --> B style A fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000 style B fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000 ``` This is the **memory wall** — the reason caches exist and why the five-unit model is paired with hierarchical storage in real designs. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1.** *List the five basic functional units of a computer and state the function of each in one sentence.* **Model Answer:** The five basic functional units of a computer are: 1. **Input Unit** — accepts data and instructions from the external environment and converts them into binary form. 2. **Memory Unit** — stores data, instructions, intermediate results, and final output for fast retrieval by the CPU. 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparison). 4. **Control Unit (CU)** — directs and coordinates the activities of all other units by generating appropriate control and timing signals. 5. **Output Unit** — conveys the processed results from the computer to the outside world in a human-readable or machine-recognizable form. > **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for correct list + 1 mark for any 2–3 unit functions + 1 mark for completeness. --- > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2.** *Explain the concept of the "stored program" in a computer. What is the von Neumann bottleneck?* **Model Answer:** The **stored-program concept** is a design principle in which the program instructions and the data on which they operate are both stored in the same main memory. The CPU fetches instructions sequentially (using the Program Counter), decodes them, and executes them — all referring to the same memory space. This concept, formalized in the **IAS machine (1946)** by John von Neumann and his team, made computers general-purpose rather than hard-wired for one task. The **von Neumann bottleneck** refers to the limitation that both instruction fetch and data access must share the **same single bus** connecting CPU and memory. As CPUs became faster, the memory bus became a choke point, because the CPU frequently has to wait for the slower memory. This bottleneck motivates modern designs such as **separate instruction and data caches, multi-bus architectures, and Harvard architecture variants**. > **Mark Allocation:** 1.5 marks for stored-program concept, 1 mark for bottleneck cause, 0.5 marks for modern mitigations. ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** Question A (14 Marks) **Q. (a)** *Draw the block diagram of a basic computer showing its five functional units. Explain the function of each unit in detail.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **[Diagram — 2 marks]** The block diagram is the standard five-unit model: - **Input Unit** ↔ **Memory Unit** ↔ **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** ↔ **Output Unit** - **Control Unit (CU)** sits in the centre sending control signals (dotted arrows) to all other units. - Solid arrows show the data/address path. **[Explanation — 5 marks]** 1. **Input Unit:** Receives raw data and programs from external devices. The interface circuit decodes the device's address, synchronizes the speed mismatch between the device and CPU using buffers, and places the converted binary data on the data bus. Examples include keyboard, mouse, scanner, and sensors. 2. **Memory Unit:** Stores both programs and data in binary form. It is organized as a linear array of addressable locations. The Memory Address Register (MAR) holds the address to access; the Memory Data Register (MDR) buffers the data being read/written. It includes both primary (cache, RAM) and secondary (disk, SSD) storage in a hierarchical design. 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all data transformations. Its arithmetic sub-unit handles integer and floating-point operations (with the FPU); its logic sub-unit handles bitwise operations and comparisons. It accepts operands from registers, processes them, and outputs the result to a register or memory, while also updating condition flags (zero, carry, sign, overflow) in the status register. 4. **Control Unit (CU):** Acts as the "conductor" of the computer. It fetches each instruction from memory (using PC), decodes it via the instruction decoder, and emits the sequence of control signals needed to coordinate the ALU, memory, registers, and I/O. It is implemented either as **hardwired logic** (fast, rigid) or **microprogrammed control** (slower, flexible, patchable). 5. **Output Unit:** Converts binary results into a form usable by humans or downstream devices. The display controller, printer driver, audio DAC, etc., interpret the data bus contents and drive the physical output device. Like the input unit, it has buffers to handle the speed difference between the device and the CPU/bus. --- **Q. (b)** *Define the term "bus" in a computer system. Explain the three types of buses with neat diagrams. What is bus arbitration?***(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **[Definition — 1 mark]** A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of one or more sets of wires that connect two or more components of a computer for the purpose of transferring data, addresses, and control signals. It is a *broadcast* medium: only one device can drive it at a time, while multiple devices listen. **[Three Bus Types — 4 marks]** | Bus Type | Function | Width Determines | Direction | |----------|----------|------------------|-----------| | **Data Bus** | Carries the actual operands/instructions being transferred | **Data word size** of the machine (8, 16, 32, 64 bits) | Bidirectional (read/write) | | **Address Bus** | Carries the memory or I/O address of the source/destination | **Maximum addressable memory:** $M_{max} = 2^a$ where $a$ = address bits | Unidirectional (CPU → memory/I/O) | | **Control Bus** | Carries command and timing signals | Number of distinct commands | Bidirectional (e.g., READ, WRITE, INTERRUPT, BUS REQUEST) | A simple text-based block diagram: ``` +-----+ +-----+ +-----+ | CPU |---DATA--| MEM |---DATA--| I/O | | |---ADDR--| |---ADDR--| | | |---CTRL--| |---CTRL--| | +-----+ +-----+ +-----+ ``` **[Bus Arbitration — 2 marks]** When multiple devices wish to use the bus simultaneously, a **bus arbitration** mechanism decides which device gets control. Methods include: - **Daisy Chaining:** A serial priority chain; the device closest to the arbiter has the highest priority. - **Polling:** The CPU queries each device in turn ("Device 1, do you need the bus?"). - **Independent Requests:** Each device has its own Bus Request and Bus Grant line, allowing parallel arbitration. - **Centralized Arbitration:** A dedicated bus controller (arbiter) collects all requests and grants via a priority encoder. --- ### **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** Question B (14 Marks) **Q. (a)** *Explain the basic operational concept of a computer. Describe the fetch–decode–execute cycle with a flowchart.***(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **[Basic Operational Concept — 2 marks]** The computer performs its work by repeatedly **fetching, decoding, and executing** instructions from main memory. A program is a sequence of machine instructions stored in memory. The CPU contains a **Program Counter (PC)** that holds the address of the next instruction to execute. After each instruction, the PC is updated to the next sequential address (or a branch target). This is the **instruction cycle** (or **fetch–execute cycle**). **[Fetch Phase — 2 marks]** 1. The contents of **PC** are copied to the **MAR**. 2. The **Control Unit** issues a **READ** signal on the control bus. 3. Memory places the instruction on the data bus, which is loaded into the **MDR** and then into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. 4. The **PC is incremented** (PC ← PC + 1, or PC ← PC + instruction size in bytes) so it points to the next instruction. **[Decode Phase — 1 mark]** The **Instruction Decoder** examines the opcode bits in the IR. The CU consults a hardwired or microprogrammed table and generates the **micro-operation sequence** (one set of control signals per clock cycle) required to execute this instruction. **[Execute Phase — 2 marks]** 1. **Operand fetch** — read source operands from registers or memory (using effective address computation if addressing mode requires it). 2. **Operation** — the ALU (or FPU) performs the required arithmetic, logic, shift, or branch operation. 3. **Result store** — write the result to a destination register or memory location. 4. **Status update** — condition flags (Z, C, S, V) are updated if the instruction is a compare or arithmetic op. 5. **Next PC** — for branches, PC is overwritten with the branch target; otherwise the increment from the fetch phase stands. The cycle then **repeats from the Fetch phase**, possibly millions to billions of times per second. **(The student should also draw the state-diagram flowchart from SECTION_4.4 — full marks only with the diagram.)** --- **Q. (b)** *A processor runs at 2.4 GHz. A benchmark program consists of 400 million instructions, with the following instruction mix:* - *Class A: 40% of instructions, CPI = 1* - *Class B: 30% of instructions, CPI = 3* - *Class C: 20% of instructions, CPI = 5* - *Class D: 10% of instructions, CPI = 7* *Compute (i) the effective CPI, (ii) the total execution time, and (iii) the MIPS rating of the processor.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Effective CPI — 2 marks** Number of instructions per class: - $IC_A = 0.40 \times 400 = 160$ million - $IC_B = 0.30 \times 400 = 120$ million - $IC_C = 0.20 \times 400 = 80$ million - $IC_D = 0.10 \times 400 = 40$ million $$CPI_{eff} = \sum (Fraction_i \times CPI_i)$$ $$\begin{aligned} CPI_{eff} &= (0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 7) \\ &= 0.40 + 0.90 + 1.00 + 0.70 \\ &= 3.00 \text{ cycles/instruction} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Total Execution Time — 3 marks** $$CPU_{time} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{eff}}{f}$$ $$\begin{aligned} CPU_{time} &= \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6} \times 3.00}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} \\ &= \dfrac{1200 \times 10^{6}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} \\ &= 0.5 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) MIPS Rating — 2 marks** $$MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI_{eff} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{3.00 \times 10^{6}} = 800 \text{ MIPS}$$ **Final Answers:** - Effective CPI = **3.0** - CPU time = **0.5 s** (500 ms) - MIPS = **800** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting to weight CPI:** Many students compute the simple arithmetic mean $\frac{1+3+5+7}{4}=4.0$ instead of the weighted average. The weighted average is *always* required when instruction mix is given. **−2 marks** for this error. > - **Unit mismatch:** MIPS requires $CPI \times 10^6$, not $CPI \times 10^3$ or just $CPI$. **−1 mark** for wrong unit conversion. > - **Mixing up T and f:** Some students substitute $T = 1/f$ into $IC \times CPI \times T$ and then forget to convert $T$ to seconds (if $f$ is in GHz, $T$ is in nanoseconds). **−1 mark** for unit inconsistency. > - **Skipping the diagram in Q1(a):** A textual description alone earns only 5/7 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram with input → memory → ALU → output and the control unit feeding into all units via dotted arrows. **−2 marks** for omitting the diagram. > - **Not writing the FDE cycle as a numbered list:** The examiner expects explicit mention of *PC, MAR, MDR, IR* and the role of each register. Vague descriptions lose 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Basic Structure of Computers (Module 1)** - ✅ **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Control, Output — must be memorized verbatim and sketcherable in 90 seconds. - ✅ **Stored-program concept:** instructions and data share the same memory → von Neumann architecture → von Neumann bottleneck. - ✅ **Bus types:** Data (bidirectional, width = word size), Address (unidirectional, width = $\log_2$ memory size), Control (timing & commands). - ✅ **Maximum addressable memory:** $M_{max} = 2^{a}$ bytes (or words), where $a$ = address bus width in bits. - ✅ **Bus arbitration methods:** Daisy chain, Polling, Independent Request, Centralized arbitration. - ✅ **Hardwired vs. Microprogrammed control:** Hardwired = fast, rigid (used in RISC); Microprogrammed = flexible, slower (used in CISC). - ✅ **Golden performance equation:** $CPU_{time} = IC \times CPI \times T = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$ — must be able to derive from scratch. - ✅ **MIPS formula:** $MIPS = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ — and its limitation: doesn't account for instruction power. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$ — diminishing returns as $n$ grows for fixed $f$. - ✅ **Power equation:** $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ — voltage scaling dominates; reason for low-power CPU designs. - ✅ **Memory hierarchy (fast → slow):** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → Main DRAM → SSD → HDD → Tape/Cloud. Each tier is **larger and slower** than the one above; locality of reference makes this efficient. - ✅ **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle:** PC → MAR → Memory → MDR → IR → Decoder → Control signals → ALU/Registers → Result store → Increment PC. Re-draw the state diagram from memory. - ✅ **Modern I/O and bus evolution:** ISA → PCI → PCIe; FSB → QPI/UPI/Infinity Fabric; Northbridge/Southbridge → SoC with on-die memory controllers. - ✅ **For numericals:** always carry units through, show substitution explicitly, and use the $10^{6}$ (or $10^{9}$) conversion factor for MIPS and GHz consistently. - ✅ **Diagram rule:** every long answer on this topic MUST have a labeled block diagram — no exceptions.

topics.title: Functional Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Functional Units # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Functional Units of a Computer (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > A digital computer system is an integrated assembly of inter-operating **functional units** that work in synchronized harmony to perform data processing, data storage, data movement, and control operations. The four classical functional units, as defined in the **Patterson & Hennessy / Hamacher-Vranesic** computer architecture framework adopted by KTU 2024, are: (1) **Input Unit**, (2) **Output Unit**, (3) **Memory (Storage) Unit**, and (4) **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — which itself is subdivided into the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Control Unit (CU)**, and **Registers**. These units are interconnected via **system buses** (Data, Address, and Control buses) that act as shared communication highways, enabling the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle that defines every modern computer. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Computer as a Human Restaurant > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine a computer as a **highly automated, ultra-fast restaurant kitchen**: > > - **Input Unit** = The **waiter** taking customer orders (keyboard, mouse, scanner, network card). > - **Memory Unit** = The **pantry and refrigerator** where raw ingredients and partially prepared dishes are stored (RAM, ROM, Cache, Hard Disk). > - **CPU (ALU)** = The **chef** who actually cooks, mixes, and assembles the dish (arithmetic and logic operations). > - **CPU (Control Unit)** = The **head chef / floor manager** who decides the order of operations, reads recipes, and tells everyone what to do next. > - **Output Unit** = The **serving counter** that delivers the finished dish to the customer (monitor, printer, speaker). > - **Buses** = The **service corridor / conveyor belts** connecting all stations. > - **Registers** = The chef's **hands and immediate cutting board** — ultra-fast but tiny in capacity. Just as a restaurant cannot function if any one of these elements breaks down, a computer fails if any functional unit is missing or malfunctioning. ## 1.3 Primary Operations Performed by a Computer System The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **four fundamental operations** that any computer, regardless of scale, must perform: | Operation | Functional Unit Responsible | Engineering Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Data Movement** | Input/Output & Buses | Transfer data between units | | **Data Storage** | Memory Unit | Hold data for later retrieval | | **Data Processing** | ALU | Arithmetic & logical transformations | | **Control** | Control Unit | Orchestrate all other operations | ## 1.4 The Central Processing Unit (CPU) — The Brain > [!NOTE] > **CPU Definition (KTU Module 1 Highlight):** > The **CPU** is the component that interprets instructions, performs arithmetic/logic operations on data, and orchestrates the flow of information through the other functional units. It is fabricated on a single **Integrated Circuit (IC)** chip and is sometimes called the **microprocessor**. Modern CPUs contain millions to billions of transistors etched onto a silicon die using **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** technology. The CPU is structurally composed of: - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all arithmetic (`+`, `−`, `×`, `÷`) and logical (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `XOR`, shifts, compares) operations. - **Control Unit (CU):** Generates timing and control signals that direct the flow of data and synchronize the operations of all units. - **Register File:** A small set of high-speed memory elements (8 to 64 in typical ISAs) used for temporary data storage inside the CPU. - **Internal CPU Bus:** A high-bandwidth internal interconnect (often 64-bit or 128-bit wide in modern processors) connecting ALU, CU, and registers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Logical Units — Step-by-Step Functional Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes a computer system into **five logical units** (Input, Output, Memory, ALU, Control). Each is examined below in operational depth. ### 2.1.1 Input Unit - **Function:** Converts external data (typed text, mouse clicks, sensor signals, network packets) into a **binary form** (0s and 1s) the CPU can process. - **Key Devices:** Keyboard, Mouse, Touchscreen, Scanner, Microphone, MICR, OCR, Barcode reader, Network Interface Card (NIC). - **Interface Logic:** Each input device is paired with a **device controller** (e.g., keyboard controller) and a **buffer** that handles speed mismatches between the slow electromechanical device and the nanosecond-speed CPU. ### 2.1.2 Memory (Storage) Unit Memory is a hierarchical, layered storage system. KTU expects you to know this hierarchy cold: | Level | Capacity (Typical) | Access Time | Cost per bit | Volatility | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Registers** | 16 – 64 registers (~64 B) | **< 1 ns** | Highest | Volatile | | **L1 Cache** | 32 – 128 KB | ~1 – 2 ns | Very High | Volatile | | **L2 Cache** | 256 KB – 1 MB | ~3 – 10 ns | High | Volatile | | **L3 Cache** | 4 – 32 MB | ~10 – 20 ns | Moderate | Volatile | | **Main Memory (RAM)** | 4 – 32 GB | ~50 – 100 ns | Moderate | Volatile | | **SSD (Flash)** | 256 GB – 2 TB | ~50 – 150 µs | Low | **Non-Volatile** | | **HDD (Magnetic)** | 500 GB – 10 TB | ~5 – 10 ms | Very Low | **Non-Volatile** | > [!NOTE] > **Two Fundamental Memory Types for KTU:** > 1. **RAM (Random Access Memory):** Volatile; loses contents on power-off. Subdivided into **SRAM** (used for caches) and **DRAM** (used for main memory). > 2. **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Non-volatile; retains contents without power. Includes PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash. ### 2.1.3 Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) - Performs **arithmetic operations** (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and **logical operations** (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shift, rotate, compare). - Operands are fetched from **registers**, and the result is stored back into a **destination register**. - The ALU sets **flag bits** in a **status register** (also called the **Program Status Word / PSW**). Common flags include: **Zero (Z)**, **Carry (C)**, **Sign (N)**, **Overflow (V)**. ### 2.1.4 Output Unit - Reverse of the input unit: takes binary data from the CPU/Memory and converts it into a human-perceivable form. - **Key Devices:** Monitor, Printer, Speaker, Plotter, LEDs, Projector. - **Hard Copy** output (paper) vs. **Soft Copy** output (screen) is a standard KTU distinction. ### 2.1.5 Control Unit (CU) > [!IMPORTANT] > The CU is the **master orchestrator**. It does not process or store data itself; it directs all other units. It fetches instructions, decodes them, and issues **micro-operations** (micro-instructions) on precise clock edges. **Two primary implementation styles of the Control Unit (high-yield for KTU):** | Style | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Hardwired Control** | Combinational logic gates + flip-flops; uses a Finite State Machine (FSM) | **Fastest**, no fetch overhead | Rigid; hard to modify | | **Microprogrammed Control** | A "microprogram" stored in a **Control Memory (ROM)** issues microinstructions | **Flexible**, easy to update, simpler design | Slower than hardwired | ## 2.2 System Buses — The Communication Backbone A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of **multiple parallel wires**. There are **three fundamental bus types**: | Bus | Width (typical) | Function | |---|---|---| | **Data Bus** | 32, 64, or 128 bits | Carries the actual data being transferred (bidirectional) | | **Address Bus** | 32 or 64 bits | Carries the memory address of the source/destination (unidirectional: CPU → Memory/I/O) | | **Control Bus** | ~10 – 20 lines | Carries timing and command signals (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock, Reset) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles the **high-yield performance equations** that govern computer functional units. These are the most frequently asked derivation and numerical problems in KTU ESE. | # | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | $T_{CPU}$: CPU time (s), $IC$: Instruction Count, $CPI$: Cycles per Instruction, $T_{clk}$: Clock cycle time (s) | Computes total execution time — the **primary performance metric** | | 2 | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | $f_{clk}$: Clock frequency in **Hertz (Hz)** | Converts cycle time to frequency | | 3 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | $MIPS$: Millions of Instructions Per Second | Quick relative performance comparison | | 4 | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{N_{FP}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | $N_{FP}$: Number of floating-point operations | Used for scientific / HPC workloads | | 5 | $Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Quantifies the benefit of an enhancement | | 6 | $Amdahl's\ Law: Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$ | $f$: fraction enhanced, $S$: speedup of enhanced part | Limits performance of any single-component improvement | | 7 | $CPI_{new} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i \bigg/ IC_{total}$ | $IC_i$: count of instruction class $i$ | Weighted average CPI for mixed instruction mixes | | 8 | $BW = \dfrac{Data\ Transferred}{Transfer\ Time}$ | $BW$: Bandwidth in **bytes/sec** or **bits/sec** | Bus and memory throughput analysis | | 9 | $Power_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$: switching activity, $C$: capacitance, $V$: voltage, $f$: frequency | CMOS power dissipation in the ALU/registers | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The `|` (vertical bar) and `÷` symbols are sometimes lost in OCR scanning of answer sheets. **Always write out** fractions, e.g., "1 / (1 − f) + (f / S)" instead of "1 \vert (1-f) + f \vert S". ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Data Centers:** Functional unit balance determines **Performance per Watt** — the modern metric of choice. - **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Designers shrink the I/O unit and remove bulky output devices to fit microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) into tiny sensors. - **HPC / Supercomputers:** Specialized functional units like **GPUs** and **TPUs** accelerate specific operations, validated by Amdahl's Law. - **Fault Tolerant Systems:** Redundant ALUs, ECC memory, and mirrored storage implement reliability directly at the functional unit level. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This section derives the **two most important KTU-expected equations** from the Functional Units topic: (a) the **CPU Time Equation** and (b) **Amdahl's Law**. These are routinely asked for 7 to 14 marks in KTU ESE. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The CPU Execution Time Equation We wish to derive a closed-form expression for the **total execution time** of a program in terms of the program's instruction count, the processor's clock rate, and the average number of clock cycles per instruction. ### Step 1 — Define Clock Cycle Time The **clock cycle time** $T_{clk}$ is the duration of one tick of the processor's master clock. If the clock has a frequency $f_{clk}$ in **Hertz (Hz)**, the cycle time is the reciprocal: $$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$$ *This converts frequency (cycles per second) into time per cycle (seconds per cycle) by inverting the dimension.* ### Step 2 — Define Cycles Per Instruction (CPI) For a given instruction class $i$, the processor consumes a fixed number of clock cycles $CPI_i$ to complete it. For an entire program containing $n$ instruction classes with respective instruction counts $IC_i$, the **average CPI** is the total cycles divided by the total instruction count: $$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i}{IC_{total}}$$ *We weight each class's CPI by the number of times its instructions appear, then normalize by the total instruction count.* ### Step 3 — Count Total Clock Cycles The total number of clock cycles the program consumes is simply the product of the **average CPI** and the **total instruction count**: $$Cycles_{total} = IC_{total} \times CPI_{avg}$$ *Each instruction, on average, takes $CPI_{avg}$ cycles; multiplying gives the aggregate cycle consumption.* ### Step 4 — Convert Cycles to Time The total execution time is the product of the total clock cycles and the duration of each cycle: $$T_{CPU} = Cycles_{total} \times T_{clk}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Consolidated Form Substituting Step 3 into Step 4 yields the **canonical CPU performance equation**, a formula KTU examiners love: $$T_{CPU} = IC_{total} \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{IC_{total} \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}}$$ *This is the most important single equation in the course: it shows that to reduce execution time, we can reduce the instruction count (better compiler / ISA), reduce the CPI (better pipeline / cache), or raise the clock frequency.* ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Amdahl's Law **Goal:** Find the overall speedup when only a *fraction* $f$ of a program's execution time is enhanced by a factor of $S$. ### Step 1 — Decompose Original Execution Time Let $T_{old}$ be the total original execution time (normalized to 1 unit): $$T_{old} = 1$$ We split this into the **enhanced portion** of duration $f$ and the **unaffected portion** of duration $(1 - f)$: $$T_{old} = f + (1 - f)$$ *The original time is partitioned into two non-overlapping parts that sum to the whole.* ### Step 2 — Apply Speedup to Enhanced Portion The enhanced portion is now $\dfrac{f}{S}$ faster, while the rest of the program remains at its original speed: $$T_{new} = \dfrac{f}{S} + (1 - f)$$ *The enhanced fraction is divided by the speedup factor S; the unenhanced fraction is unchanged.* ### Step 3 — Compute the Speedup Ratio Overall speedup is the ratio of the old time to the new time: $$Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{f}{S} + (1 - f)}$$ ### Step 4 — Canonical Amdahl Form Rearranging the denominator into a single common denominator: $$Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** As $S \to \infty$ (e.g., infinitely fast ALU), the maximum achievable speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$. This is the **fundamental limit** of incremental improvements — making a single unit infinitely fast can only speed up the fraction of work it performs. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A program has 40% integer arithmetic instructions (CPI = 1), 30% data transfer instructions (CPI = 2), and 30% floating-point instructions (CPI = 4). The clock rate is **500 MHz**. Find the average CPI, MIPS rating, and execution time for a program of 10 million instructions. ### Solution $$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{0.4(1) + 0.3(2) + 0.3(4)}{1} = 0.4 + 0.6 + 1.2 = 2.2$$ $$MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{6}}{2.2 \times 10^{6}} = 227.27\ MIPS$$ $$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{10^{7} \times 2.2}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 0.044\ s = 44\ ms$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Components to Operations | Functional Unit | Data Movement | Data Storage | Data Processing | Control | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Input Unit** | ✔ (Input → Memory) | — | — | — | | **Output Unit** | ✔ (Memory → Output) | — | — | — | | **Memory Unit** | ✔ (Read/Write) | ✔ (Primary storage) | — | — | | **ALU** | — | — | ✔ (Arithmetic + Logic) | — | | **Control Unit** | — | — | — | ✔ (Orchestrates all) | | **Registers** | ✔ (Fastest internal) | ✔ (Fastest storage) | — | — (Instruction pointer) | | **System Buses** | ✔ (Connect all) | — | — | ✔ (Control signals) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Five Functional Units of a Computer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IOP["INPUT / OUTPUT PERIPHERALS"] IN["Input Devices
Keyboard Mouse Scanner NIC"] OUT["Output Devices
Monitor Printer Speaker"] end subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT CPU"] CU["Control Unit CU
Hardwired or Microprogrammed"] ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU
Add Subtract AND OR Compare Shift"] RF["Register File
PC IR AC DR TEMP FLAGS"] INTCPU["Internal CPU Bus
64 bit or 128 bit"] end MEM["Main Memory RAM ROM
Cache L1 L2 L3"] subgraph BUSES["SYSTEM BUSES"] DBUS["DATA BUS
Bidirectional 32 64 128 bit"] ABUS["ADDRESS BUS
Unidirectional 32 64 bit"] CBUS["CONTROL BUS
Read Write Interrupt Clock Reset"] end IN -- CBUS read request --> CBUS OUT -- CBUS write request --> CBUS MEM -- DBUS --> DBUS MEM -- ABUS --> ABUS CPU -- DBUS --> DBUS CPU -- ABUS --> ABUS CPU -- CBUS --> CBUS CU -- micro operations --> ALU CU -- control lines --> RF RF -- operands --> ALU ALU -- result --> RF CU -- orchestrates --> INTCPU ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PC holds address of next instruction"] --> B["Step 1 FETCH
Address sent on ABUS
Memory returns instruction on DBUS
Instruction loaded into IR"] B --> C["Step 2 DECODE
CU decodes opcode in IR
Control signals generated"] C --> D{"Step 3 EXECUTE
Determine instruction class"} D -- Arithmetic --> E["ALU activates
Operands from RF
Result stored in RF
Flags updated in PSW"] D -- Memory Access --> F["Address on ABUS
Data on DBUS
Memory or I O read write"] D -- Control Branch --> G["Target address computed
PC updated to branch target"] E --> H["Step 4 STORE
Result written back to RF or Memory"] F --> H G --> H H --> I["PC incremented to next instruction"] I --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** The above two diagrams should be reproduced in pencil on the answer sheet using rectangles for units, arrows for data flow, and clear labels for bus types. Mermaid is a visualization aid here; the KTU valuation key awards full marks for clean, fully labeled block diagrams. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE** patterns. Marks are tagged per the KTU question paper blueprint. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern] **(3 Marks)** *List the five basic functional units of a computer and state the primary function of each in a single sentence.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Input Unit** — Accepts data and instructions from the external world and converts them into binary form for processing. 2. **Memory (Storage) Unit** — Stores data, instructions, and intermediate results during and after processing (RAM, ROM, caches). 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic (`+`, `−`, `×`, `÷`) and logical (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `XOR`, shift) operations on data. 4. **Control Unit (CU)** — Fetches, decodes, and sequences the execution of instructions by issuing control signals to all other units. 5. **Output Unit** — Converts processed binary results into a human-perceivable form (text, image, sound, hard copy). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] **(3 Marks)** *Differentiate between hardwired and microprogrammed control units. Give one advantage of each.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Hardwired Control Unit | Microprogrammed Control Unit | |---|---|---| | **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates + flip-flops forming a Finite State Machine | Microinstructions stored in a Control Memory (ROM) | | **Speed** | **Faster** — direct logic, no microinstruction fetch | Slower — extra fetch-decode of microinstructions | | **Flexibility** | **Rigid** — modification requires redesign of hardware | **Flexible** — only the microprogram in ROM needs to be rewritten | | **Complexity** | High for complex ISAs | Manageable for complex ISAs | | **Used In** | RISC processors, high-speed cores (e.g., modern ARM cores) | CISC processors (e.g., early Intel x86), educational designs | **Advantage of Hardwired:** Highest possible execution speed. **Advantage of Microprogrammed:** Easy to modify, debug, and extend the instruction set. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Question (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Part B carries **14 marks** and allows **internal choice**. The two alternative question choices below are **completely independent** (KTU 2024 pattern). --- ### 🔵 QUESTION A — Functional Units & Bus Architecture **[KTU ESE — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **basic functional units of a computer system** and explain the role of the **system buses** in interconnecting them. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** A digital computer is organized into five logically distinct functional units that operate cooperatively: 1. **Input Unit:** The interface between the user and the machine. It accepts raw data (keystrokes, mouse coordinates, sensor voltages, network packets) and converts them into a binary-coded form using a device controller and buffer. Common devices include keyboards, mice, scanners, and Network Interface Cards (NIC). 2. **Memory (Storage) Unit:** A hierarchical storage system holding both **data and instructions**. The hierarchy spans (from fastest to slowest): **CPU registers → L1/L2/L3 cache → Main Memory (RAM) → Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)**. Main memory is **byte-addressable volatile RAM**; the BIOS and firmware reside in **non-volatile ROM/Flash**. 3. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** The computational core. It receives operands from registers, executes the decoded operation (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, AND, OR, XOR, shift, compare), and writes the result back. It also updates the **status flags** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) in the **Program Status Word (PSW)**. 4. **Control Unit (CU):** The "conductor" of the orchestra. It fetches each instruction from memory, decodes its opcode, and emits precisely timed control signals that activate the ALU, registers, memory, and buses in the correct sequence. Implemented as either **hardwired (FSM)** or **microprogrammed (control store)**. 5. **Output Unit:** The reverse of the input unit. It converts binary results into human-perceivable forms — text/images on a monitor, paper from a printer, or audio from speakers. Outputs are categorized as **soft copy** (screen) or **hard copy** (paper). **Role of System Buses:** - **Data Bus:** A bidirectional highway carrying the actual operands, instructions, and results. Its width (e.g., 64 bits) determines the largest datum transferable in one cycle. - **Address Bus:** A unidirectional bus driven by the CPU, carrying the memory or I/O address of the source/destination for the current transaction. Its width determines the maximum addressable memory: an $n$-bit address bus can access $2^n$ locations. - **Control Bus:** Carries timing and command signals — `MEMREAD`, `MEMWRITE`, `IOREAD`, `IOWRITE`, `INTR`, `BUSREQ`, `CLK`, `RESET` — that coordinate the transaction. Together, the three buses form the **shared communication backbone** of the system. All units connect to the bus via **bus drivers/receivers** with tri-state buffers to avoid electrical contention. **(b)** Explain the **fetch–decode–execute cycle** of a CPU. Show how the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Instruction Register (IR)**, and **ALU** participate in each phase. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** The **Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE) cycle** is the heart-beat of every stored-program computer. It is repeated continuously from power-on to power-off. **Phase 1 — FETCH:** - The **PC** holds the address of the next instruction to be executed. Its contents are placed on the **address bus**, and a `MEMREAD` signal is asserted on the **control bus**. - Main memory decodes the address and returns the instruction word on the **data bus**. - The CPU copies this word into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. - Simultaneously, the **PC is incremented** (PC ← PC + instruction_size) so it points to the next sequential instruction, ready for the next cycle. **Phase 2 — DECODE:** - The **Control Unit** examines the **opcode field** of the instruction in the IR. - For a typical RISC-style instruction, the CU identifies: (i) the operation, (ii) the source register addresses, (iii) the destination register address, and (iv) any immediate/offset fields. - The CU generates the necessary **control signals** that will activate the appropriate data paths and functional units. **Phase 3 — EXECUTE (and MEMORY / WRITEBACK):** - **For an arithmetic/logic instruction:** The CU instructs the register file to output the named source operands. The **ALU** performs the operation. The result is written back to the destination register, and the **PSW flags** (Z, C, N, V) are updated. - **For a memory instruction (LOAD/STORE):** The ALU computes an effective address (base + offset). This address is placed on the address bus, and a `MEMREAD` or `MEMWRITE` is issued to access main memory. - **For a branch/jump instruction:** The ALU compares values (for conditional branches) or simply moves a target address into the PC, causing the next fetch to come from a non-sequential location. **Cycle Repeats:** The PC now points to the next instruction, and the cycle re-enters Phase 1. **Role Summary Table:** | Register/Unit | Role in FDE | |---|---| | **Program Counter (PC)** | Holds the address of the next instruction; incremented each cycle | | **Instruction Register (IR)** | Latches the fetched instruction so the CU can decode it | | **Memory Address Register (MAR)** | Holds the address currently being sent on the address bus | | **Memory Data Register (MDR)** | Buffers data being transferred to/from data bus | | **ALU** | Performs effective-address calculation and all data transformations | | **Control Unit** | Orchestrates timing and signal generation for all sub-operations | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):** > - **Don't confuse** the **Data Register (DR)** with the **Data Bus** — the DR is a CPU-internal storage cell, while the bus is a *path*, not a storage. > - **Never** describe the FDE cycle as a single atomic step; examiners *require* the three explicit phases with register-level detail. > - **Always label** the buses in your diagram. A diagram without `Data Bus / Address Bus / Control Bus` labels loses **2 marks** outright. --- ### 🟢 QUESTION B — Performance Metrics (Alternative Choice) **[KTU ESE — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Derive the **CPU execution time equation** in terms of instruction count, CPI, and clock cycle time. A processor runs at **2.5 GHz** with a program containing **2 × 10⁹ instructions** and an **average CPI of 1.4**. Compute (i) the CPU execution time, (ii) the MIPS rating. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Let the total program consist of $n$ instruction classes, with the $i$-th class containing $IC_i$ instructions, each requiring $CPI_i$ clock cycles. The total number of clock cycles consumed is: $$Cycles_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i$$ The average CPI is the total cycles divided by the total instruction count $IC_{total} = \sum IC_i$: $$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IC_i \times CPI_i}{IC_{total}}$$ Each clock cycle has a duration of $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{clk}$ seconds. Hence the total execution time is: $$T_{CPU} = Cycles_{total} \times T_{clk} = IC_{total} \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{IC_{total} \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}}$$ **Numerical Computation:** **Given:** $f_{clk} = 2.5\ \text{GHz} = 2.5 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Hz}$, $IC = 2 \times 10^{9}$, $CPI_{avg} = 1.4$. **(i) CPU Execution Time:** $$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{9} \times 1.4}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{9}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 1.12\ \text{s}$$ **(ii) MIPS Rating:** $$MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{9}}{1.12 \times 10^{6}} = 1785.71 \approx 1785.71\ MIPS$$ Alternatively: $$MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{1.4 \times 10^{6}} = 1785.71\ MIPS$$ **(b)** State and prove **Amdahl's Law**. A system spends **40%** of its time in a function that is to be enhanced by a speedup factor of **5**. Find the overall speedup. If the same system spends **80%** in the same function enhanced by the same factor, recompute the speedup. Comment on the results. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Statement (Amdahl's Law):** > The overall performance improvement gained by enhancing one part of a system is limited by the fraction of execution time that part originally occupies. **Proof:** Let the original execution time $T_{old} = 1$ (normalized). Let $f$ be the fraction of time spent in the enhanced portion and $S$ be the speedup of that portion. The original time decomposes as: $$T_{old} = f + (1 - f)$$ After the enhancement, the enhanced part takes time $f / S$, and the rest takes $(1 - f)$. The new execution time is: $$T_{new} = \dfrac{f}{S} + (1 - f)$$ The overall speedup is: $$Speedup = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$$ **Case 1: f = 0.4, S = 5:** $$Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.4) + \dfrac{0.4}{5}} = \dfrac{1}{0.6 + 0.08} = \dfrac{1}{0.68} = 1.471$$ **Case 2: f = 0.8, S = 5:** $$Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{5}} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 + 0.16} = \dfrac{1}{0.36} = 2.778$$ **Comments:** - The same 5× speedup of a sub-component gives a total speedup of only **1.471×** when the enhanced part is **40%** of the workload, but a much better **2.778×** when it is **80%**. - This confirms Amdahl's central insight: to achieve large overall speedups, the *enhanced fraction* must itself be large. - The **theoretical upper bound** for Case 1 is $1 / (1 - 0.4) = 1.667$ (as $S \to \infty$), and for Case 2 is $1 / (1 - 0.8) = 5.0$. Our observed speedups are well below these bounds, illustrating that even a 5× component improvement is far from the theoretical maximum. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** > - **Marks lost most often** because students write Amdahl's formula but **do not derive it**. The 7-mark sub-part demands a *derivation* (proof), not just a substitution. > - **Do not** confuse the *enhanced fraction* $f$ with the *overall speedup*. The symbol $S$ on the right-hand side is the *component* speedup, not the *system* speedup. > - Always state units in the numerical answer: write "**1.12 s**" and "**1785.71 MIPS**" — naked numbers lose **half a mark** in KTU 2024 valuation. > - **"Comment on the results"** carries 1–2 marks: an answer that omits the comment loses easy marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ⭐ A computer has **five** functional units: **Input, Output, Memory, ALU, Control** (Input/Output are often grouped, but KTU counts them as two). - ⭐ The **CPU** is composed of the **ALU + Control Unit + Register File**, all connected by an **internal CPU bus**. - ⭐ The **Memory Hierarchy** is: **Register → Cache (L1/L2/L3) → Main Memory (RAM) → Secondary Storage (SSD/HDD)**. Speed decreases, capacity increases, cost/bit decreases. - ⭐ **RAM is volatile; ROM is non-volatile.** SRAM is used for cache, DRAM for main memory. - ⭐ The **three system buses** are: **Data (bidirectional)**, **Address (unidirectional, CPU → outside)**, and **Control (timing/command)**. - ⭐ The **CPU Performance Equation** is the most important equation of the module: $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$. - ⭐ **Amdahl's Law** bounds the overall speedup: $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$. Maximum theoretical speedup as $S \to \infty$ is $\dfrac{1}{1 - f}$. - ⭐ **Hardwired CU** = fast but rigid; **Microprogrammed CU** = slow but flexible. Know at least one example of each. - ⭐ The **Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle** uses the **PC** (address), **IR** (decoded instruction), **MAR/MDR** (bus interface), and **ALU** (execution). - ⭐ **MIPS = IC / (T_CPU × 10⁶) = f_clk / (CPI × 10⁶)**. Higher MIPS = faster processor (for the same ISA). - ⭐ **Performance per Watt** is the modern figure of merit; CMOS dynamic power is $\alpha C V^{2} f$. - ⭐ **Always** present a labeled block diagram in the exam for any question on functional units — it instantly awards **1–2 easy marks**. - ⭐ **Watch the units** in numerical answers: GHz, MHz, ns, µs, MIPS, MB, GB — mismatched units are a top reason for mark loss.

topics.title: Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Computer Organization** refers to the operational units and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications of a computer system. It deals with the physical implementation of the architecture, including control signals, interfaces, memory technology, and timing.

**Computer Architecture** refers to the attributes of a system as seen by the programmer — the instruction set, addressing modes, data types, and I/O mechanisms that affect the logical execution of a program.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> A digital computer is a synchronized collection of **five (5)** functionally independent units: **Input, Memory, Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU), Output, and Control Unit.** The processor comprises the ALU + Control Unit + Registers. The **bus system** provides the communication backbone.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Model"

Think of a modern computer as a **busy restaurant kitchen**:

| Computer Component | Restaurant Kitchen Equivalent | Real-Life Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU (Processor)** | Head Chef | Decodes the "recipe" (instruction) and orchestrates cooking |
| **Main Memory (RAM)** | Refrigerator & Pantry | Stores all the raw ingredients (data & instructions) currently in use |
| **Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)** | Cold Storage Warehouse | Long-term storage of ingredients (data) |
| **Input Devices** | Food Suppliers / Waiter (taking orders) | Brings in fresh ingredients and customer orders |
| **Output Devices** | Serving Counter / Dining Hall | Delivers finished dishes to customers |
| **System Bus** | The Kitchen Pass / Counter | The "highway" where chefs exchange ingredients and orders |
| **ALU** | The Stove & Oven | Performs the actual "cooking" — arithmetic and logic |
| **Control Unit** | The Head Chef's Whistle / Conductor | Times every action and tells everyone what to do next |

### The "Kitchen" Workflow = Instruction Cycle

1. The **head chef (CPU)** reads a recipe card from the refrigerator (Fetch).
2. He **understands** the recipe (Decode).
3. He **grabs the right ingredients** from the fridge (Operand Fetch).
4. He **cooks** them on the stove (Execute).
5. He **plates the dish** on the serving counter (Store Result).

This 5-step sequence is precisely the **Instruction Execution Cycle**, the heart of "Basic Operational Concepts."

---

## 1.3 The Five Functional Units — At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Von Neumann Model (1945)** — All five units operate under the control of the **Control Unit (CU)**. The stored-program concept means instructions and data share the **same memory** and travel on the **same bus**, creating the famous *Von Neumann Bottleneck* that modern systems try to alleviate.

### Block-Level View

- **Input Unit:** Accepts coded information (keyboard, mouse, scanner, network card).
- **Memory Unit:** Stores programs + data. Subdivided into **RAM** (volatile) and **ROM** (non-volatile). Each location has a unique binary address.
- **ALU:** Performs arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `×`, `÷`), logic (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `XOR`), and shift operations.
- **Output Unit:** Returns processed results (monitor, printer, speakers).
- **Control Unit:** Fetches instructions sequentially, decodes them, and generates the **timing & control signals** (Read/Write, Memory/IO select) to orchestrate everything.

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (bold for emphasis)

- **Word length:** typically **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** per memory location.
- **Clock rate (f):** measured in **Hz (Hertz)**, commonly **GHz** (10⁹ Hz) in modern systems.
- **Cycle time (T):** $T = 1/f$ — measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
- **MIPS:** Million Instructions Per Second — a legacy performance metric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bus System — The Backbone of Communication

A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of **three functional groups** of lines:

| Bus Group | Function | Direction | Width Drives |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory/I/O address | Unidirectional (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/IO) | Address space: $2^n$ locations for n-bit bus |
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data being transferred | Bidirectional | Determines word length |
| **Control Bus** | Carries command & timing signals (Read, Write, Interrupt, Clock) | Mixed / Bidirectional | Synchronizes the operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Bus Organization:** Cost-effective but **only one transfer can occur at a time** — a major performance bottleneck. **Three-Bus / Multi-Bus Organization** (with separate Address, Data, and a dedicated internal CPU bus) allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch, operand fetch, and result store.

### Why the Bus Architecture Matters

- The **Address bus width ($n$ bits)** $\rightarrow$ limits addressable memory to $\mathbf{2^n}$ locations.
- The **Data bus width ($m$ bits)** $\rightarrow$ equals the word length; one transfer moves **$m$ bits**.
- **Control signals** include: `MEM/IO`, `READ`, `WRITE`, `BUSY`, `INTERRUPT`, `CLOCK`, `RESET`.

---

## 2.2 Internal Registers of the Processor (Basic Computer — Mano)

For the **KTU 2024 syllabus**, the *Mano's Basic Computer* register model is mandatory. The processor contains:

| Register | Symbol | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Program Counter** | PC | 12 | Holds the address of the **next** instruction to fetch |
| **Address Register** | AR | 12 | Holds the memory address currently being accessed |
| **Data Register** | DR | 16 | Holds the **operand** (data) being transferred to/from memory |
| **Accumulator** | AC | 16 | The primary working register for ALU operations |
| **Instruction Register** | IR | 16 | Holds the **fetched instruction** while it is being decoded/executed |
| **Temporary Register** | TR | 16 | Scratchpad for internal CPU operations |
| **Input Register** | INPR | 8 | Receives a character from the input device |
| **Output Register** | OUTR | 8 | Holds a character for the output device |
| **Sequence Counter (SC)** | SC | — | Used by the control unit to track the micro-operation timing (Fetch phase) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake:** The **PC always points to the NEXT instruction**, not the current one. After each fetch, the PC is incremented as part of the micro-operation sequence.

---

## 2.3 The Instruction Execution Cycle — 6 Logical Phases

Every instruction passes through a structured sequence. The **first 3 phases (Fetch)** are identical for ALL instructions; the last 3 vary.

1. **Fetch Instruction** — Load the instruction from memory (address in PC) into IR. Increment PC.
2. **Decode** — Determine the operation code (opcode) in the IR.
3. **Evaluate Effective Address** — Compute the operand address using the addressing mode (Direct, Indirect, Immediate, etc.).
4. **Fetch Operands** — Read the required operand(s) from memory or registers.
5. **Execute** — Perform the operation in the ALU.
6. **Store Result** — Write the result back to the destination (register or memory).

> [!WARNING]
> The Fetch phase is the **constant overhead** of every instruction. This is why **cache memory** and **pipelining** are engineering priorities — to overlap or eliminate the fetch cost.

---

## 2.4 Interrupt Handling — Asynchronous Service Requests

An **interrupt** is a signal that **suspends** the normal program execution, transfers control to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then **returns** to the interrupted program.

### Types of Interrupts (KTU syllabus focus)

| Type | Source | Maskable? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware / External** | External I/O devices | Yes (via Interrupt Enable flip-flop `IEN`) | Keyboard, Disk I/O, Timer |
| **Software / Internal (Trap)** | Exceptional conditions during execution | No (Non-maskable) | Division by zero, Overflow, Invalid opcode |
| **Software Interrupt (System Call)** | Program instruction (e.g., `INT n`) | No | OS service requests |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt Service Cycle:** The CPU completes the current instruction, **saves the return address** (typically into memory at a fixed location like `0` in Mano's Basic Computer), and then branches to the ISR. The **RTI (Return from Interrupt)** instruction restores PC and returns control.

### The `IEN` Flip-Flop (Interrupt Enable)

- `IEN = 1` $\rightarrow$ CPU accepts interrupts.
- `IEN = 0` $\rightarrow$ CPU ignores maskable interrupts (critical sections are protected).
- `IEN` is **cleared** to 0 during the interrupt cycle so that nested interrupts are blocked.

---

## 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Performance & Timing)

> All performance metrics derive from three fundamental quantities: **Instruction Count ($I_C$)**, **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**, and **Clock Cycle Time ($T_c$)** or **Clock Rate ($f$)**.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = 1/f$ | Clock period (seconds per cycle) |
| 2 | $T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI \times T_c$ | **Total CPU execution time** for a program |
| 3 | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{I_C \times CPI}{f}$ | Alternate form using clock rate |
| 4 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{I_C}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second (note: I_C is total instructions) |
| 5 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Form using clock rate and CPI |
| 6 | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{FN_{ops}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}$ | Floating-point ops per second |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | **Speedup** of a system enhancement |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** (f = fraction enhanced, k = speedup of that fraction) |
| 9 | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times I_i)}{I_C}$ | Weighted average CPI for mixed instruction types |
| 10 | $M = 2^n$ | Addressable memory locations for n-bit address bus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law Insight:** No matter how fast you make the enhanced fraction $k$, the **overall speedup $S$ is bounded** by $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$ as $k \rightarrow \infty$. This is the **theoretical maximum speedup** — a classic KTU question.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU design teams** use the performance formulas to balance **clock frequency**, **CPI** (instruction complexity), and **instruction count** (compiler/ISA efficiency).
- **System architects** apply Amdahl's Law to decide whether upgrading a single component (e.g., GPU, SSD) is worth the cost.
- **Embedded systems engineers** must know bus architectures to design **DMA** controllers, **interrupt controllers**, and **memory-mapped I/O** subsystems.
- **Cloud / Data-center engineers** use MIPS and MFLOPS to benchmark and **price** compute instances.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental CPU Performance Equation

We start from first principles to derive the master equation $T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI \times T_c$, which is the foundation of **every** performance problem in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Define the total number of clock cycles

Each instruction takes a certain number of clock cycles. For a program with $I_C$ total instructions and an *average* cycles-per-instruction of $CPI$, the total number of clock cycles the CPU must execute is:

$$
N_{cycles} = I_C \times CPI
$$

**Logic:** If a single instruction takes $CPI$ cycles, then $I_C$ instructions take proportionally more cycles. We use the *average* CPI because different instruction types (load, store, branch, multiply) take different cycle counts.

### Step 2: Convert cycles into time

Each clock cycle has a fixed duration called the **clock period** $T_c$ (in seconds). The total time is the number of cycles multiplied by the duration of each cycle:

$$
T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_c
$$
