**Logic:** If one cycle takes $T_c$ seconds, $N_{cycles}$ cycles take $N_{cycles}$ times longer.

### Step 3: Substitute to get the master equation

$$
T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI \times T_c
$$

### Step 4: Use the relationship $T_c = 1/f$

Since clock period and clock frequency are reciprocals:

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{I_C \times CPI}{f}
$$

This is the **most-tested equation in KTU Computer Organization papers**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the MIPS Formula

We begin with the **inverse of CPU time per million instructions**, defined as the number of million instructions executed per second.

### Step 1: Start with the definition

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I_C}{T_{CPU} \times 10^6}
$$

**Logic:** MIPS counts millions of instructions in a second. We divide total instructions by total seconds, then scale to "millions."

### Step 2: Substitute $T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI / f$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{I_C \times f}{I_C \times CPI \times 10^6}
$$

### Step 3: Cancel $I_C$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

**Logic:** The instruction count drops out — MIPS depends *only* on the **clock rate** and the **average CPI** of the program. This is a frequent KTU trick question: "A system with a faster clock may not have higher MIPS if its CPI is worse."

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

Amdahl's Law quantifies the **theoretical maximum speedup** achievable by improving only a portion of a system.

### Step 1: Let $T$ be the original execution time

Let a fraction $f$ of $T$ be enhanced by a speedup factor $k$. The remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is unchanged.

### Step 2: Express the new execution time $T_{new}$

The enhanced portion now takes $\dfrac{fT}{k}$ seconds, and the un-enhanced portion still takes $(1-f)T$ seconds:

$$
T_{new} = \frac{fT}{k} + (1-f)T
$$

### Step 3: Factor out $T$

$$
T_{new} = T \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{k} \right]
$$

### Step 4: Take the ratio $S = T / T_{new}$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

### Step 5: Examine the theoretical limit as $k \rightarrow \infty$

If the enhanced fraction becomes infinitely fast, $f/k \rightarrow 0$, and we get:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Even if the floating-point unit becomes infinitely fast, the **integer and control portions** still limit overall performance. This is why **balanced system design** matters.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A program has **50% integer** instructions with $CPI = 1$, **30% floating-point** with $CPI = 4$, and **20% branch** with $CPI = 2$. Find:
(a) Average CPI
(b) MIPS rating
(c) CPU time for a program of $I_C = 10^9$ instructions

### Part (a): Average CPI

$$
CPI_{avg} = \frac{(0.5 \times 1) + (0.3 \times 4) + (0.2 \times 2)}{1.0}
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = \frac{0.5 + 1.2 + 0.4}{1.0} = 2.1
$$

### Part (b): MIPS Rating

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{2.1 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^3}{2.1} \approx 1190.48
$$

### Part (c): CPU Time

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{I_C \times CPI_{avg}}{f} = \frac{10^9 \times 2.1}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{2.1}{2.5} = 0.84 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Trade-offs in Bus & CPU Design)

| Design Choice | Pros | Cons | KTU Exam Angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single shared bus** | Cheap, simple wiring | Only one transfer at a time — bottleneck | Throughput vs cost |
| **Three-bus (separate Address/Data)** | Parallelism: fetch + decode overlap | More pins, more complex control | Explain advantage with fetch-decode overlap |
| **Increasing clock rate $f$** | Lower $T_c$ | Power $\propto C V^2 f$ — heat wall | Limits of Dennard scaling |
| **Reducing CPI via pipelining** | Higher instruction throughput | Hazards (data, control, structural) | CPI drop from 5 to ~1 |
| **Reducing instruction count $I_C$** | Efficient ISA / compiler | Requires RISC / VLIW redesign | RISC vs CISC trade-off |
| **Adding cache memory** | Reduces memory access time | Cache miss penalty | Amdahl's Law application |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Amdahl's Law in Practice

**Problem:** A program spends **30%** of its time in a section that can be made **10× faster**. Compute:
(a) The overall speedup.
(b) The maximum possible speedup with this enhancement.

### Part (a): Overall speedup

Here $f = 0.30$ and $k = 10$.

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-0.30) + \dfrac{0.30}{10}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.73} \approx 1.370
$$

### Part (b): Maximum speedup (as $k \rightarrow \infty$)

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-0.30} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.4286
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** Even with infinite effort on the 30% portion, the program can never run more than **1.43× faster**. The un-enhanced 70% dominates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering in Mermaid v10+.

---

## 4.1 Von Neumann Block Diagram — Basic Computer Organization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CPU_BLOCK["CPU PROCESSOR"]
        CU["CONTROL UNIT CU"]
        ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
        REG["INTERNAL REGISTERS PC AR IR DR AC TR"]
    end

    MEM["MAIN MEMORY RAM ROM"]
    IN["INPUT UNIT KEYBOARD MOUSE SENSOR"]
    OUT["OUTPUT UNIT MONITOR PRINTER"]

    subgraph BUS_BLOCK["SYSTEM BUS"]
        ABUS["ADDRESS BUS 12 BIT"]
        DBUS["DATA BUS 16 BIT"]
        CBUS["CONTROL BUS READ WRITE INTERRUPT"]
    end

    CU -. Controls .-> ALU
    CU -. Controls .-> MEM
    CU -. Controls .-> IN
    CU -. Controls .-> OUT
    REG <-.-> ALU
    REG <-.-> CU

    CPU_BLOCK <==> BUS_BLOCK
    BUS_BLOCK <==> MEM
    BUS_BLOCK <==> IN
    BUS_BLOCK <==> OUT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU block** (CU + ALU + Registers) is the active master.
- The **System Bus** is the *only* shared communication path — this is the Von Neumann bottleneck.
- The Control Unit issues timing signals to **all** units via the Control Bus.

---

## 4.2 Instruction Execution Cycle — Phase Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1["FETCH INSTRUCTION FROM MEMORY USING PC"]
    F2["INCREMENT PC POINTS TO NEXT INSTRUCTION"]
    F3["DECODE OPCODE IN IR"]
    F4["EVALUATE OPERAND ADDRESS"]
    F5["FETCH OPERANDS FROM MEMORY OR REGISTERS"]
    F6["EXECUTE OPERATION IN ALU"]
    F7["STORE RESULT TO DESTINATION"]

    F1 ==> F2
    F2 ==> F3
    F3 ==> F4
    F4 ==> F5
    F5 ==> F6
    F6 ==> F7
    F7 -.-> F1
```

**Key Insight:** The first three steps (F1, F2, F3) are **identical for every instruction** and form the **Fetch Phase**. The last four steps form the **Execute Phase** and vary by opcode.

---

## 4.3 Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0["START EXECUTE CURRENT INSTRUCTION NORMALLY"]
    S1["END OF INSTRUCTION FETCH CHECK FOR INTERRUPT SIGNAL"]
    S2["IS IEN EQUAL TO 1 AND INTERRUPT PENDING"]
    S3["PROCEED TO NEXT INSTRUCTION FETCH"]
    S4["SAVE RETURN ADDRESS TO MEMORY LOCATION 0"]
    S5["CLEAR IEN FLAG SET TO 0 TO BLOCK NESTED INTERRUPTS"]
    S6["BRANCH TO ISR ADDRESS FETCHED FROM LOCATION 1"]
    S7["EXECUTE INTERRUPT SERVICE ROUTINE ISR"]
    S8["RESTORE PC FROM STACK RETURN FROM INTERRUPT"]
    S9["SET IEN EQUAL TO 1 RE ENABLE INTERRUPTS"]
    S10["RESUME NORMAL PROGRAM EXECUTION"]

    S0 ==> S1
    S1 ==> S2
    S2 -- YES --> S4
    S2 -- NO --> S3
    S4 ==> S5
    S5 ==> S6
    S6 ==> S7
    S7 ==> S8
    S8 ==> S9
    S9 ==> S10
    S3 ==> S0
    S10 ==> S0
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A **branch decision** is taken at the end of *every* instruction fetch (the classic **Mano's Basic Computer** design).
- If the branch is taken, the **return address** is saved at a hard-wired location (Mano uses M[0]).
- The **IEN flag is cleared** during the ISR to prevent further interrupts from corrupting the routine.

---

## 4.4 Three-Bus vs Single-Bus Organization — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SGL["SINGLE BUS ORGANIZATION"]
        S1B["CPU ALU REGISTERS"] --- SBL["SHARED BUS"]
        SBL --- SMEM["MEMORY"]
        SBL --- SIO["I/O DEVICES"]
    end

    subgraph TRI["THREE BUS ORGANIZATION"]
        T1B["CPU ALU REGISTERS"] --- T1L["INTERNAL CPU BUS"]
        T1L --- T1M["MEMORY BUS"]
        T1L --- T1D["DATA BUS"]
        T1M --- TMEM["MEMORY"]
        T1D --- TIO["I/O DEVICES"]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Single-bus**: All units (CPU, memory, I/O) share **one** set of wires — simple but **serial** transfers.
- **Three-bus**: The CPU has an **internal bus** for register transfers, plus **dedicated buses to memory and I/O** — allows **parallel** activity (e.g., the CPU can read from memory on one bus while writing to a register on another).

---

## 4.5 Register Transfer During Fetch Phase (Mano's Basic Computer)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0["T0 AR EQUALS PC"] --> T1["T1 IR EQUALS M AR PC EQUALS PC PLUS 1"]
    T1 --> T2["T2 D0 D1 D2 DECODE IR OPCODE"]
    T2 --> T3["T3 EXECUTE INSTRUCTION BRANCH TO APPROPRIATE ROUTINE"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **T0, T1, T2** are the **universal fetch micro-operations** for *every* instruction.
- **T3** is a *branch* to the instruction-specific execution routine.
- Each T-state corresponds to **one clock cycle** of the Sequence Counter (SC).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the following with respect to computer organization:**
**(a) Bus**
**(b) Interrupt**
**(c) Von Neumann architecture**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Each definition 1 mark, key terms highlighted):

**(a) Bus:** A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common lines (wires) through which data, addresses, and control signals are transmitted between the **CPU, memory, and I/O devices**. It typically comprises three functional groups: the **address bus, data bus, and control bus**.

**(b) Interrupt:** An **interrupt** is an asynchronous signal (from external hardware) or synchronous condition (from internal exceptions) that **temporarily suspends** the normal program execution, transfers control to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then **returns** to the interrupted program after servicing.

**(c) Von Neumann Architecture:** The **Von Neumann architecture** is a stored-program computer design in which **both instructions and data reside in the same memory unit** and share the **same bus system**. It comprises five functional units — Input, Memory, ALU, Output, and Control Unit — coordinated by a single control unit. The shared bus creates the famous **Von Neumann bottleneck**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Explain the role of the following registers in Mano's Basic Computer:**
**(a) Program Counter (PC)**
**(b) Instruction Register (IR)**
**(c) Accumulator (AC)**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Each register 1 mark):

**(a) Program Counter (PC):** A **12-bit register** that holds the **address of the next instruction** to be fetched from memory. After each fetch, the PC is **automatically incremented** by 1 (in the T1 micro-operation) so that the CPU proceeds sequentially through memory.

**(b) Instruction Register (IR):** A **16-bit register** that holds the **currently executing instruction** after it has been fetched from memory. The opcode portion (bits 12-14) is **decoded** by the control unit during the T2 micro-operation to determine which operation to perform.

**(c) Accumulator (AC):** A **16-bit register** that is the **primary working register** of the ALU. Most arithmetic and logic operations (e.g., ADD, AND, CMA, CIR) use the AC as one of the operands and store the result back into the AC. It is the most frequently accessed register in the processor.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question from the choice. Each full question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.**

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of a basic computer with all five functional units. Explain the function of the system bus, clearly distinguishing between the address bus, data bus, and control bus.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A processor runs at **3 GHz** clock frequency. For a given program, **40%** of instructions require an average of **4 clock cycles**, **35%** require **3 clock cycles**, and the remaining **25%** require **2 clock cycles**. Calculate:
1. The **average CPI** of the program.
2. The **MIPS rating** of the processor for this program.
3. The **CPU execution time** for a program with **$I_C = 2 \times 10^9$** instructions.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw the block diagram with five functional units.

The diagram should include:
- **Input Unit** (rectangle) on the left.
- **Memory Unit** (rectangle) in the center, labeled with address lines.
- **ALU** (rectangle) and **Control Unit** (rectangle) grouped inside a larger "CPU" block.
- **Output Unit** (rectangle) on the right.
- A **System Bus** (three lines) connecting all units to the CPU.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Explain the bus functions.

| Bus | Function | Direction | Width Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Carries the binary address of the memory location or I/O device | Unidirectional (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/IO) | Width $n$ bits $\rightarrow$ $2^n$ addressable locations |
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data (operand, instruction, or result) | Bidirectional | Width equals the word length of the machine |
| **Control Bus** | Carries timing and command signals: `Read`, `Write`, `Memory/IO`, `Interrupt`, `Bus Request`, `Bus Grant` | Mixed | Synchronizes all bus activities |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** State the engineering purpose.

- The **control bus** synchronizes read/write operations; without it, the CPU cannot tell memory whether to *send* data (Read) or *receive* data (Write).
- The **three-bus organization** allows simultaneous instruction fetch, operand fetch, and result storage, reducing the Von Neumann bottleneck.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the average CPI using the weighted average formula.

$$
CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{fraction}_i \times CPI_i)
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = (0.40 \times 4) + (0.35 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 2)
$$

$$
CPI_{avg} = 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.50 = 3.15
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate the MIPS rating.

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{3.15 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{3000}{3.15} \approx 952.38 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Calculate the CPU execution time.

$$
T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI_{avg} \times T_c = \frac{I_C \times CPI_{avg}}{f}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{2 \times 10^9 \times 3.15}{3 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{2 \times 3.15}{3} = \frac{6.30}{3} = 2.10 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **interrupt cycle** of a basic computer with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of the **Interrupt Enable (IEN) flip-flop** and the **Return Address (R)** register in interrupt servicing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using **Amdahl's Law**, calculate the overall speedup obtained in the following scenarios:
1. A program spends **25%** of its time in a section that is enhanced by a factor of **5**.
2. A program spends **60%** of its time in a section that is enhanced by a factor of **10**.
3. Compare and comment on the two results.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define interrupt cycle and its purpose.

The **interrupt cycle** is a special micro-operation sequence that the CPU enters *after* completing the current instruction if an interrupt signal is detected. Its purpose is to **save the return address**, **disable further interrupts**, and **branch to the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Flowchart of the interrupt cycle (Mano's Basic Computer).

The flowchart should show:
1. **After instruction fetch**, check: Is `IEN = 1` AND is there a pending interrupt?
2. If **NO**: $\rightarrow$ Continue to next instruction.
3. If **YES**:
   - `M[0] $\leftarrow$ PC`  (Save return address at hard-wired memory location 0)
   - `PC $\leftarrow$ 0`  (Branches to ISR at address 0... *in Mano's design the ISR starts at address 0, and address 1 holds the BUN to the actual ISR* — clarify with PC $\leftarrow$ 1 to fetch ISR address)
   - `IEN $\leftarrow$ 0`  (Disable further maskable interrupts)
   - `R $\leftarrow$ 1`  (Set flip-flop to mark interrupt-in-progress)
4. **RTI instruction at the end of ISR**: `PC $\leftarrow$ M[0]`, `IEN $\leftarrow$ 1`, `R $\leftarrow$ 0`

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Role of `IEN` and Return Address.

- **IEN (Interrupt Enable) Flip-Flop:** Acts as the **master switch** for maskable interrupts. When `IEN = 1`, the CPU accepts interrupts; when `IEN = 0`, all maskable interrupts are ignored. It is **cleared** automatically during the interrupt cycle to prevent **nested interrupts** from corrupting the current ISR.
- **Return Address (R) / Memory Location 0:** Stores the **address of the next instruction** of the interrupted program, allowing the CPU to **resume execution** seamlessly after the ISR completes.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:

**Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Scenario 1 — $f = 0.25$, $k = 5$

$$
S_1 = \frac{1}{(1-0.25) + \dfrac{0.25}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.75 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.80} = 1.25
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Scenario 2 — $f = 0.60$, $k = 10$

$$
S_2 = \frac{1}{(1-0.60) + \dfrac{0.60}{10}} = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.06} = \frac{1}{0.46} \approx 2.174
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Maximum possible speedup comparison.

For Scenario 1: $S_{1,max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.25} = \dfrac{1}{0.75} \approx 1.333$
For Scenario 2: $S_{2,max} = \dfrac{1}{1-0.60} = \dfrac{1}{0.40} = 2.500$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparative comment.

**Scenario 2 gives a higher absolute speedup (2.17 vs 1.25)** because a larger fraction (60%) of the program is enhanced. However, **both scenarios are limited by the un-enhanced portions** (75% and 40% respectively). The lesson from Amdahl's Law is that **the un-enhanced portion of a program is the ultimate bottleneck** — system architects must identify and optimize the *largest* possible fraction to maximize benefit.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Basic Operational Concepts" questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include ALL 5 functional units** in the block diagram — the Control Unit is often omitted, costing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the Address Bus direction** — it is **unidirectional** (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory), NOT bidirectional. Marks lost in bus description.
> 3. **MIPS formula misapplication** — Students write MIPS = $f / CPI$ *without* the $10^6$ scaling factor. Always include the $10^6$ — MIPS is "Million Instructions Per Second."
> 4. **PC vs AR confusion** — PC holds the *next* instruction address (program flow); AR holds the *current* memory address being accessed. They are different registers, even if their bit-widths match.
> 5. **Interrupt cycle omission of IEN clear** — Many students forget that `IEN $\leftarrow$ 0` happens *during* the interrupt cycle to prevent nested interrupts. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss if missed.
> 6. **Amdahl's Law: forgetting the $(1-f)$ term** — Writing $S = k/f$ instead of the correct formula. Marks lost in any 7-mark question involving Amdahl.
> 7. **Units inconsistency** — Reporting CPU time in cycles instead of seconds, or MIPS as a raw number instead of "millions." Always write "952.38 MIPS" not "952."

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Quick-Recall for KTU Module 1 — Basic Operational Concepts:**

- ✅ **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Output, **Control Unit** (the conductor).
- ✅ **Von Neumann Architecture:** Single shared memory and bus for instructions *and* data.
- ✅ **Three bus types:** Address (unidirectional, $n$ bits $\rightarrow$ $2^n$ locations), Data (bidirectional, word length), Control (Read/Write/IO/IRQ signals).
- ✅ **PC always points to the NEXT instruction**, not the current one. It auto-increments in micro-operation T1.
- ✅ **Fetch phase is constant** for all instructions (T0, T1, T2); execute phase (T3) varies by opcode.
- ✅ **Master CPU time equation:** $T_{CPU} = I_C \times CPI \times T_c = I_C \times CPI / f$.
- ✅ **MIPS = $f / (CPI \times 10^6)$** — depends only on clock rate and average CPI, NOT on instruction count.
- ✅ **Average CPI** is a *weighted* average using instruction-type fractions.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$; bounded by $S_{max} = 1/(1-f)$.
- ✅ **IEN flip-flop:** Master switch for maskable interrupts; cleared during ISR to prevent nesting.
- ✅ **Interrupt cycle saves** the return address at M[0] (in Mano's Basic Computer) and branches to the ISR.
- ✅ **Single-bus = cheap but slow**; **Three-bus = parallel, fast, complex** — trade-off principle.
- ✅ **Interrupt ≠ Trap:** Hardware/External (maskable) vs Software/Internal (often non-maskable).
- ✅ **Performance depends on THREE factors** equally: $I_C$ (ISA/Compiler), $CPI$ (Implementation), $f$ (Technology).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bus Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bus Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common physical signal lines (wires/traces) that interconnect two or more hardware subsystems of a computer. The **Bus Structure** defines the topology, arbitration rules, timing protocols, and signal grouping that govern how the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Main Memory**, and **Input/Output (I/O) devices** exchange addresses, data, and control information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** A bus is not a single wire; it is a *bundle of parallel conductors* classified by the information they carry. A complete bus structure must be analyzed across three orthogonal dimensions: **Functional Grouping**, **Physical Topology**, and **Arbitration Hierarchy.**

### 1.2 The Three Functional Bus Groups

| Group | Symbol on Schematics | Directionality | Carries | Typical Width (Modern Systems) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| **Data Bus** | DB | **Bidirectional** | Operands, instructions, I/O payload | **64 bits** |
| **Address Bus** | AB | **Unidirectional** (CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory/I/O) | Source/Destination memory locations | **32 or 64 bits** |
| **Control Bus** | CB | **Bidirectional** (mixed) | Read/Write strobes, clock, IRQ, Bus Request/Grant | **10–20 lines** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three groups?** Separating *what* (data) from *where* (address) and *when/how* (control) allows simultaneous transactions and pipelined addressing — a core principle behind the **Von Neumann / Princeton Architecture**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

Think of a computer's internal circuitry as a **city** and the bus as its **highway network**:

* **The Data Bus** is the **main highway** — wide, fast, and capable of carrying many cars (bits) in parallel lanes. Traffic flows both ways.
* **The Address Bus** is the **signage system** — one-way directional boards that only tell drivers *where* to go. The CPU alone posts the signs.
* **The Control Bus** is the **traffic police** — sirens (interrupts), traffic lights (read/write signals), and timing beacons (clock) that coordinate movement.

A **bus collision** (two devices driving onto the highway simultaneously) is prevented by **bus arbitration** — the equivalent of a roundabout with strict entry rules.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

* **Bus Width (W):** Number of parallel physical lines, measured in **bits**.
* **Bus Clock Frequency (f):** Measured in **MHz** or **GHz**.
* **Bus Bandwidth (Throughput):** Measured in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.
* **Bus Cycle Time (T):** Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
* **Propagation Delay:** Measured in **ns per meter** of trace.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Anatomy of a Bus Transfer

Every bus transaction, regardless of complexity, decomposes into **four canonical phases**:

1. **Arbitration Phase:** The potential bus master requests control. The arbiter resolves contention and issues *Bus Grant* to exactly one master.
2. **Address Phase:** The granted master places the target memory/I/O address on the **Address Bus** and asserts the *Address Strobe* control line.
3. **Data Phase:** Data flows on the **Data Bus**, gated by *Read* ($\overline{RD}$) or *Write* ($\overline{WR}$) strobes from the **Control Bus**.
4. **Termination Phase:** The transaction is closed; lines are tri-stated (high-impedance) for the next arbiter round.

### 2.2 Bus Topology Classifications

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single Bus** | All components share one common bus | Simple, low cost | Severe contention; bottleneck |
| **Two-Bus** (Local + System) | Local bus connects CPU–Cache–Main Memory; System bus for I/O | Reduces memory bottleneck | Moderate complexity |
| **Three-Bus** (Local + System + Expansion) | Local (high-speed), System (CPU–Mem), Expansion (slow I/O) | Hierarchical speed matching | Higher hardware cost |
| **Mezzanine / Backplane Bus** | Standardized slots for plug-in cards (e.g., PCI) | Modular, scalable | Pin-count limits bandwidth |

### 2.3 Bus Arbitration Methods (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized Arbitration** = one dedicated arbiter (hardware) controls access.
> **Distributed Arbitration** = all devices collectively decide; no central arbiter.

* **Daisy Chain Arbitration:** Devices connected in series. Grant signal propagates down the chain. *Simple but priority is fixed by physical position (unfair to last device).*
* **Centralized Parallel (Independent Requests):** Each master has dedicated *Request* and *Grant* lines to the arbiter. *Fastest, fairest, but requires $2N$ lines for $N$ masters.*
* **Polling (Round-Robin):** Arbiter sequentially checks each master via a counter. *Fair but slow for many masters.*
* **Distributed Arbitration (e.g., collision-detection like Ethernet):** Each device arbitrates by comparing its priority bits on shared lines. *No single point of failure.*

### 2.4 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Buses

* **Synchronous Bus:** A common **clock line** dictates timing. All events occur on clock edges. *Faster, simpler logic, but limited to short distances due to clock skew.*
* **Asynchronous Bus:** No global clock. Handshaking via *Request* and *Acknowledge* lines. *Slower per cycle but works across variable delays/long distances.*

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examiner-favorite for both 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.**

| \# | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $B = W \times f$ | Maximum theoretical bus bandwidth (bits/sec) | bits/s |
| 2 | $B_{MB} = \dfrac{W \times f}{8 \times 10^{6}}$ | Bandwidth in **MB/s** | MB/s |
| 3 | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Time for one bus clock cycle | seconds (s) |
| 4 | $T_{transfer} = n \times T_{cycle}$ | Time to transfer an $n$-byte block (synchronous) | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{useful} + T_{overhead}}$ | Bus efficiency (0 to 1, dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $N_{lines} = 2M$ (parallel arbitration) | Lines needed for $M$ masters in parallel scheme | lines |
| 7 | $P_{max} = f \times W \times \eta$ | Realistic peak throughput (practical) | bits/s |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

* **Mainboard Design:** Bus hierarchy (FSB $\rightarrow$ QPI/UPI $\rightarrow$ PCIe) directly determines CPU–RAM–GPU data flow bottlenecks.
* **Embedded Systems:** AMBA (AHB/APB) bus protocol governs SoC IP-core communication in nearly all smartphones.
* **Real-Time Systems:** Deterministic bus arbitration is critical for automotive CAN/LIN bus safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Bus Bandwidth

**Problem:** A 32-bit data bus operates at a clock frequency of **100 MHz**. Compute (a) the maximum theoretical bandwidth in MB/s, and (b) the time to transfer a 4 KB block, assuming each transfer takes exactly **one** clock cycle.

**Given Data:**
* $W = 32$ bits
* $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
* Block size $S = 4 \text{ KB} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ bytes}$

**Step 1 — Apply the base bandwidth equation:**

$$B = W \times f$$

Substitute the values:

$$B = 32 \text{ bits} \times 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$$

Compute the multiplication:

$$B = 3200 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert bits to bytes by dividing by 8:**

$$B_{bytes} = \dfrac{3200 \times 10^{6}}{8}$$

Perform the division:

$$B_{bytes} = 400 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MB/s (using $10^6$ convention as per KTU engineering norm):**

$$B_{MB} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6}}{10^{6}} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — Time to transfer a 4 KB block:**

First find clock cycle time:

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

Now total cycles needed (1 byte per cycle since bus is 8-bit aligned via 32-bit width):

$$n_{bytes} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$$

Since the bus transfers **4 bytes per cycle** (32 bits $\div$ 8), cycles required:

$$n_{cycles} = \dfrac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ cycles}$$

Total transfer time:

$$T_{transfer} = n_{cycles} \times T_{cycle} = 1024 \times 10 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{transfer} = 10240 \text{ ns} = 10.24 \text{ \mu s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (a) Bandwidth = **400 MB/s**; (b) Transfer time = **10.24 $\mu$s**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Arbitration Line Count Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the number of control lines required for **Daisy Chain** vs. **Centralized Parallel** arbitration when there are **$M = 4$** bus masters.

**Step 1 — Daisy Chain Lines:**

A daisy chain uses exactly **2 lines** regardless of $M$ (one shared *Bus Request* line, one daisy-chained *Bus Grant* line).

$$N_{daisy} = 2 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 2 — Centralized Parallel Lines:**

Each master needs a dedicated *Request* line **and** a dedicated *Grant* line from the central arbiter.

$$N_{parallel} = 2M$$

Substitute $M = 4$:

$$N_{parallel} = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Ratio:**

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{parallel}}{N_{daisy}} = \dfrac{8}{2} = 4$$

| Arbitration Type | Lines for $M=4$ | Scalability | Arbitration Speed |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Daisy Chain | **2** | Poor (priority fixed) | Slow (propagation delay) |
| Centralized Parallel | **8** | Excellent | Fast (parallel decision) |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bus Structure Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Performance Gain | Cost / Penalty | KTU Examiner Signal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Increase $W$ from 32 $\to$ 64 bits | Doubles bandwidth | More pins, routing complexity | High-yield short answer |
| Use 3-bus hierarchy | Removes I/O bottleneck | 3 arbiter circuits needed | Frequent 14-mark question |
| Switch sync $\to$ async | No clock skew | Requires handshake FSM | Conceptual question |
| Use distributed arbitration | No central arbiter = fault tolerant | Complex priority logic | Advanced topic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Bus Architecture (Classic KTU Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU1[CPU]
    MM1[Main Memory]
    IO1[I/O Devices 1]
    IO2[I/O Devices 2]
    LB[Local Bus High Speed]
    SB[System Bus CPU Mem]
    EB[Expansion Bus Slow I O]
    BR[Bridge Controller]

    CPU1 --- LB
    LB --- MM1
    LB --- BR
    BR --- SB
    SB --- IO1
    BR --- EB
    EB --- IO2

    classDef cpu fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class CPU1 cpu
    class MM1 mem
    class IO1,IO2 io
    class LB,SB,EB bus
    class BR bridge
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Bridge** (e.g., Northbridge/Southbridge in legacy PCs) decouples the high-speed Local Bus from the slower Expansion Bus, acting as a *protocol translator* and a *bus arbiter*.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Centralized Parallel Arbitration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Master 1]
    M2[Master 2]
    M3[Master 3]
    M4[Master 4]
    ARB[Central Arbiter]
    SY[System Bus]
    SL[Slave Devices]

    M1 -- Request Line R1 --> ARB
    M2 -- Request Line R2 --> ARB
    M3 -- Request Line R3 --> ARB
    M4 -- Request Line R4 --> ARB
    ARB -- Grant Line G1 --> M1
    ARB -- Grant Line G2 --> M2
    ARB -- Grant Line G3 --> M3
    ARB -- Grant Line G4 --> M4
    M1 -- Bus Master Signals --> SY
    M2 -- Bus Master Signals --> SY
    M3 -- Bus Master Signals --> SY
    M4 -- Bus Master Signals --> SY
    SY --> SL

    classDef master fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef arb fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sysBus fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class M1,M2,M3,M4 master
    class ARB arb
    class SY sysBus
    class SL slave
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Bus Transfer Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arbitration Phase Master requests bus] --> B[Address Phase Address placed on AB]
    B --> C[Data Phase Data moved on DB]
    C --> D[Termination Phase Lines tri stated]
    D --> E[Next Arbitration Cycle]

    classDef phase fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E phase
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define a bus. List the three main types of buses and state one function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of a set of common signal lines that transfer data, addresses, and control signals between CPU, memory, and I/O devices. The three types are:
* **Data Bus:** Carries the actual data (operands, instructions) — bidirectional.
* **Address Bus:** Carries the memory/I/O address location — unidirectional from CPU.
* **Control Bus:** Carries timing and control signals such as Read/Write strobes, clock, and interrupts.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing + 1 mark for function explanation.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous buses. Give one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Synchronous Bus | Asynchronous Bus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Timing | Fixed by global clock | Handshaking (Req/Ack) |
| Speed | Faster per cycle | Slower per cycle |
| Distance | Short (clock skew limit) | Long / variable delays |
| Advantage | Simpler control logic | No clock skew problem |

*(1 mark for timing + 1 mark for table + 1 mark for advantage.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **three-bus architecture** of a computer system with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a single-bus structure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **three-bus architecture** organizes the system into three distinct buses to overcome the bottlenecks of a single shared bus:
1. **Local Bus:** High-speed, connects CPU to cache and main memory.
2. **System Bus:** Medium speed, connects main memory to the bridge.
3. **Expansion Bus:** Low speed, connects slow I/O devices.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single Bus | Three-Bus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed | One speed for all | Hierarchical speed matching |
| Contention | High (all share) | Low (isolated) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Throughput | Limited | Significantly higher |

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 + 3 marking components: definition, diagram, comparison.)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **daisy chain bus arbitration**. State its major limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In **daisy chain arbitration**, bus masters are connected in a serial (priority) chain. A centralized arbiter issues a *Bus Grant* signal that propagates sequentially through the chain. The **first** master requesting the bus absorbs the grant; all downstream masters are blocked for that cycle.

**Limitation:** Priority is fixed by *physical position* — the master closest to the arbiter always wins, making the system **unfair** and prone to **starvation** of low-priority devices. Also, **propagation delay** grows with chain length, limiting scalability.

*(4 marks for diagram, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for limitation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** A 64-bit bus operates at 200 MHz. Compute (i) the maximum bandwidth in MB/s, and (ii) the time required to transfer a 16 KB block, assuming one transfer per clock cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $W = 64$ bits, $f = 200 \text{ MHz}$, $S = 16 \text{ KB} = 16384$ bytes.

**(i) Bandwidth:**

$$B = W \times f = 64 \times 200 \times 10^{6} = 12800 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$$

Convert to MB/s:

$$B_{MB} = \dfrac{12800 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 1600 \text{ MB/s}$$

**(ii) Transfer Time:**

Clock cycle:

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \text{ ns}$$

Bytes per cycle (64 bits = 8 bytes):

$$n_{cycles} = \dfrac{16384}{8} = 2048 \text{ cycles}$$

Total time:

$$T_{transfer} = 2048 \times 5 \text{ ns} = 10240 \text{ ns} = 10.24 \text{ \mu s}$$

*(3 marks for bandwidth, 3 marks for transfer time, 1 mark for unit consistency.)*

**(b)** Explain **centralized parallel arbitration** with a diagram. Why is it preferred over daisy chain despite using more lines? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In **centralized parallel arbitration**, every master has dedicated *Request* and *Grant* lines connected directly to a central arbiter. The arbiter examines all requests simultaneously and grants the bus to the highest-priority master in a **single arbitration cycle**.

**Lines required:** $N = 2M$ for $M$ masters.

**Why preferred over daisy chain:**
* **Fixed priority** (programmable) instead of position-dependent.
* **Single arbitration cycle** delay — no chain propagation.
* **Fairness** can be implemented (round-robin) easily.
* **Fault isolation** — failure of one master's line does not block others.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to divide by 8 when converting bits/s to bytes/s. This alone costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Directionality Mistake:** Marking the Address Bus as *bidirectional*. The address bus is **unidirectional** (CPU $\to$ Memory).
> 3. **Arbitration Confusion:** Confusing *polling* with *daisy chain*. Polling uses a **counter**; daisy chain uses **signal propagation**.
> 4. **Bus Width vs. Address Space:** Confusing bus width (physical lines) with addressable memory size ($2^n$ locations). These are different concepts.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** KTU examiners award **at least 2–3 marks** for a labeled block diagram in 7-mark sub-questions. Always include one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

* **Bus** = shared communication pathway (data + address + control lines).
* **Three bus types:** Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional), Control (bidirectional, mixed).
* **Single bus** is simple but creates a bottleneck — **three-bus** is the practical solution.
* **Arbitration** is required because multiple masters may compete for the bus simultaneously.
* **Daisy chain:** simple, 2 lines, but *position-dependent priority* and *starvation risk*.
* **Centralized parallel:** $2M$ lines, *fast, fair, programmable priority* — preferred for high-performance systems.
* **Polling:** arbiter uses counter; fair but slow.
* **Distributed:** no central arbiter; fault tolerant but complex.
* **Synchronous bus:** uses clock; fast but clock-skew limited.
* **Asynchronous bus:** uses handshaking; slower but works at variable delays.
* **Bandwidth formula:** $B = W \times f$ (bits/s); divide by $8 \times 10^6$ to get **MB/s**.
* **Transfer time formula:** $T = (\text{bytes}) / (\text{bytes per cycle}) \times T_{cycle}$.
* **Bridge** = device that connects two different buses (e.g., Northbridge, Southbridge).
* **Bus master** = device that can initiate a bus transaction (e.g., CPU, DMA controller).
* **Bus slave** = device that only responds to a master's requests (e.g., memory).
* **Modern standards:** PCI Express (point-to-point, not shared), AMBA, USB, CAN — all derive from the classical bus model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Locations and Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Locations and Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Location**: A *memory location* is the smallest addressable unit of physical main memory (RAM) that can be uniquely identified by an **address**. In modern byte-addressable systems, each memory location holds exactly **8 bits (1 byte)** of data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Address**: A *memory address* is a **binary number** (interpreted as a non-negative integer) assigned by the CPU's address bus to a specific memory location, enabling **read** and **write** operations via the data bus.

The address space of a processor with an **n-bit address bus** is mathematically bounded as:

$$0 \le \text{Address} \le 2^{n} - 1$$

Therefore, the **maximum addressable memory** equals $2^{n}$ distinct locations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Think of main memory as a **giant post office with $2^n$ locked P.O. Boxes**, each numbered sequentially starting from box **0** (not 1). 

- **Memory Location** = A single P.O. Box (holds one item — for us, one byte).
- **Memory Address** = The unique box number printed on the front.
- **CPU** = The customer standing at the counter. To fetch an item, the customer tells the clerk (the **Memory Controller**) the *box number* (address) and whether they want to **deposit** (write) or **retrieve** (read).
- **Address Bus Width** = The number of digits the customer can read aloud at once. A 32-bit bus means the customer can only quote box numbers from **0** to **2³² − 1 = 4,294,967,295**, capping the warehouse at roughly **4 GB** of addressable storage.

## 1.3 Why Byte-Addressability?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that virtually all modern general-purpose processors (Intel x86, ARM, RISC-V) use **byte-addressable** memory. This is the *default* addressing mode unless otherwise specified.

| Architecture | Native Addressable Unit |
|:---:|:---:|
| Intel x86 / x86-64 | **1 byte** |
| ARM / RISC-V | **1 byte** |
| Older mainframes (e.g., CDC 6600) | 60-bit word |
| DSP processors | 16-bit word |

## 1.4 Key Terminology At-a-Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| **Word** | The natural data size of the CPU (e.g., **32 bits / 4 bytes** for a 32-bit processor) |
| **Byte** | **8 bits** — the smallest addressable unit |
| **Nibble** | **4 bits** (half a byte) |
| **MSB** | Most Significant Bit (leftmost) |
| **LSB** | Least Significant Bit (rightmost) |
| **Big-Endian** | Stores the **MSB** at the **lowest** memory address |
| **Little-Endian** | Stores the **LSB** at the **lowest** memory address |
| **Aligned Access** | Address is an exact **multiple of the word size** |
| **Unaligned Access** | Address is **not** a multiple of the word size |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Memory Read/Write Cycle

A CPU interacts with memory through a strict **bus protocol**. The process follows these steps:

- **Step 1 — Address Placement**: CPU places the target address on the **Address Bus** (e.g., 32 lines for a 32-bit address bus).
- **Step 2 — Control Signal**: CPU asserts `M/IO#` and `RD#` (read) or `WR#` (write) on the **Control Bus**.
- **Step 3 — Memory Decode**: The **Memory Controller** (e.g., Northbridge / memory controller hub) decodes the address using logic gates to activate exactly **one** memory chip and **one** location within it.
- **Step 4 — Data Transfer**: For a *read*, the selected location drives its contents onto the **Data Bus**. For a *write*, the CPU places data on the bus and the memory latches it.
- **Step 5 — Acknowledgment**: The memory returns `READY#` to the CPU; if not ready, the CPU inserts **wait states**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters for KTU**: Board questions frequently ask students to *explain* the bus handshake. A common pitfall is forgetting that the **address bus is unidirectional** (CPU → Memory) while the **data bus is bidirectional**.

## 2.2 Address Space vs. Memory Capacity

A critical distinction the KTU syllabus tests:

- **Address Space** = the *range* of addresses a CPU can generate = $2^{n}$ locations (where $n$ = address bits).
- **Memory Capacity** = the *actual* physical memory installed = (number of locations) × (bits per location).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Confusing address space with installed memory. A 32-bit CPU has a **4 GB address space** regardless of whether 1 GB or 4 GB of RAM is physically present. Unused addresses simply do not decode to any chip.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE/IA questions on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | Addressable locations | $N = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address bus width (bits) |
| 2 | Total memory capacity | $C = 2^{n} \times w$ | $w$ = word size (bits) |
| 3 | Capacity in bytes | $C_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{2^{n} \times w}{8}$ | if $w$ is in bits |
| 4 | Capacity in KB | $C_{\text{KB}} = \dfrac{2^{n} \times w}{8 \times 1024}$ | $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10}$ bytes |
| 5 | Capacity in MB / GB | divide by $2^{20}$ / $2^{30}$ | SI vs binary ambiguity |
| 6 | Number of address lines for $M$ bytes | $n = \log_{2}(8M)$ | for byte-addressable memory |
| 7 | Bytes per word | $B = \dfrac{w}{8}$ | e.g., $w=32 \Rightarrow B=4$ |
| 8 | Words in memory of $C$ bytes | $W = \dfrac{C}{B}$ | only if aligned |
| 9 | Aligned address check | $A \mod B = 0$ | $B$ = bytes per word |
| 10 | Big/Little endian MSB offset | offset = $B-1$ / $0$ | for 0-based byte addressing |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility**: These formulas are the **backbone of embedded systems design**. A firmware engineer selecting a microcontroller (e.g., STM32 with 32-bit bus) must compute the maximum flash/RAM it can address, and a systems programmer writing DMA buffers must enforce **alignment** or risk bus faults on strict-architecture CPUs (e.g., older ARMv7).

## 2.4 Address Decoding — The Bridge Between CPU and Chips

Address decoding converts a logical address into a **chip select** signal. Two main schemes:

- **Full Decoding**: Every address bit is used. *Advantage*: No address aliasing. *Disadvantage*: More logic gates.
- **Partial Decoding**: Some high-order bits are ignored. *Advantage*: Simpler hardware. *Disadvantage*: **Address aliasing** (multiple addresses map to the same physical location — *mirroring*).

## 2.5 Physical Memory Organization Patterns

| Organization | Description | KTU-Relevant? |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Byte-Addressable** | Each address → 1 byte | ✓ Default |
| **Word-Addressable** | Each address → 1 word (e.g., 4 bytes) | ✓ Common exam variant |
| **Byte-Addressable with 32-bit Data Bus** | Each address → 1 byte, but CPU fetches 4 bytes per access using $A_{2},A_{3},\ldots$ | ✓ Most realistic |

> [!NOTE]
> When a problem states *"memory is 32-bit word-addressable"*, you must remember that $2^{n}$ refers to **words**, not bytes. Convert carefully.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Problem 1 — Address Space to Physical Memory

**Problem Statement**: A 32-bit processor uses byte-addressable memory. How much physical memory can it address? Express the answer in **bytes, KB, MB, and GB**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We start from the formal definition of address space. With an address bus of $n = 32$ bits, the number of distinct addresses that the CPU can generate is the cardinality of the set $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{n}-1\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{locations}} &= 2^{n} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{32} \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the memory is **byte-addressable**, each location holds exactly **1 byte = 8 bits**. So the total capacity in bytes equals the number of locations.

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{32} \text{ bytes}
$$

Now we convert to higher units. We use the binary prefixes $1 \text{ KiB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ MiB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30} \text{ bytes}$ (engineering standard, although KTU problems often loosely write "KB, MB, GB").

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{KB}} &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{10}} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{MB}} &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{20}} = 2^{12} = 4{,}096 \text{ MB} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{GB}} &= \frac{2^{32}}{2^{30}} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer**: A 32-bit, byte-addressable processor can address **$2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ bytes = 4 GB** of memory.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Problem 2 — Finding Address Line Count for a Given Capacity

**Problem Statement**: A processor has byte-addressable memory of size **16 MB**. How many address lines are required on the address bus?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We invert the capacity formula. Let $C$ be the capacity in bytes. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{n} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
16 \text{ MB} &= 2^{n} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert 16 MB into bytes using the binary definition $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
16 \text{ MB} &= 16 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{4} \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
&= 2^{24} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating both sides:

$$
2^{n} = 2^{24} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 24
$$

> **Final Answer**: **24 address lines** are required.

---

## 3.3 Derivative Problem 3 — Endianness Byte Mapping

**Problem Statement**: Store the 32-bit hexadecimal value **`0x12345678`** at memory address **4000** in both **Big-Endian** and **Little-Endian** formats. Assume byte-addressable memory with 4 bytes per word.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

First decompose the 32-bit value into 4 individual bytes. Reading from the **most significant** byte to the **least significant** byte:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{B}_3 \text{ (MSB)} &= \texttt{0x12} \\[2pt]
\text{B}_2 &= \texttt{0x34} \\[2pt]
\text{B}_1 &= \texttt{0x56} \\[2pt]
\text{B}_0 \text{ (LSB)} &= \texttt{0x78}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Big-Endian Mapping** (MSB at the lowest address):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr } 4000 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x12} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4001 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x34} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4002 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x56} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4003 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x78}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Little-Endian Mapping** (LSB at the lowest address):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Addr } 4000 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x78} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4001 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x56} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4002 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x34} \\[2pt]
\text{Addr } 4003 &\rightarrow \texttt{0x12}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Memory Tip for KTU**: Many students remember the rule with the mnemonic **"Big-endian = Big-end first"** (i.e., MSB first / low address). A faster check: in **little-endian**, the bytes appear *reversed* when read left-to-right in memory.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Problem 4 — Aligned vs. Unaligned Access

**Problem Statement**: A CPU has a 32-bit data bus (4 bytes per word). Determine whether the following addresses are **aligned** or **unaligned** for a single-word load: (a) `0x00001000`, (b) `0x00001001`, (c) `0x00001004`, (d) `0x00001007`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a 32-bit word, the natural alignment boundary is **4 bytes**. A word access starting at address $A$ is aligned **if and only if**:

$$
A \mod 4 = 0
$$

Apply to each address. Note that $16 = 2^{4}$ and $4 = 2^{2}$, so checking $\mod 4$ is equivalent to inspecting the last 2 bits of the address in hexadecimal.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) } A &= \texttt{0x00001000}: \quad \texttt{0x1000} \mod 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ALIGNED} \\[4pt]
\text{(b) } A &= \texttt{0x00001001}: \quad \texttt{0x1001} \mod 4 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{UNALIGNED} \\[4pt]
\text{(c) } A &= \texttt{0x00001004}: \quad \texttt{0x1004} \mod 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{ALIGNED} \\[4pt]
\text{(d) } A &= \texttt{0x00001007}: \quad \texttt{0x1007} \mod 4 = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{UNALIGNED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight**: x86 CPUs *tolerate* unaligned access (with a microarchitectural penalty — 2 bus cycles). ARM cores in **strict-alignment mode** (default for ARMv7-A) *trap* (raise a `SIGBUS`) on unaligned access. RISC-V mandates alignment for natural-width loads. This is why C structs should be designed with `aligned` attributes in firmware.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Quick Reference for Exam)

| Symptom in Question | Look For | Action |
|:---|:---|:---|
| "How many address lines?" | Byte-addressable, $X$ MB | Use $n = \log_2(8X \cdot 2^{20})$ |
| "How much memory?" | $n$-bit address, word size $w$ | Capacity = $2^{n} \cdot (w/8)$ bytes |
| "Store this value…" | Endian + value | MSB-first (Big) or LSB-first (Little) |
| "Aligned or not?" | Address & word size | Check $A \mod (\text{bytes/word}) = 0$ |
| "Chip select logic" | Decoder mapping | Full vs. partial decoding |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: CPU–Memory Bus Interaction

The diagram below illustrates the canonical **Von Neumann** bus structure that governs every memory location access.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Core"]
    ABUS["Address Bus\nn bits unidirectional"]
    DBUS["Data Bus\nw bits bidirectional"]
    CBUS["Control Bus\nRD WR MIO signals"]
    CTRL["Memory Controller\nAddress Decoder"]
    BANK0["Memory Bank 0\nByte 0x0000"]
    BANK1["Memory Bank 1\nByte 0x0001"]
    BANKN["Memory Bank N-1\nByte 0xFFFF"]

    CPU -- "Address" --> ABUS
    CPU -- "Data In/Out" --> DBUS
    CPU -- "RD WR MIO" --> CBUS
    ABUS --> CTRL
    CBUS --> CTRL
    CTRL -- "Chip Select 0" --> BANK0
    CTRL -- "Chip Select 1" --> BANK1
    CTRL -- "Chip Select N-1" --> BANKN
    DBUS <--> BANK0
    DBUS <--> BANK1
    DBUS <--> BANKN

    classDef bus fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000;
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000;
    classDef mem fill:#E6F5E6,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000;
    classDef cpu fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000;
    class CPU cpu;
    class ABUS,DBUS,CBUS bus;
    class CTRL ctrl;
    class BANK0,BANK1,BANKN mem;
```

## 4.2 Address Space Layout Map (Logical View)

This diagram shows how a CPU's address space is partitioned into logical regions. Each region is a contiguous range of addresses (memory locations).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPACE["32-Bit Virtual Address Space (0x00000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF)"]
        direction TB
        REG0["0x00000000 - 0x000FFFFF\nReserved / Real-Mode IVT\n1 MB"]
        REG1["0x00100000 - 0xBFFFFFFF\nUser Program Space\napprox 3 GB"]
        REG2["0xC0000000 - 0xFFFFFFFF\nKernel / OS Space\n1 GB"]
    end

    REG0 --> REG1
    REG1 --> REG2

    classDef region fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000;
    class REG0,REG1,REG2 region;
```

## 4.3 Endianness Byte-Ordering Schematic

Visual comparison of how the 32-bit word `0x12345678` is laid out in memory for both endian conventions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BE["BIG-ENDIAN Layout"]
        direction LR
        BE_A["Addr +0\n0x12"] --- BE_B["Addr +1\n0x34"] --- BE_C["Addr +2\n0x56"] --- BE_D["Addr +3\n0x78"]
    end

    subgraph LE["LITTLE-ENDIAN Layout"]
        direction LR
        LE_A["Addr +0\n0x78"] --- LE_B["Addr +1\n0x56"] --- LE_C["Addr +2\n0x34"] --- LE_D["Addr +3\n0x12"]
    end

    classDef msb fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000;
    classDef lsb fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000;
    classDef mid fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000;

    class BE_A,LE_D msb;
    class BE_D,LE_A lsb;
    class BE_B,BE_C,LE_B,LE_C mid;
```

## 4.4 Address Decoding Logic Flow

This flowchart shows the decision logic inside a memory controller when an address arrives on the bus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Address arrives on bus"] --> DECODE{"Decode high-order\naddress bits"}
    DECODE -- "Match Region 0" --> CS0["Assert CS0\nfor Bank 0"]
    DECODE -- "Match Region 1" --> CS1["Assert CS1\nfor Bank 1"]
    DECODE -- "Match Region N" --> CSN["Assert CSN\nfor Bank N-1"]
    DECODE -- "No match" --> ERR["Bus Error / No device\n(floating data bus)"]
    CS0 --> READY0["Bank 0 drives data\non Data Bus"]
    CS1 --> READY1["Bank 1 drives data\non Data Bus"]
    CSN --> READYN["Bank N-1 drives data\non Data Bus"]

    classDef startnode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000;
    classDef decision fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000;
    classDef action fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000;
    classDef errnode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000;

    class START startnode;
    class DECODE decision;
    class CS0,CS1,CSN,READY0,READY1,READYN action;
    class ERR errnode;
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Big-Endian | Little-Endian |
|:---|:---|:---|
| MSB placement | Stored at the **lowest** address | Stored at the **highest** address |
| LSB placement | Stored at the **highest** address | Stored at the **lowest** address |
| Used in | Motorola 68k, SPARC, network byte order (RFC 1700) | Intel x86, x86-64, ARM (default little) |
| Example: Store `0xA1B2C3D4` at address 2000 | `[2000]=A1, [2001]=B2, [2002]=C3, [2003]=D4` | `[2000]=D4, [2001]=C3, [2002]=B2, [2003]=A1` |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for clean example.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an aligned memory access? Why is alignment important in modern processors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **aligned memory access** occurs when the address $A$ of a data item of size $S$ bytes satisfies:

$$A \mod S = 0$$

For example, a 4-byte integer must be stored at an address divisible by 4.

**Importance (1.5 marks)**: Alignment is important because —
- It allows the memory controller to fetch the entire word in **a single bus cycle** (the address bits $A_{k-1}\ldots A_0$ are zero, so the byte enables are clean).
- It avoids **spanning two memory rows/banks**, which would require two bus cycles.
- Many RISC architectures (ARM, RISC-V, MIPS) **trap** on unaligned access, so compiler-generated code *must* respect alignment.
- DMA engines and cache line fills (typically 16/32/64 bytes) work most efficiently with aligned buffers.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Omit units** → Lose 0.5–1 mark. Always write "bytes", "KB", or "MB" explicitly.
> - **Confuse address space vs. installed memory** → Loses 2 marks in Part B. State the assumption (e.g., "assume fully populated") clearly.
> - **Endianness diagrams** → Must show *addresses* as column headers, not just bytes. A wrong layout loses up to 3 marks.
> - **Don't simplify $2^{10} = 1024$ as $1000$** → KTU strictly uses $2^{10} = 1 \text{ KiB}$. Using $10^3$ loses a mark.
> - **Forgot to show address line derivation steps** → A common 1-mark loss on the "how many address lines" variant.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A computer system uses a **32-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. The memory is **byte-addressable** and uses **2M × 8 memory chips** to construct the main memory. Calculate:
   (i) The total addressable memory capacity in **bytes, KB, and MB**.
   (ii) The **number of address lines** required.
   (iii) The **number of memory chips** needed to fill the entire address space, and the **chip-select decoding scheme**.

**(b)** Explain the difference between **word-addressable** and **byte-addressable** memory. If a 16-bit processor has an address bus of 20 bits and uses **word-addressable** memory, calculate the maximum memory it can address in **words** and **bytes**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) (7 marks)**

**(i) Capacity** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{32} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
  &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
  &= 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ KB} = 2^{22} \text{ KB} \\[4pt]
  &= 4{,}096 \text{ MB} = 2^{12} \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Address lines** (1 mark): The 32-bit address bus already specifies the address lines. **Answer: 32 address lines**.

**(iii) Chips needed and decoding** (4 marks):

Each chip stores $2\text{M} \times 8 = 2^{21} \times 8$ bits = 2 MB. We need to fill $2^{32}$ bytes = $4$ GB. Convert to MB:

$$
2^{32} \text{ bytes} = 2^{32} / 2^{20} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ MB}
$$

Number of chips:

$$
N_{\text{chips}} = \frac{4096 \text{ MB}}{2 \text{ MB/chip}} = 2048 \text{ chips}
$$

Since each chip is 8 bits wide and the data bus is 16 bits, we need **2 chips in parallel per bank** (to form a 16-bit word). The chips can be organized in a $2 \times 1024$ matrix (2 chips wide × 1024 banks deep).

**Decoding scheme**: The 32-bit address is split as follows —
- Lowest bit $A_0$ → byte select within the 16-bit word.
- Next 10 bits $A_{10}\ldots A_1$ → selects 1 of 1024 banks.
- Remaining 21 bits $A_{31}\ldots A_{11}$ → used for **full address decoding** (no aliasing).

---

**Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Definitions** (3 marks):

| Property | Byte-Addressable | Word-Addressable |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Smallest addressable unit | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1 word (e.g., 16 bits) |
| Address increment | +1 per byte | +1 per word (jumps by 2 bytes for 16-bit) |
| Granularity | Finer | Coarser |

In word-addressable memory, an address refers to an *entire word*; you cannot directly address a single byte without additional byte-select logic.

**Calculation** (4 marks):

For a 20-bit address bus with 16-bit (2-byte) word-addressable memory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of words} &= 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ words} \\[4pt]
\text{Number of bytes} &= 2^{20} \times 2 = 2^{21} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer**: 1,048,576 words (= 1 MW) **OR** 2,097,152 bytes (= 2 MB).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **memory location** and **memory address**. A 36-bit processor uses byte-addressable memory. How much memory can it address? Express in **GB**. How many address lines are unused if only **64 GB** of physical RAM is installed?

**(b)** Consider the 64-bit value `0x1122334455667788` stored at memory address `0x2000` in a byte-addressable system.
   (i) Draw the byte-wise memory map for both **Big-Endian** and **Little-Endian** formats.
   (ii) If the CPU is **little-endian** and performs a 16-bit load from address `0x2004`, what 16-bit value does it read? Express in hex.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Definitions** (2 marks):

- **Memory Location**: The smallest addressable storage cell in main memory; in a byte-addressable system, it holds 8 bits.
- **Memory Address**: A unique binary number assigned to a memory location by the CPU, used to read/write that location.

**Total addressable memory** (3 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{36} \text{ bytes} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{2^{36}}{2^{30}} \text{ GB} = 2^{6} \text{ GB} = \mathbf{64 \text{ GB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Unused address lines** (2 marks):

Physical RAM = 64 GB. Since the address space is already 64 GB, **0 address lines are unused in the sense of address space**. However, if the CPU's address pins are 36 and we install exactly 64 GB = $2^{36}$ bytes, then **no address lines are wasted** — they are all used to decode chips. If only 16 GB were installed, then 2 of the 36 lines (the MSBs) would be left unconnected to any chip, and **2 address lines would be unused** (or aliased under partial decoding).

> **Final Answer**: 64 GB addressable; with 64 GB installed, **0 address lines are unused**.

---

**Part (b) (7 marks)**

**(i) Memory Map** (5 marks):

Decompose `0x1122334455667788` into 8 bytes (MSB → LSB):

$$
\texttt{0x11}, \texttt{0x22}, \texttt{0x33}, \texttt{0x44}, \texttt{0x55}, \texttt{0x66}, \texttt{0x77}, \texttt{0x88}
$$

**Big-Endian Memory Map**:

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 | 0x2004 | 0x2005 | 0x2006 | 0x2007 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Byte | 0x11 | 0x22 | 0x33 | 0x44 | 0x55 | 0x66 | 0x77 | 0x88 |

**Little-Endian Memory Map**:

| Address | 0x2000 | 0x2001 | 0x2002 | 0x2003 | 0x2004 | 0x2005 | 0x2006 | 0x2007 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Byte | 0x88 | 0x77 | 0x66 | 0x55 | 0x44 | 0x33 | 0x22 | 0x11 |

**(ii) 16-bit load at 0x2004 on a little-endian CPU** (2 marks):

In little-endian, address 0x2004 holds the byte `0x44` (LSB of the 16-bit half-word) and 0x2005 holds `0x33` (MSB). The CPU reconstructs the 16-bit value as `(MSB << 8) \vert LSB`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Value} &= \texttt{0x33} \cdot 2^{8} \; + \; \texttt{0x44} \cdot 2^{0} \\[4pt]
            &= \texttt{0x3300} + \texttt{0x44} \\[4pt]
            &= \mathbf{\texttt{0x3344}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer**: The 16-bit load returns **`0x3344`**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** (use this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):

- [x] A **memory location** = smallest addressable unit; in modern CPUs = **1 byte**.
- [x] An **address** is just an integer index from **0** to **$2^n - 1$** where $n$ = address bus width.
- [x] **Address space** = $2^n$ locations; **capacity** = $2^n \times$ (bits/location).
- [x] $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10}$ bytes, $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20}$, $1 \text{ GB} = 2^{30}$, $1 \text{ TB} = 2^{40}$ (in KTU binary convention).
- [x] **Big-endian** = MSB at lowest address; **Little-endian** = LSB at lowest address.
- [x] **Aligned** = address is a multiple of the data size; **Unaligned** otherwise.
- [x] 32-bit address bus → **4 GB** of byte-addressable memory.
- [x] 36-bit address bus → **64 GB** of byte-addressable memory.
- [x] 64-bit address bus → **$2^{64}$ bytes** = **16 EB (exabytes)**.
- [x] To find address lines for $M$ MB of byte-addressable memory: $n = \log_2(8 \times M \times 2^{20})$.
- [x] To find address lines for $W$ words of $b$-byte word-addressable memory: $n = \log_2(W)$.
- [x] **Partial decoding** causes address aliasing; **full decoding** does not.
- [x] Always include **units** (bytes / KB / MB / GB) in your final answer.
- [x] Show **address columns** in endianness diagrams — not just byte values.
- [x] For 16-bit loads in little-endian, combine as `(high_byte << 8) \vert low_byte`.
- [x] Memorize the unit conversion: $2^{10}, 2^{20}, 2^{30}, 2^{40}$ → K, M, G, T.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Memory Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In computer architecture, **Memory Operations** refer to the fundamental read and write transactions executed between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** and the **memory subsystem**. The two primitive operations supported by every memory unit are: - **Memory Read (Load) Operation** – A non-destructive transfer of a data word from a specified memory location to the CPU register. - **Memory Write (Store) Operation** – A transfer of a data word from a CPU register to a specified memory location, overwriting the previous content. Every memory transaction requires three fundamental signals on the system bus: 1. **Address Bus** – carries the unique location identifier ($n$ address lines allow $2^n$ distinct locations). 2. **Data Bus** – carries the bidirectional payload (the word being read or written). 3. **Control Bus** – carries the Read/$\overline{Write}$ strobe signal that distinguishes the operation type. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates understanding of memory organization, basic read/write transactions, memory access methods, and performance metrics. These form the foundation for pipelining, cache design, and virtual memory in higher modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Library System" Think of the computer's main memory as a **massive library** with $2^n$ uniquely numbered cubbyholes (lockers): - **Read Operation** = You walk up to the librarian and say, *"Please fetch me the contents of Cubbyhole #1047."* The librarian walks over, opens the locker, hands you the book (data) — and critically, **the book is NOT removed** from the locker. The original contents remain intact. - **Write Operation** = You hand a new book to the librarian and say, *"Please place this in Cubbyhole #2218."* The librarian opens the locker, removes whatever was there, and inserts your new book. **The old content is destroyed.** - **Address** = The unique cubbyhole number. - **Data Bus** = Your hand carrying the book back and forth. - **Memory Controller** = The librarian managing the catalog. - **Access Time** = The time it takes the librarian to walk to the cubbyhole and retrieve the book. ## 1.3 The Two Primitive Memory Signals | Signal | Direction | Function | Active Logic | |---|---|---|---| | $\overline{RD}$ (Read) | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory | Enables memory output drivers | **Active LOW** | | $\overline{WR}$ (Write) | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory | Latches data into memory | **Active LOW** | | $M/\overline{IO}$ | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory | Selects memory vs I/O device | M=1 for memory | | Address $A_{n-1}\ldots A_0$ | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory | Selects one of $2^n$ locations | Always valid | > [!NOTE] > The overbar (e.g., $\overline{RD}$) denotes **active-low logic**, meaning the operation is triggered when the line drops to **logic 0** (0 V). This convention is inherited from TTL/CMOS bus standards and is tested frequently in KTU examinations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Memory Read Operation The CPU initiates a read by performing the following precisely ordered sequence: 1. **Place the address** on the address bus ($A_{n-1}\ldots A_0$). 2. **Assert the $\overline{RD}$ control signal** (pull low) to inform the memory that a read is requested. 3. **De-assert the $\overline{WR}$ signal** to avoid bus contention. 4. The memory's **internal decoder** selects the targeted word-line, charging the bit-lines with the stored data. 5. After the elapse of **access time $t_A$**, the memory places the data word on the data bus. 6. The CPU **latches the data** into the destination register on the next active clock edge. 7. The CPU **de-asserts $\overline{RD}$**; the memory removes its drivers from the bus (high-impedance state). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A read operation is **non-destructive** — the data remains in the memory cell after being read. This is why main memory cells use bistable elements (SRAM flip-flops) or refreshed capacitors (DRAM) that retain state passively. ## 2.2 Anatomy of a Memory Write Operation 1. **Place the address** on the address bus. 2. **Place the data** to be written on the data bus. 3. **Assert the $\overline{WR}$ control signal** (pull low). 4. The memory's **write circuitry** decodes the address and enables the target cell's write transistors. 5. The data on the bus is **latched into the addressed cell**. 6. The CPU **de-asserts $\overline{WR}$**; the data hold requirement must be satisfied. > [!WARNING] > **Data Hold Time:** The data must remain stable on the bus for a minimum duration after the trailing edge of $\overline{WR}$ — this is called the **data hold time $t_{DH}$**. Violating it causes metastability and silent data corruption — a common KTU viva question. ## 2.3 Memory Access Methods — A Comparative Study KTU frequently tests this classification. Memory devices are categorized by *how* locations are accessed: | Access Method | Mechanism | Example Device | Access Time | |---|---|---|---| | **Sequential Access** | Read/write heads must traverse from current position linearly to target | Magnetic Tape | Highly **variable** (depends on position) | | **Direct Access** | Jumps to a track, then searches sequentially within the track | Hard Disk, CD-ROM | **Semi-variable** (track seek + rotational latency) | | **Random Access** | Any location accessed in **equal, fixed time** regardless of address | RAM (SRAM, DRAM), ROM | **Constant** $t_A$ | | **Associative Access** | Search by **content** (not address) using parallel comparators | Cache Memory (CAM), TLB | **Constant** $t_A$ | ## 2.4 Memory Hierarchy — The Speed–Cost–Capacity Trade-off The **memory hierarchy** is a layered arrangement of storage types designed to minimize the **average access time** while keeping the **cost per bit** low. Moving *downward* in the hierarchy: capacity increases, cost/bit decreases, and access time increases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchical Principle:** The CPU accesses the fastest level first (Registers → Cache → Main Memory → SSD/HDD). Only on a *miss* does the request propagate to the next slower level. This locality-of-reference exploitation gives the **illusion of a large, fast memory**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of addressable locations | $N = 2^n$ | locations | $n$ = number of address lines | | 2 | Memory capacity (bits) | $C = 2^n \times w$ | bits | $w$ = word size in bits | | 3 | Memory capacity (bytes) | $C = \dfrac{2^n \times w}{8}$ | bytes | Divide by 8 to convert bits $\rightarrow$ bytes | | 4 | Access Time | $t_A$ | ns / $\mu$s | Time from address valid $\rightarrow$ data valid | | 5 | Memory Cycle Time | $t_C$ | ns / $\mu$s | $t_C \geq t_A$; minimum gap between successive ops | | 6 | Transfer Rate (Bandwidth) | $B = \dfrac{w}{t_C}$ | bits/second | Higher word size $\Rightarrow$ higher bandwidth | | 7 | Hit Ratio | $H = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq H \leq 1$ | | 8 | Miss Ratio | $M = 1 - H$ | dimensionless | Probability of going to next level | | 9 | **Average Memory Access Time (2-level)** | $T_{AMAT} = H \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot (t_1 + t_2)$ | ns | Simplifies to $t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2$ | | 10 | Access Method Cost Ratio | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{C_{fast}\cdot S_{fast} + C_{slow}\cdot S_{slow}}{S_{fast}+S_{slow}}$ | ₹/bit | Weighted average cost per bit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP — Memory Cycle Time vs Access Time:** $t_C$ is *always* $\geq t_A$ because, after a read, the memory cells need a *recovery time* to re-stabilize the sense amplifiers before the next operation. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why can't we start a new read immediately after the previous read?* — Answer: due to the **memory cycle time** overhead. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Memory operations underpin every computing domain: **embedded systems** use deterministic memory access times for real-time guarantees, **GPU computing** uses wide-word burst memory operations for parallel throughput, **database engines** optimize memory access patterns for query latency, and **AI accelerators** use High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) stacks where 1024-bit-wide buses deliver terabytes/sec bandwidth — all fundamentally driven by the read/write contract described above. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a Two-Level Hierarchy **Problem Setup:** Consider a system with a Cache (Level 1) and Main Memory (Level 2). - Cache access time = $t_1$ (e.g., 5 ns) - Main memory access time = $t_2$ (e.g., 100 ns) - Cache hit ratio = $H$ (e.g., 0.90) **Step 1 — Enumerate the two possible outcomes per access.** When the CPU issues a memory request, two mutually exclusive events can occur: - **Event A — Cache Hit** (probability $= H$): The requested word is found in cache. Only the cache is accessed; time taken is $t_1$. - **Event B — Cache Miss** (probability $= 1-H$): The requested word is NOT in cache. The cache must be accessed (to confirm the miss), and then the main memory must be accessed. Total time taken is $t_1 + t_2$. **Step 2 — Compute the expected (average) access time.** Using the discrete random variable expectation formula $E[X] = \sum_i p_i x_i$: $$ T_{AMAT} = P(\text{Hit}) \cdot T_{hit} + P(\text{Miss}) \cdot T_{miss} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the symbolic values.** $$ T_{AMAT} = H \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot (t_1 + t_2) $$ **Step 4 — Algebraically expand and simplify.** $$ T_{AMAT} = H \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2 $$ $$ T_{AMAT} = t_1 \cdot \bigl[H + (1-H)\bigr] + (1-H) \cdot t_2 $$ **Step 5 — Recognize the bracketed term as a probability identity.** Since $H + (1-H) = 1$, the first term collapses: $$ \boxed{T_{AMAT} = t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical sanity check with typical values.** Let $t_1 = 5$ ns, $t_2 = 100$ ns, $H = 0.90$: $$ T_{AMAT} = 5 + (1 - 0.90) \times 100 = 5 + 0.10 \times 100 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ ns} $$ This represents a **6.67× speedup** over a no-cache baseline of 100 ns, achieved purely by the **principle of locality**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Capacity and Address Line Calculation **Problem:** A memory system has $n = 16$ address lines and a word size of $w = 32$ bits.
