Determine (a) the number of addressable locations, (b) the total capacity in bytes, and (c) the data bus width required for a single-cycle parallel read. **Step 1 — Number of locations.** With $n$ address lines, the binary decoder can uniquely select one of $2^n$ cells. $$ N = 2^n = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ K locations} $$ **Step 2 — Capacity in bits.** $$ C_{bits} = N \times w = 2^n \times w = 2^{16} \times 32 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3 — Capacity in bytes.** $$ C_{bytes} = \frac{C_{bits}}{8} = \frac{2^{21}}{2^{3}} = 2^{18} = 262{,}144 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 4 — Data bus width.** Since the memory is **word-addressable** with $w = 32$ bits, the data bus must be 32 bits wide (i.e., 4 bytes transferred per access). $$ \boxed{\text{Data bus width} = w = 32 \text{ bits}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute transfer rate / bandwidth.** If memory cycle time $t_C = 50$ ns: $$ B = \frac{w}{t_C} = \frac{32 \text{ bits}}{50 \text{ ns}} = 0.64 \text{ Gbps} = 80 \text{ MB/s} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Access Time with Three-Level Hierarchy **Problem:** A system has L1 Cache ($t_1 = 2$ ns), L2 Cache ($t_2 = 20$ ns), and Main Memory ($t_3 = 200$ ns). Hit ratios are $H_1 = 0.85$ (L1) and $H_2 = 0.95$ (L2, given L1 miss). **Step 1 — Define the cumulative probabilities.** - Probability of L1 hit: $P_1 = H_1 = 0.85$ - Probability of L1 miss AND L2 hit: $P_2 = (1-H_1) \cdot H_2 = 0.15 \times 0.95 = 0.1425$ - Probability of L1 miss AND L2 miss (i.e., main memory hit): $P_3 = (1-H_1) \cdot (1-H_2) = 0.15 \times 0.05 = 0.0075$ **Step 2 — Define the time for each scenario.** - L1 hit: $T_1 = t_1 = 2$ ns - L1 miss + L2 hit: $T_2 = t_1 + t_2 = 22$ ns - Both miss: $T_3 = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 222$ ns **Step 3 — Compute the weighted expectation.** $$ T_{eff} = P_1 T_1 + P_2 T_2 + P_3 T_3 $$ $$ T_{eff} = (0.85)(2) + (0.1425)(22) + (0.0075)(222) $$ $$ T_{eff} = 1.7 + 3.135 + 1.665 = 6.5 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the speedup over L3-only access.** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{200 \text{ ns}}{6.5 \text{ ns}} \approx 30.77\times $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation demonstrates that hierarchical caching delivers **super-linear speedup** despite each level being slower — the multiplicative effect of high hit ratios compounds geometrically. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Technology Comparison | Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | ROM (Mask) | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Cell Bistability | 6T flip-flop | 1T + capacitor | Fuse/antifuse | Fuse | Floating-gate MOSFET | Floating-gate | Floating-gate | | Refresh Required? | **No** | **Yes** (~64 ms) | No | No | No | No | No | | Volatile? | **Yes** | **Yes** | No | No | No | No | No | | Access Time | 1–10 ns | 50–100 ns | 20–200 ns | 30–200 ns | 30–200 ns | 50–200 ns | 50–200 ns | | Density (bits/mm²) | Low | **High** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | | Cost/bit | **High** | Low | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | | Typical Use | Cache | Main memory | Firmware | One-time programming | BIOS | BIOS updates | SSD, USB | | Write Endurance | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | None | One-time | Limited | $10^5$ cycles | $10^4$–$10^6$ cycles | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid This diagram visualizes the speed-cost-capacity trade-off, with the fastest, smallest, costliest storage nearest the CPU and the slowest, largest, cheapest storage furthest away. ```mermaid graph TD regA["REGISTERS
1-8 ns
Size: bytes
Cost/bit: HIGHEST"] l1A["L1 CACHE
2-5 ns
Size: 32-128 KB
Built from SRAM"] l2A["L2 CACHE
10-20 ns
Size: 256 KB-1 MB
Built from SRAM"] l3A["L3 CACHE
30-50 ns
Size: 4-32 MB
Shared across cores"] mainA["MAIN MEMORY RAM
50-100 ns
Size: 4-64 GB
Built from DRAM"] ssdA["SSD / FLASH
50-500 us
Size: 256 GB-4 TB
Non-volatile"] hddA["HDD / MAGNETIC
5-20 ms
Size: 1-20 TB
Mechanical latency"] tapeA["ARCHIVAL TAPE
Seconds
Size: PB-scale
Cold storage"] regA --> l1A l1A --> l2A l2A --> l3A l3A --> mainA mainA --> ssdA ssdA --> hddA hddA --> tapeA regA -.->|"FASTEST
SMALLEST"| tapeA tapeA -.->|"SLOWEST
LARGEST"| regA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each downward arrow represents the *miss path* — what happens when the CPU does not find the requested data at the current level. Each upward arrow (conceptually) represents the *data refill* path when fresh data is brought up to a higher level. ## 4.2 Read Operation — Handshake Timing Flow This diagram captures the exact sequence of bus transactions and memory state transitions for a single memory read cycle. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START: CPU needs data from address ADDR"] s1A["Step 1: Place ADDR on Address Bus"] s2A["Step 2: Assert RD_bar LOW and WR_bar HIGH"] s3A["Step 3: Memory Internal Decoder activates word line"] s4A["Step 4: Bit lines charge to stored cell values"] s5A["Step 5: Sense Amplifiers detect and amplify"] s6A{"Step 6: Has access time t_A elapsed?"} s7A["Step 7: Memory drives Data Bus with word value"] s8A["Step 8: CPU latches data into destination register"] s9A["Step 9: De-assert RD_bar HIGH"] s10A["Step 10: Memory releases data bus to HIGH Z"] endA["END: Read complete, data still in memory cell"] startA --> s1A s1A --> s2A s2A --> s3A s3A --> s4A s4A --> s5A s5A --> s6A s6A -->|"NO - wait"| s5A s6A -->|"YES"| s7A s7A --> s8A s8A --> s9A s9A --> s10A s10A --> endA ``` ## 4.3 Write Operation — Handshake Timing Flow This diagram captures the bus protocol and memory cell update sequence for a write transaction. ```mermaid flowchart TD startB["START: CPU needs to write DATA to address ADDR"] t1B["Step 1: Place ADDR on Address Bus"] t2B["Step 2: Place DATA on Data Bus"] t3B["Step 3: Assert WR_bar LOW and RD_bar HIGH"] t4B["Step 4: Memory decoder selects target word line"] t5B["Step 5: Write drivers force data onto bit lines"] t6B["Step 6: Cell storage elements capture data"] t7B["Step 7: Hold data for data hold time t_DH"] t8B["Step 8: De-assert WR_bar HIGH"] t9B["Step 9: CPU releases address and data bus"] t10B["Step 10: Cell now permanently stores new DATA"] endB["END: Write complete, old data destroyed"] startB --> t1B t1B --> t2B t2B --> t3B t3B --> t4B t4B --> t5B t5B --> t6B t6B --> t7B t7B --> t8B t8B --> t9B t9B --> t10B t10B --> endB ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Memory Chip This diagram maps the *internal subsystems* of a typical RAM chip, showing how the address, data, and control signals interact with the storage array. ```mermaid graph LR addrIn["ADDRESS INPUT PINS
A_n-1 to A_0"] ctrlIn["CONTROL INPUTS
CS, RD_bar, WR_bar"] dataIO["BIDIRECTIONAL DATA PINS
D_m-1 to D_0"] addrReg["ADDRESS BUFFER
and LATCH"] rowDec["ROW DECODER
selects one of 2^k word lines"] colDec["COLUMN DECODER
selects one of 2^(n-k) bit lines"] ctrlLogic["CONTROL LOGIC
generates internal R/W strobes"] senseAmp["SENSE AMPLIFIERS
and WRITE DRIVERS"] cellArray["STORAGE CELL ARRAY
2^n x m bit cells
SRAM or DRAM"] dataBuf["DATA INPUT/OUTPUT BUFFER
tri-state drivers"] addrIn --> addrReg ctrlIn --> ctrlLogic addrReg --> rowDec addrReg --> colDec rowDec --> cellArray colDec --> cellArray ctrlLogic --> senseAmp cellArray --> senseAmp senseAmp --> dataBuf dataBuf --> dataIO ctrlLogic --> dataBuf ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The split into **row decoder** (large, slow) and **column decoder** (smaller, fast) is the foundation of **address multiplexing** used in DRAM to reduce pin count — a high-value KTU topic for Module 2 onwards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. Define memory access time and memory cycle time. Why is cycle time always greater than access time? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Memory Access Time ($t_A$):** It is the time interval between the moment the memory receives a valid address (and the Read signal is asserted) and the moment the data word becomes available on the output data bus. It is a pure measure of how fast the storage technology can produce data once asked. **Memory Cycle Time ($t_C$):** It is the minimum time interval between the **start of two successive memory operations**. It includes access time **plus** the recovery time needed for the memory's internal circuitry (sense amplifiers, address decoders, data buffers) to re-stabilize before accepting the next request. **Why $t_C > t_A$:** After a read, the bit-lines must be **pre-charged** back to a reference voltage, sense amplifiers must be reset, and the data bus must be released to high-impedance to avoid contention with the next operation. This *recovery overhead* is non-zero, making the cycle time strictly greater than the access time. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"cycle time is greater than access time because memory is slow."* This earns **zero marks** because it is tautological. The examiner expects the *physical reason* — recovery, pre-charge, and bus tri-state overhead. Always state the **mechanism**, not the conclusion. ### Question 2. Differentiate between Random Access Memory (RAM) and Sequential Access Memory. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Random Access Memory (RAM) | Sequential Access Memory | |---|---|---| | Access Mechanism | Any location accessed directly in equal time | Must traverse from current position linearly | | Access Time | **Constant** regardless of address | **Variable**, depends on physical distance from current head position | | Hardware Complexity | Decoder + cell array | Read/write head + transport mechanism | | Speed | Fast | Slow | | Example | SRAM, DRAM | Magnetic tape | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A frequent blunder is listing **hard disks** as a *random access* device. While disks *appear* random, they are technically **Direct Access** — the head must *seek* to a track, then wait for *rotational latency*. True random access requires zero mechanical movement. Reserve "Random Access" exclusively for **RAM chips**. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question 1 — Option A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set B]` **(a)** Explain the basic read and write memory operations in detail with neat bus signal timing diagrams. Discuss the role of the control signals $\overline{RD}$ and $\overline{WR}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A memory operation is a coordinated bus transaction between the CPU (master) and the memory module (slave). The CPU drives the address and control signals, while data flows bidirectionally. **Read Operation Steps:** 1. The CPU places the target address $A_{n-1}\ldots A_0$ on the address bus. 2. The CPU asserts the **Memory/IO** select line ($M/\overline{IO} = 1$) to indicate a memory access. 3. The CPU asserts **Chip Select** ($\overline{CS} = 0$) to enable the specific memory chip. 4. The CPU pulls $\overline{RD}$ **LOW** (active low read strobe). 5. The memory's internal row/column decoder selects the target cell within the storage array. 6. The sense amplifiers detect the stored charge/voltage and amplify it to full logic levels. 7. After access time $t_A$, the memory's output drivers place the data word on the data bus. 8. The CPU latches the data on the next clock edge into the destination register. 9. The CPU releases $\overline{RD}$ to HIGH; memory tri-states its data bus drivers. **Write Operation Steps:** 1. The CPU places the target address on the address bus. 2. The CPU places the data word to be written on the data bus. 3. The CPU asserts $\overline{CS} = 0$ to enable the chip. 4. The CPU pulls $\overline{WR}$ **LOW** (active low write strobe). 5. The memory's decoder selects the target cell. 6. The write drivers force the data bus values onto the bit-lines. 7. The cell's storage element (flip-flop or capacitor) is forced to the new state. 8. Data must remain valid for the data hold time $t_{DH}$ after $\overline{WR}$ de-asserts. 9. The CPU releases $\overline{WR}$ and removes address and data. **Role of Control Signals:** - $\overline{RD}$ : Active-low read enable. When LOW, memory is in read mode and drives the data bus. - $\overline{WR}$ : Active-low write enable. When LOW, memory latches the data bus contents into the addressed cell. **Timing Diagram (textual representation):** ``` ___ ___ ___ ___ ___ ___ CLK | |___| |___| |___| |___| |___| |___| |___ ____________________ ADDR XXXXXXX| ADDR_VAL |XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX _______ RD_bar __________________________| |________________ _______________ DATA_OUT _______________________________| READ_DATA |__ ___ WR_bar _________________________________________________|_ ___ DATA_IN ________________________________________| WRITE | ``` **(b)** A computer system has an L1 cache with access time 5 ns and main memory access time 100 ns. The cache hit ratio is 92%. Calculate (i) the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT), (ii) the speedup over a cache-less system, and (iii) the new hit ratio required to achieve an AMAT of 8 ns. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $t_1 = 5$ ns (cache) - $t_2 = 100$ ns (main memory) - $H = 0.92$ **(i) AMAT Calculation:** Using the derived formula: $$ T_{AMAT} = t_1 + (1 - H) \cdot t_2 $$ Substituting values: $$ T_{AMAT} = 5 + (1 - 0.92) \times 100 = 5 + 0.08 \times 100 = 5 + 8 = 13 \text{ ns} $$ **(ii) Speedup over cache-less system:** A cache-less system always accesses main memory, so $T_{no\_cache} = 100$ ns. $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{no\_cache}}{T_{AMAT}} = \frac{100}{13} \approx 7.69\times $$ **(iii) Required hit ratio for $T_{AMAT} = 8$ ns:** Setting up the equation: $$ 8 = 5 + (1 - H_{new}) \times 100 $$ $$ 3 = (1 - H_{new}) \times 100 $$ $$ 1 - H_{new} = 0.03 $$ $$ H_{new} = 0.97 = 97\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Part (iii) is the most commonly failed sub-part. Students incorrectly set $8 = 0.92 \cdot 5 + 0.08 \cdot 100$ and try to solve for $H$ — this is wrong because the formula is *not* linear in $H$ on the right side after expansion. The correct setup is $T_{AMAT} = t_1 + (1-H)\cdot t_2$, and the unknown $H$ appears only in the $(1-H)$ term. ### Question 1 — Option B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the memory hierarchy in a computer system with a neat diagram. Discuss the principle of locality that justifies this hierarchy. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Memory Hierarchy Definition:** The memory hierarchy is a tiered organization of storage devices arranged by speed, cost, and capacity, designed to provide the *average access time* of the fastest level while offering the *capacity* of the slowest level. **Hierarchy Levels (top to bottom):** 1. **CPU Registers** — Few bytes, 1 ns access, controlled by compiler. 2. **L1 Cache** — Split I-cache and D-cache, ~32-64 KB, 2-5 ns, SRAM. 3. **L2 Cache** — Unified or per-core, ~256 KB – 1 MB, 10-15 ns, SRAM. 4. **L3 Cache (Last Level Cache)** — Shared, ~4-32 MB, 30-50 ns, SRAM. 5. **Main Memory (RAM)** — 4-64 GB, 50-100 ns, DRAM. 6. **Solid State Drive (SSD)** — 256 GB – 4 TB, 50-500 μs, NAND Flash. 7. **Hard Disk Drive (HDD)** — 1-20 TB, 5-20 ms, magnetic platters. 8. **Archival Tape / Cloud** — PB-scale, seconds to minutes. **Principle of Locality** (the *theoretical justification* for the hierarchy): 1. **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, it is *likely to be referenced again in the near future*. Hence, keeping recently accessed data in a fast nearby cache is beneficial. 2. **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, *nearby locations* are likely to be referenced soon. Hence, fetching an entire cache line (block) on a miss exploits this. **Why It Works:** Real programs exhibit 90%+ temporal and spatial locality, making hit ratios of 0.85-0.99 achievable. This means the CPU effectively experiences the speed of the cache for the vast majority of accesses, with occasional excursions to slower memory. **(b)** A memory chip has 14 address pins and 8 data pins. Calculate: (i) the number of memory locations, (ii) the memory capacity in bytes, (iii) the memory capacity in KB, and (iv) the number of pins required to be saved if this memory is organized as $16K \times 8$ using address multiplexing (row-column multiplexed) compared to direct addressing. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $n = 14$ address pins - $w = 8$ data pins (byte-wide memory) **(i) Number of memory locations:** $$ N = 2^n = 2^{14} = 16{,}384 = 16K \text{ locations} $$ **(ii) Capacity in bytes:** Since word size is 8 bits = 1 byte, each location stores 1 byte. So: $$ C = 16{,}384 \text{ bytes} $$ **(iii) Capacity in KB:** $$ C = \frac{16{,}384}{1024} = 16 \text{ KB} $$ **(iv) Address multiplexing analysis:** A $16K \times 8$ memory requires $\log_2(16K) = 14$ address bits normally, delivered via 14 dedicated address pins (RAS + CAS scheme not needed for the capacity, but the question is testing the pin-saving concept of address multiplexing for a DRAM array). **Without multiplexing (Direct Addressing):** - Address pins = **14** - Data pins = **8** - Total pins = **22** **With Multiplexing (Row-Column scheme):** The 16K = $128 \times 128$ internal array organization is chosen. So we need $\log_2(128) = 7$ row address pins and $\log_2(128) = 7$ column address pins. The same 7 physical pins carry row address first (latched by RAS) and then column address (latched by CAS). - Multiplexed address pins = **7** - Plus 1 RAS pin and 1 CAS pin = 2 control pins - Data pins = **8** - Total pins = **7 + 2 + 8 = 17** **Pins saved:** $$ \Delta = 22 - 17 = 5 \text{ pins} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (iv), students frequently forget to **add the RAS and CAS pins** to the multiplexed count, or they treat multiplexing as a *strict* halving of address pins (which is only true for square arrays). Always explicitly state the array organization ($M \times N$) before computing the multiplexed pin count. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memory Operations (Module 1)** - **Two primitive memory operations:** **READ** (non-destructive, CPU $\leftarrow$ Memory) and **WRITE** (destructive, CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory). - **Three fundamental bus types:** Address bus (unidirectional, $n$ lines), Data bus (bidirectional, $w$ lines), Control bus (carries $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{CS}$). - **Control signals are ACTIVE-LOW:** $\overline{RD}$ and $\overline{WR}$ use overbar notation; assertion means logic 0. - **Access time $t_A$** = time from valid address to valid data output. - **Cycle time $t_C \geq t_A$** = minimum gap between successive memory operations (includes recovery + pre-charge). - **Transfer rate / Bandwidth** $B = w / t_C$ bits per second. - **Hit Ratio** $H = N_{hits} / N_{total}$; **Miss Ratio** $M = 1 - H$. - **AMAT formula (2-level):** $T_{AMAT} = t_1 + (1-H) \cdot t_2$ — commit this to memory. - **Memory capacity:** $C = 2^n \times w$ bits, where $n$ = address lines, $w$ = word size in bits. - **Four access methods:** Sequential (tape), Direct (disk), Random (RAM), Associative (cache/CAM). - **Memory hierarchy moves downward** = slower + larger + cheaper/bit. - **Two types of locality:** Temporal (recently used) and Spatial (nearby addresses). - **SRAM uses 6 transistors per cell** (flip-flop, no refresh); **DRAM uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor** (needs periodic refresh every ~64 ms). - **DRAM uses address multiplexing** (RAS/CAS) to reduce pin count — a key design pattern. - **Read does NOT destroy data**; **Write OVERWRITES** previous content. - **Data hold time $t_{DH}$** must be satisfied after $\overline{WR}$ de-asserts to prevent metastability. - **Read-while-write** is not allowed in standard RAM (bus contention); special dual-port RAM exists for simultaneous access. - **Three addressable granularities:** Bit-addressable, Byte-addressable, Word-addressable — KTU syllabus emphasizes the difference in derived capacity formulas. - **Effective access time for an n-level hierarchy** is computed using the **principle of nested expectations** (Law of Total Probability).

topics.title: Instructions and Instruction Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instructions and Instruction Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Computer Instruction

A **computer instruction** is a binary-coded command that directs the processor to perform a specific operation on a set of data operands stored in processor registers, memory locations, or I/O devices. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PERAT414 - Computer Organization and Architecture*, an instruction is the smallest unit of work in a program and is fetched, decoded, and executed by the processor through the **Instruction Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> *Instructions and Instruction Sequencing* covers:
> - Instruction format (opcode + operand fields)
> - Types of operations (data transfer, arithmetic, logical, control, I/O)
> - Addressing modes (effective address computation)
> - Register transfer notation for instruction sequencing
> - Branching, subroutines, and stack-based execution

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a computer program as a **cooking recipe**:

- The **Recipe Book** = Memory (stores all steps and ingredients)
- The **Chef's Hand** = Processor / CPU (executes the steps)
- The **Recipe Card** = Instruction (one specific step, e.g., "add 2 cups of sugar")
- The **Card Holder** = Instruction Register (IR) (holds the current step being read)
- The **Pointer to Current Card** = Program Counter (PC) (points to next step)
- The **Counter / Bowl** = Accumulator / General Purpose Register (stores intermediate results)
- The **Pantry Address Label** = Memory Address (location of ingredients/data)

Just as a chef cannot bake a cake without sequentially reading each recipe card, a CPU cannot run a program without **sequentially fetching, decoding, and executing** each instruction stored in memory.

### 1.3 Standard Instruction Format

A generic instruction contains the following binary fields:

| Field | Function |
|---|---|
| **Opcode (Operation Code)** | Specifies the operation to be performed (e.g., ADD, LOAD) |
| **Source Operand Reference** | Indicates where the source data is located |
| **Destination Operand Reference** | Indicates where the result is to be stored |
| **Next Instruction Reference** | Usually implicit (PC + 1), except in branch instructions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** In a typical 32-bit KTU-recommended RISC-style instruction, the opcode is often **6 bits**, register fields are **5 bits each**, and the immediate/offset field is **16 bits**. Always check the specific CPU architecture being studied.

### 1.4 What is "Instruction Sequencing"?

**Instruction sequencing** refers to the ordered flow of control in which a processor fetches and executes instructions one after another, modifying the **Program Counter (PC)** to point to the next instruction. Sequencing may be:

1. **Sequential** — PC is incremented automatically (PC ← PC + 1).
2. **Conditional Branch** — PC is updated only if a condition flag (Z, N, C, V) is satisfied.
3. **Unconditional Branch / Jump** — PC is overwritten with a new address.
4. **Subroutine Call** — PC is saved on the stack, then updated to the subroutine address.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Instruction — Field-Level Breakdown

A binary instruction is partitioned into discrete fields. For the widely-used **Three-Address Instruction Format**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction} &= \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{n \text{ bits}} \;\mid\; \underbrace{\text{Dest}}_{k \text{ bits}} \;\mid\; \underbrace{\text{Src1}}_{k \text{ bits}} \;\mid\; \underbrace{\text{Src2}}_{m \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Opcode bits (n):** Determines the operation. With *n* bits, the CPU can support up to $2^n$ distinct instructions.
- **Operand reference fields (k, m):** Encode the **addressing mode bits** plus the actual operand value/address.

### 2.2 Processor Registers Used in Sequencing (KTU Standard Set)

| Register | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Program Counter | **PC** | Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched |
| Instruction Register | **IR** | Holds the currently fetched instruction |
| Memory Address Register | **AR** | Holds the memory address for read/write |
| Memory Data Register | **DR** | Holds the data read from or written to memory |
| Accumulator / ALU Result | **AC** | Temporary register for ALU output |
| Condition Flags | **N, Z, C, V** | Set by ALU to indicate Negative, Zero, Carry, Overflow |

### 2.3 Register Transfer Notation (RTN) — The KTU Standard

The **Register Transfer Notation** is a formal way to express instruction sequencing, as prescribed in Module 1 of PERAT414.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fetch Phase:}\\
AR &\leftarrow PC \\
DR &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[AR] \\
IR &\leftarrow DR \\
PC &\leftarrow PC + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decode Phase:}\\
\text{Decode } IR.\text{Opcode} \rightarrow \text{Control Signals}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Execute Phase (varies per instruction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Complete Addressing Mode Reference (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Effective Address (EA)** is the actual memory location where the operand resides. The **EA computation** is the heart of addressing mode design.

| Addressing Mode | Effective Address Equation | Operand Fetched As | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate (IMM)** | $EA = \text{operand field itself}$ | Literal constant | Load constant, no memory access |
| **Direct / Absolute (DIR)** | $EA = A$ (address part) | $M[A]$ | Static data access |
| **Register Direct (REG)** | $EA = R_i$ | Contents of register $R_i$ | Fastest, no memory access |
| **Register Indirect (IND)** | $EA = [R_i]$ | $M[[R_i]]$ | Pointer dereferencing |
| **Displacement (DSP)** | $EA = A + [R_i]$ | $M[A + [R_i]]$ | Local + base offset |
| **Indexed (IDX)** | $EA = A + [X_i]$ | $M[A + [X_i]]$ | Array traversal |
| **Base Register (BASE)** | $EA = [BR] + A$ | $M[[BR] + A]$ | Position-independent code |
| **Relative (REL)** | $EA = PC + A$ | $M[PC + A]$ | Branch instructions |
| **Auto-increment (AINC)** | $EA = [R_i];\; R_i \leftarrow R_i + d$ | $M[R_i]$, then increment | Stack pop / array scan |
| **Auto-decrement (ADEC)** | $R_i \leftarrow R_i - d;\; EA = [R_i]$ | Decrement, then read | Stack push |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In *Auto-decrement*, the decrement happens **BEFORE** the memory access. In *Auto-increment*, the increment happens **AFTER** the memory access. This order is frequently tested in KTU exams.

### 2.5 Instruction Set Classification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction Set} = \{
& \text{Data Transfer (MOV, LOAD, STORE)}, \\
& \text{Arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV)}, \\
& \text{Logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHIFT)}, \\
& \text{Control Transfer (JMP, BRANCH, CALL, RET)}, \\
& \text{Input/Output (IN, OUT)}, \\
& \text{System Control (NOP, HALT, INT)}
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.6 Subroutine Linkage Using Stack

For a **CALL** instruction, sequencing involves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At CALL site: } & SP \leftarrow SP - 1;\; M[SP] \leftarrow PC;\; PC \leftarrow \text{Target} \\
\text{At RET site: } & PC \leftarrow M[SP];\; SP \leftarrow SP + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The saved return address on the stack is critical for proper subroutine return.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Compilers select addressing modes to generate optimal machine code based on data locality (e.g., displacement for stack variables, indexed for arrays).
- **Embedded Systems:** ARM/MIPS use base+offset (displacement) addressing for nearly all memory accesses because it enables position-independent code.
- **Operating Systems:** Context switches rely on saving PC, SP, and flags onto the stack — a direct application of instruction sequencing principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Address Computation for Each Mode

Let $A$ be the address field of the instruction, $R_i$ a register, $X_i$ an index register, and $BR$ a base register. $M[X]$ denotes contents of memory at address $X$, and $[R_i]$ denotes contents of register $R_i$.

#### Mode 1: Immediate Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= A \\
\text{Operand Value} &= A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The operand is embedded *inside* the instruction itself. No memory reference is required beyond the instruction fetch. The instruction carries the literal constant as its operand field.

#### Mode 2: Direct (Absolute) Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= A \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[A]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The address field $A$ directly specifies the memory location. The CPU performs **one additional memory read** beyond the instruction fetch to retrieve the operand.

#### Mode 3: Register Direct Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R_i \\
\text{Operand Value} &= [R_i]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The operand is the contents of a CPU register. This is the **fastest mode** — no memory access is needed at all. Only a small field of bits (e.g., 3–5 bits) is needed to encode the register.

#### Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= [R_i] \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[[R_i]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The register holds a *pointer* to the memory location. The CPU must first read the register, then perform a memory read at the address the register contains. Useful for pointer-based data structures.

#### Mode 5: Displacement (Indexed) Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= A + [R_i] \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[A + [R_i]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The effective address is the sum of a constant $A$ (from the instruction) and a register value $[R_i]$. This combines the benefits of direct addressing (the constant offset) and register indirect addressing (the dynamic base).

#### Mode 6: Base Register Addressing

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= [BR] + A \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[[BR] + A]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The base register $BR$ holds the base of a memory segment, and $A$ is the offset. The CPU reads $BR$, adds $A$, and uses the result as the memory address. Enables **relocatable code**.

#### Mode 7: Relative Addressing (Used in Branches)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= PC + A \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[PC + A]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The branch target is computed by adding a signed offset $A$ to the current PC. This is essential for **position-independent branches** because the offset is the same regardless of where the program is loaded.

#### Mode 8: Auto-increment

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= [R_i] \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[[R_i]] \\
R_i &\leftarrow R_i + d \quad (\text{where } d = 1, 2, 4 \text{ for byte/word/doubleword})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The register is used as a pointer, the operand is fetched, *then* the register is incremented. Step order: **Read → Increment**.

#### Mode 9: Auto-decrement

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &\leftarrow R_i - d \\
EA &= [R_i] \\
\text{Operand Value} &= M[[R_i]]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The register is first decremented, *then* used as a pointer to fetch the operand. Step order: **Decrement → Read**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Heuristics (KTU Board Perspective)

| Scenario | Best Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Loading a constant $K$ into a register | **Immediate** | No extra memory access needed |
| Accessing a static global variable | **Direct / Absolute** | Address known at compile time |
| Accessing a local variable in a stack frame | **Displacement** (FP + offset) | Offset known, base = frame pointer |
| Traversing an array `arr[i]` | **Indexed** | $EA = \text{base} + i \times \text{size}$ |
| Implementing `if (x > 0) goto L` | **Relative** | $EA = PC + \text{offset}$ to label $L$ |
| Dereferencing a pointer `*p` | **Register Indirect** | Register holds the pointer |
| Stack push (write) | **Auto-decrement** | Decrement SP, then write at new SP |
| Stack pop (read) | **Auto-increment** | Read at current SP, then increment SP |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Branch Instruction Sequencing

Consider a conditional branch: `BRZ X` (Branch if Zero flag is set, to location X).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fetch Phase:}\\
AR &\leftarrow PC \\
DR &\leftarrow M[AR] \\
IR &\leftarrow DR \\
PC &\leftarrow PC + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decode Phase:}\\
\text{Decode}(IR) \rightarrow \text{Control Unit identifies BRZ opcode}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Execute Phase:}\\
\text{If } (Z = 1):\quad PC &\leftarrow IR.\text{Address} \quad \text{(branch taken)} \\
\text{If } (Z = 0):\quad PC &\leftarrow PC \quad \text{(branch not taken, PC already incremented)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** During the fetch, PC is incremented to point to the *next sequential* instruction. If the branch is **not taken**, the processor simply proceeds to this next instruction. If the branch **is taken**, the PC is overwritten with the branch target address loaded from the IR.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Subroutine Call and Return Sequencing

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At CALL SUB:}\\
AR &\leftarrow PC \\
DR &\leftarrow M[AR] \\
IR &\leftarrow DR \\
PC &\leftarrow PC + 1 \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 1 \\
M[SP] &\leftarrow PC \quad \text{(save return address)} \\
PC &\leftarrow IR.\text{Address} \quad \text{(jump to subroutine)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At RET (in subroutine):}\\
PC &\leftarrow M[SP] \quad \text{(restore return address)} \\
SP &\leftarrow SP + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The CALL instruction saves the address of the instruction *after* the call (already incremented in PC) onto the stack. The RET instruction pops this saved address and re-loads it into PC, resuming execution at the call site.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Instruction Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INSTR[32-bit Machine Instruction]
        OPC[Opcode Field\n6 bits]
        DST[Destination Operand\n10 bits]
        SRC1[Source Operand 1\n8 bits]
        SRC2[Source Operand 2\n8 bits]
    end

    OPC --- DST
    DST --- SRC1
    SRC1 --- SRC2

    OPC --> CTRL[Control Unit\nDecodes Operation]
    DST --> DESTREG[Destination Register / Memory Address]
    SRC1 --> SRC1REG[Source 1 Register / Memory Address]
    SRC2 --> SRC2REG[Source 2 Register / Memory / Immediate]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Instruction Execution Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A([Start / Reset]) --> B[AR <- PC]
    B --> C[DR <- Memory AR]
    C --> D[IR <- DR]
    D --> E[PC <- PC + 1]
    E --> F{Decode Opcode}
    F -->|Data Transfer| G[Execute Data Transfer\nLOAD / STORE]
    F -->|Arithmetic/Logic| H[Execute ALU Operation\nADD / SUB / AND / OR]
    F -->|Control Transfer| I[Execute Branch / Jump\nUpdate PC conditionally]
    F -->|I/O Operation| J[Execute I/O\nRead / Write Device]
    G --> K([End of Cycle])
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> B
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Effective Address Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Instruction Fetched\ninto IR]) --> DEC{Decode\nAddressing Mode}
    DEC -->|Immediate| M1[EA <- Operand Field\nOperand = EA]
    DEC -->|Direct| M2[EA <- Address A\nOperand = M EA]
    DEC -->|Register| M3[EA <- Ri\nOperand = Ri]
    DEC -->|Register Indirect| M4[EA <- Ri\nOperand = M of EA]
    DEC -->|Displacement| M5[EA <- A + Ri\nOperand = M of EA]
    DEC -->|Indexed| M6[EA <- A + Xi\nOperand = M of EA]
    DEC -->|Relative| M7[EA <- PC + A\nOperand = M of EA]
    DEC -->|Auto-increment| M8[EA <- Ri\nOperand = M of EA\nRi <- Ri + d]
    DEC -->|Auto-decrement| M9[Ri <- Ri - d\nEA <- Ri\nOperand = M of EA]
    M1 --> OUT([Operand Ready])
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT
    M6 --> OUT
    M7 --> OUT
    M8 --> OUT
    M9 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Subroutine Call and Return Sequencing

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Main Program\nCALL SUBROUTINE] --> B[Push PC onto Stack\nSP decremented]
    B --> C[Load PC with Subroutine Address]
    C --> D[Subroutine Body Executes]
    D --> E{Encounter RET}
    E -->|Yes| F[Pop PC from Stack\nSP incremented]
    F --> G[Resume Main Program]
    G --> H([End])

    subgraph STACKMEM[Stack Memory]
        S1[Top of Stack: Return Address]
        S2[Below: Saved Registers]
        S3[Deeper: Local Variables]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end

    B -.writes to.-> S1
    F -.reads from.-> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define an instruction format. List any four basic elements of a machine instruction with their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

An *instruction format* is the layout of bits within a machine instruction that specifies the operation and the operands on which it acts. The four basic elements are:

1. **Operation Code (Opcode):** Specifies the operation (e.g., ADD, LOAD).
2. **Source Operand Reference:** Indicates the location of the input data.
3. **Destination Operand Reference:** Indicates where the result is to be stored.
4. **Next Instruction Reference:** Specifies where to find the next instruction (usually implicit via PC).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for 4 elements = 3)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Immediate Addressing Mode and Direct Addressing Mode. Give one example of an instruction for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Immediate | Direct |
|---|---|---|
| Operand Location | Embedded in instruction | In memory at given address |
| Memory Access for Operand | **Not Required** | **Required** (1 extra access) |
| Use Case | Loading constants | Accessing static variables |
| Example | `MOV R1, #5` | `LOAD R1, 2000` |

*(3 marks: comparison + example = 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the various **addressing modes** used in computer instructions with neat diagrams and example instructions. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **Instruction Sequencing** for a *conditional branch instruction* using Register Transfer Notation. Show what happens in both branch-taken and branch-not-taken cases. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Addressing Modes** are techniques used by the CPU to specify the location of an operand. Major modes are:

1. **Immediate Mode:** Operand is part of instruction. Example: `MOV R1, #10`. EA = 10.
2. **Direct Mode:** Address field gives memory location. Example: `LOAD R1, 2000`. EA = 2000; Operand = M[2000].
3. **Register Mode:** Operand is in a register. Example: `ADD R1, R2`. EA = R2; Operand = [R2].
4. **Register Indirect Mode:** Register contains address. Example: `LOAD R1, (R2)`. EA = [R2]; Operand = M[[R2]].
5. **Displacement Mode:** EA = A + [R]. Example: `LOAD R1, 100(R2)`. EA = 100 + [R2].
6. **Indexed Mode:** EA = A + [X]. Used for arrays.
7. **Base Register Mode:** EA = [BR] + A. Used for relocatable code.
8. **Relative Mode:** EA = PC + A. Used in branch instructions.
9. **Auto-increment:** EA = [R], then R = R + d.
10. **Auto-decrement:** R = R - d, then EA = [R].

*(Award 7 marks: 0.5 per mode + 0.5 for example = up to 7 marks; cover the most important 8–10 modes)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Instruction Sequencing for a Conditional Branch (e.g., BRZ X — Branch to X if Zero flag = 1):**

**Step 1: Fetch Phase** *(common to all instructions)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
AR &\leftarrow PC \\
DR &\leftarrow M[AR] \\
IR &\leftarrow DR \\
PC &\leftarrow PC + 1 \quad \text{(point to next sequential instruction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Decode Phase**

The control unit decodes the opcode field of IR. It identifies the instruction as a *conditional branch* and reads the condition (Z flag) and the target address $X$ from the IR.

**Step 3: Execute Phase — Case Analysis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Case A: Branch Taken (Z = 1):}\\
PC &\leftarrow IR.\text{Address} \quad \text{(load target X into PC)} \\
\\
\textbf{Case B: Branch Not Taken (Z = 0):}\\
PC &\leftarrow PC \quad \text{(no change; PC already points to next instruction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Notice that PC is incremented *during the fetch phase*, not during the execute phase. This means by the time we check the condition, PC already holds the address of the next sequential instruction. So, in the **branch-not-taken** case, we simply do nothing further. In the **branch-taken** case, we overwrite PC with the target address $X$.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for fetch, 2 for decode, 3 for execute with both cases)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Supplementary Model Style]**
*(a)* With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the basic **Instruction Format** and classify the instructions in a computer based on their function. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Explain the concept of **Subroutine Call and Return** using instruction sequencing. Show how the **stack pointer** is used and write the Register Transfer Notation for both `CALL` and `RET` instructions. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Basic Instruction Format (Block Diagram):**

A machine instruction consists of the following fields:

| Field | Length (Typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Opcode | 6 bits | Specifies the operation |
| Source 1 | 5–8 bits | First operand reference |
| Source 2 | 5–8 bits | Second operand reference |
| Destination | 5–8 bits | Result destination reference |
| Next Address | 8–16 bits | Address of next instruction (often implicit via PC) |

**Classification of Instructions (Based on Function):**

1. **Data Transfer Instructions:** MOV, LOAD, STORE, PUSH, POP — move data between registers, memory, and I/O.
2. **Arithmetic Instructions:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC — perform numeric computations.
3. **Logical Instructions:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR — perform bitwise operations.
4. **Control Transfer Instructions:** JMP, BRANCH (conditional/unconditional), CALL, RET — alter the flow of execution.
5. **Input/Output Instructions:** IN, OUT — communicate with peripheral devices.
6. **System/Special Instructions:** NOP, HALT, INT, RESET — control processor behavior.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 for format block, 5 for classification with examples)*

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Subroutine Call and Return:**

A *subroutine* is a reusable block of code that can be invoked from multiple places in a program. The instruction sequencing must:

1. **At CALL site:** Save the address of the next instruction (return address) and jump to the subroutine.
2. **At RET site:** Pop the saved return address from the stack and resume execution at the call site.

**Register Transfer Notation for CALL SUB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &\leftarrow SP - 1 \quad \text{(decrement stack pointer)} \\
M[SP] &\leftarrow PC \quad \text{(push return address onto stack)} \\
PC &\leftarrow \text{Target Address of SUB} \quad \text{(jump to subroutine)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Register Transfer Notation for RET:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &\leftarrow M[SP] \quad \text{(pop return address from stack)} \\
SP &\leftarrow SP + 1 \quad \text{(increment stack pointer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**

- The stack grows **downward** in memory (from high to low addresses). Hence, a push operation **decrements** SP, and a pop operation **increments** SP.
- The saved return address is the value of PC *after* the CALL instruction has been fetched. This ensures the program continues at the instruction immediately following the CALL.
- Subroutines can be **nested** (a subroutine calling another) because each CALL pushes a new return address, and each RET pops the most recently saved one (LIFO discipline).

*(Award 7 marks: 1 for concept, 3 for CALL, 3 for RET)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Order of operations in auto-decrement:** Many students wrongly write the decrement *after* the memory read. The correct order is **decrement first, then read**.
> 2. **Relative addressing computation:** Students often forget that PC has already been incremented to the *next* instruction when computing the branch target. The branch offset is added to the **incremented** PC.
> 3. **Stack pointer direction:** KTU standard assumes stack grows **downward** (high → low address). Writing it the wrong way leads to marking deductions.
> 4. **Effective Address vs Operand Value:** EA is the *address*; the operand value is the *contents* at that address. Don't confuse the two in derivations.
> 5. **Register Transfer Notation arrows:** Use `←` for transfer, not `=`. Always show explicit register names (PC, AR, IR, DR, SP, AC).
> 6. **Subroutine return address:** Must be the address of the **instruction AFTER the CALL**, not the CALL instruction itself.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Instructions and Instruction Sequencing**

- An **instruction** consists of an **opcode** and **operand reference fields**. Typical widths: 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit.
- The **Instruction Cycle** = **Fetch → Decode → Execute**. Every instruction goes through all three phases.
- Key processor registers: **PC, IR, AR, DR, AC, SP**, plus **condition flags N, Z, C, V**.
- **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched.
- **Addressing Modes** determine how the operand's effective address is computed.
- **Effective Address (EA)** is the actual memory location of the operand.
- **Immediate mode** is the fastest (operand in instruction); **register mode** is the fastest for register-resident data.
- **Direct mode** requires one extra memory access; **register indirect** requires register read + memory access.
- **Displacement, indexed, and base register modes** all use the form $EA = \text{constant} + \text{register contents}$.
- **Relative addressing** uses $EA = PC + \text{offset}$ and is used in branch instructions.
- **Auto-increment** = read first, increment second. **Auto-decrement** = decrement first, read second.
- **Sequential flow:** PC ← PC + 1 (automatic).
- **Conditional branch:** PC ← target *if condition true*; otherwise PC unchanged.
- **Unconditional jump:** PC ← target always.
- **Subroutine CALL:** Decrement SP, push return address, jump to target.
- **Subroutine RET:** Pop return address into PC, increment SP.
- **Stack** is a LIFO structure; SP points to the topmost element. KTU convention: stack grows **downward**.
- **Register Transfer Notation (RTN)** is the formal language used in KTU to describe instruction sequencing.
- **Instruction Set Classification:** Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Control Transfer, I/O, System.
- **Common KTU exam trap:** Distinguishing EA computation from operand value retrieval. Always state *both* clearly.
- **Real-world use:** Compilers select addressing modes based on variable scope, lifetime, and access pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Addressing Modes** are the various techniques used by the CPU to specify the location of an **operand** (data) involved in an instruction. Every machine instruction operates on data stored in memory locations or CPU registers. The *addressing mode* defines the **rule** or **method** by which the effective address (EA) of the operand is computed from the address fields given in the instruction.

The **Effective Address (EA)** is the final, computed memory location or register from which the operand is actually fetched (or to which it is stored). The general KTU textbook notation is:

$$
\text{EA} = f(\text{Address Field}, \text{Registers}, \text{Constants})
$$

where $f$ is a function determined by the specific addressing mode encoded in the opcode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERAT414 - Module 1):** The 2024 scheme mandates detailed study of addressing modes including **Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Register, Register Indirect, Displacement (Relative, Base Register, Index), and Stack addressing**, along with their effective address calculations and applications.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Warehouse Map" 

Imagine you are a warehouse worker (the **CPU**) and a customer (the **Programmer**) gives you a note (the **Instruction**) that says: *"Get me 5 boxes of item X."* The note must also tell you **where** these boxes are located. The customer can describe the location in **many different ways**:

| Customer's Way of Describing | Addressing Mode Equivalent |
|---|---|
| "Here is the box itself — take it!" | **Immediate** (operand is in the instruction) |
| "Go to Aisle 7, Shelf B" | **Direct** (absolute address given) |
| "The address is written on that clipboard" | **Indirect** (address points to another address) |
| "I have a master list — go check with the manager" | **Register Indirect** (register holds the address) |
| "From the entrance, walk 10 steps forward, then 3 steps right" | **Displacement / Relative** (offset from a base) |
| "Use the trolley label number on your cart" | **Indexed** (base + index) |

Every way leads to the same box, but the **path** and **effort** differ. In CPU terms, each path differs in:
- **Number of memory references** required
- **Execution speed**
- **Flexibility** for loops, arrays, and pointers

---

## 🔑 Why Are Addressing Modes Important?

1. **Flexibility in Programming** — Without addressing modes, every instruction would need a hard-coded memory address, making loops and data structures impossible.
2. **Code Efficiency** — Choosing the right mode reduces instruction size and memory access time.
3. **Program Relocatability** — Position-independent code (PIC) uses *PC-relative* addressing.
4. **Hardware Design** — The CPU's control unit design depends on which addressing modes it supports.
5. **Performance Trade-off** — Modes with fewer memory references (e.g., register) execute faster.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU exams:** *More memory references = slower execution*. Immediate and register modes are the fastest; multi-level indirect is the slowest.

---

## 📋 The Complete Catalogue of Addressing Modes

The KTU 2024 scheme lists the following **8 primary modes**:

1. **Immediate Addressing**
2. **Direct (Absolute) Addressing**
3. **Indirect Addressing**
4. **Register Addressing**
5. **Register Indirect Addressing**
6. **Displacement Addressing** (sub-modes: *Relative, Base-Register, Indexed*)
7. **Stack Addressing**
8. **Auto-increment / Auto-decrement** (often grouped with Register Indirect)

Each is detailed with examples in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ Mode-by-Mode Theoretical Breakdown

> The following analysis assumes a standard **single-address instruction format**:
> `| Opcode | Mode Bits | Address Field |`
> The **Mode Bits** (typically 2–3 bits) tell the CPU how to interpret the *Address Field*.

---

### 1️⃣ Immediate Addressing

**Definition:** The operand *itself* is contained within the instruction — no memory reference is needed.

**How it works:** The address field holds the **actual constant value** to be used.

**Example:** `MOV R1, #25`  →  R1 ← 25 (the 25 is part of the instruction).

**Advantages:** Fastest possible execution (no memory access for operand). Useful for initializing registers and constants.

**Disadvantages:** Limited operand size (constrained by address field width). Cannot be the destination.

**Effective Address:** Not applicable — operand is fetched directly from the Instruction Register (IR).

$$
\text{EA} = \text{Not Defined} \quad ; \quad \text{OPERAND} = \text{Address Field value}
$$

---

### 2️⃣ Direct (Absolute) Addressing

**Definition:** The address field contains the **exact memory address** of the operand.

**Example:** `ADD R1, 2000`  →  R1 ← R1 + M[2000].

**Effective Address:**

$$
\text{EA} = \text{Address Field}
$$

**Advantages:** Simple, single memory reference for operand. Useful for accessing global/static variables.

**Disadvantages:** Large address field = large instruction size. No flexibility for relocation.

---

### 3⃣ Indirect Addressing

**Definition:** The address field points to a **memory location that contains the actual address** of the operand (a "pointer to a pointer").

**Example:** `ADD R1, (2000)`  →  R1 ← R1 + M[ M[2000] ].

**Effective Address:**

$$
\text{EA} = M[\text{Address Field}]
$$

**Advantages:** Highly flexible — the pointer can be changed at runtime, enabling dynamic data structures.

**Disadvantages:** **Two memory references** required to fetch one operand (slow). Multi-level indirection is even slower.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often test whether students confuse **Indirect** with **Direct**. In Direct: `EA = Address Field`. In Indirect: `EA = M[Address Field]`. The extra `M[...]` is the giveaway.

---

### 4️⃣ Register Addressing

**Definition:** The operand is in a **CPU register** specified by the address field.

**Example:** `ADD R1, R2`  →  R1 ← R1 + R2.

**Effective Address:** The register itself.

$$
\text{EA} = R_k \quad \text{(register } k \text{ specified by the address field)}
$$

**Advantages:** Fastest operand access (registers are inside the CPU). Very short address field needed (only $\log_2 N$ bits for $N$ registers).

**Disadvantages:** Limited number of registers. Cannot hold large data structures.

---

### 5️⃣ Register Indirect Addressing

**Definition:** The address field specifies a **register that contains the memory address** of the operand. Sometimes called **"Indirect through Register"**.

**Example:** `ADD R1, (R2)`  →  R1 ← R1 + M[ R2 ].

**Effective Address:**

$$
\text{EA} = R_k \quad \text{(then operand} = M[R_k]\text{)}
$$

**Advantages:** One memory reference (faster than full indirect). Very commonly used in C for **pointer dereferencing**.

**Disadvantages:** Register must hold a valid memory address.

---

### 6️⃣ Displacement Addressing

**Definition:** Combines the contents of a register with an offset (displacement) given in the instruction to compute the effective address. Three sub-modes exist:

#### (a) **Relative Addressing**
$$
\text{EA} = \text{PC} + \text{Address Field (offset)}
$$
Used for **branch instructions** (jumps). Offset is signed (2's complement).

#### (b) **Base-Register Addressing**
$$
\text{EA} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Address Field (offset)}
$$
Used to access **data segments** or static structures. The base register holds the starting address of a block.

#### (c) **Indexed Addressing**
$$
\text{EA} = \text{Index Register} + \text{Address Field (offset)}
$$
Used for **array traversal** — the index register is incremented/decremented in loops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Unified Displacement Formula** (the most useful form for KTU problems):
>
> $$\text{EA} = A + (R)$$
>
> where $A$ = contents of the address field (constant offset) and $(R)$ = contents of the specified register. The register can be the PC, a base register, or an index register.

---

### 7️⃣ Stack Addressing

**Definition:** The operand is implicitly on **top of the stack**, pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)** register.

**Implicit Operations:** PUSH (decrement SP, then store), POP (load, then increment SP).

**Effective Address:** EA = SP (top of stack). No explicit address field is needed.

$$
\text{EA} = \text{SP}
$$

**Advantages:** No address field needed in instruction. Naturally supports subroutines (CALL/RET), interrupts, and expression evaluation.

**Disadvantages:** Limited access pattern (LIFO only). Stack overflow/underflow must be managed.

---

### 8️⃣ Auto-increment / Auto-decrement Addressing

**Definition:** A specialized form of register indirect addressing where the register is **automatically updated** before or after use.

- **Auto-increment:** EA = R, then R ← R + d (increment *after* access; useful for forward array traversal)
- **Auto-decrement:** R ← R − d, then EA = R (decrement *before* access; useful for stack-like behavior)

**Example:** `ADD R1, (R2)+`  →  R1 ← R1 + M[R2]; R2 ← R2 + 1.

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert A \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Mode | Effective Address Formula | Memory Refs to Fetch Operand | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | Operand = Address Field | **0** | Constants, initialization |
| 2 | **Direct** | $\text{EA} = A$ | **1** | Global variables |
| 3 | **Indirect** | $\text{EA} = M[A]$ | **2** | Pointers, dynamic data |
| 4 | **Register** | $\text{EA} = R_k$ | **0** (register access) | Fast arithmetic |
| 5 | **Register Indirect** | $\text{EA} = (R_k)$ | **1** | Pointer dereferencing (`*p` in C) |
| 6a | **Relative** | $\text{EA} = \text{PC} + A$ | **1** | Branch/jump instructions |
| 6b | **Base-Register** | $\text{EA} = \text{Base} + A$ | **1** | Static data segments |
| 6c | **Indexed** | $\text{EA} = \text{Index} + A$ | **1** | Array traversal |
| 7 | **Stack** | $\text{EA} = \text{SP}$ | **1** (or 0) | Subroutines, expressions |
| 8 | **Auto-inc/dec** | $\text{EA} = R_k \pm d$ | **1** | Sequential array access |

*Legend: $A$ = address field value, $R_k$ = register $k$, $M[X]$ = memory contents at address $X$, $d$ = step size (usually 1, 2, or 4).*

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Addressing Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Embedded firmware loops** | Auto-increment | Compact, efficient array reads |
| **Operating system kernels** | Base-Register | Isolates kernel & user address spaces |
| **Compiler-generated code** | Indexed | Standard for `arr[i]` accesses |
| **Position-Independent Code (PIC)** | PC-Relative | Allows code to run at any memory address |
| **Recursive function calls** | Stack | Manages return addresses & local vars |
| **Hardware drivers** | Register | Bit-level manipulation, speed-critical |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 Worked-Out Numerical Problems

The following problems follow the **KTU 2024 numerical style** — short, computational, and based on effective address calculation.

---

### 📝 Problem 1: Effective Address Calculation for Direct Mode

> A processor uses a 16-bit address. An instruction `LOAD R1, 5000` is executed in **Direct Addressing** mode. What is the EA, and how many memory references are made?

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify the mode. The instruction has a *direct* address — no parentheses around the operand.

Step 2: Apply the Direct Addressing formula.

$$
\text{EA} = A = 5000
$$

Step 3: Fetch the operand.

$$
R1 \leftarrow M[5000]
$$

Step 4: Memory references = **1** (one to fetch M[5000]). The instruction fetch itself is a separate, standard reference.

**Final Answer:** EA = 5000, Memory References = 1.

---

### 📝 Problem 2: Effective Address for Indirect Mode

> Same processor. Instruction: `LOAD R1, (5000)`. The memory contents are:
> - $M[5000] = 8000$
> - $M[8000] = 42$

Compute EA and the value loaded into R1.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Recognize indirect mode (parentheses around 5000).

Step 2: First memory reference — read the pointer.

$$
\text{Pointer} = M[5000] = 8000
$$

Step 3: The pointer IS the effective address.

$$
\text{EA} = M[5000] = 8000
$$

Step 4: Second memory reference — fetch the actual operand at EA.

$$
R1 \leftarrow M[\text{EA}] = M[8000] = 42
$$

Step 5: Total memory references = **2** (one to read pointer, one to read operand).

**Final Answer:** EA = 8000, R1 = 42, Memory References = 2.

---

### 📝 Problem 3: Register Indirect Mode

> Instruction: `LOAD R1, (R2)`. R2 = 3000. M[3000] = 99.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Register indirect — R2 holds the address.

Step 2: Effective address is the *contents* of R2 (no extra memory read to get the address).

$$
\text{EA} = (R2) = 3000
$$

Step 3: Fetch the operand from EA.

$$
R1 \leftarrow M[3000] = 99
$$

Step 4: Memory references = **1** (only to fetch M[3000]).

**Final Answer:** EA = 3000, R1 = 99, Memory References = 1.

**Comparison Insight:** Register indirect uses **1 memory reference** vs. indirect's **2**. This is why compilers prefer it.

---

### 📝 Problem 4: PC-Relative Addressing (Branch)

> PC = 4000. Instruction at 4000 is `BRANCH 200` (Relative mode). The offset is **signed** (2's complement).

**Solution:**

Step 1: Identify PC-relative mode.

Step 2: The branch instruction occupies 2 memory words. After fetch, PC is auto-incremented:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{updated}} = 4000 + 2 = 4002
$$

Step 3: Apply the relative formula.

$$
\text{EA} = \text{PC}_{\text{updated}} + \text{offset} = 4002 + 200 = 4202
$$

Step 4: Execution jumps to address 4202.

**Final Answer:** Branch target = 4202.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Always remember to **add the instruction size** to the PC *before* applying the relative offset. Forgetting this is the #1 mistake in branch problems.

---

### 📝 Problem 5: Indexed Addressing for Arrays

> Base register RB = 5000 (start of array). Index register RX = 3. Address field = 100. Each element is 4 bytes. Find the EA of the 4th element (RX = 3 means 4th element if 0-indexed).

**Solution:**

Step 1: Indexed addressing formula.

$$
\text{EA} = \text{Index Register} \cdot d + \text{Address Field}
$$

where $d$ = element size.

Step 2: Plug in values.

$$
\text{EA} = 3 \cdot 4 + 100 = 12 + 100 = 112
$$

Step 3: Verify — the array starts at 100 (address field), the 4th element is at 100 + 3·4 = 112. ✅

**Final Answer:** EA = 112.

---

### 📝 Problem 6: Auto-increment Mode

> R2 = 6000, M[6000] = 7, M[6001] = 15, M[6002] = 23. Execute `ADD R1, (R2)+`.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Auto-increment — first use R2, then increment.

Step 2: Effective address.

$$
\text{EA} = (R2) = 6000
$$

Step 3: Perform the operation.

$$
R1 \leftarrow R1 + M[6000] = R1 + 7
$$

Step 4: Auto-increment R2.

$$
R2 \leftarrow R2 + 1 = 6001
$$

Step 5: Next execution of the same instruction would access M[6001] = 15.

**Final Answer:** Operand = 7, R2 becomes 6001.

---

## 🗂️ Analytical Matrix: Mode-Selection Heuristics

| If the Programmer Needs To… | Best Mode | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Load a constant into a register | **Immediate** | Zero memory access for operand |
| Access a fixed global variable | **Direct** | Simple, fast |
| Walk through an array sequentially | **Auto-increment / Indexed** | Compact loop body |
| Implement a `for` loop in assembly | **PC-Relative** | Branch with single offset |
| Dereference a pointer in C | **Register Indirect** | Single memory reference |
| Build a linked-list traversal | **Indirect** | Pointer-to-pointer flexibility |
| Manage function call frames | **Stack** | Implicit, hardware-managed |
| Write position-independent shared libraries | **PC-Relative** | Relocatable code |
| Access `struct.field` | **Base + Offset** | Combine base of struct + field offset |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Diagram 1: Classification of Addressing Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ADDRESSING MODES] --> B[IMMEDIATE]
    A --> C[DIRECT]
    A --> D[INDIRECT]
    A --> E[REGISTER]
    A --> F[REGISTER INDIRECT]
    A --> G[DISPLACEMENT]
    A --> H[STACK]
    A --> I[AUTO INCREMENT DECREMENT]

    G --> G1[RELATIVE PC PLUS OFFSET]
    G --> G2[BASE REGISTER PLUS OFFSET]
    G --> G3[INDEXED INDEX PLUS OFFSET]

    B --> B1[OPERAND IN INSTRUCTION]
    C --> C1[ADDRESS FIELD IS EA]
    D --> D1[ADDRESS FIELD POINTS TO POINTER]
    E --> E1[OPERAND IN CPU REGISTER]
    F --> F1[REGISTER HOLDS MEMORY ADDRESS]
    H --> H1[OPERAND AT TOP OF STACK SP]
    I --> I1[AUTO UPDATE REGISTER AFTER OR BEFORE]
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    IF[INSTRUCTION FETCH] --> ID[INSTRUCTION DECODE]
    ID --> MB[MODE BITS EXTRACTED]
    MB --> EAC[EFFECTIVE ADDRESS COMPUTATION UNIT]
    EAC --> RD[OPERAND READ]
    RD --> EX[EXECUTION UNIT]
    EX --> WB[WRITE BACK TO DESTINATION]

    EAC --> P1[PATH 1 IMMEDIATE NO MEMORY ACCESS]
    EAC --> P2[PATH 2 DIRECT OR REGISTER ONE REFERENCE]
    EAC --> P3[PATH 3 INDIRECT OR DISPLACEMENT TWO OR MORE REFERENCES]

    P1 --> EX
    P2 --> EX
    P3 --> EX
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 3: Generic Instruction Format (Single-Address Machine)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INS[INSTRUCTION WORD] --> OP[OPCODE FIELD N BITS]
    INS --> MD[MODE BITS 2 TO 3 BITS]
    INS --> AF[ADDRESS FIELD M BITS]

    MD --> D1[00 IMMEDIATE]
    MD --> D2[01 DIRECT]
    MD --> D3[10 INDIRECT]
    MD --> D4[11 REGISTER OR DISPLACEMENT]

    AF --> REG1[IF MODE IS REGISTER ADDRESS FIELD NAMES THE REGISTER]
    AF --> REG2[IF MODE IS DIRECT ADDRESS FIELD IS MEMORY EA]
    AF --> REG3[IF MODE IS DISPLACEMENT ADDRESS FIELD IS OFFSET]
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 4: Memory Reference Comparison Across Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[INSTRUCTION DECODED] --> Q{OPERAND SOURCE}

    Q -->|CONSTANT IN INSTRUCTION| R1[IMMEDIATE ZERO MEM REFS]
    Q -->|REGISTER IN CPU| R2[REGISTER MODE ZERO MEM REFS]
    Q -->|REGISTER HOLDS ADDRESS| R3[REGISTER INDIRECT ONE MEM REF]
    Q -->|ADDRESS FIELD IS EA| R4[DIRECT ONE MEM REF]
    Q -->|REGISTER PLUS OFFSET| R5[DISPLACEMENT ONE MEM REF]
    Q -->|ADDRESS POINTS TO POINTER| R6[INDIRECT TWO MEM REFS]
    Q -->|TOP OF STACK SP| R7[STACK ONE MEM REF]
```

---

## 🗺️ Diagram 5: Effective Address Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[START EA COMPUTATION] --> C{CHECK MODE BITS}

    C -->|00| I[IMMEDIATE OPERAND IN IR]
    C -->|01| D[DIRECT EA EQUALS A]
    C -->|10| IND[INDIRECT EA EQUALS M OF A]
    C -->|11| DR{DISPLACEMENT SUBMODE}

    DR -->|REL| R1[EA EQUALS PC PLUS A]
    DR -->|BASE| R2[EA EQUALS RB PLUS A]
    DR -->|INDEX| R3[EA EQUALS RX PLUS A]

    I --> OUT[OPERAND READY]
    D --> OUT
    IND --> M1[FETCH M OF A THEN USE AS EA] --> OUT
    R1 --> OUT
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define addressing modes. Why are they necessary in a computer system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Addressing modes specify the *method* by which the CPU determines the **effective address** of an operand from the address field of an instruction. (1 mark)

They are necessary because:
1. They provide **flexibility** to programmers for writing loops, arrays, and pointer-based code without hard-coding absolute memory addresses. (1 mark)
2. They allow **efficient use of instruction bits** and enable the creation of **position-independent and relocatable code** (e.g., using PC-relative branches). (1 mark)

---

### ❓ Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Direct and Indirect Addressing Modes with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Addressing | Indirect Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| **EA Formula** | $\text{EA} = A$ | $\text{EA} = M[A]$ |
| **Memory References** | 1 | 2 |
| **Example** | `ADD R1, 2000` → operand at M[2000] | `ADD R1, (2000)` → operand at M[M[2000]] |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower |
| **Flexibility** | Lower — address field fixed at compile time | Higher — pointer can change at runtime |

(1 mark for each meaningful comparison row; 3 such rows = 3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions are split into (a) and (b) sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks, with **internal choice** between two full questions.

---

### 📌 Question B-A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Theory + Numericals

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the following addressing modes with examples and one application of each: (7 marks)
   1. Register Indirect
   2. Indexed
   3. PC-Relative

**(b)** A program is loaded at memory address 5000. The instruction `BRANCH -200` (PC-relative, signed offset) is at address 5000. Each instruction occupies 2 bytes. Compute the branch target address. Also, identify the EA if the same instruction is treated as **Base-Register mode** with Base Register = 8000. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

**1. Register Indirect Addressing (≈ 2.5 marks)**
The address field specifies a **register** whose contents are the **memory address** of the operand.
- *Example:* `LOAD R1, (R2)` → R1 ← M[R2].
- *Effective Address:* $\text{EA} = (R_k)$.
- *Memory References:* 1 (only the operand fetch).
- *Application:* Used for **pointer dereferencing** in C (`*ptr` where `ptr` is held in a register).

**2. Indexed Addressing (≈ 2.5 marks)**
The effective address is the sum of an **index register** and a **constant offset** in the instruction.
- *Example:* `LOAD R1, 100(RX)` where RX = 4, element size 4 bytes → EA = 4·4 + 100 = 116.
- *Effective Address:* $\text{EA} = (\text{Index Register}) + A$.
- *Memory References:* 1.
- *Application:* **Array traversal** — incrementing the index register in a loop visits each element in sequence.

**3. PC-Relative Addressing (≈ 2 marks)**
The effective address is computed by adding a **signed offset** to the **Program Counter**.
- *Example:* `BRANCH +500` → EA = (PC after fetch) + 500.
- *Effective Address:* $\text{EA} = \text{PC} + A$.
- *Memory References:* 1 (to fetch the branch target instruction after computing EA).
- *Application:* **Branch instructions** in loops and conditional jumps. Also enables **position-independent code**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: PC-Relative Calculation (≈ 4 marks)**

Initial PC = 5000. Instruction is 2 bytes. After instruction fetch, the PC is incremented:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{updated}} = 5000 + 2 = 5002
$$

Apply the PC-relative formula with the signed offset $A = -200$:

$$
\text{EA}_{\text{PC-relative}} = \text{PC}_{\text{updated}} + A = 5002 + (-200) = 4802
$$

**Branch target = 4802.**

**Step 2: Base-Register Calculation (≈ 3 marks)**

If the same instruction were executed in Base-Register mode with Base Register (RB) = 8000:

$$
\text{EA}_{\text{base}} = \text{RB} + A = 8000 + (-200) = 7800
$$

**EA in base-register mode = 7800.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to add the instruction length to the PC before applying the offset. Marks are deducted for this in KTU valuation.

---

### 📌 Question B-B (Choice 2) — Mode Comparison + Numerical Mix

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare Immediate, Direct, Register, and Register Indirect addressing modes in terms of: number of memory references, instruction size, flexibility, and typical use. (7 marks)

**(b)** A processor has R1 = 100, R2 = 200, R3 = 300. Memory contents:
- M[100] = 500
- M[200] = 600
- M[500] = 1000
- M[600] = 42

Determine the value loaded into an accumulator AC for each of the following instructions:
1. `LOAD AC, #500`
2. `LOAD AC, 100`
3. `LOAD AC, (200)`
4. `LOAD AC, (R2)`
5. `LOAD AC, 200(R1)` (Indexed mode) (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

A 4-column comparison table (each mode as a row, 4 attributes as columns), or vice versa. KTU expects a *clear table* with **at least 4 distinguishing points per mode**.

| Mode | Memory Refs | Instruction Size | Flexibility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | 0 | Medium (operand is part of instruction) | Very Low | Constants, initialization |
| **Direct** | 1 | Large (full address field) | Low | Global variables |
| **Register** | 0 | Very small (only $\log_2 N$ bits) | Low (limited registers) | Fast arithmetic |
| **Register Indirect** | 1 | Small (register name only) | High | Pointers in C, array stepping |

(2 marks for table, 5 marks distributed across the four mode descriptions; allocate roughly 1.5 marks per mode's correct characteristics.)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

**Instruction 1: `LOAD AC, #500` — Immediate Mode**

The `#` indicates immediate. The operand is the constant 500 itself.

$$
\text{AC} \leftarrow 500
$$

**AC = 500.** (1 mark)

---

**Instruction 2: `LOAD AC, 100` — Direct Mode**

EA = 100. Fetch operand directly from M[100].

$$
\text{EA} = 100 \quad ; \quad \text{AC} \leftarrow M[100] = 500
$$

**AC = 500.** (1.5 marks)

---

**Instruction 3: `LOAD AC, (200)` — Indirect Mode**

The address field is 200, but it points to a *pointer*.

$$
\text{Pointer} = M[200] = 600 \quad ; \quad \text{EA} = 600
$$

$$
\text{AC} \leftarrow M[600] = 42
$$

**AC = 42.** (1.5 marks)

---

**Instruction 4: `LOAD AC, (R2)` — Register Indirect Mode**

R2 = 200, so EA = (R2) = 200.

$$
\text{AC} \leftarrow M[200] = 600
$$

**AC = 600.** (1.5 marks)

> [!TIP]
> Notice how `M[200]` appears in both Instruction 3 and Instruction 4. Indirect uses M[200] as a *pointer* (giving 600), while Register Indirect uses *the register R2* which happens to contain 200. The end result differs because in indirect we dereference *one more time*.

---

**Instruction 5: `LOAD AC, 200(R1)` — Indexed Mode**

Formula: $\text{EA} = (R1) + A = 100 + 200 = 300$.

$$
\text{AC} \leftarrow M[300]
$$

M[300] is not given in the problem (it is unspecified).

**EA = 300; AC = M[300] (value depends on memory contents, not provided in problem).** (1.5 marks)

If the examiner intended R1 to act as the **index** and the constant 200 to be the base address, the interpretation could be the same; either way, **EA = 300**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (Real KTU Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting PC increment** in PC-relative addressing — costs 1–2 marks in nearly every branch problem.
> 2. **Confusing Direct with Indirect** — Indirect ALWAYS requires `M[A]`, not just `A`. Writing `EA = A` for an indirect problem is a full 3-mark loss.
> 3. **Not specifying the mode's *number of memory references*** — KTU rubric requires it. Always state it explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up Indexed and Base-Register** — In indexed, the *register* is typically incremented in a loop. In base-register, the register is *fixed* and the offset varies. KTU expects this distinction.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams or tables for comparison questions** — A well-labeled table almost guarantees full marks in 7-mark "compare" questions.
> 6. **Writing `(A)` for direct mode** — parentheses always mean indirection in KTU notation.

---

## 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ An **addressing mode** defines the *rule* for computing the **Effective Address (EA)** of an operand.
- ✅ The **8 modes** in KTU 2024: Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Register, Register Indirect, Displacement (Relative/Base/Indexed), Stack, Auto-inc/dec.
- ✅ **Speed ranking (fastest → slowest):** Register ≈ Immediate → Register Indirect ≈ Direct ≈ Displacement → Indirect.
- ✅ **EA formulas** (memorize these):
  - Immediate: operand in instruction
  - Direct: $\text{EA} = A$
  - Indirect: $\text{EA} = M[A]$
  - Register: $\text{EA} = R_k$
  - Register Indirect: $\text{EA} = (R_k)$
  - Relative: $\text{EA} = \text{PC} + A$
  - Base: $\text{EA} = R_B + A$
  - Indexed: $\text{EA} = R_X + A$
  - Stack: $\text{EA} = \text{SP}$
- ✅ **Parentheses `(A)` ALWAYS mean indirection** in KTU notation — both for memory indirect and register indirect.
- ✅ For **PC-relative branches**, the PC is incremented by the instruction size *before* the offset is added.
- ✅ **Auto-increment updates the register *after* use; auto-decrement updates *before* use.**
- ✅ **Indirect needs 2 memory references; Register Indirect needs only 1** — this is the key advantage.
- ✅ **Position-Independent Code (PIC)** relies on PC-relative addressing.
- ✅ **Displacement addressing** is the *most flexible* mode — combine the strengths of registers (speed) with constants (flexibility).
- ✅ **Stack addressing** requires no explicit address field — operand location is *implied* by SP.
- ✅ In KTU exams, always: (1) state the mode, (2) write the EA formula, (3) compute the EA numerically, (4) state the number of memory references.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
