# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Processing Unit (BPU)** — The **Basic Processing Unit** of a computer is the hardware sub-system within the Central Processing Unit (CPU) responsible for the **fetching, decoding, and execution of instructions**. Architecturally, it comprises three tightly coupled components: the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, a set of high-speed **processor registers**, and a **Control Unit (CU)**. These components are interconnected by one or more **internal data paths (buses)** through which binary information, in the form of **micro-operations**, is transferred and processed.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT414), the *Basic Processing Unit* is the foundational block upon which the entire instruction execution cycle is built. It is the **datapath + control logic** that physically realizes every machine instruction.

### 1.1.1 Key Sub-Components

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) and logic (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHIFT`) operations.
2. **Register File** — A small, ultra-fast bank of on-chip storage locations (e.g., Program Counter `PC`, Instruction Register `IR`, Accumulator `AC`, general-purpose registers `R0 – Rn-1`).
3. **Control Unit (CU)** — Generates the precise sequence of **control signals** that orchestrate register transfers, ALU operation selection, and bus activation.
4. **Internal Buses** — Carry data, addresses, and control signals between registers, ALU, and external memory.
5. **Shifter** — A specialized combinational circuit that performs logical, circular, and arithmetic shifts in a single clock cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 requires students to analyze the *single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus* internal organizations of the processor and to derive the control signal sequences for the **fetch**, **decode**, and **execute** phases.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Kitchen

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a professional restaurant kitchen. The **chef** is the *Control Unit* — they read the recipe (the *instruction*) and shout precise orders ("stir!", "add salt!"). The **cooking pots, pans, and the stove** are the *ALU + Shifter* — they transform the raw ingredients. The **small prep trays on the counter** (containing pre-chopped onions, measured spices) are the *processor registers* — items needed frequently are kept *right next to the chef* for instant access. The **waiter who fetches ingredients from the pantry (main memory)** is the *bus system*. The pantry itself is *main memory*. The chef cannot cook without the ingredients being on the counter — similarly, the CPU cannot operate on data not present in its registers.

This analogy maps cleanly to computer architecture:

| Kitchen Element | Computer Architecture Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Recipe card | Machine instruction |
| Chef's reading of recipe | Instruction Decode phase |
| Chef's verbal orders | Control signals |
| Prep trays (pre-measured ingredients) | Processor Registers |
| Stove & pans | ALU + Shifter |
| Waiter carrying trays | Internal/External Buses |
| Pantry shelves | Main Memory (RAM) |

---

## 1.3 The Register Transfer Notation (RTN) — KTU Standard

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Register Transfer Notation (RTN):** A symbolic shorthand used universally in KTU board papers to describe the *micro-operations* performed by the BPU. The general form is:
>
> `R2 ← R1`
>
> This denotes that the contents of register `R1` are *transferred* (copied, not physically moved) into `R2` during a single clock edge. Conditional transfers use `IF (condition) THEN (operation)`.

**Standard KTU RTN Symbols:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\leftarrow$ | Transfer / Copy | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$ |
| $+$ | Addition | $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ |
| $-$ | Subtraction | $R3 \leftarrow R1 - R2$ |
| $\land$ | Bitwise AND | $R3 \leftarrow R1 \land R2$ |
| $\lor$ | Bitwise OR | $R3 \leftarrow R1 \lor R2$ |
| $\oplus$ | Bitwise XOR | $R3 \leftarrow R1 \oplus R2$ |
| $\overline{A}$ | Bitwise NOT (1's complement) | $AC \leftarrow \overline{AC}$ |
| $:$ | Concatenation | $PC \leftarrow PC : IR(Address)$ |

---

## 1.4 Why is the BPU "Basic"?

The qualifier **"Basic"** distinguishes this single-cycle, single-instruction-at-a-time processing model from advanced concepts like *pipelined* or *superscalar* units (covered in higher modules). In the BPU model:

- **One micro-operation** is completed per **clock cycle**.
- All register transfers are **synchronous** to a master clock.
- Control is **hardwired** (combinational logic), not microprogrammed — at this introductory level.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Students often confuse the *Basic Processing Unit* (the hardware datapath) with the *CPU* as a whole. Strictly, the **CPU = BPU + Control Unit + Bus Interface Unit**. The BPU is *only* the datapath — registers, ALU, and their interconnect.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Bus Organizations — The Three KTU Archetypes

The structural complexity of the BPU is classified by the **number of parallel internal data buses** connecting the registers to the ALU inputs. KTU 2024 places heavy emphasis on deriving control sequences for all three organizations.

### 2.1.1 Single-Bus Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Bus BPU:** All registers share **one common data bus**. Only **one register can drive the bus** at a time. The ALU has only **one input** connected to this bus; the second ALU input must be hard-wired or buffered.

**Operational Consequences:**
- An operation like `R3 ← R1 + R2` requires **THREE sequential micro-steps**:
  1. `BUS ← R1` (drive R1 onto bus)
  2. `ALU_in2 ← BUS` (capture into ALU's second input latch)
  3. `R3 ← ALU_output` (write result back)
- High **clock cycle count** per instruction → slower throughput.
- Minimal **hardware complexity** (single multiplexer tree).

### 2.1.2 Two-Bus Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Bus BPU:** Two independent internal buses (**Bus A, Bus B**) feed the two ALU inputs simultaneously. Each bus can be driven by *any* register, selected by a multiplexer.

**Operational Consequence:**
- `R3 ← R1 + R2` reduces to **TWO micro-steps**:
  1. `BusA ← R1 ; BusB ← R2` (simultaneous)
  2. `R3 ← ALU_output`
- ALU output can also be **written back** to a register on the next edge.
- Balanced trade-off between **speed and hardware cost** — most commonly tested in KTU.

### 2.1.3 Three-Bus Organization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Bus BPU:** Two source buses (Bus A, Bus B) feed the ALU, and a **dedicated third bus (Bus C)** carries the ALU output directly to a destination register in the **same clock cycle**.

**Operational Consequence:**
- `R3 ← R1 + R2` completes in **ONE micro-step**:
  1. `BusA ← R1 ; BusB ← R2 ; R3 ← ALU_out (via Bus C)`
- Highest throughput; maximum hardware (two large input muxes + one output bus driver).
- Required for **single-cycle CPU designs** (e.g., MIPS-style RISC).

---

## 2.2 Processor Registers — Functional Classification

| Register | Symbol | Width | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Program Counter | $PC$ | $n$ bits | Holds the address of the *next* instruction to fetch. |
| Instruction Register | $IR$ | $m$ bits | Holds the *currently executing* instruction. |
| Memory Address Register | $MAR$ | $n$ bits | Holds the address for memory read/write. |
| Memory Data Register (MDR) | $MDR$ | $k$ bits | Holds data being read from / written to memory. |
| Accumulator | $AC$ | $k$ bits | Default ALU operand and result register. |
| General Purpose Registers | $R0 \ldots R_{n-1}$ | $k$ bits | Hold operands, intermediate results. |
| Temporary Register | $T$ or $TR$ | $k$ bits | Hidden register for ALU's second input. |
| Status / Flags Register | $FR$ | few bits | Holds condition codes: $N, Z, V, C$ (Negative, Zero, oVerflow, Carry). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the standard KTU textbook register set (Patterson-Hennessy / Hamacher style), the ALU is assumed to have **two inputs** (one from a bus, one from a hidden temporary register $T$), and **one output** going to a register-file write port. This forces a 3-step micro-operation sequence in single-bus designs.

---

## 2.3 The ALU — Functional Anatomy

The Arithmetic Logic Unit is a **combinational network** that performs a set of $2^{k}$ operations selected by an **operation code** (`OPcode` or `SEL` input). It has:

- **Data inputs:** $A$ (typically $k$ bits), $B$ (typically $k$ bits).
- **Control input:** $SEL$ (typically $3$ to $5$ bits).
- **Data outputs:** $Result$ ($k$ bits), and flag outputs $C_{out}, V, N, Z$.

The ALU is internally built from:
1. A **k-bit parallel adder** (built from cascaded full adders, or using fast carry-lookahead).
2. A **logic unit** (k parallel gates: AND, OR, XOR).
3. A **k:1 multiplexer** at the output, steered by the `SEL` lines.
4. A **flag generation block** computing $\{N, Z, C, V\}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A frequently asked question involves designing the **function table** for an ALU that supports $\{ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, INC, DEC\}$ with minimum number of control bits. The **minimum SEL width** is $\lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3$ bits.

---

## 2.4 The Shifter — Co-Processor to the ALU

The shifter is a combinational unit that can perform:

| Operation | Notation | Effect on $k$-bit operand $R$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Logical Shift Left | $LSL$ | $\text{shift left by 1, fill LSB with } 0$ |
| Logical Shift Right | $LSR$ | $\text{shift right by 1, fill MSB with } 0$ |
| Circular Shift Left | $CSL$ (or $ROL$) | rotate left, MSB wraps to LSB |
| Circular Shift Right | $CSR$ (or $ROR$) | rotate right, LSB wraps to MSB |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | $ASR$ | shift right, replicate sign bit into MSB |

Mathematically, a **logical left shift by $i$ bits** is equivalent to multiplication by $2^{i}$:

$$
R_{\text{after}} = R_{\text{before}} \cdot 2^{i}
$$

A **logical right shift by $i$ bits** is equivalent to integer division by $2^{i}$:

$$
R_{\text{after}} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{\text{before}}}{2^{i}} \right\rfloor
$$

---

## 2.5 Micro-Operations and the Instruction Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Instruction Cycle** for any von-Neumann machine consists of:
> 1. **Fetch Phase** — read instruction from memory into $IR$.
> 2. **Decode Phase** — determine the operation and identify operands.
> 3. **Operand Fetch Phase** — read required operands from registers/memory.
> 4. **Execute Phase** — perform the operation in the ALU.
> 5. **Store Phase** — write the result back to a register or memory.
> 6. **Update PC** — increment or branch the Program Counter.

Each of these phases decomposes into **micro-operations**. For the canonical **Fetch micro-sequence** (single-bus BPU), the KTU standard sequence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \quad MAR \leftarrow PC \\
T_1 &: \quad MDR \leftarrow M[MAR] \quad ; \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1 \\
T_2 &: \quad IR \leftarrow MDR
\end{aligned}
$$

These three micro-cycles together are called the **Fetch Routine** and consume **3 clock cycles** per instruction in a single-bus BPU. In a 3-bus BPU, the entire fetch completes in **1 cycle**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU high-yield equations for the Basic Processing Unit topic, consolidated for last-mile revision:**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Address bus width vs. Memory size | $M = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address bits, $M$ = addressable words |
| Memory data transfer time | $t_{MEM} = T_{clk} \cdot n_{cycles}$ | seconds |
| Shift Left = Multiply by power of 2 | $Y = X \cdot 2^{i}$ | $i$ = shift count |
| Shift Right = Divide by power of 2 | $Y = \lfloor X / 2^{i} \rfloor$ | logical shift, unsigned |
| Overflow (2's complement signed) | $V = C_{in}^{\text{MSB}} \oplus C_{out}^{\text{MSB}}$ | XOR of carry-in and carry-out of MSB |
| Zero flag | $Z = 1 \iff R = 0$ | $R$ is $k$-bit result |
| Negative flag | $N = R_{k-1}$ | MSB of result |
| Min SEL bits for $p$ ALU ops | $s = \lceil \log_{2} p \rceil$ | bits |
| Single-bus cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 \text{ op } R2$ | $3$ cycles | $T_1: T \leftarrow R1; \quad T_2: T \leftarrow T \text{ op } R2; \quad T_3: R3 \leftarrow T$ |
| Two-bus cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 \text{ op } R2$ | $2$ cycles | $T_1: A \leftarrow R1, B \leftarrow R2; \quad T_2: R3 \leftarrow \text{ALU}$ |
| Three-bus cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 \text{ op } R2$ | $1$ cycle | $A \leftarrow R1, B \leftarrow R2, R3 \leftarrow \text{ALU}$ |
| Bus width vs. Word size | identical | bus is $k$-bit parallel for $k$-bit words |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BPU design philosophy directly determines the **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)**, **clock period**, **power dissipation**, and **die area** of a commercial processor. For example:

- **Intel x86 microarchitectures** historically used elaborate **microcoded** control units on multi-bus datapaths, trading simplicity for backward compatibility.
- **ARM Cortex-A** cores use **3-bus, single-cycle issue** datapaths for energy efficiency in mobile devices.
- **RISC-V** open architectures explicitly mandate a 3-bus BPU organization to enable easy pipelining (Module 3) and out-of-order execution (Module 4).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum SEL Lines for a Custom ALU

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Design a function table for an ALU that supports exactly **$p = 12$ operations**. Determine the minimum number of selector (control) lines required.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**
A binary selector of $s$ bits can encode $2^{s}$ distinct code words. To support $p$ operations, we need:

$$
2^{s} \geq p
$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-2 logarithm on both sides.**

$$
s \geq \log_{2} p
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ceiling operator** (since $s$ must be a non-negative integer):

$$
s_{min} = \left\lceil \log_{2} p \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $p = 12$:**

$$
s_{min} = \left\lceil \log_{2} 12 \right\rceil
$$

We compute $\log_2 12 = \log_2 (4 \cdot 3) = 2 + \log_2 3 = 2 + 1.585 = 3.585$.

**Step 5 — Apply the ceiling:**

$$
s_{min} = \lceil 3.585 \rceil = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification.** With $s = 4$ bits, we can encode $2^{4} = 16$ operations — enough to support the required 12 (with 4 unused code patterns). With $s = 3$, we get only $2^{3} = 8$ codes, which is **insufficient**.

$$
\boxed{s_{min} = 4 \text{ selector lines}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Two's Complement Overflow Flag

**Problem:** Given a $k$-bit parallel adder adding signed numbers $A$ and $B$, derive the expression for the **overflow flag** $V$ using only the carry-in and carry-out of the most significant bit (MSB).

**Step 1 — Define signed overflow.** Overflow occurs when the sign of the mathematical result of two same-signed operands is wrong. Equivalently, when the carry *into* the MSB differs from the carry *out* of the MSB.

**Step 2 — Let** $C_{n-1}$ be the carry-out of the $(n-1)$-th bit (the MSB) and $C_{n-2}$ be the carry-in to the MSB.

**Step 3 — Express as an XOR:**

$$
V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification by truth-table analysis.**
For two positive numbers ($A_{n-1} = 0$, $B_{n-1} = 0$), a correct result is positive. If both MSBs add with $C_{n-2} = 0$ and produce $C_{n-1} = 1$, the result is negative — *overflow has occurred*. This is exactly the case $C_{n-1} \neq C_{n-2}$.

For two negative numbers ($A_{n-1} = 1$, $B_{n-1} = 1$), the MSB sum includes the implicit $-2^{n-1}$ term. A correct result is negative ($R_{n-1} = 1$). If $C_{n-2} = 1$ and $C_{n-1} = 0$, the result is positive — *overflow*.

In both cases, overflow $\iff$ carries differ:

$$
\boxed{V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Control Signal Sequence for a Single-Bus ADD Instruction

**Problem:** Derive the RTN and corresponding control signal sequence for executing the instruction `ADD R1, R2` (i.e., $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$) on a single-bus BPU with registers $\{PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, TR\}$ and a hidden ALU temporary register $T$.

**Step 1 — Decode.** The decoded instruction signals $Y_{R1}$ and $Y_{R2}$ to select source registers. Assume $T$ is the hidden latch for the second ALU input.

**Step 2 — Micro-step 1:** Move $R1$ onto the bus and latch into $T$.

$$
T_1 : \quad T \leftarrow R1
$$

This requires: `BUSIN ← R1`, `T_latch ← ENABLE`, `Y_R1` asserted.

**Step 3 — Micro-step 2:** Add $R2$ to $T$ through the ALU.

$$
T_2 : \quad T \leftarrow T + R2
$$

This requires: `ALU_SEL ← ADD`, `BUSIN ← R2`, `Y_R2` asserted, `T_latch ← ENABLE`.

**Step 4 — Micro-step 3:** Write the ALU result (currently in $T$) back into $R1$.

$$
T_3 : \quad R1 \leftarrow T
$$

This requires: `R1_load ← ENABLE`, `T_outbuf ← ENABLE`.

**Step 5 — Summary Table.**

| Cycle | Micro-operation | Active Control Signals |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_1$ | $T \leftarrow R1$ | $Y_{R1}, T_{\text{in}}$ |
| $T_2$ | $T \leftarrow T + R2$ | $Y_{R2}, ALU_{ADD}, T_{\text{in}}$ |
| $T_3$ | $R1 \leftarrow T$ | $T_{\text{out}}, \text{Load } R1$ |

**Step 6 — Total instruction time:**

$$
t_{\text{ADD}} = 3 \cdot T_{clk}
$$

where $T_{clk}$ is the clock period (must exceed the worst-case register-to-register propagation delay through the ALU).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Equivalent Cycles on Three-Bus BPU for the Same Instruction

**Problem:** Show that the same `ADD R1, R2` instruction completes in **1 cycle** on a 3-bus BPU.

**Step 1 — Single micro-step.** Both operands are placed on buses A and B simultaneously; the ALU output flows through bus C into the destination register $R1$ in the *same* clock edge.

$$
T_1 : \quad \text{BusA} \leftarrow R1 \ ; \ \text{BusB} \leftarrow R2 \ ; \ R1 \leftarrow \text{ALU}_{\text{ADD}}(\text{A}, \text{B})
$$

**Step 2 — Cycle count comparison.**

$$
\frac{t_{\text{single-bus}}}{t_{\text{three-bus}}} = \frac{3 \, T_{clk}}{1 \, T_{clk}} = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup.** The 3-bus organization offers a $3\times$ throughput improvement *for ALU-bound operations* — at the cost of two extra $k$-bit-wide buses and three large $n$-to-$1$ multiplexers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Bus Organizations

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is a KTU 2024 high-yield summary table. Memorize the row "Cycles per ALU op" and "Hardware Cost" — these appear in nearly every board exam.**

| Parameter | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ALU input buses | 1 (shared) | 2 (Bus A, Bus B) | 2 (Bus A, Bus B) |
| ALU output bus | same as input | 1 shared with B | dedicated Bus C |
| Cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 \text{ op } R2$ | $3$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| Cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 + 1$ | $2$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| Multiplexers required | one large $n:1$ | two medium $n:1$ | two large $n:1$ + output buffer |
| Clock period | longest (worst-case path) | medium | medium |
| Power consumption | lowest | medium | highest |
| Typical use | teaching, toy CPUs | mid-range embedded | RISC pipelined cores |
| KTU exam frequency | very high | very high | high |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Flag Generation for $k$-bit ALU

**Step 1 — Zero flag ($Z$).** $Z = 1$ if and only if **all** $k$ result bits are zero. This is the NOR of all output bits:

$$
Z = \overline{R_{k-1} \lor R_{k-2} \lor \ldots \lor R_1 \lor R_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Negative flag ($N$).** For $k$-bit two's complement, the sign is the MSB:

$$
N = R_{k-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Carry flag ($C$).** For unsigned addition, $C$ is the carry-out of the MSB:

$$
C = C_{out}^{\text{MSB}}
$$

**Step 4 — Overflow flag ($V$).** As derived in Section 3.2:

$$
V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final flag register update.** At the end of every ALU operation, the flags latch in parallel:

$$
FR \leftarrow \left[ V : C : N : Z \right]
$$

This 4-bit value is read by the Control Unit to make **conditional branch decisions** (e.g., $BNZ$ — branch if not zero).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Basic Processing Unit (BPU)

The diagram below depicts the **top-level BPU architecture** with all major sub-blocks and the data-flow between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXT[External Memory and I-O]
        MEM[MAIN MEMORY]
        IO[I-O DEVICES]
    end

    subgraph CPU[CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT]
        CU[CONTROL UNIT]
        REGFILE[REGISTER FILE R0 to Rn-1]
        PC[PROGRAM COUNTER]
        IR[INSTRUCTION REGISTER]
        MAR[MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER]
        MDR[MEMORY DATA REGISTER]
        ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT]
        SHIFT[SHIFTER]
        FR[FLAGS REGISTER]
    end

    BUSADDR[ADDRESS BUS]
    BUSDATA[DATA BUS]
    BUSCTL[CONTROL BUS]

    PC --> MAR
    MAR --> BUSADDR
    BUSADDR --> MEM
    MEM --> BUSDATA
    BUSDATA --> MDR
    MDR --> IR
    IR --> CU
    CU --> BUSCTL
    BUSCTL --> REGFILE
    BUSCTL --> ALU
    BUSCTL --> SHIFT
    REGFILE --> ALU
    REGFILE --> SHIFT
    ALU --> FR
    SHIFT --> FR
    ALU --> REGFILE
    SHIFT --> REGFILE
    CU --> PC
    EXT --> BUSDATA
    BUSDATA --> EXT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent **data/address flow**; the **Control Unit** broadcasts timing & operation signals downward to the datapath elements. The Flags Register is read by the CU to implement *conditional branches*.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three-Bus Internal Organization of the BPU

This detailed diagram shows the **internal data path** of a 3-bus BPU — the configuration most often asked in KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REGSRC1[Source Register Group 1]
        R0A[R0]
        R1A[R1]
        R2A[R2]
        R3A[R3]
    end

    subgraph REGSRC2[Source Register Group 2]
        R0B[R0]
        R1B[R1]
        R2B[R2]
        R3B[R3]
    end

    subgraph DEST[Destination Register Group]
        D0[R0]
        D1[R1]
        D2[R2]
        D3[R3]
    end

    MUXA[MUX A]
    MUXB[MUX B]
    ALUBLK[ALU CORE]
    SHIFTB[SHIFTER]
    MUXOUT[OUTPUT SELECTOR]
    MUXC[OUTPUT BUS C]

    R0A --> MUXA
    R1A --> MUXA
    R2A --> MUXA
    R3A --> MUXA

    R0B --> MUXB
    R1B --> MUXB
    R2B --> MUXB
    R3B --> MUXB

    MUXA --> ALUBLK
    MUXB --> ALUBLK
    ALUBLK --> SHIFTB
    SHIFTB --> MUXOUT
    MUXOUT --> MUXC
    MUXC --> D0
    MUXC --> D1
    MUXC --> D2
    MUXC --> D3
```

> [!TIP]
> **MUX A** selects the first operand; **MUX B** selects the second; **MUX C** selects the destination register. The output selector chooses between *ALU-direct* and *ALU-then-Shifter* paths. The control unit drives all three muxes via select lines $SEL_A$, $SEL_B$, $SEL_DEST$ and the ALU/Shift operation codes.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Single-Bus BPU Data Path

The single-bus variant — frequently asked in KTU derivations — uses **one shared internal bus** plus a **hidden temporary register $T$** for the second ALU operand.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC1[PC] --> BUS1[INTERNAL BUS]
    AR1[AR] --> BUS1
    DR1[DR] --> BUS1
    AC1[AC] --> BUS1
    IR1[IR] --> BUS1
    R01[R0] --> BUS1
    R11[R1] --> BUS1

    BUS1 --> TLATCH[T LATCH hidden]
    BUS1 --> ALUMAIN[ALU PRIMARY INPUT]
    ALUMAIN --> ALUCORE[ALU CORE]
    TLATCH --> ALUSEC[ALU SECONDARY INPUT]
    ALUCORE --> ALURES[ALU RESULT BUS]
    ALURES --> AC2[AC write]
    ALURES --> DR2[DR write]
    ALURES --> R02[R0 write]
    ALURES --> R12[R1 write]
    ALURES --> IR2[IR write]
    ALURES --> PC2[PC write]
    ALURES --> AR2[AR write]
    ALUCORE --> FLAGBLK[FLAGS BLOCK]
    FLAGBLK --> FRREG[FR]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The single shared bus enforces **strict serialization** — only one register can drive it per cycle, and the $T$ latch is mandatory because the ALU's secondary input cannot read the bus while another register is driving it (in the same cycle). This is why every ALU operation needs **at least 3 cycles** on a single-bus BPU.

---

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram: Micro-Operation Flow for `ADD R1, R2` on 2-Bus BPU

This diagram shows the **temporal sequence** of register transfers and bus activations. Each horizontal lane is a hardware element; each numbered step is one clock cycle.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CU as CONTROL UNIT
    participant MUXA as MUX A
    participant MUXB as MUX B
    participant R1 as R1 register
    participant R2 as R2 register
    participant R3 as R3 destination
    participant ALU as ALU CORE

    Note over CU,ALU: Clock Cycle T1
    CU->>MUXA: SEL = R1
    CU->>MUXB: SEL = R2
    MUXA->>ALU: operand A
    MUXB->>ALU: operand B
    CU->>ALU: OP = ADD

    Note over CU,ALU: Clock Cycle T2 - rising edge
    ALU->>R3: R3 latches ALU result
    R3-->>R3: R3 holds R1 plus R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-bus observation:** Notice that the *ALU computes* in cycle T1 (combinational propagation) and the *result is latched* in cycle T2. This is the canonical **2-cycle** execution for an ALU-register operation on a 2-bus BPU.

---

## 4.5 State Machine: BPU Instruction Cycle Controller

The control unit's micro-operation sequencer can be modelled as a **finite state machine** with the major states shown below.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FETCH1

    FETCH1: T0 MAR gets PC
    FETCH2: T1 MDR gets M MDR, PC gets PC plus 1
    FETCH3: T2 IR gets MDR
    DECODE: T3 Decode IR
    EXEC: T4 Execute micro ops
    STORE: T5 Write back result
    BRANCH: T6 Update PC if branch taken

    FETCH1 --> FETCH2
    FETCH2 --> FETCH3
    FETCH3 --> DECODE
    DECODE --> EXEC
    EXEC --> STORE
    STORE --> BRANCH
    BRANCH --> FETCH1
    STORE --> FETCH1
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner's note:** State names must be alphanumeric and *cannot* match Mermaid reserved keywords such as `end`. The state `END` would be rejected by the Mermaid parser — always use `TERMINATE` or `HALT` instead.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **Define the term "Basic Processing Unit". List any four registers that are part of the BPU and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~80 words):**

The **Basic Processing Unit (BPU)** is the data-processing sub-system of the CPU consisting of the ALU, the register file, and the internal data paths (buses) that interconnect them, all orchestrated by a control unit. It is responsible for fetching, decoding, and executing machine instructions.

**Four essential registers:**

1. **Program Counter (PC)** — Holds the memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched.
2. **Instruction Register (IR)** — Latches the *current* instruction fetched from memory.
3. **Memory Address Register (MAR)** — Holds the address used to access main memory.
4. **Accumulator (AC)** — Default operand and result register for ALU operations.

> **[1 mark] for the correct definition, [2 marks] for listing and explaining four registers (0.5 mark each).**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **What is the difference between a single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus organization of the BPU? Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~70 words):**

| Organization | Distinctive Feature | Advantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single Bus** | All registers share **one** common internal data bus. | **Lowest hardware cost** — only one multiplexer tree and minimal wiring. |
| **Two Bus** | Two independent buses (**Bus A, Bus B**) feed the two ALU inputs. | **Faster execution** — ALU operation reduces from 3 cycles to 2 cycles. |
| **Three Bus** | Two source buses + a dedicated output bus (**Bus C**) write back in one cycle. | **Highest throughput** — ALU operation completes in 1 clock cycle. |

> **[1 mark] for the three correct distinctions, [2 marks] for the three advantages with valid reasoning.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** and must be answered in approximately **30–35 minutes**. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student is given an **internal choice** between two full questions.

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal organization of a single-bus Basic Processing Unit**. Show clearly all the major registers, the ALU, the shifter, and the common internal bus. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the single-bus BPU described in part (a), derive the **complete sequence of micro-operations and control signals** required to execute the instruction **`ADD R1, R2`** (i.e., $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$). Assume the presence of a hidden temporary register $T$ at the ALU's second input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Draw and label the single-bus architecture.** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes for the canonical block diagram. The diagram should show:

- Registers: $PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, R0, R1, T$
- A common **internal bus** that all registers can drive (via a multiplexer)
- An **ALU** with two inputs — one from the bus, one from the hidden latch $T$
- A **shifter** cascaded after the ALU
- A **flags register** $(N, Z, V, C)$ fed by the ALU
- The **Control Unit** broadcasting control signals to every register's `IN` and `OUT` enable pins

**Step 2 — Functional description of the common bus.**
All registers are connected to a single shared bus. A central **bus multiplexer** selects which register's output is currently placed on the bus. Only one register can drive the bus at any instant — this is the central architectural constraint.

**Step 3 — Role of the hidden register $T$.**
The ALU requires two operands per operation. Because the bus is occupied by the first operand, the second operand must be latched into the hidden register $T$ *before* the ALU operation cycle. $T$ is therefore an *internal scratchpad* invisible to the programmer.

**Step 4 — Control signal summary.**
Each register $R_i$ has two associated control signals: $R_{i,\text{OUT}}$ (tri-state driver to the bus) and $R_{i,\text{IN}}$ (clocked load enable). The ALU and shifter each have operation-select lines (e.g., $ALU_{SEL}$, $SHIFT_{SEL}$).

**Mark Allocation:**
- Block diagram with all labels: **3 marks**
- Explanation of bus + $T$ register: **2 marks**
- Description of control signals: **2 marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the requirement.** The instruction `ADD R1, R2` performs the operation $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R2$. Both operands are in registers — no memory access is required during the execute phase.

**Step 2 — Decode phase.** The instruction `ADD R1, R2` is in the IR. The decoder asserts two register-select lines: $Y_{R1}$ and $Y_{R2}$.

**Step 3 — Micro-step 1: Latch the first operand into $T$.**

$$
T_1 : \quad T \leftarrow R1
$$

**Active signals:** $R1_{\text{OUT}}$, $T_{\text{IN}}$. (Bus carries $R1$; $T$ latches it.)

**Step 4 — Micro-step 2: Add the second operand through the ALU.**

$$
T_2 : \quad T \leftarrow T + R2
$$

**Active signals:** $R2_{\text{OUT}}$, $ALU_{SEL} = ADD$, $T_{\text{IN}}$. (Bus carries $R2$; ALU computes $T + R2$; result latched back into $T$.)

**Step 5 — Micro-step 3: Write the result to the destination register $R1$.**

$$
T_3 : \quad R1 \leftarrow T
$$

**Active signals:** $T_{\text{OUT}}$, $R1_{\text{IN}}$. (Bus carries $T$'s contents; $R1$ latches them.)

**Step 6 — Tabulated control signal sequence.**

| Cycle | Micro-operation | Control Signals Asserted |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $T_1$ | $T \leftarrow R1$ | $R1_{\text{OUT}},\; T_{\text{IN}}$ |
| $T_2$ | $T \leftarrow T + R2$ | $R2_{\text{OUT}},\; ALU_{ADD},\; T_{\text{IN}}$ |
| $T_3$ | $R1 \leftarrow T$ | $T_{\text{OUT}},\; R1_{\text{IN}}$ |

**Step 7 — Total execution time.**

$$
t_{\text{ADD}} = 3 \cdot T_{clk}
$$

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct identification of 3-step decomposition: **2 marks**
- Each correctly derived micro-step with active control signals: **4 marks** (≈ 1.3 marks each)
- Final timing equation: **1 mark**

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Compare the **single-bus, two-bus, and three-bus internal organizations** of the BPU in terms of (i) the number of cycles required to perform `R3 ← R1 + R2`, (ii) the hardware cost, and (iii) the typical application. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design the **function table** of a $4$-bit ALU that supports the following $8$ operations: `ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, NOT A, INC A, DEC A`. Specify the minimum number of selector lines, the function table, and the corresponding control logic equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Tabulate the comparative analysis** (extends Section 3.5 above):

| Aspect | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Cycles for $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$** | $3$ cycles | $2$ cycles | $1$ cycle |
| **(ii) Hardware Cost** | Lowest — 1 bus, 1 mux, hidden $T$ register | Medium — 2 buses, 2 muxes, no hidden $T$ | Highest — 2 input buses + 1 output bus + 3 muxes |
| **(iii) Typical Application** | Teaching/educational CPUs, simple microcontrollers | Mid-range embedded processors | RISC pipelined cores (e.g., MIPS, RISC-V) |

**Step 2 — Justify the cycle counts.**

- **Single-bus:** Needs $T_1: T \leftarrow R1$, $T_2: T \leftarrow T + R2$, $T_3: R3 \leftarrow T$. Three steps.
- **Two-bus:** Needs $T_1: A \leftarrow R1, B \leftarrow R2$ (parallel) and $T_2: R3 \leftarrow ALU_{out}$. Two steps.
- **Three-bus:** Needs $T_1: A \leftarrow R1, B \leftarrow R2, R3 \leftarrow ALU_{out}$ all in one cycle. One step.

**Step 3 — Hardware justification.**

- A single-bus BPU uses $1$ large $n:1$ multiplexer feeding the bus. A two-bus BPU uses $2$ medium $n:1$ muxes. A three-bus BPU uses $2$ large $n:1$ input muxes **plus** a $n:1$ output mux for Bus C.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Correct cycle counts for all three: **2 marks**
- Hardware cost comparison: **3 marks**
- Application mapping: **2 marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Determine the minimum number of selector lines.**
With $p = 8$ operations:

$$
s_{min} = \lceil \log_{2} 8 \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}
$$

Let the selector lines be $S_2, S_1, S_0$.

**Step 2 — Construct the function table.**

| $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Operation | Function | Result $F$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | `ADD` | $F = A + B$ | $A$ plus $B$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $1$ | `SUB` | $F = A - B$ | $A$ plus $\overline{B} + 1$ |
| $0$ | $1$ | $0$ | `AND` | $F = A \land B$ | bitwise AND |
| $0$ | $1$ | $1$ | `OR`  | $F = A \lor B$ | bitwise OR |
| $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | `XOR` | $F = A \oplus B$ | bitwise XOR |
| $1$ | $0$ | $1$ | `NOT A` | $F = \overline{A}$ | bitwise NOT of A only |
| $1$ | $1$ | $0$ | `INC A` | $F = A + 1$ | increment A only |
| $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | `DEC A` | $F = A - 1$ | decrement A only |

**Step 3 — Derive the control logic equations.**
Let $X$ denote the output of a $4$-bit sub-unit. The final result $F_i$ (for each bit $i$) is steered by a 3-to-1 mux that selects among the eight sub-results.

Using the 3-line selector $S_2 S_1 S_0$, the final expression is:

$$
F_i = \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}\,(A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_{in,i})_{ADD} + \ldots
$$

For brevity, this is implemented as an $8:1$ multiplexer per bit, with $S_2, S_1, S_0$ as the select lines and the eight pre-computed results as data inputs.

**Step 4 — Block diagram description.**
Internally, the ALU consists of:
- A **4-bit parallel adder** (ripple-carry or carry-lookahead) used for `ADD`, `SUB`, `INC`, `DEC` (with appropriate $B$-inversion and $C_{in} = 1$ for `SUB`/`DEC`, and $B = 0, C_{in} = 1$ for `INC`).
- A **4-bit logic unit** computing $A \land B$, $A \lor B$, $A \oplus B$, and $\overline{A}$ in parallel.
- A **4-bit 8:1 output multiplexer** steered by $S_2 S_1 S_0$.

**Mark Allocation:**
- Minimum SEL derivation: **1 mark**
- Correct function table with 8 rows: **3 marks**
- Internal sub-units identified: **2 marks**
- Mux steering logic: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on "Basic Processing Unit" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Register Transfer *with* the bus mechanism.** Writing `R3 ← R1 + R2` is *not* enough — the examiner expects to see the **multi-step micro-operation breakdown** (e.g., $T \leftarrow R1$; $T \leftarrow T + R2$; $R3 \leftarrow T$) for a single-bus BPU. **Loss: 2–3 marks** if you skip the micro-step expansion.
>
> 2. **Omitting the role of the hidden $T$ register.** In any single-bus analysis, the $T$ latch is *mandatory* — failing to mention it signals a fundamental misunderstanding of the bus contention problem. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Cycle-count mismatches.** Many students write "1 cycle" for a 3-bus `ADD` but then draw a *2-bus* block diagram. The cycle count **must** match the architecture drawn. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the carry-in $C_0 = 1$ for `SUB` in ALU design.** The subtraction $A - B$ is implemented as $A + \overline{B} + 1$. Omitting the `+1` is a classic 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Not specifying the width of buses and registers.** A block diagram with no width labels (e.g., "$k$-bit") is considered incomplete. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 6. **Mixing up PC and MAR.** The Program Counter feeds the MAR, but they are *separate* registers. Conflating them costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Mile Revision Checklist — Basic Processing Unit (Module 1)**

- **BPU = ALU + Register File + Internal Bus(es) + Control Unit.**
- **Three internal bus organizations exist** — single, two, three — with cycle counts of **3, 2, 1** for a generic 2-operand ALU instruction.
- **Single-bus BPUs always need a hidden register $T$** at the ALU's secondary input to break the bus contention deadlock.
- **RTN** (Register Transfer Notation) is the KTU-standard language for describing micro-operations; symbols: $\leftarrow, +, -, \land, \lor, \oplus, \overline{\cdot}$.
- **The fetch sequence** is always $MAR \leftarrow PC$; $MDR \leftarrow M[MAR], PC \leftarrow PC+1$; $IR \leftarrow MDR$ — three cycles on a single-bus BPU.
- **ALU operations** are selected by $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$ control lines, where $p$ is the number of distinct operations supported.
- **Overflow flag** $V = C_{\text{out}}^{\text{MSB}} \oplus C_{\text{in}}^{\text{MSB}}$ for two's-complement signed arithmetic.
- **Subtraction in ALU** is implemented as $A + \overline{B} + 1$ (with $C_{in} = 1$).
- **Logical shift left by $i$ bits** = multiplication by $2^{i}$; **Logical shift right by $i$ bits** = unsigned division by $2^{i}$.
- **Flags register** holds $\{N, Z, C, V\}$; used by conditional branch instructions.
- **Three-bus BPU is the foundation** of single-cycle RISC datapaths (MIPS, RISC-V) — connects directly to the pipelined designs of Module 3.
- **Control signals** include register `IN` / `OUT` enables, ALU `SEL` lines, shifter `SEL` lines, and bus driver enables.
- **Speedup of 3-bus over single-bus** for ALU ops is exactly **3×**; over two-bus is **2×**.
- **The instruction cycle always ends** with $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ (or branch target) before the next fetch — this is the only place PC is written during straight-line execution.
- **Block diagrams MUST label bus widths** and **register names** — an unlabeled diagram earns only partial credit.
- **Common sub-units**: parallel adder, logic unit, output mux, flag generation block, hidden $T$ register, shifter, status register.
- **Real-world examples** of 3-bus BPUs include ARM Cortex-A, RISC-V RV32I cores, and MIPS R3000.
- **KTU favourite exam hooks**: minimum SEL bits, control signal tables, RTN micro-step sequences, fetch cycle timing, and ALU function table design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computer Organization and Architecture?

**Computer Architecture** refers to the *attributes* of a system as seen by the programmer — i.e., the conceptual structure and functional behavior of the computer as observed by the user (instruction set, addressing modes, data types, I/O mechanisms).

**Computer Organization** refers to the *operational units* and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications — i.e., the transparent hardware details (control signals, interfaces, memory technology, bus widths) that are programmer-invisible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Frequency):**
> - *Architecture* = "What the system does" (logical/abstract)
> - *Organization* = "How it does it" (physical/structural)
>
> **Example:** Whether a system uses a single bus or a multi-bus is an *organizational* decision; whether it uses RISC or CISC is an *architectural* decision.

## 1.2 The Von Neumann Architecture (Stored-Program Concept)

A **Von Neumann Architecture** is a computer design model where **both instructions and data reside in a single, shared memory unit** and are fetched through the *same bus*. The CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions sequentially from this unified memory.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Chef's Recipe Book
Imagine a chef working in a kitchen:
- The **Recipe Book** = Memory (stores both recipes [instructions] and ingredients [data])
- The **Cook's Hands** = CPU (reads recipe, performs actions)
- The **Counter** = ALU (where actual cutting/mixing happens)
- The **Order Ticket** = Program Counter (points to the current recipe step)
- The **Speaking Tube to the Pantry** = System Bus (the single path to fetch items)

The chef reads the recipe, fetches ingredients from the same pantry via the same path, and prepares the dish. The bottleneck? That single speaking tube — it can carry *either* a recipe page *or* an ingredient at a time. This is the famous **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.

## 1.3 The Harvard Architecture

A **Harvard Architecture** uses **physically separate storage and pathways** for instructions and data. It has two independent memory units and two independent buses — eliminating the Von Neumann bottleneck.

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single, shared | Separate I-memory and D-memory |
| Bus | Single shared bus | Separate buses |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallel fetch) |
| Cost | Cheaper | Costlier (more hardware) |
| Used In | General desktops/laptops | DSPs, Microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M) |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern processors use a **Modified Harvard Architecture** — where cache memories are split (L1 I-cache and L1 D-cache) while main memory remains unified. This is the practical hybrid found in Intel/AMD CPUs.

## 1.4 Functional Units of a Computer

A computer system consists of **five major functional units**:

1. **Input Unit** — Accepts data/instructions from outside (keyboard, mouse, scanner)
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores programs, data, and intermediate results
   - **Primary Memory (RAM, ROM)** — Fast, volatile/non-volatile, directly accessed by CPU
   - **Secondary Memory (HDD, SSD)** — Slow, large capacity, persistent
3. **Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations
4. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs all system operations; fetches, decodes, and orchestrates execution
5. **Output Unit** — Returns processed results to the user (monitor, printer, speaker)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Definition:** The **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** is a single IC chip containing the **ALU + Control Unit + Registers** — collectively called the *Processor*. The CPU along with memory and I/O forms the *Computer System*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The System Bus — The Communication Backbone

A **System Bus** is a shared communication pathway consisting of **three functional groups of lines**:

### (a) Data Bus
- **Function:** Carries *actual data* between modules (memory, CPU, I/O).
- **Width:** $n$ bits → can transfer $n$ bits in parallel.
- **Bidirectional.**

### (b) Address Bus
- **Function:** Carries the *source or destination address* of data.
- **Width:** $k$ bits → can address $2^k$ unique memory locations.
- **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O).

### (c) Control Bus (Command Bus)
- **Function:** Carries *timing and control signals* (Read, Write, Clock, Interrupt, Bus Request, Bus Grant).
- **Bidirectional/Varied** depending on signal.

> [!NOTE]
> A 32-bit address bus can address $2^{32} = 4,294,967,296 = 4 \text{ GB}$ of memory locations.

## 2.2 Basic Operational Concept — The Instruction Cycle

The CPU repeatedly executes an **Instruction Cycle** comprising:

1. **Fetch Phase:** Read next instruction from memory (address in PC) into IR.
2. **Decode Phase:** CU decodes opcode in IR, determines required operands.
3. **Execute Phase:** ALU performs operation or data is read/written.
4. **Store Phase:** Result is written back to register/memory.

The cycle continues until a **HALT** or **STOP** instruction is encountered.

## 2.3 Basic Performance Equation

The **CPU Execution Time** for a program is governed by three fundamental parameters:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock Cycle Time}$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{CPU Time} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$$

Where:
- **Instruction Count (IC)** — Total number of instructions executed in the program
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — Average clock cycles required per instruction
- **Clock Rate** — Frequency of the processor clock (Hz)
- **Clock Cycle Time** — Reciprocal of Clock Rate ($T = 1/f$)

### MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) Rating

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Clock Rate}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$$

### Amdahl's Law (Speedup Bound)

When we enhance a portion of a system by speedup factor $s$ (and that portion represents a fraction $f$ of total execution time):

$$\text{Overall Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As $s \to \infty$, the maximum achievable speedup tends to $\frac{1}{1-f}$. This is the *theoretical ceiling* — improving the most-used part yields the greatest gains.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Execution Time | $\text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clk}$ | **seconds** |
| 2 | CPU Time (alt form) | $\text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \,/\, f_{clk}$ | **seconds** |
| 3 | MIPS Rating | $f_{clk} \,/\, (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$ | **MIPS** |
| 4 | Clock Cycle Time | $T_{clk} = 1 \,/\, f_{clk}$ | **seconds** |
| 5 | Addressable Memory | $2^k$ locations | $k$ = address bus width |
| 6 | Amdahl's Law | $S = 1 \,/\, \left[(1-f) + f/s\right]$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | MFLOPS | $(\text{FP Ops}) \,/\, (\text{Time} \times 10^6)$ | For scientific workloads |
| 8 | Avg CPI (mixed program) | $\sum_i (\text{IC}_i \times \text{CPI}_i) \,/\, \text{IC}_{total}$ | Weighted by IC |

> [!WARNING]
> When using the vertical bar in any math expression **inside** markdown tables, **always** use `\vert` or `\mid`. Never use the raw `|` symbol — it breaks the table renderer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The performance equation directly drives **CPU design decisions**:
- A chip designer can reduce CPU time by **lowering IC** (better compiler, RISC), **lowering CPI** (pipelining, caching), or **raising clock rate** (smaller transistors, deeper pipelining).
- **Amdahl's Law** is used in cloud computing to decide which subsystem to upgrade for maximum ROI.
- **MIPS** is used in embedded benchmarking (Dhrystone, CoreMark).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Instruction Count to CPU Time

We begin with the most basic unit — **one instruction** — and build up to total program time.

**Step 1: Time for one instruction**

If an instruction requires $C$ clock cycles and each cycle takes $T_{clk}$ seconds:

$$
T_{inst} = C \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 2: Time for all instructions**

If a program contains $\text{IC}$ instructions, each consuming on average $\overline{\text{CPI}}$ cycles:

$$
T_{prog} = \text{IC} \times \overline{\text{CPI}} \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Clock Cycle Time with frequency**

Since $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{clk}$:

$$
T_{prog} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \overline{\text{CPI}}}{f_{clk}}
$$

This is the **fundamental CPU performance equation** used in every architecture textbook.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A program has **100,000 instructions** distributed as follows: 30% ALU instructions (CPI = 1), 50% Load/Store (CPI = 3), 20% Branch (CPI = 2). Calculate (a) Average CPI, (b) CPU Time, (c) MIPS rating.

### Part (a): Average CPI

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = \sum_i \left( \text{Fraction}_i \times \text{CPI}_i \right)
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = (0.30 \times 1) + (0.50 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 2)
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = 0.30 + 1.50 + 0.40
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = 2.20
$$

### Part (b): CPU Time

$$
T_{cpu} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \overline{\text{CPI}}}{f_{clk}}
$$

$$
T_{cpu} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 2.20}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{cpu} = \frac{220{,}000}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{cpu} = 88 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 88 \, \mu s
$$

### Part (c): MIPS Rating

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\overline{\text{CPI}} \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{2.20 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2500}{2.20} = 1136.36 \text{ MIPS} \approx 1136 \text{ MIPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** As a sanity check, IC/Time = $100{,}000 / 88 \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.136 \times 10^9$ instructions/sec, which equals $1136$ MIPS. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem: Amdahl's Law Application

> **Problem:** A system spends **40%** of its time in floating-point operations. We upgrade the FP unit to be **5× faster**. What is the overall speedup?

**Given:** $f = 0.40$, $s = 5$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{s}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{5}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.08}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.68} \approx 1.47
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite making FP 5× faster, overall program runs only ~1.47× faster — a classic Amdahl demonstration.

**Maximum possible speedup (if $s \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} \approx 1.67
$$

So even with an *infinitely* fast FP unit, we can never exceed 1.67× speedup — the remaining 60% is the hard limit.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Architecture vs. Organization

| Aspect | Architecture (Logical) | Organization (Physical) |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Set | Defined (RISC/CISC) | Implemented in hardware |
| Addressing Modes | 5, 8, 12 modes? | How each mode is realized |
| Data Types | byte, word, float | 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit buses |
| Interrupts | Mechanism defined | Daisy-chain or vectored? |
| Memory | Logical address space | Cache size, associativity |
| I/O | Memory-mapped or isolated? | Programmed, DMA, or channel? |
| Visibility | **Visible** to programmer | **Transparent** to programmer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If a question says *"Is the use of cache memory architectural or organizational?"* — the answer is **organizational** (it's a performance optimization invisible to the programmer).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Basic Computer (Von Neumann Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU - Central Processing Unit]
    ALU[ALU - Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    CU[Control Unit]
    REG[Register File - PC, IR, MAR, MBR, AC]
    MEM[Main Memory - RAM and ROM]
    INPUT[Input Devices - Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner]
    OUTPUT[Output Devices - Monitor, Printer]
    BUS[System Bus - Data, Address, Control Lines]

    CPU --> ALU
    CPU --> CU
    CPU --> REG
    CPU <--> BUS
    MEM <--> BUS
    INPUT --> BUS
    BUS --> OUTPUT

    ALU <--> REG
    CU <--> REG
    CU -.controls.-> BUS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows represent **data flow**.
- Dotted arrows represent **control signals**.
- Every block connects via the **System Bus** — the central nervous system.

---

## 4.2 Detailed CPU Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[PC - Program Counter]
    MAR[MAR - Memory Address Register]
    MBR[MBR - Memory Buffer Register]
    IR[IR - Instruction Register]
    AC[AC - Accumulator]
    DR[DR - Data Register]
    ALUU[ALU Core]
    CUU[Control Unit]
    DECODER[Instruction Decoder]
    SEQ[Sequencer and Timing]

    PC --> MAR
    MAR --> BUSADDR[Address Bus]
    BUSADDR --> MEMORY[External Memory]
    MEMORY --> BUSDATA[Data Bus]
    BUSDATA --> MBR
    MBR --> IR
    IR --> DECODER
    DECODER --> CUU
    CUU --> SEQ
    SEQ -.control signals.-> ALUU
    AC --> ALUU
    DR --> ALUU
    ALUU --> AC
    SEQ -.controls.-> BUSADDR
    SEQ -.controls.-> BUSDATA
```

**Key register roles:**
| Register | Function |
|---|---|
| **PC** | Holds address of *next* instruction to fetch |
| **MAR** | Buffer that drives the *address bus* |
| **MBR** | Buffer that holds data *coming in/going out* on data bus |
| **IR** | Holds the *currently executing* instruction |
| **AC** | Primary accumulator for ALU operations |
| **DR** | Holds operand data for ALU |

---

## 4.3 Modified Harvard Architecture (Modern CPUs)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CORE[CPU Core - Fetch and Decode Unit]
    I1[L1 Instruction Cache]
    D1[L1 Data Cache]
    L2[Unified L2 Cache]
    L3[Shared L3 Cache]
    RAM[Main Memory - DDR DRAM]

    CORE <--> I1
    CORE <--> D1
    I1 <--> L2
    D1 <--> L2
    L2 <--> L3
    L3 <--> RAM
```

**Interpretation:** Although main memory is unified (Von Neumann), the *Level-1 cache is split* into separate I-cache and D-cache, giving the **parallelism benefit of Harvard** at the cache level — a pragmatic hybrid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Computer Architecture and Computer Organization. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Computer Architecture refers to the *attributes* of a computer system visible to the programmer — such as the **instruction set, addressing modes, data types, and I/O mechanisms** — that define its functional behavior. Example: *Use of RISC vs. CISC instruction set philosophy*.

Computer Organization refers to the *operational units* and their interconnections that implement the architecture — such as **control signals, bus structures, memory technology, and clocking schemes** — that are transparent to the user. Example: *Use of a 3-bus internal structure with separate address and data paths*.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition of each, 1 for example)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the Von Neumann bottleneck with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
In a **Von Neumann architecture**, both instructions and data share a *single memory* and are accessed via a *single bus*. Since the bus can carry only one type of information (instruction OR data) at a time, the CPU must **idle** while data is being fetched — and vice versa. This sequential, shared-path access limits the throughput of the system, and is called the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.

**Remedies:** Harvard architecture, separate caches for instructions and data, wider bus widths, and pre-fetch queues.

*(3 marks — 1 for concept, 1 for diagram description, 1 for remedy)*

---

## Part B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the basic operational concept of a computer with a **block diagram** showing the fetch-decode-execute cycle. List the **five functional units** and state the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a **clock rate of 3 GHz**. A benchmark program contains:
- 20% integer ALU instructions (CPI = 1)
- 40% floating-point instructions (CPI = 4)
- 30% memory load/store (CPI = 5)
- 10% branch instructions (CPI = 2)

Calculate: (i) Average CPI, (ii) CPU execution time for $10^9$ instructions, (iii) MIPS rating, (iv) MFLOPS (assuming FP instructions are the only floating-point operations). **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
    +-------+        +---------+       +-----------+
    | INPUT |  --->  |  MEMORY |  <--> |   CPU     |
    +-------+        | (RAM,ROM)|       | (ALU+CU+  |
                     +---------+       |  Registers)|
                          ^            +-----------+
                          |                 ^
                          v                 |
                     +---------+            |
                     | OUTPUT  |  <--------
                     +---------+
                        (Connected via SYSTEM BUS)
```

**Five Functional Units (3 marks):**

1. **Input Unit** — Translates external data (from keyboard/mouse) into binary form for processing.
2. **Memory Unit** — Stores programs and data; provides fast access to active information. (Primary: RAM/ROM; Secondary: HDD/SSD)
3. **ALU** — Performs all arithmetic and logical computations on data fetched from memory/registers.
4. **Control Unit** — Directs the flow of data and instructions; interprets opcodes and generates control signals.
5. **Output Unit** — Converts binary results into human-readable form (monitor, printer).

**Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle (2 marks):**
1. **Fetch:** PC content placed on address bus → Memory returns instruction on data bus → Stored in IR.
2. **Decode:** CU examines opcode in IR, determines operation and required operands.
3. **Execute:** ALU performs the operation; result is stored in AC or memory.
4. **PC Increment:** PC ← PC + 1 (or branch target). Cycle repeats.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Average CPI (2 marks):**

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = (0.20 \times 1) + (0.40 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 2)
$$

$$
\overline{\text{CPI}} = 0.20 + 1.60 + 1.50 + 0.20 = 3.50
$$

**(ii) CPU Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{cpu} = \frac{\text{IC} \times \overline{\text{CPI}}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{10^9 \times 3.50}{3 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{cpu} = \frac{3.50}{3} = 1.1667 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iii) MIPS Rating (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{3.50 \times 10^6} = 857.14 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(iv) MFLOPS (1.5 marks):**

Number of FP instructions = $0.40 \times 10^9 = 4 \times 10^8$

$$
\text{MFLOPS} = \frac{4 \times 10^8}{1.1667 \times 10^6} = 342.86 \text{ MFLOPS}
$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Harvard architecture** and contrast it with the Von Neumann architecture. State two real-world examples where Harvard is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program runs in **10 seconds** on a processor. **30%** of the execution time is spent in a function `compute()`. We optimize `compute()` and make it run **4× faster**. Using **Amdahl's Law**, compute:
(i) Overall speedup achieved.
(ii) Maximum possible speedup if `compute()` were infinitely fast.
(iii) If we now also speed up another 20% portion by 2×, what is the new total speedup? **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
```
                +-------------+
                |             |
   IMemory ---> | CPU Core    |  <--- DMemory
   (Inst Bus)   |             |  (Data Bus)
                +-------------+
                  ^       ^
                  |       |
              I-Bus    D-Bus  (Separate physical paths)
```

**Key Features of Harvard (3 marks):**
- **Two separate memories:** Instruction Memory (IM) and Data Memory (DM).
- **Two separate buses:** Instruction bus and Data bus (each with its own address + data lines).
- **Parallel access:** CPU can fetch the *next instruction* and read *current operand data* simultaneously.
- **Eliminates the Von Neumann bottleneck.**
- **Disadvantage:** More complex hardware, higher cost.

**Real-World Examples (2 marks):**
1. **DSP chips** (e.g., Texas Instruments TMS320 series) — used in audio/signal processing where instruction and data throughput must be high and parallel.
2. **Microcontrollers** (e.g., 8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M) — used in embedded systems where deterministic real-time execution is critical.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Overall Speedup (3 marks):**

Given: $f = 0.30$, $s = 4$

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.075} = \frac{1}{0.775}
$$

$$
S = 1.2903 \approx 1.29
$$

**(ii) Maximum Speedup (2 marks):**

As $s \to \infty$:

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.43
$$

**(iii) Sequential Speedup Application (2 marks):**

Apply Amdahl's Law *twice* or compute the combined un-optimized fraction:
- New optimized fraction: $f_{new} = 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.50$
- Average speedup $s_{new}$: weighted by time = $\frac{0.30 \times 4 + 0.20 \times 2}{0.50} = \frac{1.20 + 0.40}{0.50} = 3.2$

$$
S_{total} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.50) + \frac{0.50}{3.2}} = \frac{1}{0.50 + 0.15625} = \frac{1}{0.65625}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 1.524 \approx 1.52
$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MIPS with execution speed:** A higher MIPS does NOT always mean a faster processor — instruction complexity differs (RISC vs CISC).
> 2. **Wrong units in CPU Time:** Mixing nanoseconds and seconds, or omitting $\times 10^6$ in MIPS denominator.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law mis-application:** Using $(1 - f) - f/s$ instead of the correct form. *Remember the denominator is sum, not difference.*
> 4. **Von Neumann vs Harvard confusion:** Modern CPUs are *Modified Harvard* — do not state "all CPUs are Von Neumann" or "all CPUs are Harvard."
> 5. **Forgetting to show derivation steps** in Part B sub-questions — partial credit is awarded for intermediate steps; a single number without work = zero.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture** = what the system does (programmer-visible). **Organization** = how it does it (hardware-implementation).
- **Von Neumann** uses a single shared memory and bus → *Von Neumann Bottleneck*.
- **Harvard** uses separate memory and bus for instructions and data → parallel fetch.
- **Modified Harvard** is the modern standard (split L1 cache, unified main memory).
- The **System Bus** = **Data Bus** (bidirectional, $n$ bits) + **Address Bus** (unidirectional, $k$ bits, $2^k$ addressable locations) + **Control Bus** (Read/Write/Interrupt signals).
- **CPU Time** = $\text{IC} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clk} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI} / f_{clk}$.
- **MIPS** = $f_{clk} / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$ — measures instruction throughput, not actual workload speed.
- **Amdahl's Law** = $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/s]$ — quantifies the *ceiling* of speedup from any single-component optimization.
- **Registers** to memorize: **PC, MAR, MBR, IR, AC, DR** — each has a specific role in the fetch–execute cycle.
- **Performance optimization hierarchy:** Lower IC (better ISA/compiler) → Lower CPI (pipelining/cache) → Higher clock (technology). All three appear in the master equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Module 1 questions almost always test (a) one theory concept (architecture vs organization / Von Neumann / Harvard / Bus structure) and (b) one numerical on the **performance equation** or **Amdahl's Law**. Practice both until they're second nature!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Instruction Cycle

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instruction Cycle** is the complete sequence of micro-operations performed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to fetch, decode, execute, and store the result of a single machine-language instruction. It is the fundamental operational heartbeat of every stored-program computer.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, the **Instruction Cycle** is decomposed into four canonical **sub-cycles**:

1. **Fetch Cycle** – Retrieves the next instruction from main memory.
2. **Indirect Cycle** – Resolves effective address if the addressing mode is indirect.
3. **Execute Cycle** – Performs the arithmetic, logical, or control operation specified by the opcode.
4. **Interrupt Cycle** – Handles external interrupt requests and updates the Program Counter (PC).

The cycle is orchestrated by the **Control Unit (CU)** using a sequence of precisely timed **control signals** and **micro-operations** expressed in **Register Transfer Notation (RTN)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Chef Following a Recipe Book**
>
> Imagine a chef in a kitchen:
> - The **recipe book** lying on the counter is the **Main Memory** containing all instructions and data.
> - The chef's **finger pointing at the current step** is the **Program Counter (PC)**.
> - The **page the chef is reading** is the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
> - The chef's **hands performing actions** (chopping, stirring) are the **ALU and Execution Unit**.
> - The **timer and heat controls** are the **Control Unit generating timing signals**.
>
> **Step 1 (Fetch):** The chef turns the page his finger points to (reads the next instruction).
> **Step 2 (Decode):** The chef interprets what the step says (e.g., "add 2 eggs").
> **Step 3 (Operand Fetch / Indirect):** If the step says "look at page 42 for details", the chef flips to that page (indirect addressing).
> **Step 4 (Execute):** The chef performs the action (cracks 2 eggs into the bowl).
> **Step 5 (Store):** The result is in the bowl (or stored in a register).
> **Step 6 (Interrupt):** If the doorbell rings (an interrupt), the chef notes where he stopped, attends to the door, then returns to the exact same step.
>
> This continuous, repetitive process is the **Instruction Cycle**.

### Key Registers Involved (Standard KTU Notation)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard KTU 2024 syllabus lists the following **fundamental CPU registers**:
>
> | Register | Full Name | Function |
> |---|---|---|
> | **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of the *next* instruction to be fetched |
> | **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the memory address for read/write operations |
> | **MBR (MDR)** | Memory Buffer Register | Temporarily stores data being transferred to/from memory |
> | **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the currently executing instruction |
> | **AC** | Accumulator | Primary register for ALU operands and results |
> | **AR** | Address Register | Holds operand address during indirect cycles |
> | **DR** | Data Register | Temporary data holding register |
> | **TR** | Temporary Register | Scratchpad register used by the control unit |
> | **INPR** | Input Register | Holds data from input device |
> | **OUTR** | Output Register | Holds data for output device |
> | **E (IEN, FGI, FGO)** | Flag/Enable Registers | IEN = Interrupt Enable, FGI/FGO = Input/Output Flags |

The standard **word size** assumed in the KTU PERAT414 syllabus is **16 bits**, with a **memory address space of 12 bits** (4096 words) and an **opcode field of 3 bits** (8 basic operations).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Four Sub-Cycles in Detail

The **Instruction Cycle** is hierarchical. Each complete cycle is a sequence of sub-cycles:

#### **A. Fetch Cycle (Always Mandatory)**

The fetch cycle is **invariant** for every instruction. It is the first three micro-operations executed regardless of the instruction type.

- **Step 1:** Load the address of the next instruction from PC into MAR.
- **Step 2:** Issue a *Memory Read* signal. Memory places the instruction on the data bus into MBR. Simultaneously, PC is incremented (to point to the *next* instruction for the following cycle).
- **Step 3:** Transfer the opcode portion of MBR into IR.
- **Step 4:** Decode the opcode in IR using the instruction decoder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The fetch cycle always takes exactly **3 clock cycles** (or *3 memory access micro-cycles*) in the standard Mano-style CPU. PC increment happens *in parallel* with the memory read, not in a separate cycle.

#### **B. Indirect Cycle (Conditional)**

This sub-cycle is executed **only if** the decoded instruction uses **indirect addressing**. Its sole purpose is to replace the operand address portion of IR with the *effective address* stored in memory at that operand address.

- **Step 1:** Transfer the address field of IR (bits 0–11) into AR.
- **Step 2:** Issue a *Memory Read* to fetch the effective address from memory location AR.
- **Step 3:** Transfer the effective address from MBR into AR (overwriting the original operand address).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Indirect"?** Because the instruction does not contain the operand's *direct* memory address. Instead, it contains the address of a memory location that *itself* contains the address. This is a *pointer dereference* operation.

#### **C. Execute Cycle (Instruction-Specific)**

The execute cycle is the **most variable** part of the instruction cycle. Its micro-operations depend entirely on the opcode. For example:

- For an **ADD** instruction: `AC ← AC + MBR`
- For a **STA (Store Accumulator)** instruction: `Memory[MAR] ← AC, SC ← 0`
- For a **BUN (Branch Unconditionally)** instruction: `PC ← AR, SC ← 0`
- For an **ISA (Increment Skip if Zero)** instruction: Increment MBR; if MBR = 0, skip next instruction (PC ← PC + 1).

The Control Unit uses a **sequence counter (SC)** to advance through micro-operations. After execution, SC is reset to 0 to begin fetching the next instruction.

#### **D. Interrupt Cycle (Conditional on IEN and Interrupt Signal)**

If the **Interrupt Enable flip-flop (IEN) = 1** AND an external interrupt request is present, the CPU performs the interrupt cycle.

- **Step 1:** Clear IEN to 0 (disable further interrupts during handling).
- **Step 2:** Store the return address (current PC value) into a reserved memory location, typically **memory location 0** (RTN: `M[0] ← PC`).
- **Step 3:** Insert the address of the *Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)* into PC, typically **from memory location 1** (RTN: `PC ← M[1]`).
- **Step 4:** IEN is re-enabled at the end of the ISR (RTN: `IEN ← 1`).
- **Step 5:** SC is reset to 0 to begin fetching the first instruction of the ISR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The interrupt cycle is what enables **multitasking, real-time I/O, and operating system preemptive scheduling**. Without it, the CPU would be unable to respond to external events in a timely manner.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **high-yield formula and notation sheet** for the Instruction Cycle. Master this for KTU ESE 2024 examinations.

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $t_0, t_1, \ldots, t_n$ | Discrete time instances (clock ticks) |
| $MAR \leftarrow PC$ | Transfer content of PC into MAR |
| $M[MAR] \leftarrow AC$ | Store AC into memory at address MAR |
| $SC \leftarrow 0$ | Reset Sequence Counter (start a new cycle) |
| $IEN \leftarrow 0$ | Disable interrupts |
| $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Increment Program Counter |

| Cycle | Number of Memory References | Mandatory? | Variable Length? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fetch** | 1 (Read) | **Yes** | No (always 3 micro-ops) |
| **Indirect** | 1 (Read) | No (only if indirect) | No (always 3 micro-ops) |
| **Execute** | 0, 1, or 2 (Read/Write) | **Yes** | **Yes (instruction-dependent)** |
| **Interrupt** | 2 (Write to M[0], Read from M[1]) | No (only if interrupt pending) | No (always 3 micro-ops) |

| Memory Address | Standard KTU Reserved Use |
|---|---|
| $M[0]$ | Stores **return address** during interrupt |
| $M[1]$ | Stores **ISR starting address** |
| $M[2]$ to $M[4095]$ | Available for user programs and data |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Instruction Cycle concept is foundational to **every** computing domain:
> - **Processor Design (VLSI):** Hardware engineers design pipelined instruction cycles (e.g., Intel x86, ARM Cortex) that overlap fetch-decode-execute of consecutive instructions to achieve instruction-level parallelism.
> - **Compiler Optimization:** Compilers reorder instructions to minimize pipeline stalls (hazards) that disrupt the instruction cycle.
> - **Operating Systems:** The interrupt cycle mechanism enables preemptive multitasking, device drivers, and system calls.
> - **Cybersecurity:** Return-oriented programming (ROP) attacks exploit the interrupt/return address save mechanism (writing to $M[0]$).
> - **Embedded Systems:** Real-time OS kernels use instruction cycle timing for deterministic scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

### Exhaustive Register Transfer Notation (RTN) for the Complete Instruction Cycle

The following is the **complete, line-by-line RTN** for the standard Mano-type basic computer as prescribed in the KTU PERAT414 syllabus. Every micro-operation, control signal, and register transfer is explained in exhaustive detail.

#### **A. Fetch Cycle — Full Derivation**

**Micro-operation 1 ($t_0$): Address Loading**

$$MAR \leftarrow PC$$

**Explanation:** The address of the next instruction to be executed is held in the **Program Counter (PC)**. The CPU transfers this address to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**, which is connected to the address bus. This makes the address available to the memory subsystem.

**Control signal asserted:** `Address load from PC` (sometimes denoted as $LD_{MAR}$).

---

**Micro-operation 2 ($t_1$): Memory Read + PC Increment**

$$MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$$
$$PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

**Explanation:** The CPU issues a *Memory Read* control signal. The memory subsystem reads the word at the address in $MAR$ and places it on the data bus. The CPU captures this word into the **Memory Buffer Register (MBR)**. Simultaneously, in the same clock cycle, the **PC is incremented by 1** so that it points to the *next sequential instruction* — this is critical because the CPU will need the next address at the *start* of the next fetch cycle.

**Control signals asserted:** `Read` (memory), $LD_{MBR}$, $INC_{PC}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is PC incremented in parallel with memory read?** Because both operations take one clock cycle. Doing them sequentially would waste a cycle. This is a key KTU exam point.

---

**Micro-operation 3 ($t_2$): Load IR**

$$IR \leftarrow MBR$$

**Explanation:** The full instruction word (which was just fetched into $MBR$) is transferred into the **Instruction Register (IR)**. The $MBR$ is then free to be reused for operand fetches in subsequent sub-cycles.

**Control signal asserted:** $LD_{IR}$.

---

**Micro-operation 4 ($t_3$): Decode**

$$D_0 \ldots D_7 \leftarrow \text{Decode } IR(12\text{-}14)$$
$$AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$$

**Explanation:** The **opcode bits** ($IR$ bits 12, 13, 14 in a 16-bit word) are sent to the **Instruction Decoder**, which activates the appropriate control lines. Simultaneously, the **address field** ($IR$ bits 0–11) is transferred to the **Address Register (AR)**. If the instruction uses direct addressing, $AR$ is the effective address; if indirect, the next sub-cycle (Indirect) will resolve it.

**Control signals asserted:** Decoder enable, $LD_{AR}$.

#### **B. Indirect Cycle — Full Derivation (Conditional)**

**Micro-operation ($t_4$): AR already loaded from $IR(0\text{-}11)$.** This was done in $t_3$ of the fetch cycle.

**Micro-operation ($t_5$): Memory Read for Effective Address**

$$MBR \leftarrow M[AR]$$

**Explanation:** Since the instruction uses indirect addressing, the value in $AR$ is *not* the operand's location — it is the location of the operand's *address*. A memory read is performed, and the effective address is loaded into $MBR$.

**Control signal asserted:** `Read`, $LD_{MBR}$.

**Micro-operation ($t_6$): Effective Address Transfer**

$$AR \leftarrow MBR$$

**Explanation:** The effective address is moved from $MBR$ to $AR$, replacing the indirect pointer. Now $AR$ holds the *true* address of the operand, ready for the execute cycle.

**Control signal asserted:** $LD_{AR}$.

#### **C. Execute Cycle — Worked Examples by Instruction Type**

The execute cycle is **opcode-dependent**. Below are the RTNs for the seven common instructions in the KTU PERAT414 syllabus:

| Instruction | Opcode | RTN | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AND** | $D_0$ | $D_0 T_4: DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $D_0 T_5: AC \leftarrow AC \wedge DR$, $D_0 T_6: SC \leftarrow 0$ | Logical AND of AC and memory word |
| **ADD** | $D_1$ | $D_1 T_4: DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $D_1 T_5: AC \leftarrow AC + DR$, $E \leftarrow C_{out}$, $D_1 T_6: SC \leftarrow 0$ | Binary addition; $E$ is the carry flip-flop |
| **LDA** | $D_2$ | $D_2 T_4: DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $D_2 T_5: AC \leftarrow DR$, $D_2 T_6: SC \leftarrow 0$ | Load AC from memory |
| **STA** | $D_3$ | $D_3 T_4: M[AR] \leftarrow AC$, $D_3 T_5: SC \leftarrow 0$ | Store AC into memory |
| **BUN** | $D_4$ | $D_4 T_4: PC \leftarrow AR$, $D_4 T_5: SC \leftarrow 0$ | Unconditional branch to AR |
| **BSA** | $D_5$ | $D_5 T_4: M[AR] \leftarrow PC, AR \leftarrow AR + 1$, $D_5 T_5: PC \leftarrow AR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Branch and save return address |
| **ISZ** | $D_6$ | $D_6 T_4: DR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $D_6 T_5: DR \leftarrow DR + 1$, $D_6 T_6: M[AR] \leftarrow DR$, if $DR=0$ then $PC \leftarrow PC+1$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | Increment memory, skip if zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $D_i T_j$ means "decoder output $i$ AND timing signal $T_j$" — the AND gate output that triggers the micro-operation. This is the standard hardwired control design pattern.

**Worked Example: ADD Instruction (Full Derivation)**

Given the instruction `ADD X` where $X$ is a direct address holding operand 5, and $AC$ currently contains 3.

- **Fetch (t0–t2):** $MAR \leftarrow PC$ (say 200), $MBR \leftarrow M[200]$ (the ADD instruction), $PC \leftarrow 201$, $IR \leftarrow MBR$ (now IR holds the ADD opcode + address 50), $D_1$ decoder line goes HIGH.
- **Decode (t3):** $AR \leftarrow 50$ (address of operand).
- **Execute (t4):** $DR \leftarrow M[50]$ → $DR = 5$.
- **Execute (t5):** $AC \leftarrow AC + DR = 3 + 5 = 8$. $E \leftarrow 0$ (no carry).
- **Execute (t6):** $SC \leftarrow 0$ (reset for next fetch).

**Total time:** 4 clock cycles (3 for fetch + 1 for execute — no indirect).

#### **D. Interrupt Cycle — Full Derivation**

**Micro-operation ($t_0'$): Check and Clear IEN**

$$IEN \leftarrow 0$$
$$R \leftarrow 1$$
