**Explanation:** If the $IEN$ flip-flop is HIGH and an interrupt request is pending (or just $R \leftarrow 1$ per simplified KTU notation), the CPU acknowledges the interrupt. It first *disables* further interrupts by clearing $IEN$ to 0 — this prevents nested interrupts from corrupting the return address.

---

**Micro-operation ($t_1'$): Save PC to Memory[0]**

$$MBR \leftarrow PC$$

**Explanation:** The current value of $PC$ (the address of the *next* instruction that was about to be executed) is loaded into $MBR$. This is the **return address** — the location we must come back to after the interrupt service routine (ISR) completes.

---

**Micro-operation ($t_2'$): Write Return Address to M[0]**

$$M[0] \leftarrow MBR$$

**Explanation:** The return address in $MBR$ is written to **memory location 0**, which is reserved by hardware convention for this purpose.

---

**Micro-operation ($t_3'$): Load ISR Address from M[1]**

$$PC \leftarrow M[1]$$

**Explanation:** The address of the first instruction of the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is fetched from **memory location 1** and loaded into $PC$. The CPU will now fetch its *next* instruction from this ISR address, beginning the service routine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU/Mano convention:** $M[0]$ = return address storage, $M[1]$ = ISR address pointer. The ISR itself typically ends with a `BUN 0` (indirect branch to the saved return address) instruction to resume the main program.

### Analytical Matrix: Cycle Time vs Instruction Type

| Instruction | Fetch | Indirect | Execute | Interrupt | Total Cycles (no interrupt) | Total Cycles (with interrupt) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct ADD | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 (or 3) | 6 | 9 |
| Indirect ADD | 3 | 3 | 3 | 0 (or 3) | 9 | 12 |
| Direct STA | 3 | 0 | 2 | 0 (or 3) | 5 | 8 |
| Indirect STA | 3 | 3 | 2 | 0 (or 3) | 8 | 11 |
| ISZ (no skip) | 3 | 0 | 4 | 0 (or 3) | 7 | 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state that the **fetch cycle is constant at 3 cycles**, while the **execute cycle is variable**. This is a guaranteed question in KTU ESE.

### Time Complexity Analysis

Let $T_m$ = memory access time, $T_c$ = clock period. For a basic computer with one memory access per micro-cycle:

$$T_{\text{instruction}} = (T_{\text{fetch}} + T_{\text{indirect}} + T_{\text{execute}} + T_{\text{interrupt}}) \times T_c$$

$$T_{\text{instruction}} = (3 + 3 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{indirect}} + T_{\text{exec}} + 3 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{interrupt}}) \times T_c$$

where $\mathbb{1}_{\text{condition}}$ is the **indicator function** (1 if condition true, 0 otherwise), and $T_{\text{exec}} \in \{2, 3, 4\}$ cycles depending on the instruction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Top-Level Instruction Cycle State Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **state transition diagram** of the complete instruction cycle, including all four sub-cycles and decision branches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([Start of CPU Power On]) --> A2[PC is loaded with starting address]
    A2 --> A3[Fetch Cycle Begins]
    A3 --> A4{Interrupt Pending AND IEN is 1}
    A4 -->|Yes| A5[Interrupt Cycle: Save PC to M 0, Load ISR addr from M 1]
    A5 --> A3
    A4 -->|No| A6[Decode IR opcode]
    A6 --> A7{Is Addressing Mode Indirect}
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Indirect Cycle: Read effective address from M AR into AR]
    A8 --> A9
    A7 -->|No| A9[Execute Cycle based on opcode]
    A9 --> A10[Perform ALU operation or memory transfer]
    A10 --> A11[Reset Sequence Counter SC to 0]
    A11 --> A3
```

### Diagram 2: CPU–Memory Interaction Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **register-level data paths** involved in the fetch and execute cycles. Arrows indicate data flow during a typical instruction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU [Central Processing Unit]
        PC[PC Program Counter 16 bit]
        AR[AR Address Register 12 bit]
        IR[IR Instruction Register 16 bit]
        AC[AC Accumulator 16 bit]
        DR[DR Data Register 16 bit]
        SC[SC Sequence Counter 4 bit]
        CU[Control Unit generates timing signals]
    end
    subgraph MEM [Main Memory 4096 words x 16 bit]
        M0[M Address 0 Return Address Slot]
        M1[M Address 1 ISR Pointer Slot]
        MDATA[M Address AR or PC Operand Slot]
    end
    PC -->|MAR receives PC| MAR[MAR Memory Address Register]
    MAR -->|Address bus 12 bit| MDATA
    MDATA -->|Data bus 16 bit| MBR[MBR Memory Buffer Register]
    MBR --> IR
    MBR --> DR
    IR -->|Opcode bits 12 to 14| CU
    IR -->|Address bits 0 to 11| AR
    AR --> MAR
    DR --> AC
    AC --> MBR
    PC --> M0
    M1 --> PC
    CU -->|Control signals| PC
    CU --> AR
    CU --> IR
    CU --> AC
    CU --> DR
    CU --> SC
```

### Diagram 3: Detailed Micro-Operation Sequence Timing Diagram

The following diagram shows the **temporal sequence of micro-operations** during one full instruction cycle, including the parallel PC increment and memory read.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Clock t0: MAR receives PC address] --> B2[Clock t1 in parallel: MBR receives Memory M AR AND PC receives PC plus 1]
    B2 --> B3[Clock t2: IR receives MBR instruction word]
    B3 --> B4[Clock t3: Decode IR opcode and AR receives IR 0 to 11]
    B4 --> B5{Indirect addressing mode}
    B5 -->|Yes| B6[Clock t4: AR already has address]
    B6 --> B7[Clock t5: MBR receives M AR effective address]
    B7 --> B8[Clock t6: AR receives MBR true operand address]
    B8 --> B9
    B5 -->|No| B9[Execute Cycle opcode dependent]
    B9 --> B10[Clock t exec: ALU operation or memory write]
    B10 --> B11[Clock t end: SC reset to 0]
    B11 --> B12[Loop back to Clock t0 for next instruction]
```

### Diagram 4: Memory Layout Convention (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M0[Address 0: Return Address Save Slot]
    M1[Address 1: ISR Entry Point Slot]
    M2[Address 2: First User Instruction]
    M3[Address 3 to 4095: Program and Data Space]
    M0 --- M1
    M1 --- M2
    M2 --- M3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The reservation of $M[0]$ and $M[1]$ for interrupt handling is a **hardware convention** in the Mano-type basic computer. In real processors (Intel, ARM), the interrupt mechanism uses a **stack** in main memory and a **vector table** (e.g., x86 IDT, ARM Vector Table), but the principle is identical.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** Define the term *Instruction Cycle*. List and briefly explain its four basic sub-cycles. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Instruction Cycle** is the complete sequence of micro-operations performed by the CPU to fetch, decode, execute, and store the result of a single machine instruction (1 mark).

The four basic sub-cycles are:

1. **Fetch Cycle:** Retrieves the next instruction from main memory into the IR (1 mark).
2. **Indirect Cycle:** Resolves the effective operand address for indirect-addressing instructions (0.5 mark).
3. **Execute Cycle:** Performs the operation specified by the opcode (0.5 mark).
4. **Interrupt Cycle:** Handles external interrupt requests and transfers control to the ISR (0.5 mark — partial credit if student names only 3 sub-cycles).

---

**Question 2.** Differentiate between the **Fetch Cycle** and the **Execute Cycle** of the instruction cycle. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Fetch Cycle | Execute Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Retrieves the instruction word from memory | Performs the operation defined by the opcode |
| **Memory Access** | Always one memory **read** | Depends on instruction (0, 1, or 2 accesses) |
| **Duration** | **Fixed** (3 micro-operations) | **Variable** (2–4 micro-operations) |
| **Registers Used** | PC, MAR, MBR, IR | IR, AR, DR, AC, MBR, Memory |
| **Mandatory?** | **Yes, always executed** | **Yes, always executed** (after fetch) |
| **Uniformity** | **Same** for every instruction | **Different** for each instruction (1.5 marks for the table; 1.5 marks for explanatory bullet points). |

---

### Part B: Extended-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Do NOT skip the explanation of PC increment timing.** Many students write $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ as a separate step *after* memory read, losing 2 marks. The correct RTN has PC increment **in parallel** with MBR load.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the Indirect cycle with the Execute cycle.** The Indirect cycle only *fetches the effective address*; the actual operand fetch and operation happen in Execute.
> 3. **Always mention reserved memory locations** $M[0]$ and $M[1]$ when describing interrupts — failure to do so costs 1 mark.
> 4. **In diagrams, label ALL registers and ALL data flows.** A block diagram without arrows and register names gets only 3 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Mention the Sequence Counter (SC)** when describing cycle progression — it controls the micro-operation sequencing.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal organization of a basic computer showing all the registers, memory, and common bus system. List the functions of any seven registers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Fetch Cycle** in detail with the help of Register Transfer Statements. Explain why PC is incremented in the same clock cycle as the memory read. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram of Basic Computer (7 Marks):**

The basic computer consists of a **CPU** and a **Main Memory** connected by a **common bus system**. The CPU contains:
- A **Memory Address Register (MAR)** connected to the 12-bit address bus.
- A **Program Counter (PC)**, **Address Register (AR)**, and **Instruction Register (IR)** of 16 bits each.
- An **Accumulator (AC)** and **Data Register (DR)** of 16 bits each.
- An **Input Register (INPR)** and **Output Register (OUTR)** of 8 bits each.
- A **Sequence Counter (SC)** of 4 bits to control micro-operation timing.
- An **Interrupt Enable (IEN)** flip-flop, plus **Input/Output flags FGI and FGO**.
- A **Control Unit** with hardwired control logic (decoder + timing generator + sequence counter logic).

**Seven Register Functions (1 mark each — list any 7):**

1. **PC:** Holds address of next instruction to be fetched.
2. **AR:** Holds operand address during indirect cycles.
3. **IR:** Holds the current instruction being decoded/executed.
4. **AC:** Primary ALU operand and result register.
5. **DR:** Temporary data holding register for memory ↔ CPU transfers.
6. **SC:** 4-bit counter controlling which micro-operation is active.
7. **INPR:** Receives 8-bit input from peripheral device.
8. **OUTR:** Sends 8-bit output to peripheral device.
9. **IEN:** Master interrupt enable flip-flop.
10. **E:** Carry flip-flop for arithmetic operations.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks × 1 = 5 marks for register functions = 7 marks total.)*

**(b) Fetch Cycle in Detail (7 Marks):**

The **Fetch Cycle** is the first phase of every instruction cycle. It consists of the following register transfer statements:

| Time | RTN | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ | Address of next instruction loaded into MAR |
| $t_1$ | $MBR \leftarrow M[MAR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | Memory read into MBR; PC incremented in **parallel** |
| $t_2$ | $IR \leftarrow MBR$ | Instruction loaded into IR |
| $t_3$ | $D_0\ldots D_7 \leftarrow \text{Decode } IR(12\text{-}14), \quad AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | Opcode decoded; operand address loaded into AR |

**Why PC is incremented in the same clock cycle as memory read (3 marks):**

- The memory read operation ($MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$) and the PC increment operation ($PC \leftarrow PC + 1$) are **independent data transfers** — one involves the data bus (MBR), the other involves the PC register's internal incrementer.
- Both operations can be completed in **one clock cycle** by asserting both control signals ($Read$ and $INC_{PC}$) simultaneously.
- Doing them sequentially would require **two clock cycles**, wasting 50% of the fetch time. This is unacceptable in a performance-critical fetch path.
- The separation of address bus (for MAR) and data bus (for MBR) ensures there is no contention.
- Therefore, parallelism is exploited to make the fetch cycle complete in just **3 micro-operations** instead of 4.

*(Marks: 2 marks for the RTN table, 2 marks for explaining the parallel transfer, 3 marks for the justification = 7 marks total.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Indirect Cycle** and the **Execute Cycle** of the instruction cycle. For the **ADD** and **BUN** instructions, write the complete register transfer statements. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Interrupt Cycle** in detail. Why is the Interrupt Enable flip-flop (IEN) cleared at the start of the interrupt cycle? What is stored in $M[0]$ and $M[1]$ and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Indirect Cycle and Execute Cycle (7 Marks):**

**Indirect Cycle:** This cycle is executed only when the decoded instruction uses **indirect addressing** (indicated by bit $I = 1$ in the instruction format). Its purpose is to fetch the **effective address** of the operand from memory.

| Time | RTN | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $t_4$ | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | *(Done in t3 of fetch, but re-stated)* |
| $t_5$ | $MBR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | Read effective address from memory |
| $t_6$ | $AR \leftarrow MBR$ | AR now holds the **true** operand address |

**Execute Cycle:** Instruction-dependent. For two key instructions:

**ADD Instruction (Opcode $D_1 = 0001$ binary):**

| Time | RTN | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1 t_4$ | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | Fetch operand from memory into DR |
| $D_1 t_5$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR, \quad E \leftarrow C_{out}$ | Add DR to AC; update carry flip-flop E |
| $D_1 t_6$ | $SC \leftarrow 0$ | Reset sequence counter to begin next fetch |

**BUN Instruction (Branch Unconditionally, Opcode $D_4 = 0100$ binary):**

| Time | RTN | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $D_4 t_4$ | $PC \leftarrow AR$ | Load effective address into PC |
| $D_4 t_5$ | $SC \leftarrow 0$ | Reset sequence counter |

*(Marks: 2 marks for Indirect cycle, 2 marks for ADD RTN, 1 mark for BUN RTN, 2 marks for descriptions = 7 marks total.)*

**(b) Interrupt Cycle (7 Marks):**

The **Interrupt Cycle** is executed when the CPU detects an external interrupt request AND the **Interrupt Enable flip-flop (IEN) = 1**. Its purpose is to save the current program state and transfer control to the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**.

**Register Transfer Statements for Interrupt Cycle:**

| Time | RTN | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $t_0'$ | $R \leftarrow 1$ (or check IEN and interrupt) | Acknowledge interrupt |
| $t_1'$ | $MBR \leftarrow PC$ | Save current PC (return address) into MBR |
| $t_2'$ | $M[0] \leftarrow MBR$ | Write return address to reserved location 0 |
| $t_3'$ | $PC \leftarrow M[1]$ | Load ISR address from reserved location 1 |
| End of ISR | $IEN \leftarrow 1$ | Re-enable interrupts |
| End of ISR | $BUN \ 0$ (indirect) | Branch back to saved return address |

**Why is IEN cleared at the start of the interrupt cycle? (2 marks)**

- IEN is cleared to **disable further interrupts** during the execution of the current ISR.
- This prevents **nested interrupts** from overwriting $M[0]$ (the return address storage location) before the current ISR has had a chance to complete and return.
- Without this protection, an incoming higher-priority interrupt could corrupt the saved return address, causing the main program to jump to a wrong location upon return — a critical system bug.
- IEN is re-enabled at the end of the ISR, restoring normal interrupt handling capability.

**What is stored in $M[0]$ and $M[1]$? (2 marks)**

- **$M[0]$** stores the **return address** — the value of $PC$ at the moment the interrupt was acknowledged. This is the address of the *next* instruction in the main program that should resume after the ISR finishes.
- **$M[1]$** stores the **starting address of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. This is the address of the first instruction of the handler routine, which the CPU will load into $PC$ to begin servicing the interrupt.

**Why these specific locations?** They are **reserved by hardware convention** in the Mano-type basic computer design. Real-world processors use interrupt vector tables (x86 IDT, ARM Vector Table) which can handle many interrupt sources, but the principle of saving return address and loading handler address is universal.

*(Marks: 2 marks for RTN table, 2 marks for IEN explanation, 2 marks for M[0]/M[1] explanation, 1 mark for concluding statement = 7 marks total.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE**
>
> ✅ The **Instruction Cycle** = Fetch + (Indirect) + Execute + (Interrupt) sub-cycles.
>
> ✅ **Fetch Cycle is ALWAYS 3 micro-operations:** $t_0: MAR \leftarrow PC$, $t_1: MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ and $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ (parallel), $t_2: IR \leftarrow MBR$.
>
> ✅ **Decode happens at $t_3$** and is technically part of the fetch-decode phase, before the indirect/execute decision.
>
> ✅ **Indirect Cycle** is conditional (only if $I=1$) and is exactly 3 micro-operations.
>
> ✅ **Execute Cycle duration varies**: ADD = 3 cycles, STA = 2 cycles, BUN = 2 cycles, ISZ = 4 cycles.
>
> ✅ **Interrupt Cycle** uses reserved memory locations: $M[0]$ = return address, $M[1]$ = ISR address.
>
> ✅ **IEN is cleared** at the start of the interrupt cycle to prevent nested interrupts; **re-enabled** at the end of the ISR.
>
> ✅ **Sequence Counter (SC)** controls which micro-operation is currently active; reset to 0 at the end of execute or interrupt.
>
> ✅ **Carry flip-flop E** is updated only on arithmetic operations (ADD).
>
> ✅ **Standard KTU word size:** 16 bits, **opcode:** 3 bits, **address field:** 12 bits, **memory:** 4096 words.
>
> ✅ **The PC is incremented in parallel with memory read** — this is a frequently asked question in KTU ESE.
>
> ✅ **BSA (Branch and Save Return)** is the closest direct analog to a function call in the Mano basic computer.
>
> ✅ **ISZ** (Increment and Skip if Zero) is used to implement loops and is the only conditional skip instruction in the basic ISA.
>
> ✅ **FGI (Input Flag)** and **FGO (Output Flag)** are used for handshaking with I/O devices — they are part of the I/O interrupt mechanism.
>
> ✅ **Pipeline Note (Bonus):** Modern processors use pipelining to overlap fetch-decode-execute of consecutive instructions, achieving near-1-cycle-per-instruction throughput — but a single instruction still requires the full 3+ cycles *latency*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execution of a Complete Instruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Execution of a Complete Instruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Execution of a Complete Instruction** refers to the entire sequence of micro-operations and control signals that the Central Processing Unit (CPU) performs to retrieve, interpret, and act upon a single machine-level instruction. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework aligned with the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) model, this topic sits at the heart of **Course Outcome CO1** of PERAT414, requiring students to demonstrate the ability to *describe the basic organization of a computer and the execution of a complete instruction*.

In technical terms, a complete instruction cycle consists of three major phases:

1. **Fetch Phase** – The instruction is read from memory using the address held in the Program Counter (PC).
2. **Decode Phase** – The opcode portion of the instruction is interpreted by the Control Unit, and the required operands are located.
3. **Execute Phase** – The actual operation (arithmetic, logical, data transfer, or control) is performed, and results are stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The instruction cycle is the *fundamental building block* of all computer operations. Every program, regardless of complexity, is decomposed into this cycle.

The instruction is treated as a binary word composed of an **opcode** (operation code) and **operands** (data/addresses). The CPU coordinates with **memory** and **I/O devices** through a shared **system bus** consisting of address, data, and control lines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Order System

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as the CPU, a **waiter** as the bus, and the **kitchen recipe book** as the memory:

- The **head chef** (Control Unit) checks the order ticket — this is the **Fetch** step.
- The chef reads the dish name and ingredients — this is the **Decode** step.
- The chefs actually cook the dish using pans, ovens, and spices — this is the **Execute** step.

Just as a waiter carries one order at a time, the CPU fetches **one instruction at a time**, processes it completely, and only then moves to the next. The "order ticket" is analogous to the **Instruction Register (IR)**, and the "next order number" is the **Program Counter (PC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **CPU Clock** acts like the restaurant's ticking kitchen timer — it synchronizes every micro-operation (chopping, stirring, plating) into a predictable rhythm so that nothing collides.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Architectural Metrics

The following standard parameters govern instruction execution and are heavily tested in KTU examinations:

- **Clock Frequency (f):** Typically ranges from **1 GHz to 5 GHz** in modern processors.
- **Clock Period (T):** The reciprocal of frequency, where **T = 1/f**.
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** Average number of clock cycles needed per instruction.
- **Memory Access Time:** Typically **5 ns to 100 ns** depending on the memory hierarchy.
- **Word Length:** Commonly **32 bits or 64 bits** in modern architectures.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, you will frequently assume a single-bus CPU organization where the data path is shared among all registers, memory, and the ALU. This simplifies the timing analysis considerably.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases — Deep Dive

### Phase 1: The Fetch Cycle

The fetch cycle is **identical for every instruction** regardless of its type. This uniformity is what makes pipelining possible in advanced architectures. The fetch micro-operations are:

- The **PC** sends its contents to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**.
- A **Read** signal is asserted to the memory.
- The memory places the instruction on the data bus, which is captured into the **Memory Data Register (MDR)**.
- The PC is **incremented** to point to the next instruction (assuming instructions are sequentially placed).
- The contents of MDR are transferred to the **Instruction Register (IR)**.

### Phase 2: The Decode Cycle

The decode phase is generally **overlapped with the fetch** in modern designs but conceptually involves:

- The **opcode** field of the IR is routed to the **Control Unit**.
- The **Control Unit** activates the appropriate control signals.
- The **operand fields** are extracted (register numbers, immediate values, or memory addresses).

### Phase 3: The Execute Cycle

This phase **varies significantly** based on the instruction class. Common instruction categories include:

- **Register-Register Operations** (e.g., ADD R1, R2) — Operands already in CPU registers.
- **Register-Memory Operations** (e.g., LOAD R1, addr) — One operand in memory.
- **Memory-Memory Operations** — Both operands in memory (rare in modern RISC).
- **Control Flow Operations** (e.g., JUMP, BRANCH) — Modify the PC.

## 2.2 Register Transfer Notation (RTN)

KTU questions frequently use **RTN** to describe micro-operations. The basic syntax is:

$$
\text{Register}_{\text{out}}, \text{Register}_{\text{in}}
$$

A more formal conditional form:

$$
P: \text{Register}_2 \leftarrow \text{Register}_1
$$

Where **P** is a control condition (clock pulse or control signal). Common control signals include:

- **Rin** / **Rout** — Register input/output enable
- **MARin**, **MDRin**, **IRin**, **PCin**, **PCout** — Special register controls
- **Read**, **Write** — Memory control
- **WMFC** — Wait for Memory Function Complete
- **SelectY**, **Add**, **Sub** — ALU controls
- **End** — Terminate execution

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $f$ in Hz, $T$ in seconds | Determines the basic timing unit of CPU |
| Instruction Execution Time | $T_{exec} = N \cdot T \cdot CPI$ | $N$ = instruction count, $CPI$ = cycles/instruction | Performance estimation |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \cdot CPI}{f}$ | All dimensionless except $f$ (Hz) | Used in benchmarking |
| Memory Wait Cycles | $W = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{mem}}{T_{clk}} \right\rceil$ | $T_{mem}$ = memory access time, $T_{clk}$ = clock period | Calculates stalls for slow memory |
| Effective Address (Direct) | $EA = \text{Address Field}$ | — | Used in direct addressing mode |
| Effective Address (Indirect) | $EA = M[\text{Address Field}]$ | $M[\cdot]$ denotes memory contents | Used in indirect addressing mode |
| Effective Address (Indexed) | $EA = R_{index} + \text{Offset}$ | — | Used in array processing |
| PC Update | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | — | Sequential flow control |
| Branch Target | $PC \leftarrow PC + \text{Offset}$ | — | Conditional/unconditional branches |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU formula table, all set memberships and absolute values are denoted using $\vert$ for clarity without breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of instruction execution is the foundation of:

- **Processor Design** — Every commercial CPU (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex) is engineered around optimizing the instruction cycle.
- **Compiler Optimization** — Compilers reorder instructions to minimize fetch-execute stalls.
- **Performance Engineering** — Benchmarks like SPEC CPU measure millions of instruction cycles per second.
- **Embedded Systems** — Microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR) are designed with minimal CPI for predictable real-time behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern processors use **pipelining** to overlap the fetch of instruction $N+1$ with the decode of instruction $N$, achieving an effective CPI approaching 1. KTU Module 4 covers this in greater depth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Complete Execution of `ADD R1, R2`

Consider a **single-bus CPU organization** with the following components: PC, MAR, MDR, IR, R0–R3 general-purpose registers, Y and Z ALU temporary registers, and a single shared internal bus.

**Instruction Semantics:** Add the contents of R2 to R1, store the result back in R1. Operands are read directly from the register file (no memory access required for operands).

### Step 1 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t0

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & PC_{out}, \ MAR_{in}, \ \text{Read}, \ \text{Select4}, \ \text{Add}, \ Z_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{PC contents placed on bus} \rightarrow \text{MAR} \\
& \text{ALU computes } Z = PC + 4 \text{ (for 32-bit alignment)} \\
& \text{Read signal sent to memory}
\end{aligned}
$$

The PC is connected to the bus via $PC_{out}$. The MAR captures the address via $MAR_{in}$. The ALU is set up with **Select4** to add the constant 4 (assuming 32-bit instructions) to the PC, and the result is stored in **Z** via $Z_{in}$.

### Step 2 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t1

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & Z_{out}, \ PC_{in}, \ Y_{in}, \ \text{WMFC} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{Z contents (PC+4)} \rightarrow \text{PC} \\
& \text{Z contents also transferred to Y for later use} \\
& \text{CPU waits for memory function to complete}
\end{aligned}
$$

This step **updates the PC** so that it points to the next instruction in sequence. The **WMFC (Wait for Memory Function Complete)** signal stalls the processor until the memory read operation finishes.

### Step 3 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t2

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & MDR_{outE}, \ IR_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{Instruction word from MDR} \rightarrow \text{IR}
\end{aligned}
$$

The instruction is now safely in the IR. The **opcode bits** are decoded by the Control Unit to determine that this is an **ADD register-register** operation. The **source register** (R1) and **destination register** (R2) fields are extracted from the instruction encoding.

### Step 4 — Execute Phase, Clock Cycle t3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & R1_{out}, \ Y_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{Contents of R1} \rightarrow Y \text{ (ALU input register)}
\end{aligned}
$$

R1's contents are placed on the internal bus and loaded into the **Y register**, which serves as one input to the ALU.

### Step 5 — Execute Phase, Clock Cycle t4

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & R2_{out}, \ \text{SelectY}, \ \text{Add}, \ Z_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{ALU computes } Z = Y + R2 = R1 + R2
\end{aligned}
$$

R2's contents are placed on the bus. The ALU is configured with **SelectY** (to use Y as the first operand) and **Add** control. The sum is stored in the **Z register**.

### Step 6 — Execute Phase, Clock Cycle t5

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & Z_{out}, \ R1_{in}, \ \text{End} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{Contents of Z} \rightarrow R1 \\
& \text{Execution terminates}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sum is finally written back to R1. The **End** signal marks the completion of this instruction's execution cycle.

### Total Cycle Count for `ADD R1, R2`:

$$
T_{total} = 6 \text{ clock cycles}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Complete Execution of `ADD (R3), R1`

This is a **memory-reference instruction** that adds the contents of a memory location (whose address is held in R3) to R1, then stores the result in R1.

**Instruction Format (assumed):** `[Opcode \vert R3 \vert R1]` where R3 holds the memory address.

### Step 1 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t0

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & PC_{out}, \ MAR_{in}, \ \text{Read}, \ \text{Select4}, \ \text{Add}, \ Z_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{PC} \rightarrow \text{MAR}, \quad Z = PC + 4, \quad \text{Memory Read initiated}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t1

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & Z_{out}, \ PC_{in}, \ Y_{in}, \ \text{WMFC} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & PC \leftarrow PC + 4, \quad Y \leftarrow PC + 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Fetch Phase, Clock Cycle t2

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & MDR_{outE}, \ IR_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{IR} \leftarrow \text{Instruction word}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Effective Address Calculation, Clock Cycle t3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & R3_{out}, \ MAR_{in}, \ \text{Read}, \ R1_{out}, \ \text{SelectY}, \ \text{Add}, \ Z_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{MAR} \leftarrow R3, \quad \text{Memory Read initiated} \\
& Z = R1 + R3 \ \text{(preparing for potential indirect addressing or just Y load)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This step loads R3 (the address) into MAR to start a memory read for the operand.

### Step 5 — Operand Read, Clock Cycle t4

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & Z_{out}, \ R1_{in}, \ \text{WMFC} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & \text{Wait for memory to place operand on bus}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In some textbook formulations, R1 is also preserved via Z in this step. The exact signal set depends on the assumed instruction format.

### Step 6 — ALU Operation, Clock Cycle t5

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & MDR_{outE}, \ \text{SelectY}, \ \text{Add}, \ Z_{in} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & Z = R1 + M[R3]
\end{aligned}
$$

The operand from memory (now in MDR) is added to R1 (preserved in Y from a prior cycle) and the sum is stored in Z.

### Step 7 — Result Storage, Clock Cycle t6

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Control signals:} \quad & Z_{out}, \ R1_{in}, \ \text{End} \\
\text{Action:} \quad & R1 \leftarrow Z = R1 + M[R3], \quad \text{Execution ends}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Total Cycle Count for `ADD (R3), R1`:

$$
T_{total} = 7 \text{ clock cycles}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Instruction Types

| Instruction Type | Example | Fetch Cycles | Execute Cycles | Total Cycles | Memory Accesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register-Register | `ADD R1, R2` | 3 | 3 | **6** | 1 (instruction) |
| Register-Memory (Load) | `LOAD R1, (R3)` | 3 | 3 | **6** | 2 (instruction + data) |
| Register-Memory (Store) | `STORE R1, (R3)` | 3 | 4 | **7** | 2 (instruction + data) |
| Register-Memory (Add) | `ADD (R3), R1` | 3 | 4 | **7** | 2 (instruction + data) |
| Branch | `JUMP offset` | 3 | 1 | **4** | 1 (instruction) |
| Conditional Branch | `BZ R1, offset` | 3 | 2 | **5** | 1 (instruction) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The matrix above is *examination gold*. Questions often ask you to derive the number of cycles for a *given* instruction by tracing the steps. Always start by drawing a table with three columns: **Clock Cycle**, **Active Control Signals**, and **Micro-operation Performed**.

## 3.4 Derivation of CPU Time from Instruction Mix

Suppose a program has the following instruction mix (typical benchmark workload):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Register-Register instructions} &= N_{RR} = 1{,}000{,}000 \\
\text{Number of Load instructions} &= N_{L} = 300{,}000 \\
\text{Number of Store instructions} &= N_{S} = 200{,}000 \\
\text{Number of Branch instructions} &= N_{B} = 100{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume the following CPI for each class (from the analytical matrix above):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{RR} &= 6, \quad CPI_{L} = 6, \quad CPI_{S} = 7, \quad CPI_{B} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total CPU cycles required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cycles} &= N_{RR} \cdot CPI_{RR} + N_{L} \cdot CPI_{L} + N_{S} \cdot CPI_{S} + N_{B} \cdot CPI_{B} \\
&= (1{,}000{,}000 \cdot 6) + (300{,}000 \cdot 6) + (200{,}000 \cdot 7) + (100{,}000 \cdot 4) \\
&= 6{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}800{,}000 + 1{,}400{,}000 + 400{,}000 \\
&= 9{,}600{,}000 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective CPI (weighted average):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \frac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Total Instructions}} = \frac{9{,}600{,}000}{1{,}600{,}000} = 6.0 \text{ cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CPU Time at 2 GHz clock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \frac{\text{Total Cycles}}{f} = \frac{9{,}600{,}000}{2 \times 10^{9}} \\
&= 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} \\
&= 4.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation pattern is **routinely tested** in KTU end-semester exams under the CO2 (design performance metrics) outcome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Instruction Execution Cycle (Flowchart)

The following flowchart visualizes the complete instruction execution cycle, including the three primary phases and their internal micro-operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[PC out, MAR in, Read]
    B --> C[Z out, PC in, Y in, WMFC]
    C --> D[MDR out E, IR in]
    D --> E{Decode Opcode}
    E -->|Register ALU| F[R src out, Y in]
    F --> G[R dst out, SelectY, Add, Z in]
    G --> H[Z out, R dst in]
    H --> ZEND([END])
    E -->|Memory Reference| I[R addr out, MAR in, Read]
    I --> J[Wait for Memory WMFC]
    J --> K[MDR out E, ALU Op, Z in]
    K --> L[Z out, R dst in or MDR in]
    L --> ZEND
    E -->|Branch| M[Evaluate Condition]
    M -->|Taken| N[Z out, PC in]
    M -->|Not Taken| O[Increment PC only]
    N --> ZEND
    O --> ZEND
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Decode Opcode** decision block (node E) is the *only point* where the execution path diverges based on instruction type. This is the central insight that makes pipelining feasible.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Single-Bus CPU Organization (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram shows the internal organization of a typical single-bus CPU used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Program Counter PC] --> BUS((Internal Bus))
    MAR[Memory Address Register MAR] --> BUS
    MDR[Memory Data Register MDR] --> BUS
    IR[Instruction Register IR] --> BUS
    R0[Register R0] --> BUS
    R1[Register R1] --> BUS
    R2[Register R2] --> BUS
    R3[Register R3] --> BUS
    Y[Y Register] --> ALU[ALU]
    BUS --> Y
    Z[Z Register] --> BUS
    ALU --> Z
    BUS --> PC
    BUS --> MAR
    BUS --> MDR
    BUS --> IR
    BUS --> R0
    BUS --> R1
    BUS --> R2
    BUS --> R3
    MAR --> MEM[(Main Memory)]
    MDR <--> MEM
    CU[Control Unit] -.-> PC
    CU -.-> MAR
    CU -.-> MDR
    CU -.-> IR
    CU -.-> ALU
    CU -.-> R0
    CU -.-> R1
    CU -.-> R2
    CU -.-> R3
    CU -.-> MEM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **dashed arrows** from the Control Unit (CU) represent *control signal lines* (not data flow). The **solid arrows** represent the *data path*. The single shared **Internal Bus** means that only **one data transfer** can occur per clock cycle — this is why register-register ADD takes 6 cycles rather than 3.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Timing Sequence for `ADD R1, R2`

The following sequence diagram illustrates the temporal ordering of control signals for the `ADD R1, R2` example.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as Clock
    participant CU as Control Unit
    participant BUS as Internal Bus
    participant PC as PC
    participant MAR as MAR
    participant MEM as Memory
    participant MDR as MDR
    participant IR as IR
    participant R1 as R1
    participant R2 as R2
    participant Y as Y Reg
    participant Z as Z Reg

    CLK->>CU: t0 Tick
    CU->>PC: PCout
    CU->>MAR: MARin
    CU->>MEM: Read Signal
    Note over PC,MAR: MAR receives address from PC
    CLK->>CU: t1 Tick
    CU->>Z: Zout
    CU->>PC: PCin
    CU->>Y: Yin
    CU->>MEM: WMFC Wait
    Note over PC: PC updated to PC plus 4
    CLK->>CU: t2 Tick
    CU->>MDR: MDRoutE
    CU->>IR: IRin
    Note over IR: Instruction decoded as ADD R1 R2
    CLK->>CU: t3 Tick
    CU->>R1: R1out
    CU->>Y: Yin
    Note over Y: Y holds R1 contents
    CLK->>CU: t4 Tick
    CU->>R2: R2out
    CU->>Y: SelectY
    CU->>ALU: Add
    CU->>Z: Zin
    Note over Z: Z holds R1 plus R2
    CLK->>CU: t5 Tick
    CU->>Z: Zout
    CU->>R1: R1in
    CU->>CU: End
    Note over R1: R1 updated with sum
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, a **timing diagram** is often drawn as a Gantt-like chart with control signals on the Y-axis and clock cycles on the X-axis. A "high" pulse on a signal line indicates the signal is **active** during that clock cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the instruction cycle. List its three main phases with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

The **instruction cycle** is the complete set of micro-operations performed by the CPU to fetch, decode, and execute a single machine instruction.

The three main phases are:

1. **Fetch Phase:** The instruction is retrieved from memory using the address in the Program Counter and loaded into the Instruction Register.
2. **Decode Phase:** The Control Unit interprets the opcode field of the instruction to determine the required operation and identifies the operands.
3. **Execute Phase:** The actual operation is carried out by the ALU and associated data path, and the results are stored in the appropriate destination.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the role of the Program Counter (PC) and the Instruction Register (IR) during instruction execution?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Program Counter (PC):** It is a special-purpose register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched. After every instruction fetch, the PC is automatically incremented (typically by 4 for 32-bit instructions) so that the processor can proceed to the next instruction in sequence. It is also loaded with a new value during branch and jump instructions.

- **Instruction Register (IR):** It is a special-purpose register that holds the **currently fetched instruction word**. The Control Unit examines the opcode bits of the IR to decode the operation. The operand fields of the IR identify the source and destination registers or memory addresses involved in the operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Consider a single-bus CPU organization with registers PC, MAR, MDR, IR, R0–R3, Y, and Z. Trace the complete execution of the instruction `ADD (R3), R1`, listing all control signals activated in each clock cycle. Assume the instruction format contains the register fields R3 and R1, where R3 holds the memory address and R1 is the destination.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Draw the complete timing table with control signals and micro-operations.

**Model Solution:**

| Clock Cycle | Active Control Signals | Micro-operation Performed |
|---|---|---|
| t0 | $PC_{out}$, $MAR_{in}$, Read, Select4, Add, $Z_{in}$ | $MAR \leftarrow PC$; $Z \leftarrow PC + 4$; Memory Read initiated |
| t1 | $Z_{out}$, $PC_{in}$, $Y_{in}$, WMFC | $PC \leftarrow PC + 4$; $Y \leftarrow PC + 4$; Wait for memory |
| t2 | $MDR_{outE}$, $IR_{in}$ | $IR \leftarrow \text{Instruction word from MDR}$ |
| t3 | $R3_{out}$, $MAR_{in}$, Read, $R1_{out}$, $Y_{in}$, SelectY, Add, $Z_{in}$ | $MAR \leftarrow R3$; $Y \leftarrow R1$; $Z \leftarrow R1 + R3$ (auxiliary); Memory Read started |
| t4 | $Z_{out}$, $R1_{in}$, WMFC | $R1 \leftarrow R1 + R3$ (temporary save); Wait for memory operand |
| t5 | $MDR_{outE}$, SelectY, Add, $Z_{in}$ | $Z \leftarrow R1 + M[R3]$ |
| t6 | $Z_{out}$, $R1_{in}$, End | $R1 \leftarrow R1 + M[R3]$; Execution terminates |

**Total clock cycles required = 7**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Draw the block diagram of the single-bus CPU and explain the role of the WMFC signal.

**Model Solution:**

*(Refer to SECTION 4.2 for the block diagram.)*

**Role of WMFC (Wait for Memory Function Complete):**

The **WMFC** signal is a synchronization mechanism between the CPU and the main memory. Since memory operations are typically **slower** than CPU clock cycles, the CPU cannot immediately proceed after issuing a Read or Write signal. The WMFC signal:

1. **Stalls the processor pipeline** by inserting wait states until the memory operation is complete.
2. Ensures that **data integrity** is maintained — the CPU only reads valid data from the MDR after memory has placed it on the data bus.
3. Acts as a **handshaking protocol** in asynchronous memory systems.

The number of wait cycles inserted is calculated as:

$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{T_{mem}}{T_{clk}} \right\rceil
$$

For example, if $T_{mem} = 40$ ns and $T_{clk} = 10$ ns, then $W = 4$ wait cycles.

---

### Question B2 (Option B) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the complete execution of a three-address instruction `ADD R1, R2, R3` (adds R2 and R3, stores in R1) in a single-bus CPU. Also compute the total execution time assuming a 2 GHz clock.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Trace all micro-operations with control signals.

**Model Solution:**

| Clock Cycle | Active Control Signals | Micro-operation Performed |
|---|---|---|
| t0 | $PC_{out}$, $MAR_{in}$, Read, Select4, Add, $Z_{in}$ | $MAR \leftarrow PC$; $Z \leftarrow PC + 4$; Memory Read initiated |
| t1 | $Z_{out}$, $PC_{in}$, $Y_{in}$, WMFC | $PC \leftarrow PC + 4$; $Y \leftarrow PC + 4$; Wait for memory |
| t2 | $MDR_{outE}$, $IR_{in}$ | $IR \leftarrow \text{Instruction word}$ (decoded as ADD R1 R2 R3) |
| t3 | $R2_{out}$, $Y_{in}$ | $Y \leftarrow R2$ (load ALU first operand) |
| t4 | $R3_{out}$, SelectY, Add, $Z_{in}$ | $Z \leftarrow Y + R3 = R2 + R3$ |
| t5 | $Z_{out}$, $R1_{in}$, End | $R1 \leftarrow R2 + R3$; Execution terminates |

**Total clock cycles required = 6**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Calculate total execution time and effective CPI for a benchmark.

**Model Solution:**

**Execution Time per Instruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.5 \text{ ns per cycle} \\
T_{exec} &= 6 \text{ cycles} \times 0.5 \text{ ns/cycle} = 3.0 \text{ ns per instruction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For a benchmark with 10 million instructions (mixed types):**

Suppose the instruction mix gives an effective CPI of 5.4 cycles/instruction (typical of SPEC CPU benchmarks).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cycles} &= 10{,}000{,}000 \times 5.4 = 54{,}000{,}000 \text{ cycles} \\
T_{CPU} &= 54{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 \text{ ns} = 27 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup if CPI is reduced to 4.0 (e.g., via pipelining):**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{5.4}{4.0} = 1.35\times
$$

This represents a **35% performance improvement** purely from architectural enhancement, with no clock frequency change.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update the PC** — Many students omit the PC increment step during the fetch phase. The PC update is **mandatory** and worth 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing the WMFC signal** — The wait-for-memory-complete step is **not optional** in KTU problems. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing MDRout and MDRoutE** — `MDRout` puts the *entire* MDR on the bus (instruction or data), while `MDRoutE` is for the *external* data path. Using the wrong one shows incomplete understanding.
> 4. **Not specifying the End signal** — Every instruction execution **must terminate** with the End signal. Examiners deduct marks for incomplete traces.
> 5. **Mixing up SelectY and Add** — `SelectY` tells the ALU to use the Y register as input; `Add` specifies the operation. Both are required in ALU steps.
> 6. **Ignoring bus conflicts** — In a single-bus system, only ONE source can drive the bus per cycle. Listing two `out` signals in one cycle is **architecturally invalid**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PERAT414 Module 1:**

- ✅ The **instruction cycle** consists of three phases: **Fetch → Decode → Execute**.
- ✅ The **PC** always points to the **next** instruction to be fetched (not the current one).
- ✅ The **PC is incremented** during the fetch phase itself, typically by **4 bytes** for a 32-bit instruction set.
- ✅ The **fetch phase is identical** for all instructions; only the execute phase varies.
- ✅ The **Instruction Register (IR)** holds the currently executing instruction word.
- ✅ The **Memory Data Register (MDR)** acts as a buffer between the CPU and main memory.
- ✅ The **WMFC signal** is essential for synchronization with slow memory.
- ✅ The **single-bus organization** allows only **one data transfer per clock cycle**, which directly determines the CPI.
- ✅ The **ALU uses two temporary registers Y and Z** to hold operands and results, since the single bus cannot simultaneously provide both inputs.
- ✅ The **Add/Sub control signals** of the ALU determine the operation; **SelectY/Select4** determine the input source.
- ✅ The **End signal** terminates instruction execution and triggers the fetch of the next instruction.
- ✅ The total **execution time** is computed as: $T = (\text{Cycles per Instruction}) \times (\text{Clock Period})$.
- ✅ The **effective CPI** is the weighted average of CPIs across all instruction types in a program.
- ✅ Memory-reference instructions require **two memory accesses**: one for the instruction and one for the operand.
- ✅ Branch instructions are the **shortest** to execute (typically 4–5 cycles) because they do not require ALU operations on data.
- ✅ The **control unit** decodes the opcode and generates all timing signals — it is the "brain" orchestrating every micro-operation.
- ✅ Always use the **single-bus timing table format** (Clock Cycle, Control Signals, Micro-operation) for full marks in KTU board exams.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Execution of a Complete Instruction**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single Bus Organization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Single Bus Organization):**
> *Single Bus Organization is a CPU datapath architecture in which a single common set of wires (one internal bus) is shared by all major registers, the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), and the memory interface (MAR/MDR). At any given clock cycle, only ONE data transfer between two components can occur across this shared bus.*

In this organization, all registers (PC, AC, IR, GPRs, MAR, MDR, Y, Z) are connected to a **single internal CPU bus** through tri-state buffers or multiplexers. Since the bus can carry data from only one source at a time, multi-step operations like an ALU addition must be **sequenced over multiple clock cycles**:
- **Step 1:** Place operand 1 on the bus → load into a temporary register.
- **Step 2:** Place operand 2 on the bus → ALU performs operation with latched operand 1.
- **Step 3:** Store the result back.

### 1.2 Multiple Bus Organization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Multiple Bus Organization):**
> *Multiple Bus Organization is a CPU datapath architecture in which TWO or THREE independent internal buses are used in parallel to interconnect the registers, ALU, and memory. This permits simultaneous data movements in a single clock cycle, drastically reducing the number of micro-operations required per instruction.*

Common variants:
- **Two-Bus Organization** — Two buses (Bus A, Bus B) feed the two ALU inputs simultaneously.
- **Three-Bus Organization** — Three buses (Bus A, Bus B, Bus C) where two feed ALU inputs and the third captures the ALU output back into a destination register in the **same cycle**.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Highway Metaphor:**
> - **Single Bus** = a **single-lane bridge** between two towns. Only one car (data word) can cross at a time, so traffic must queue up.
> - **Two Bus** = a **two-lane bridge** — two cars can cross side-by-side.
> - **Three Bus** = a **three-lane bridge with an exit ramp** — two cars enter, processing happens, and one car exits the other side, **all in the same time unit**.

The *bus* is the "lane", the *clock cycle* is the "time unit", and the *register* is the "car". More buses = more parallel data flow = faster instruction throughput.

### 1.4 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Meaning | Standard Value |
|------|---------|----------------|
| **Bus Width** | Number of parallel wires in a bus | **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** |
| **Tri-state Buffer** | Switch that puts a register ON/OFF the bus | Logic states: **0, 1, High-Z (floating)** |
| **Clock Cycle Time** | Time for one micro-operation | Typically **few nanoseconds** (e.g., **0.5 ns – 5 ns**) |
| **Micro-operation** | Elementary register-transfer operation | Executed in **one clock cycle** |
| **GPR** | General Purpose Register (R0–R(n–1)) | Usually **8, 16, or 32 registers** |
| **ALU Operand Latch** | Temporary holding register inside ALU | One latch per ALU input port |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps — Single Bus Organization

For the instruction `ADD R1, R2, R3` (i.e., R2 + R3 → R1):

| Cycle | Micro-operation | Action |
|-------|----------------|--------|
| T1 | `R2out, Yin` | Place R2 on the bus; latch it into temporary register Y |
| T2 | `R3out, ADD, Zin` | Place R3 on the bus; ALU computes Y + R3; store in Z |
| T3 | `Zout, R1in` | Place result Z on bus; load into R1 |

**Total = 3 clock cycles** for one simple ADD.

### 2.2 Why the Single Bus Needs an Internal ALU Latch (Y)

The single bus forces the ALU to have a **latch on its first input** (commonly called **Y**). Without it, both ALU operands would have to appear on the bus *simultaneously* — impossible, since the bus has only one source at a time.

### 2.3 Operational Steps — Three-Bus Organization

For the same `ADD R1, R2, R3`:

| Cycle | Micro-operation | Action |
|-------|----------------|--------|
| T1 | `R2out_A, R3out_B, ADD, R1in_C` | R2 on Bus A, R3 on Bus B, ALU adds, result on Bus C → R1 |

**Total = 1 clock cycle** for one simple ADD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speedup Factor:** Three-bus organization is up to **3× faster** than single-bus for register-to-register arithmetic. The KTU board examiner typically tests this comparison directly.

### 2.4 Control Signal Anatomy

For a single-bus CPU, the control word for one micro-operation has the following fields:

$$\text{Control Word} = \text{SelA} \mid \text{SelB} \mid \text{SelY} \mid \text{OpCode} \mid \text{Reg/Imm}$$

- **SelA (Source register selector):** chooses which GPR drives the bus.
- **SelB (Destination register selector):** chooses which GPR latches from the bus.
- **SelY (Y-register control):** enables the ALU input latch.
- **OpCode:** selects the ALU function (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, etc.).
- **Reg/Imm:** selects between a register operand and an immediate value.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Bus width (data per transfer) | $W = n \text{ bits}$ | Determines one word size |
| Single-bus cycles for ADD | $N_{SB} = 3$ | cycles per operation |
| Two-bus cycles for ADD | $N_{TB} = 2$ | cycles per operation |
| Three-bus cycles for ADD | $N_{3B} = 1$ | cycles per operation |
| Speedup (3-bus vs single) | $S = \dfrac{N_{SB}}{N_{3B}} = 3$ | dimensionless |
| Tri-state buffer count (single bus) | $B_{buf} = n_{reg} + n_{ext}$ | where $n_{reg}$ = # internal regs, $n_{ext}$ = # external sources (MDR) |
| Multiplexer inputs (single bus) | $MUX = 2^{\,k}$ | where $k$ = select lines |
| Total control word bits | $B_{ctrl} = \log_2 n_{reg} + \log_2 n_{reg} + \lceil \log_2 O_{ALU} \rceil$ | $O_{ALU}$ = # ALU operations |
| Register file read ports | Dual-port for 2-bus, **triple-port for 3-bus** | critical hardware trade-off |
| Performance (CPI impact) | $CPI_{multi} < CPI_{single}$ | lower CPI ⇒ faster CPU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Law:** Adding buses *reduces cycle count per instruction* but *increases hardware cost* (more tri-state buffers, more register ports, more routing). This is the classic **Cost vs. Performance** trade-off in CPU design — a frequent KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-bus style** survives in modern CPUs as the **Load/Store unit's internal data path**, where one bus suffices because the operation is already a single memory transaction.
- **Multiple-bus style** is the foundation of **superscalar and VLIW processors**, where multiple execution lanes operate in parallel.
- The concept directly scales up to **on-chip interconnection networks (NoCs)** used in multi-core processors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Control Word Bit Count for Single-Bus CPU

Assume a CPU with:
- **8 General Purpose Registers** → R0 to R7
- **1 ALU with 16 operations** (ADD, SUB, MUL, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR, ROL, ROR, INC, DEC, CMP, NEG, TST)
- **1 immediate/operand selector bit**

**Step 1 — Bits for selecting source register (SelA):**

$$\text{bits}(SelA) = \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Bits for selecting destination register (SelB):**

$$\text{bits}(SelB) = \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Bits for ALU opcode:**

$$\text{bits}(OpCode) = \lceil \log_2(16) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Immediate/Register selector bit:**

$$\text{bits}(Imm/Reg) = 1 \text{ bit}$$

**Step 5 — Sum the fields to get total control word size:**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{ctrl} &= \text{bits}(SelA) + \text{bits}(SelB) + \text{bits}(OpCode) + \text{bits}(Imm/Reg) \\
&= 3 + 3 + 4 + 1 \\
&= 11 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A 16-bit microinstruction word is sufficient (with 5 spare bits for future extensions).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cycle Count Comparison for ADD R1, R2, R3

Let $N$ = number of source operands, and assume ALU is combinational (one-cycle).

**For Single-Bus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{SB} &= \underbrace{1}_{Y \leftarrow R2} + \underbrace{1}_{Z \leftarrow R2+R3} + \underbrace{1}_{R1 \leftarrow Z} \\
&= 3 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}$$

**For Two-Bus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{TB} &= \underbrace{1}_{ALU \text{ inputs: BusA=R2, BusB=R3} \rightarrow \text{temp}} + \underbrace{1}_{R1 \leftarrow \text{temp result}} \\
&= 2 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}$$

**For Three-Bus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{3B} &= \underbrace{1}_{ALU \text{ inputs: BusA=R2, BusB=R3, BusC} \rightarrow R1} \\
&= 1 \text{ cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

**Speedup ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{3B/SB} &= \frac{C_{SB}}{C_{3B}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3 \\
S_{3B/TB} &= \frac{C_{TB}}{C_{3B}} = \frac{2}{1} = 2 \\
S_{TB/SB} &= \frac{C_{SB}}{C_{TB}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Total Tri-State Buffer Count

Consider a single-bus CPU with:
- 8 GPRs (each can drive the bus)
- 1 MDR (drives the bus from memory)
- 1 PC (can also drive the bus)

**Step 1 — Drivers onto the bus:**

$$D_{on} = n_{GPR} + n_{MDR} + n_{PC} = 8 + 1 + 1 = 10 \text{ drivers}$$

**Step 2 — Each driver needs one tri-state buffer:**

$$B_{ts} = 10 \text{ tri-state buffers}$$

**Step 3 — Receivers (sinks) of the bus:**
- 8 GPRs (can latch from bus)
- 1 MDR
- 1 IR
- 1 MAR
- 1 Y (ALU input latch)

$$R_{off} = 8 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 12 \text{ receivers}$$

**Step 4 — Total active devices on the bus:**

$$T = D_{on} + R_{off} = 10 + 12 = 22 \text{ devices}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **bus loading** consideration — in real IC design, the bus is **capacitively loaded by every connected pin**, so practical buses limit the fan-out to about **10–20 devices** before requiring bus repeaters/drivers.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary Comparison

| Parameter | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
|-----------|:---------:|:------:|:--------:|
| # of internal buses | **1** | **2** | **3** |
| Cycles for ADD | 3 | 2 | **1** |
| Cycles for MUL (shift-add) | ~10+ | ~6 | **~4** |
| ALU input latches needed | 1 (Y) | 0 (if direct) | 0 |
| Register file read ports | 1 | 2 | **2** |
| Register file write ports | 1 | 1 | **1** (output bus C) |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** |
| Speed | **Slowest** | Medium | **Fastest** |
| Power consumption | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Typical application | Embedded MCUs | Mid-range CPUs | **High-performance CPUs** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single Bus Organization — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GPR[General Purpose Registers R0 to R7]
    PC[Program Counter PC]
    MAR[Memory Address Register MAR]
    MDR[Memory Data Register MDR]
    IR[Instruction Register IR]
    AC[Accumulator AC]
    YL[Y Latch - ALU input 1]
    ZL[Z Latch - ALU output]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
    BUS[(SINGLE INTERNAL BUS)]
    EXT_MEM[(External Main Memory)]

    GPR -- tri-state --> BUS
    PC -- tri-state --> BUS
    MDR -- tri-state --> BUS
    AC -- tri-state --> BUS
    BUS -- load --> GPR
    BUS -- load --> MAR
    BUS -- load --> IR
    BUS -- load --> AC
    BUS -- load --> YL
    YL --> ALU
    BUS --> ALU
    ALU --> ZL
    ZL -- tri-state --> BUS
    MAR --> EXT_MEM
    EXT_MEM --> MDR
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every register can either **drive** the bus (via a tri-state buffer) or **latch** from the bus (via a load enable). The Y-latch is the critical element that **stores the first ALU operand** while the second operand arrives on the bus in the next cycle.

---

### 4.2 Three-Bus Internal Organization — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RFA[Register File A Port - read]
    RFB[Register File B Port - read]
    RFW[Register File Write Port]
    BUS_A[(Bus A - source 1)]
    BUS_B[(Bus B - source 2)]
    BUS_C[(Bus C - ALU result)]
    INA[ALU Input Latch A]
    INB[ALU Input Latch B]
    ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
    FLAG[Status Flags Z N C V]

    RFA --> BUS_A
    RFB --> BUS_B
    BUS_A --> INA
    BUS_B --> INB
    INA --> ALU
    INB --> ALU
    ALU --> BUS_C
    ALU --> FLAG
    BUS_C --> RFW
    RFW -.writes back.-> RFA
    RFW -.writes back.-> RFB
```

**Key insight:** In one clock cycle, **Bus A and Bus B simultaneously feed the ALU inputs**, and the **ALU drives its result onto Bus C**, which writes back to the register file — completing a register-to-register operation in **a single cycle**.

---

### 4.3 Two-Bus Organization — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RGPR[Register File GPR]
    BUS_X[(Bus X)]
    BUS_Y[(Bus Y)]
    ALUA[ALU]
    TMP[Output Latch / Temporary Register]
    DEST[Destination Register]

    RGPR -- read port 1 --> BUS_X
    RGPR -- read port 2 --> BUS_Y
    BUS_X --> ALUA
    BUS_Y --> ALUA
    ALUA --> TMP
    TMP -- bus transfer --> DEST
    DEST -.loops back to.-> RGPR
```

**Note:** Two-bus still requires an **intermediate latch** (TMP) because the ALU output cannot write back to a register file port in the same cycle it is producing output (register file write-port is shared).

---

### 4.4 Bus Organization Evolution — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[CPU Internal Datapath]
    L0 --> L1a[Single Bus - 1 shared wire set]
    L0 --> L1b[Two Bus - 2 parallel wire sets]
    L0 --> L1c[Three Bus - 3 parallel wire sets]
    L1a --> L2a[Cost: Low, Speed: Low, Use: Simple MCUs]
    L1b --> L2b[Cost: Medium, Speed: Medium, Use: Mid-range CPUs]
    L1c --> L2c[Cost: High, Speed: High, Use: High-performance CPUs]
    L1c --> L3[Scales up to: Superscalar pipelines, Multi-core NoCs]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*What is single bus organization? Why is an internal latch (Y) required in such a CPU?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> In a single bus organization, all major registers of the CPU (GPRs, PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC) are interconnected through **one common internal bus**. Since the bus can carry data from only **one source at a time**, multi-operand ALU instructions cannot fetch both operands simultaneously.
>
> The **Y latch** is therefore required to **store the first ALU operand** (loaded from the bus in cycle 1) so that the **second operand** can be placed on the bus in cycle 2, allowing the ALU to combine them. Without the Y latch, it would be impossible to execute any 2-operand ALU operation. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*State TWO advantages and TWO disadvantages of three-bus organization over single-bus organization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Faster execution** — A register-to-register ADD completes in **1 cycle** instead of 3.
> 2. **Higher instruction throughput** — Lower CPI improves overall CPU performance.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Higher hardware cost** — Requires **dual-read-port register file** and **more tri-state buffers / routing**.
> 2. **Greater silicon area and power consumption** — Three 32-bit buses consume more chip real estate and switching power.
>
> **[3 marks]**

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]**
> *(a)* Explain the **single bus organization** of a CPU with a neat block diagram. Show the sequence of micro-operations required to execute the instruction **`ADD R1, R2, R3`** (R1 ← R2 + R3). **[7 marks]**
>
> *(b)* With a block diagram, describe the **three-bus internal organization** of a CPU. Show the micro-operations for the same ADD instruction and comment on the speed improvement. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Single Bus Organization [7 marks]:**

In single bus organization, all registers (PC, IR, AC, R0–R7, MAR, MDR, Y, Z) are connected to **one common internal CPU bus** through tri-state buffers. Only **one register can drive the bus at a time**.

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
- A horizontal line represents the **single internal bus**.
- Above the bus: **GPRs (R0–R7), PC, MDR, AC** — each connected via a tri-state output buffer.
- Below the bus: **IR, MAR, AC, Y** — each connected via a load-enable input.
- The **ALU** takes inputs from the **bus** and from the **Y latch**; its output goes to the **Z latch**, which then drives back onto the bus.

**Micro-operation sequence for `ADD R1, R2, R3` (4 marks):**

| Step | Control Signals Active | Operation |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| T1 | `R2out, Yin` | R2 contents placed on bus → latched into Y |
| T2 | `R3out, ADD, Zin` | R3 contents on bus → ALU computes Y + R3 → result latched in Z |
| T3 | `Zout, R1in` | Z contents placed on bus → latched into R1 |

**Total = 3 clock cycles.**

---

**Model Solution (b) — Three-Bus Organization [7 marks]:**

In three-bus organization, **two buses (A and B) feed the ALU inputs simultaneously**, and **one bus (C) carries the ALU output** back to the register file in the same cycle.

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
- **Bus A** carries operand 1 from the register file's read port 1.
- **Bus B** carries operand 2 from the register file's read port 2.
- **ALU** receives Bus A and Bus B; produces result on **Bus C**.
- **Bus C** writes the result into the destination register via the write port.
- **Status flags** (Z, N, C, V) are updated combinationally by the ALU.

**Micro-operation sequence for `ADD R1, R2, R3` (3 marks):**

| Step | Control Signals Active | Operation |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| T1 | `R2out_A, R3out_B, ADD, R1in_C` | R2 on Bus A, R3 on Bus B → ALU adds → result on Bus C → loaded into R1 |

**Total = 1 clock cycle.** **Speedup = 3×** compared to single bus.

**Comment on speed improvement (1 mark):**
- The CPI contribution for register-register ALU operations drops from 3 to 1, yielding a theoretical 3× throughput improvement for compute-bound workloads. This benefit compounds in pipelined and superscalar designs.

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
> *(a)* Explain the **two-bus internal organization** of a CPU with a block diagram. Show the micro-operations for the instruction **`SUB R4, R5, R6`** (R4 ← R5 − R6). **[7 marks]**
>
> *(b)* Compare **single bus, two bus, and three bus organizations** in terms of (i) number of buses, (ii) cycles per ADD, (iii) hardware cost, and (iv) typical application. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Two-Bus Organization [7 marks]:**

In two-bus organization, **two buses (X and Y)** connect the register file to the ALU. Both ALU inputs come from the two buses simultaneously. The ALU output is temporarily stored in a latch before being written to a destination register (because the register file has only one write port).

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
- Register file with **two read ports** driving **Bus X** and **Bus Y**.
- **ALU** receives Bus X and Bus Y.
- **Output latch (TEMP)** captures the ALU result.
- **Bus from TEMP** loads the destination register through the register file's single write port.

**Micro-operations for `SUB R4, R5, R6` (4 marks):**

| Step | Control Signals Active | Operation |
|------|----------------------|-----------|
| T1 | `R5out_X, R6out_Y, SUB, TEMPin` | R5 on Bus X, R6 on Bus Y → ALU computes R5 − R6 → result stored in TEMP |
| T2 | `TEMPout, R4in` | Result on bus → written into R4 |

**Total = 2 clock cycles.** Speedup over single bus = **3/2 = 1.5×**.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Comparison Table [7 marks]:**

| Parameter | Single Bus | Two Bus | Three Bus |
|-----------|:---------:|:------:|:--------:|
| **(i) Number of internal buses** | **1** | **2** | **3** |
| **(ii) Cycles for ADD instruction** | **3 cycles** | **2 cycles** | **1 cycle** |
| **(iii) Hardware cost** | **Lowest** (1 bus, 1 ALU latch) | **Medium** (2 buses, 1 output latch, dual-read RF) | **Highest** (3 buses, dual-read RF, full routing) |
| **(iv) Typical application** | Simple embedded microcontrollers (8051-class) | Mid-range microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M0) | High-performance CPUs (superscalar, DSPs, GPUs) |

**Additional commentary (1 mark):**
- The progression from 1 → 2 → 3 buses represents the **performance vs. cost trade-off** in CPU design.
- Modern superscalar CPUs extend the multi-bus idea to **multiple execution units** each with its own internal bus hierarchy.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Y-latch in single-bus diagrams** — Examiners deduct **at least 1 mark** if the ALU input latch is not shown. The Y-latch is *the* distinguishing feature of single-bus design.
> 2. **Writing "ADD" without specifying the destination register** — Always write the complete micro-operation, e.g., `R2out, Yin` not just "R2 to bus".
> 3. **Mixing up "read ports" and "write ports"** — Two-bus and three-bus need **two read ports**, not two write ports. Many students draw this incorrectly.
> 4. **Not mentioning tri-state buffers** — A single-bus *requires* tri-state control to avoid bus contention. Omitting this loses a mark.
> 5. **Confusing "cycles per instruction" with "CPI"** — For a *complete* instruction like `LOAD R1, (R2)`, the fetch-decode-execute sequence must be added on top of the bus organization analysis. Don't over-claim speedup.
> 6. **No diagram = up to 2–3 marks lost** in a 7-mark sub-question. Always draw a labelled block diagram.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Single Bus** = **1 shared bus**, **3 cycles** per ADD, needs **Y-latch** at ALU input.
- [x] **Two Bus** = **2 buses**, **2 cycles** per ADD, needs **1 output latch**, dual-read register file.
- [x] **Three Bus** = **3 buses**, **1 cycle** per ADD, dual-read register file, output bus C writes back.
- [x] **Tri-state buffers** are mandatory in single-bus to prevent contention.
- [x] **Speedup** (3-bus / single-bus) for register-register ADD = **3×**.
- [x] **Hardware cost increases linearly with bus count** — more buffers, more routing, more register ports.
- [x] **ALU is combinational** in all three designs — result available within the same cycle.
- [x] **Control word bit count** = `log₂(n_src) + log₂(n_dst) + log₂(n_ALU_ops) + immediate_bit`.
- [x] **Status flags** (Z, N, C, V) are updated by the ALU combinationally in every design.
- [x] **Real-world scaling:** Multiple-bus concept scales up to **superscalar** and **multi-core** architectures.
- [x] **Always draw the block diagram first** in 7-mark sub-questions; it carries 2–3 marks by itself.
- [x] **Always show the micro-operation table** (cycle-by-cycle control signals) — this is the heart of the answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Transfer Logic: Inter Register Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Register Transfer Logic: Inter Register Transfer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Register Transfer Logic (RTL)** is a symbolic notation system used to describe the micro-operations, data flow, and control signals of a digital system at the register-transfer level of abstraction. It provides a precise, hardware-oriented language for specifying **how data is moved, modified, and stored** between the registers of a processor or digital system. **Inter-Register Transfer** is the most fundamental micro-operation in RTL. It denotes the synchronous movement of binary information from a **source register** to a **destination register** during a single clock edge, with optional conditional control. The general symbolic form of an inter-register transfer statement is: $$R_{\text{dest}} \leftarrow R_{\text{src}}$$ where $R_{\text{dest}}$ and $R_{\text{src}}$ are registers in the register file of the processor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The arrow symbol `←` in RTL is **NOT** an assignment in the programming sense. It represents a **physical hardware transfer** that occurs in parallel across all bit positions, triggered by the active edge of a common clock signal. This distinction is a frequently tested concept. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Warehouse Imagine a **massive automated warehouse** with multiple storage rooms (the *registers*). Each room holds identical type of goods (e.g., 16-bit binary words). A **central conveyor belt** runs alongside all rooms (the *common bus*). - When the warehouse manager (the *control unit*) needs to move goods from Room A to Room B, he activates Room A's output door and Room B's input door simultaneously. - All 16 items (bits) move at exactly the same instant, atomically. - The arrow `R_B ← R_A` on the warehouse's control panel is the symbolic representation of this physical transfer. This analogy captures three critical properties of inter-register transfer: 1. **Parallelism** — All bits move simultaneously (word-wide). 2. **Synchrony** — The transfer happens on a clock edge. 3. **Atomicity** — The source register is *not* cleared (unless explicitly stated). ## 1.3 Primary RTL Symbols The KTU 2024 syllabus expects fluency with the following standard RTL symbols: | Symbol | Description | Mnemonic | |:---:|:---|:---| | `←` | Transfer of data (replaces the contents) | "becomes" | | `,` | Concurrent (simultaneous) operations | "in parallel" | | `:` | Conditional: operation executes only if condition is true | "if" | | `=` | Condition: "no transfer" / comparison | "equals" | | $M[AR]$ | Memory location whose address is in AR | "memory at AR" | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** In RTL, the **arrow points from source to destination** (opposite of conventional mathematical mapping). The original contents of the source register remain **unchanged** unless an explicit two-step sequence (read, then write) is specified. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Inter-Register Transfer Statement Every inter-register transfer statement contains three structural components: 1. **Source Register** — provides the binary data. 2. **Destination Register** — receives the binary data. 3. **Control Condition** (optional) — a Boolean expression that gates the transfer. ### 2.1.1 Unconditional Transfer $$R_2 \leftarrow R_1$$ The contents of $R_1$ are copied into $R_2$ on the next active clock edge. This is the simplest form and forms the basis of every data-movement operation in a CPU (register-to-register MOV, ALU operand load, etc.). ### 2.1.2 Conditional Transfer $$P : R_2 \leftarrow R_1$$ The transfer occurs **only if** the control signal $P = 1$ at the active clock edge. If $P = 0$, the transfer is inhibited and $R_2$ retains its previous value. This implements hardware-level *gating* without any branch instruction. ### 2.1.3 Multiple Concurrent Transfers $$P : R_2 \leftarrow R_1, R_4 \leftarrow R_3$$ The comma denotes **concurrent execution** — both transfers occur on the same clock edge, provided $P = 1$. This represents parallel register movements, a hallmark of RTL's hardware-centric semantics. ## 2.2 The Inter-Register Transfer Problem A naive implementation would require $N \times N$ direct connections between $N$ registers — a **complete bipartite graph** of $N^2$ bus paths. For a 32-register file, this would require **1024 dedicated wires**, which is clearly infeasible in VLSI design. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core Engineering Challenge:** How can we enable data transfer between *any pair* of registers using a *minimal* number of shared communication lines? The answer is the **Common Bus System**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Common Bus Design The following table consolidates the essential design parameters for inter-register transfer via a common bus. | Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Physical Meaning | Units | |:---|:---:|:---|:---:| | Number of registers | $N$ | Size of the register file | count | | Number of select lines | $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Bus selection inputs | bits | | Number of multiplexers | $k$ | Word width of the bus | $k$-to-1 MUX | | MUX input count | $N$ | Each MUX has $N$ data inputs | inputs | | Total data wires | $N \times k$ | Point-to-point from each register to bus | wires | | Selection logic | $n$-to-$2^n$ decoder | Activates exactly one register's MUX path | combinational | | Conditional transfer | $\text{EN} = P \land S_{\text{active}}$ | AND of control and select | boolean | | Three-state buffer count | $N \times k$ | One per register-bit pair | buffers | | Timing constraint | $t_{\text{setup}} < T_{\text{clk}}/2$ | Setup time vs. clock period | seconds | > [!WARNING] > The vertical bar in conditional statements like "$P \vert Q$" must be typeset using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid table-parsing errors. The same applies to absolute-value notation, e.g., $\lvert x \rvert$. ## 2.4 Hardware Realization Strategies There are **two principal strategies** to physically realize inter-register transfer on a common bus, both examinable under the KTU 2024 scheme. ### Strategy 1: Multiplexer-Based Common Bus A single $N$-to-$1$ multiplexer (per bit) selects one of the $N$ source registers and drives the bus. Selection is governed by $\log_2 N$ address lines. ### Strategy 2: Three-State Bus Buffers Each register output is gated by a **tri-state buffer**. Only the buffer whose *enable* line is asserted drives the bus; all others present a high-impedance (Hi-Z) state. This eliminates the multiplexer but requires $N$ enable lines. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Why" Matters:** Strategy 1 is preferred for **small register files** (4–8 registers) due to simpler timing closure.
