Strategy 2 scales better to **large register files** (32, 64 registers) because tri-state buffers consume less silicon area than a wide multiplexer tree, although they introduce bus-contention risks if two enables are simultaneously high. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CPU datapath design:** Every MOV, PUSH, POP, and arithmetic instruction is built from one or more inter-register transfers. - **GPU register files:** Thousands of registers are interconnected via hierarchical bus systems to support parallel thread execution. - **FPGA design synthesis:** RTL descriptions (Verilog/VHDL) are compiled directly into hardware implementing these transfers. - **ASIC design verification:** Register-transfer-level simulation is the standard pre-fabrication verification method. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Common Bus System for 4 Registers ### Problem Setup Design a common bus system to enable inter-register transfer between **four 4-bit registers** $R_0, R_1, R_2, R_3$. ### Step 1: Determine the Number of Select Lines The number of select lines is the minimum number of bits needed to uniquely address $N$ registers: $$n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$$ So we need **2 select lines** $S_1$ and $S_0$. ### Step 2: Determine the Multiplexer Configuration Since each register is 4 bits wide and there are 4 registers, we require **4 parallel 4-to-1 multiplexers** (one per bit position $b_0, b_1, b_2, b_3$). The number of data inputs to each MUX equals $N$: $$\text{MUX inputs} = N = 4$$ ### Step 3: Build the Selection Truth Table | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Active Register | Bus Source | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | $R_0$ | $R_0[i]$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ | | 0 | 1 | $R_1$ | $R_1[i]$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ | | 1 | 0 | $R_2$ | $R_2[i]$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ | | 1 | 1 | $R_3$ | $R_3[i]$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ | ### Step 4: Derive the Boolean Output Equation For an arbitrary bit position $i$, the bus output $B_i$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} B_i &= \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R_0[i] + \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot R_1[i] \\ &\quad + S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R_2[i] + S_1 \cdot S_0 \cdot R_3[i] \end{aligned} $$ This is the canonical sum-of-products (SOP) form of a 4-to-1 multiplexer. Each product term is a **minterm** of the select lines ANDed with the corresponding register's bit. ### Step 5: Implement Using a 2-to-4 Decoder A cleaner implementation uses a **2-to-4 line decoder** to generate one-hot enable signals $D_0, D_1, D_2, D_3$: $$ \begin{aligned} D_0 &= \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \\ D_1 &= \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \\ D_2 &= S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \\ D_3 &= S_1 \cdot S_0 \end{aligned} $$ Each decoder output $D_k$ is then used as the **enable signal** for a dedicated AND-gate chain connecting register $R_k$ to the bus. ### Step 6: Add Conditional Control To implement the conditional transfer $P : R_2 \leftarrow R_1$, the control signal $P$ is ANDed with the relevant decoder output: $$\text{EN}_1 = P \cdot D_1 = P \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0$$ Only when both $P = 1$ and $(S_1, S_0) = (0,1)$ does the bus carry $R_1$'s contents. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Three-State Bus Buffer System ### Step 1: Tri-State Buffer Logic A tri-state buffer has the following truth table: | Enable ($E$) | Data In ($D$) | Output ($Y$) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | X | Hi-Z (High Impedance) | | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 1 | 1 | ### Step 2: Connection to the Bus Each register $R_k$ is connected to bus line $B_i$ through a tri-state buffer. The enable of the $k$-th register's buffer is: $$E_k = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \quad \text{for } k=0$$ The bus is **floating** unless exactly one $E_k = 1$. ### Step 3: Bus-Contention Prevention Since multiple $E_k$ can theoretically be active, the design must enforce the constraint: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} E_k \leq 1$$ This is guaranteed by the one-hot property of the decoder outputs. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MUX vs. Tri-State Comparison | Design Aspect | MUX-Based Bus | Tri-State Bus | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Silicon area (small $N$) | Lower | Higher | | Silicon area (large $N$) | Higher (MUX tree grows) | Lower (linear growth) | | Speed | Faster (combinational path) | Slower (bus capacitance) | | Power consumption | Static power higher | Lower static power | | Contention risk | Impossible by construction | Possible if enables overlap | | Best suited for | 4–8 register datapaths | 16+ register files | | KTU exam frequency | Very High | High | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Common Bus System Architecture (MUX-Based) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the complete inter-register transfer architecture for **four 4-bit registers** using a 4-to-1 multiplexer-based common bus. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph REGS["REGISTER FILE 4 x 4 BIT"] r0["R0
4 BITS"] r1["R1
4 BITS"] r2["R2
4 BITS"] r3["R3
4 BITS"] end subgraph SELECT["SELECTION LOGIC"] sLines["SELECT INPUTS
S1 S0"] dec["2 TO 4
LINE DECODER"] sLines --> dec end subgraph MUXBANK["4 PARALLEL 4-TO-1 MULTIPLEXERS"] m0["MUX BIT 0"] m1["MUX BIT 1"] m2["MUX BIT 2"] m3["MUX BIT 3"] end subgraph BUS["COMMON BUS"] cb["4 BIT BUS
B3 B2 B1 B0"] end r0 --> m0 r0 --> m1 r0 --> m2 r0 --> m3 r1 --> m0 r1 --> m1 r1 --> m2 r1 --> m3 r2 --> m0 r2 --> m1 r2 --> m2 r2 --> m3 r3 --> m0 r3 --> m1 r3 --> m2 r3 --> m3 dec -.ENABLE PATH.-> m0 dec -.ENABLE PATH.-> m1 dec -.ENABLE PATH.-> m2 dec -.ENABLE PATH.-> m3 m0 --> cb m1 --> cb m2 --> cb m3 --> cb ``` ## 4.2 Three-State Bus Buffer Architecture The following diagram shows the alternative tri-state buffer implementation for the same 4-register system. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph REGFILE["REGISTER FILE"] tR0["R0 4 BITS"] tR1["R1 4 BITS"] tR2["R2 4 BITS"] tR3["R3 4 BITS"] end subgraph DEC2["2 TO 4 DECODER"] inSels["S1 S0 INPUTS"] d0["D0 ENABLE"] d1["D1 ENABLE"] d2["D2 ENABLE"] d3["D3 ENABLE"] end subgraph BUFFS["TRI STATE BUFFER ARRAY 16 BUFFERS"] b0["TSB R0 BUF"] b1["TSB R1 BUF"] b2["TSB R2 BUF"] b3["TSB R3 BUF"] end subgraph TBUS["COMMON BUS 4 LINES"] tbus["B3 B2 B1 B0"] end tR0 --> b0 tR1 --> b1 tR2 --> b2 tR3 --> b3 inSels --> d0 inSels --> d1 inSels --> d2 inSels --> d3 d0 --> b0 d1 --> b1 d2 --> b2 d3 --> b3 b0 --> tbus b1 --> tbus b2 --> tbus b3 --> tbus ``` ## 4.3 Complete Conditional Transfer Control Flow This flow shows the logical sequence that governs a conditional inter-register transfer operation. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([CLOCK EDGE ARRIVES]) check{"P CONTROL SIGNAL
EQUAL TO 1"} decode["ACTIVATE 2 TO 4 DECODER
WITH S1 S0"] assert["ASSERT ENABLE LINE
FOR SOURCE REGISTER RS"] transfer["SOURCE BITS FLOW
THROUGH MUX OR TRI STATE BUF"] latch["DESTINATION REGISTER RD
LATCHES NEW DATA"] skip["RD RETAINS OLD VALUE
NO TRANSFER OCCURS"] finish([END OF CLOCK CYCLE]) start --> check check -->|YES| decode check -->|NO| skip decode --> assert assert --> transfer transfer --> latch latch --> finish skip --> finish ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Register Transfer Language (RTL). Explain any four symbols used in RTL with examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Register Transfer Language is a symbolic notation used to describe the micro-operations and data transfers among the registers of a digital system. It operates at the register-transfer level of abstraction, which is one level above gate-level logic. Four key RTL symbols: 1. **Arrow `←` (Transfer):** Indicates data movement. Example: $R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ means contents of $R_1$ are copied into $R_2$. 2. **Comma `,` (Concurrency):** Separates micro-operations that occur in the same clock cycle. Example: $R_2 \leftarrow R_1, R_4 \leftarrow R_3$. 3. **Colon `:` (Condition):** Gates the operation. Example: $P : R_2 \leftarrow R_1$ — transfer occurs only if $P = 1$. 4. **Square brackets `[ ]` (Memory access):** Denotes memory operand. Example: $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ reads memory location addressed by $AR$ into $DR$. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 0.5 marks per symbol with a correct example. Simply listing the symbol without a corresponding example forfeits half the marks for that symbol. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is meant by inter-register transfer? Why is a common bus system preferred over direct point-to-point connections between registers?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Inter-register transfer** is the micro-operation of moving binary data from one register (source) to another register (destination) within the processor, typically synchronized to a clock edge. A **common bus system** is preferred over point-to-point connections because: 1. **Hardware Economy:** A common bus with $\log_2 N$ select lines and $N \times k$ data wires replaces $N^2$ dedicated interconnections. For 32 registers of 32 bits each, point-to-point requires 1024 wires, while a common bus needs only 32 data + 5 select = 37 wires. 2. **Scalability:** Adding a new register requires adding only one MUX input or one tri-state buffer, not $N$ new connections. 3. **Design Simplicity:** The control logic is centralized in the bus selector (decoder or MUX control inputs). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **ONE** question from a Module-Internal-Choice pair. The two alternatives below are designed with **equivalent cognitive depth and mark distribution** to mirror the KTU pattern. ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Design and explain a common bus system using multiplexers to perform inter-register transfer between four 4-bit registers. Add a brief comparison with the tri-state buffer approach.** #### Part (a): MUX-Based Common Bus Design (7 Marks) **Step 1: System Specification** Given four registers $R_0, R_1, R_2, R_3$ each of 4 bits, we need a bus that can carry the contents of any selected register. **Step 2: Calculate Select Lines** $$n = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2 \text{ select lines } (S_1, S_0)$$ **Step 3: Multiplexer Configuration** We require **four 4-to-1 multiplexers**, one per bit position. The output of MUX-$i$ is: $$ \text{BUS}_i = \overline{S_1}\overline{S_0}R_0[i] + \overline{S_1}S_0 R_1[i] + S_1\overline{S_0}R_2[i] + S_1 S_0 R_3[i] $$ **Step 4: Selection Truth Table** | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Bus Source | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | $R_0$ | | 0 | 1 | $R_1$ | | 1 | 0 | $R_2$ | | 1 | 1 | $R_3$ | **Step 5: 2-to-4 Decoder Implementation** A 2-to-4 decoder produces one-hot outputs $D_0, D_1, D_2, D_3$. Each $D_k$ is the chip-select-like enable for the corresponding register's path through the MUX bank. **Step 6: Conditional Transfer Implementation** For a conditional statement $P : R_2 \leftarrow R_1$: - The control unit asserts $P = 1$. - The select lines are set to $(S_1, S_0) = (0, 1)$, activating $D_1$. - The effective enable for $R_1$'s path is $\text{EN}_1 = P \cdot D_1$. - The destination register $R_2$ is enabled to *latch* on the same clock edge, completing the transfer. #### Part (b): Comparison with Tri-State Buffer Approach (7 Marks) | Criterion | MUX Bus | Tri-State Bus | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Active components | $k$ multiplexers of size $N$-to-1 | $N \times k$ tri-state buffers | | Select logic | Decoder + MUX control | Decoder drives buffer enables directly | | Bus contention | Impossible (MUX has single output driver) | Possible if two enables overlap | | Speed | Faster (combinational only) | Slower (bus has distributed capacitance) | | Area scaling | $O(N \cdot k \cdot \log N)$ | $O(N \cdot k)$ — linear in $N$ | | Application | CPU datapaths (small N) | Register files, memory I/O buses | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the multiplexer equation as $B = S_1 S_0 R_0 + \ldots$ (inverted select line interpretation). This is a **2-mark deduction** in valuation. Always verify: when $(S_1, S_0) = (0,0)$, the output should be $R_0$, requiring $\overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot R_0$. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **common bus** with **system bus**. A common bus is a *processor-internal* shared data path; a system bus connects CPU, memory, and I/O. Conflating them costs 1–2 marks. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to specify the **clock-edge behavior**. A correct RTL answer must mention that the latch occurs on the *active edge* of the clock. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the RTL notation for inter-register transfer with suitable examples. Design the bus selection logic using a 3-to-8 decoder for an 8-register, 8-bit common bus system. Discuss the timing considerations.** #### Part (a): RTL Notation and Examples (7 Marks) **Basic Transfer:** $$R_1 \leftarrow R_0$$ Unconditional: on every clock edge, $R_1$ is updated with $R_0$'s contents. **Conditional Transfer with Concurrency:** $$\overline{E} \cdot I : R_1 \leftarrow R_0, \text{ } R_0 \leftarrow R_1$$ This represents a simultaneous **swap** of $R_0$ and $R_1$ — the hallmark of a true hardware swap, which is impossible in software as a single statement. **Memory-to-Register Transfer:** $$DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$ The data at memory location whose address is in $AR$ is loaded into the Data Register $DR$. **Register-to-Memory Transfer:** $$M[AR] \leftarrow R_1$$ The contents of $R_1$ are written to the memory location addressed by $AR$. **Swap Operation (Extended):** $$K_1 : R_2 \leftarrow R_0, K_1 : R_0 \leftarrow R_2, \overline{K_1} : R_2 \leftarrow R_0$$ If $K_1 = 1$: swap $R_0$ and $R_2$. If $K_1 = 0$: $R_2$ keeps its value (idle). #### Part (b): 3-to-8 Decoder Bus Selection for 8 Registers (7 Marks) **Step 1: Select Line Count** $$n = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3 \text{ select lines } (S_2, S_1, S_0)$$ **Step 2: Decoder Outputs** The 3-to-8 decoder generates: $$ \begin{aligned} D_0 &= \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} \quad (\text{selects } R_0) \\ D_1 &= \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,S_0 \quad (\text{selects } R_1) \\ &\;\;\vdots \\ D_7 &= S_2\,S_1\,S_0 \quad (\text{selects } R_7) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Bus Driver Logic** For an 8-bit bus, we need **8 parallel banks of tri-state buffers** (or 8 parallel 8-to-1 MUXes). Each bit $B_j$ of the bus is driven by the OR of all enabled buffer outputs: $$B_j = \sum_{k=0}^{7} (D_k \cdot R_k[j])$$ The decoder guarantees exactly one $D_k = 1$, so the sum reduces to a single term — **no contention**. **Step 4: Timing Analysis** The critical timing path is: $$t_{\text{critical}} = t_{\text{decoder}} + t_{\text{buffer}} + t_{\text{setup}}$$ For a 74LS138 decoder (typical $t_{pd} = 15$ ns) and a tri-state buffer (typical $t_{pd} = 8$ ns) with register setup time $t_{su} = 5$ ns: $$t_{\text{critical}} = 15 + 8 + 5 = 28 \text{ ns}$$ The maximum clock frequency is therefore: $$f_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{\text{critical}}} = \frac{1}{56 \text{ ns}} \approx 17.86 \text{ MHz}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the decoder output as $D_0 = S_2 + S_1 + S_0$ (OR instead of AND with complements). The decoder output is a **product of literals**, not a sum. This single error in the Boolean expression forfeits the entire 7-mark sub-question. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **timing margin**. The 50% duty cycle assumption is only valid for *edge-triggered* registers; level-sensitive latches require a different (typically stricter) timing analysis. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Not mentioning the **one-hot property** of the decoder output — i.e., $\sum D_k = 1$ exactly — which is the formal proof that no bus contention occurs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Inter-register transfer** moves data between CPU registers in a single clock cycle, executed in parallel across all bits. - ✅ The RTL arrow `←` is **destructive at the destination** but **non-destructive at the source** (read-only). - ✅ A **common bus** is a *shared* set of wires; only one register may drive it at a time. - ✅ For $N$ registers, the **minimum number of select lines** is $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$. - ✅ A **2-to-4 decoder** is used to select among 4 registers; a **3-to-8 decoder** for 8 registers. - ✅ **MUX-based bus** has faster timing, impossible contention, but scales poorly with $N$. - ✅ **Tri-state bus** has linear area scaling, faster for large $N$, but requires careful enable management. - ✅ **Conditional transfer** $P : R_d \leftarrow R_s$ requires both $P = 1$ *and* correct select lines, ANDed to form the effective enable. - ✅ **Concurrent operations** separated by `,` occur on the **same clock edge** — this is the hardware basis for atomic swaps and parallel data movement. - ✅ **Memory transfer** uses $M[AR]$ notation; the bus is also used as the address/data highway between CPU and memory. - ✅ The total number of bus data wires is always equal to the **word length** $k$ of the registers, regardless of $N$. - ✅ The critical timing path in a bus system = decoder delay + buffer/MUX delay + register setup time. - ✅ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Be ready to draw the *complete block diagram* (registers, MUXes/buffers, decoder, bus) for any value of $N$ from 2 to 8.

topics.title: Arithmetic, Logic, and Shift Micro-Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic, Logic, and Shift Micro-Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **micro-operation** is an elementary operation performed on information stored in the registers of a digital computer during one single clock pulse (clock cycle). Micro-operations are the most basic, atomic, and indivisible data-processing operations that the Central Processing Unit (CPU) can execute at the register-transfer level (RTL). When several micro-operations are sequenced to form a complete instruction, the sequence is referred to as a **micro-program** (or **micro-routine**).

The general form of a register transfer micro-operation is given by the symbolic notation:
**R ← f(R, Memory, I/O)**

Where:
- **R** = Source and destination registers
- **f** = The function to be performed (arithmetic, logic, shift, or transfer)
- **Memory / I/O** = Optional secondary operands

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Classification of Micro-Operations:**
> 1. **Register Transfer Micro-Operations** – Move data between registers
> 2. **Arithmetic Micro-Operations** – Perform numerical computation (Add, Subtract, etc.)
> 3. **Logic Micro-Operations** – Perform bit-wise logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, etc.)
> 4. **Shift Micro-Operations** – Perform displacement of bits (Logical, Circular, Arithmetic)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a computer's CPU as a **high-precision industrial assembly line**:
- **Registers** = The workstations where the items (data) are held.
- **Clock pulse** = The factory bell that rings to start the next single action.
- **Micro-operation** = The single, small hand motion of a worker (e.g., "place a rivet" or "rotate the piece").

Just as a complex product is built by sequencing many tiny hand motions, a complex instruction (like addition of two numbers) is built by sequencing multiple micro-operations (load operand, perform binary addition, store result).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common misconception among students is to confuse **micro-operations** with **instructions**. An *instruction* is the user-level command (e.g., "ADD R1, R2"). A *micro-operation* is the hardware-level step that physically realizes that instruction (e.g., "R1 ← R1 + R2, activate ALU, route output to R1").

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Hardware Constants

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Number of registers in basic CPU | **16** (R0 – R15) |
| Common bus width | **16-bit** (for KTU standard models) |
| Clock pulse duration | Typically **microseconds / nanoseconds** |
| ALU function select lines | **2ⁿ = 4** select lines for **n = 2** control inputs (S₁, S₀) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Arithmetic Micro-Operations (Numerical Computation)

Arithmetic micro-operations are those that involve the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** of the processor to perform numerical computations on numeric data stored in registers. The fundamental ALU operations (using select variables **S₁** and **S₀**) are tabulated below.

#### 2.1.1 Basic Add Micro-Operation
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$$

The contents of register R1 are added to the contents of R2, and the sum is transferred to R3.

#### 2.1.2 Add with Carry
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2 + \overline{C}$$

Where $\overline{C}$ is the **complemented carry input** from a previous arithmetic operation (ripple carry).

#### 2.1.3 Subtract with Borrow
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_2} + C$$

This implements binary subtraction using 2's complement arithmetic: $R_1 - R_2 = R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1$.

#### 2.1.4 Increment / Decrement
$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1 + 1 \quad \text{(Increment)}$$
$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1 - 1 \quad \text{(Decrement)}$$

#### 2.1.5 Multiply / Divide (Hardware Shift + Add/Subtract)
$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1 \times 2 \quad \text{(Shift left by 1 bit)}$$
$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1 \div 2 \quad \text{(Shift right by 1 bit)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In 2's complement representation, $R_1 - R_2$ is implemented as $R_1 + (\text{1's complement of } R_2) + 1$. The "+1" in this expression is the initial carry-in **C** in the expression $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_2} + C$.

---

### 2.2 Logic Micro-Operations (Bit-wise Logical Operations)

Logic micro-operations perform bit-wise Boolean operations on the corresponding pairs of bits stored in two registers. They are executed in parallel on all **n** bits simultaneously. Let the registers R1 and R2 be n-bit wide. The result is stored in R3.

For each bit position **i** (where $0 \leq i \leq n-1$):
$$R_{3_i} \leftarrow f(R_{1_i}, R_{2_i})$$

The **16 possible logic micro-operations** (since there are $2^{2^2} = 16$ possible truth tables of 2 input variables) are given by:

| S₃ | S₂ | S₁ | S₀ | Output (F) | Active Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $F = 0$ | Clear (always 0) |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $F \leftarrow A \wedge B$ | AND |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $F \leftarrow A + B$ | OR |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $F \leftarrow A \oplus B$ | XOR (Exclusive-OR) |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A} \wedge \overline{B}$ | NOR (NOT-OR) |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $F \leftarrow \overline{B}$ | NOT B (complement B) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A} \oplus \overline{B}$ | Equivalence |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A} \wedge B$ | Complement A AND B |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A} + B$ | Complement A OR B |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | $F \leftarrow \overline{A \oplus B}$ | XNOR (Equivalence) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $F \leftarrow B$ | Transfer B |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $F \leftarrow A \wedge B$ | AND (with A complement) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | $F \leftarrow A$ | Transfer A |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $F \leftarrow A \vee B$ | OR (with A complement) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $F \leftarrow A + B$ | OR |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | $F = 1$ | Set (always 1) |

#### Applications of Logic Micro-Operations
- **Selective Set:** $A \leftarrow A \lor B$ (sets bits where B has 1s)
- **Selective Clear:** $A \leftarrow A \wedge \overline{B}$ (clears bits where B has 1s)
- **Selective Complement:** $A \leftarrow A \oplus B$ (complements bits where B has 1s)
- **Mask Operation:** $A \leftarrow A \wedge B$ (extracts bits of A where B has 1s)

---

### 2.3 Shift Micro-Operations (Bit Displacement)

Shift micro-operations are used for **serial transfer of data**, and also for **arithmetic, logic, and control operations**. They are of three primary types:

#### 2.3.1 Logical Shift
Bits shifted out of the register are **lost** (discarded), and the vacant bit position is filled with **0**.

$$R \leftarrow \text{shl} \, R \quad \text{(Shift Left)}$$
$$R \leftarrow \text{shr} \, R \quad \text{(Shift Right)}$$

#### 2.3.2 Circular Shift (Rotate)
Bits shifted out from one end are **fed back** into the other end. No bits are lost.

$$R \leftarrow \text{cil} \, R \quad \text{(Circular Shift Left)}$$
$$R \leftarrow \text{cir} \, R \quad \text{(Circular Shift Right)}$$

#### 2.3.3 Arithmetic Shift
- **Arithmetic Shift Left (asl):** Same as logical shift left (sign bit may be lost).
- **Arithmetic Shift Right (asr):** The **sign bit (MSB) is preserved** during the right shift, and the LSB is lost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Arithmetic Shift Right** is equivalent to **division by 2** for signed numbers in 2's complement form. **Arithmetic Shift Left** is equivalent to **multiplication by 2** (with potential overflow).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Micro-Operation Type | Symbolic Form | Mathematical Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Add | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ | Binary addition | Addition, Increment |
| Subtract | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$ | 2's complement subtraction | $R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1$ |
| Increment | $R_2 \leftarrow R_1 + 1$ | Add 1 | Loop counter |
| Decrement | $R_2 \leftarrow R_1 - 1$ | Subtract 1 | Loop counter |
| AND | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \wedge R_2$ | Bit-wise AND | Masking, bit extraction |
| OR | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \vee R_2$ | Bit-wise OR | Bit set, data merge |
| XOR | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2$ | Bit-wise XOR | Bit complement, compare |
| NOT | $R_2 \leftarrow \overline{R_1}$ | 1's complement | Sign extension |
| Shl (Logical) | $R \leftarrow \text{shl} R$ | $\times 2$ | Bit shift, multiply |
| Shr (Logical) | $R \leftarrow \text{shr} R$ | $\div 2$ (unsigned) | Bit shift, divide |
| Cil (Circular) | $R \leftarrow \text{cil} R$ | End-around bit rotation | Bit rotation, hashing |
| Asr (Arithmetic) | $R \leftarrow \text{asr} R$ | $\div 2$ (signed) | Signed division |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hardware Implementation of Subtraction via 2's Complement

**Goal:** Prove that the micro-operation $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$ can be realized using only an adder and inverters, without a separate subtractor circuit.

**Step 1: Express subtraction using 2's complement.**
We know that the 2's complement of a binary number $R_2$ is defined as:
$$\overline{R_2} + 1$$

Therefore, the subtraction $R_1 - R_2$ can be written as:
$$R_1 - R_2 = R_1 + (-R_2) = R_1 + (\overline{R_2} + 1)$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the micro-operation.**
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2 = R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1$$

**Step 3: Identify the carry-in variable.**
In the standard binary adder, the carry-in to the LSB position is denoted by **C**. Therefore:
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_2} + C \quad \text{where } C = 1 \text{ for subtraction}$$

**Step 4: Generate the 1's complement of R2 using NOT gates.**
The expression $\overline{R_2}$ is generated by routing R2 through a bank of NOT gates before feeding it to the adder inputs. A single control line **M** (Mode) selects between ADD and SUBTRACT:
- When $M = 0$ (ADD): Inputs to adder are $R_1$ and $R_2$, with $C = 0$.
- When $M = 1$ (SUBTRACT): Inputs to adder are $R_1$ and $\overline{R_2}$, with $C = 1$.

**Step 5: Final hardware expression.**
The complete binary subtractor is implemented as:
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + (R_2 \oplus M) + M$$

Where $\oplus$ is the XOR operation, and $M$ acts as a "select" line. This is a fundamental KTU derivation often asked in examinations.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Arithmetic Micro-Operations

**Given:** Two 5-bit registers, R1 = 01001 (+9 in 2's complement) and R2 = 00101 (+5 in 2's complement).

**Task 1: Compute $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 01001 \quad (+9) \\
R_2 &= 00101 \quad (+5) \\
\hline
R_1 + R_2 &= 01110 \quad (+14) \\
R_3 &= 01110
\end{aligned}
$$

**Task 2: Compute $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$ (i.e., $9 - 5 = +4$)**

**Logic:** Take 2's complement of R2 and add to R1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{R_2} &= 11010 \quad (\text{1's complement of } R_2) \\
\overline{R_2} + 1 &= 11011 \quad (\text{2's complement of } R_2 = -5) \\
R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1 &= 01001 + 11011 = 100100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Discarding the carry-out (since 5-bit register), $R_3 = 00100$ (+4). **Result verified.**

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Shift Micro-Operations

**Given:** R1 = 10011001 (8-bit register, 2's complement = -103 in decimal)

| Operation | Symbolic Form | Result (Binary) | Decimal Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logical Shift Left | $R \leftarrow \text{shl} R$ | 00110010 | Discard MSB (1) lost |
| Logical Shift Right | $R \leftarrow \text{shr} R$ | 01001100 | 0 shifted in at MSB |
| Circular Shift Left | $R \leftarrow \text{cil} R$ | 00110011 | MSB fed back to LSB |
| Circular Shift Right | $R \leftarrow \text{cir} R$ | 11001100 | LSB fed back to MSB |
| Arithmetic Shift Right | $R \leftarrow \text{asr} R$ | 11001100 | Sign bit (1) preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice that the Logical Shift Right of 10011001 (which is -103) yields 01001100 (+76), changing both the value AND the sign. In contrast, **Arithmetic Shift Right** yields 11001100 (-52), preserving the sign and approximating division by 2 (-103 / 2 = -51.5, truncated to -52). This is a critical distinction frequently tested in KTU problems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Combining Micro-Operations

The 4-bit control word for the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) of a standard processor is structured as follows:

| Bit Position | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **S₁** | **S₀** | **Cᵢₙ** | **M** |
| Function | Selects Operation Group | Sub-select within group | Carry-in to LSB | Mode (Arithmetic / Logic) |

- **M = 0** → Logic micro-operations (uses S₁, S₀).
- **M = 1** → Arithmetic micro-operations (uses S₁, S₀, Cᵢₙ).
- **Cᵢₙ** is meaningful only in arithmetic mode (used for carry-in during ADD with carry, or for forcing C = 1 during SUB).

| S₁ | S₀ | M = 0 (Logic) | M = 1 (Arithmetic, Cᵢₙ = 0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $F = A \wedge B$ | $F = A + B$ |
| 0 | 1 | $F = A \vee B$ | $F = A + B + 1$ |
| 1 | 0 | $F = A \oplus B$ | $F = A + \overline{B}$ |
| 1 | 1 | $F = \overline{A}$ | $F = A + \overline{B} + 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a 1-Bit ALU Slice (General Structure)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a single bit-slice of an Arithmetic Logic Unit is interconnected. The select lines (S₁, S₀, Cᵢₙ, M) control the multiplexer to choose between arithmetic, logic, and shift outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input A] --> and1[AND gate]
    A --> or1[OR gate]
    A --> xor1[XOR gate]
    A --> not1[NOT gate]
    B[Input B] --> and1
    B --> or1
    B --> xor1
    B --> not1
    and1 --> mux1[MUX 4 to 1 - Logic]
    or1 --> mux1
    xor1 --> mux1
    not1 --> mux1
    mux1 --> mux2[MUX 2 to 1 - Arithmetic vs Logic]
    F1[Full Adder Stage] --> mux2
    Cin[Carry In Cin] --> F1
    B --> F1
    A --> F1
    mux2 --> Fout[Output F]
    Cout[Carry Out Cout] --> F1
    S1[S1] --> mux1
    S0[S0] --> mux1
    S1 --> mux2
    S0 --> mux2
    M[Mode M] --> mux2
    Cin --> mux2
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Register with Shift Capability

This Mermaid diagram represents the architecture of an n-bit register that supports all four types of shift micro-operations, controlled by two select inputs (H₁ and H₀) — a classic KTU question (frequently asked in Module 2).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Bus[Common Bus D] --> MuxA[Multiplexer A]
    ShiftOut[Shift Right Serial Input SRI] --> MuxA
    ShiftIn[Shift Left Serial Input SLI] --> MuxA
    MuxA --> Reg[Parallel Load Register n bits]
    Reg --> MuxB[Output Multiplexer]
    MuxB --> BusOut[Bus D Output]
    H0[Select H0] --> MuxA
    H1[Select H1] --> MuxB
    H0 --> MuxB
    H1 --> MuxA
    Reg --> MuxC[End around carry MUX]
    MuxC --> ShiftIn
    MuxC --> ShiftOut
    Cout[Serial Output] --> MuxC
```

#### Function Table of the 4-Bit Shift Register

| H₁ | H₀ | Register Operation | Functional Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | No shift | Hold previous data, output = 0 (Clear) |
| 0 | 1 | Shift Right | Load SRI, shift existing bits right, output = MSB |
| 1 | 0 | Shift Left | Load SLI, shift existing bits left, output = 0 (No change) |
| 1 | 1 | Load | Parallel load from bus, output = input bit |

---

### 4.3 Data Flow Diagram: Micro-Operation Execution Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Program Counter] --> MAR[Memory Address Register]
    MAR --> MM[Main Memory]
    MM --> MBR[Memory Buffer Register]
    MBR --> IR[Instruction Register]
    IR --> CU[Control Unit]
    CU -->|Control Signals| ALU
    CU -->|S1 S0 M Cin| ALU
    R1[Register R1] --> ALU
    R2[Register R2] --> ALU
    ALU --> R3[Register R3 Output]
    ALU --> Status[Status Flags Z N C V]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, 3 Marks]`
**Define the term "micro-operation". Differentiate between arithmetic, logic, and shift micro-operations with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **micro-operation** is an elementary operation performed on the data stored in registers or memory during a single clock pulse. It is the most basic data-processing activity of the CPU.

- **Arithmetic Micro-Operations:** Involve numerical computation by the ALU. Example: $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ (Addition).
- **Logic Micro-Operations:** Perform bit-wise Boolean operations on registers. Example: $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 \wedge R_2$ (AND).
- **Shift Micro-Operations:** Transfer bits to adjacent positions in the register. Example: $R_2 \leftarrow \text{shl} \, R_1$ (Shift Left).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, 3 Marks]`
**Explain the function of the mode control input **M** in a basic ALU.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Mode control input (M)** in a basic ALU determines whether the ALU performs **arithmetic** or **logic** micro-operations.

- When **M = 0**, the ALU operates in **Logic mode** — the select inputs S₁ and S₀ choose between AND, OR, XOR, or NOT (i.e., the 4 basic logic operations).
- When **M = 1**, the ALU operates in **Arithmetic mode** — the inputs S₁, S₀, and the carry-in **Cᵢₙ** determine whether the operation is ADD, ADD with carry, Subtract, or Subtract with borrow.

This single control line **multiplexes** the output of the logic and arithmetic circuits within the ALU.

---

### 📗 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **arithmetic micro-operations** performed by a basic ALU. Show the function table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design the **hardware implementation of subtraction** $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$ using an adder, NOT gates, and a 2-to-1 multiplexer. Derive the Boolean expression for the adder inputs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Arithmetic Micro-Operations in a Basic ALU:**

A basic ALU is a combinational circuit that performs arithmetic and logic operations on n-bit operands. The block diagram consists of:
- An **n-bit full adder** as the central arithmetic engine.
- Two multiplexer stages: one to select between $R_2$ and $\overline{R_2}$, and another to route either the adder output (M=1) or the logic output (M=0) to the final output $F$.
- Four control inputs: **S₁, S₀, Cᵢₙ, M**.

**Function Table (Arithmetic Mode, M = 1):**

| S₁ | S₀ | Cᵢₙ | Y = Output | Description | Micro-Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | $D = A + B$ | Addition | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2$ |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | $D = A + B + 1$ | Add with carry | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2 + 1$ |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | $D = A + \overline{B}$ | Subtract (A - B - 1) | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_2}$ |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | $D = A + \overline{B} + 1$ | Subtract (A - B) | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$ |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $D = A$ | Transfer A | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1$ |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | $D = A + 1$ | Increment A | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + 1$ |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | $D = A - 1$ | Decrement A | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - 1$ |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | $D = A$ | Transfer A | $R_3 \leftarrow R_1$ |

**(b) Hardware Implementation of Subtraction:**

We need to implement: $R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 - R_2 &= R_1 + (-R_2) \\
&= R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1 \quad \text{(2's complement of } R_2\text{)} \\
&= R_1 + \overline{R_2} + C \quad \text{where } C = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hardware Realization:**

- **Step 1:** A bank of n **NOT gates** takes R₂ as input and produces $\overline{R_2}$.
- **Step 2:** A **2-to-1 MUX** selects between $R_2$ and $\overline{R_2}$ based on the mode control **M**:
  - When $M = 0$ (ADD): MUX output = $R_2$
  - When $M = 1$ (SUB): MUX output = $\overline{R_2}$
- **Step 3:** The MUX output is connected to input **B** of the **n-bit binary adder**. The other input **A** is connected directly to $R_1$.
- **Step 4:** The carry-in **Cᵢₙ** to the adder is also connected to **M** (since $C = 1$ for subtraction).

**Final Boolean Expression for Adder Inputs:**

$$Y_i = R_1_i$$
$$B_i = R_2_i \oplus M \quad \text{(using XOR to conditionally invert)}$$
$$C_{\text{in}} = M$$

**Result:** The output of the adder is the desired result. The same circuit handles BOTH addition (M=0) and subtraction (M=1), saving hardware.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **four types of shift micro-operations** with suitable diagrams and examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A register R1 contains the 8-bit value **R1 = 10110011**. Perform the following micro-operations and show the result in binary and decimal (assume 2's complement representation):
- (i) Logical Shift Right
- (ii) Circular Shift Left
- (iii) Arithmetic Shift Right
- (iv) $R_2 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_1} + 1$ **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Four Types of Shift Micro-Operations:**

**Type 1: Logical Shift Right (shr)**
Bits are shifted one position to the right. The LSB is lost, and a **0** enters at the MSB position.
- Example: $R = 10011001$ → $\text{shr} \, R = 01001100$
- Operation: $R_2 \leftarrow \text{shr} \, R_1$

**Type 2: Circular Shift Right (cir)**
Bits are shifted one position to the right. The LSB is **rotated back** to the MSB position (no bit is lost).
- Example: $R = 10011001$ → $\text{cir} \, R = 11001100$ (1 wraps around to MSB)

**Type 3: Logical Shift Left (shl)**
Bits are shifted one position to the left. The MSB is lost, and a **0** enters at the LSB position.
- Example: $R = 10011001$ → $\text{shl} \, R = 00110010$

**Type 4: Arithmetic Shift Right (asr)**
Bits are shifted one position to the right. The **sign bit (MSB) is preserved**, and the LSB is lost.
- Example: $R = 10011001$ ($-103$) → $\text{asr} \, R = 11001100$ ($-52$)
- This operation effectively performs signed division by 2.

**(b) Numerical Computations for R1 = 10110011 (2's complement, 8-bit):**

First, identify the signed value of R1:
$$R_1 = 10110011 = -1 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = -77$$

**(i) Logical Shift Right (shr):**
$$R_1 = 10110011 \xrightarrow{\text{shr}} 01011001 \quad = +89 \text{ (unsigned)}$$
MSB filled with 0, LSB (1) discarded.

**(ii) Circular Shift Left (cil):**
$$R_1 = 10110011 \xrightarrow{\text{cil}} 01100111$$
MSB (1) discarded, remaining bits shift left, MSB value wraps to LSB.

**(iii) Arithmetic Shift Right (asr):**
$$R_1 = 10110011 \xrightarrow{\text{asr}} 11011001$$
Sign bit (1) preserved. Decimal value: $-39$. Notice that $-77 \div 2 = -38.5$, truncated to $-39$. ✓

**(iv) Compute $R_2 \leftarrow R_1 + \overline{R_1} + 1$:**

This expression is the **2's complement of R1 added to R1**, which should yield **0** (or -128 in 8-bit overflow, depending on representation).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 10110011 \\
\overline{R_1} &= 01001100 \\
\overline{R_1} + 1 &= 01001101 \quad \text{(2's complement of } R_1 = +77) \\
R_1 + \overline{R_1} + 1 &= 10110011 + 01001101 = 100000000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Discarding the carry-out (9th bit): $R_2 = 00000000 = 0$. **The result is zero**, confirming the property $X + \overline{X} + 1 = 0$ (ignoring carry).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Arithmetic vs Logical Shift Right** — In logical shift, the MSB becomes 0; in arithmetic shift, the MSB is preserved. This costs **2-3 marks** in Part B questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in 2's complement subtraction** — Students often write $R_1 + \overline{R_2}$ and forget the carry-in. The correct expression is $R_1 + \overline{R_2} + C_{\text{in}}$ with $C_{\text{in}} = 1$.
> 3. **Selecting wrong rows of the 16-function logic table** — The KTU logic table has 4 select inputs (S₃, S₂, S₁, S₀), not 2. Always verify the number of select lines before writing the table.
> 4. **Confusing "Register Transfer" with "Micro-operation"** — Register transfer describes the movement of data between registers (e.g., $R_2 \leftarrow R_1$). Micro-operations include arithmetic, logic, and shift — not just transfer.
> 5. **Missing the Discard of Carry-Out** — In 8-bit arithmetic, a 9th carry bit is always produced. **Always state explicitly** that the carry-out is discarded for the result to be valid.
> 6. **Assuming unsigned representation** — Unless the problem says "unsigned", the KTU default for arithmetic shift is **2's complement signed representation**.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Micro-operations** are the most basic, atomic CPU actions executed in a single clock pulse.
- The **four categories** are: Register Transfer, Arithmetic, Logic, and Shift.
- **Arithmetic operations** (ADD, SUB, INC, DEC) are executed by the ALU's adder and use 2's complement representation.
- **Subtraction via addition**: $R_1 - R_2 = R_1 + \overline{R_2} + 1$ — this is the **single most important formula** in Module 2.
- **Logic operations** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT) are bit-wise and operate in parallel on all bits of the registers.
- The **16 logic micro-operations** require **4 select lines** ($S_3, S_2, S_1, S_0$).
- **Shift operations** come in four flavors: **Logical Shift Right, Logical Shift Left, Circular Shift, Arithmetic Shift Right**.
- **Arithmetic Shift Right preserves the sign bit** — it implements signed division by 2.
- **Circular shift (rotation)** does not lose any bit — the MSB wraps to the LSB and vice versa.
- The **ALU control word** uses signals **S₁, S₀, Cᵢₙ, M** to select between 8 arithmetic and 4 logic operations.
- The **Mode bit (M)** distinguishes arithmetic (M=1) from logic (M=0) operations within the ALU.
- **Selective Set, Clear, and Complement** are three classic KTU applications of logic micro-operations.
- A **shift register with 4 operations** uses **2 select lines (H₁, H₀)** to choose between No shift, Shift Right, Shift Left, and Parallel Load.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processor Logic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Processor Logic Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Processor Logic Design** is the systematic engineering discipline concerned with the design, interconnection, and operational coordination of the **data path**, the **control unit**, and the **register organization** within a Central Processing Unit (CPU). It defines *how instructions are fetched, decoded, executed, and stored back*, and *how micro-operations are sequenced and routed* through the processor's internal hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Mano & Hamacher Standard):** Processor logic design defines the logical structure of a CPU including:
> (1) A **register set** for temporary storage
> (2) An **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** for data transformations
> (3) A **Control Unit (CU)** that orchestrates micro-operations
> (4) **Internal data paths and buses** for information transfer

The discipline is fundamentally classified into two major design philosophies:
- **Hardwired (Wired) Control Design** — control logic built from fixed combinational/sequential circuits (FSMs).
- **Microprogrammed Control Design** — control logic stored as microinstructions in a **Control Memory (CM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in KTU:** Processor logic design forms the conceptual backbone for understanding pipelining, RISC/CISC trade-offs, and modern CPU micro-architectures (e.g., ARM Cortex pipeline stages). Most 14-mark KTU questions in Module 2 are drawn directly from data path design and control unit synthesis.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the **CPU as a highly disciplined restaurant kitchen**:

| Kitchen Element | Processor Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Head Chef issuing orders | **Control Unit** | Decodes "instruction" and shouts micro-instructions |
| Cooking stations (grill, oven, salad) | **ALU + functional units** | Performs transformations on ingredients |
| Storage containers (fridge, shelves) | **Register File** | Holds temporary ingredients/data |
| Waiter carrying dishes | **Internal Buses** | Transports data between units |
| Recipe book | **Control Memory (microprogram)** or **Hardwired FSM** | Defines the precise sequence of operations |
| Order ticket | **Instruction Register (IR)** | Holds the current operation to be performed |

When a customer places an order (**machine instruction**), the head chef (**control unit**) reads the recipe book, picks the right ingredients from the fridge (**registers**), sends them to the appropriate station (**ALU**), and delivers the finished dish to the pass (**destination register/memory**). Processor logic design is the *blueprint* of this entire workflow.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Knobs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Board Exams):**
> - **Clock Cycle Time ($T_{clk}$)** — measured in **nanoseconds (ns) or picoseconds (ps)**
> - **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$)** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, typically **MHz / GHz**
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** — dimensionless, typically **1 to 5+**
> - **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)** — unit: **10⁶ instructions/sec**
> - **Execution Time ($T_{exec}$)** — measured in **seconds (s)**

$$T_{exec} = (\text{Instruction Count}) \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

$$MIPS = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$$

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Processor Logic Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Triad of CPU Architecture — Memorize This for KTU:**
> 1. **Data Path** — the "muscles" that move and transform data (registers, ALU, multiplexers, buses).
> 2. **Control Unit** — the "brain" that issues control signals to orchestrate the data path.
> 3. **Interface (Bus System)** — the "nervous system" connecting CPU to memory and I/O.

Every KTU processor logic design problem reduces to designing one or more of these three subsystems, then tying them together with **Register Transfer Language (RTL)** and a precise **micro-operation sequence**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the CPU — Block-Level Decomposition

A canonical **single-bus CPU** (Mano's classic model) is composed of the following functional blocks:

1. **Register File** — contains **PC, AR (Address Register), DR (Data Register), AC (Accumulator), IR (Instruction Register), TR (Temporary Register), INPR (Input Register), OUTR (Output Register)**.
2. **ALU** — performs arithmetic (ADD, SUB, INC, DEC) and logic (AND, OR, XOR, COM, SHL, SHR) operations.
3. **Control Unit** — generates timing signals: $D_0, D_1, \ldots, D_7$ (decode signals) and control signals $C_0 \ldots C_n$.
4. **Memory Address Register (AR)** and **Memory Data Register (MDR / DR)**.
5. **Common Bus** — a single set of $n$ lines shared by all registers, controlled by multiplexers / bus-select signals $S_2 S_1 S_0$.

---

## 2.2 The Register File (Mano's Standard Set)

| Register | Symbol | Width (bits) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Counter | **PC** | 12 | Holds address of next instruction |
| Address Register | **AR** | 12 | Holds memory address for read/write |
| Data Register | **DR** | 16 | Holds memory operand (data) |
| Accumulator | **AC** | 16 | Primary ALU operand / result holder |
| Instruction Register | **IR** | 16 | Holds fetched instruction |
| Temporary Register | **TR** | 16 | Scratchpad for ALU operations |
| Input Register | **INPR** | 8 | Receives data from external input device |
| Output Register | **OUTR** | 8 | Holds data for external output device |
| Memory Buffer Register | **MBR** | 16 | Optional — buffer between CPU & memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "List the registers in a basic computer and state the function of each." This table is the *exact answer expected by examiners*.

---

## 2.3 Common Bus System & Multiplexer Selection

A **common bus** allows multiple registers to share a single data path. Selection is via a **multiplexer** controlled by select lines $S_2, S_1, S_0$ (3 lines → 8 registers, octal selection).

**Bus Selection Logic:**

| $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | Register Placed on Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | **PC** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **AR** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **DR** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **AC** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | **IR** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **TR** |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | **INPR** |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **OUTR** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory-Mapped I/O Insight:** In Mano's basic computer, INPR and OUTR communicate with the I/O device via the **FGI (Input Flag)** and **FGO (Output Flag)** interrupt-like handshakes. The CPU checks these flags before servicing I/O.

---

## 2.4 Micro-Operations — The Atom of Processor Behavior

**Micro-operations** are the elementary register-transfer operations executed in **one clock cycle**. They are of three fundamental types:

### Type 1: Register Transfer Micro-Operations
$$R_2 \leftarrow R_1$$
Transfer content of $R_1$ to $R_2$. Activated by a single control signal: $R_2^{in} \cdot R_1^{out} \cdot \text{CLK}$.

### Type 2: Arithmetic Micro-Operations
$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 + R_2 \quad \text{(Addition)}$$

$$R_3 \leftarrow R_1 - R_2 \quad \text{(Subtraction via 2's complement)}$$

$$R_2 \leftarrow \overline{R_2} \quad \text{(1's complement, i.e., bitwise NOT)}$$

$$R_2 \leftarrow \overline{R_2} + 1 \quad \text{(2's complement / Negation)}$$

$$R \leftarrow R + 1 \quad \text{(Increment)}$$

$$R \leftarrow R - 1 \quad \text{(Decrement)}$$

### Type 3: Logic Micro-Operations
$$P: \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 \oplus R_2 \quad \text{(XOR)}$$

$$P: \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 \wedge R_2 \quad \text{(AND)}$$

$$P: \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 \vee R_2 \quad \text{(OR)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shift Micro-Operations** (Often a 4th category in KTU):
> - **Logical Shift Left (SHL):** $R \leftarrow \text{shl}(R)$
> - **Logical Shift Right (SHR):** $R \leftarrow \text{shr}(R)$
> - **Circular Shift (Rotate):** End-around bit wrap
> - **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASHR):** Preserves sign bit (sign extension)

---

## 2.5 The Instruction Cycle — Three Phases

The **Instruction Cycle** is the master loop that the CPU executes continuously. It is decomposed into:

### Phase 1: Fetch Phase (Universal — Same for Every Instruction)
$$T_0: \quad AR \leftarrow PC$$
$$T_1: \quad IR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$
$$T_2: \quad D_0 \ldots D_7 \leftarrow \text{Decode}(IR(12\text{-}14)), \quad AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$$

### Phase 2: Decode Phase
The **3-bit opcode** ($IR_{12\text{-}14}$) activates exactly one decode line $D_i$, which sets up the control signals for the next phase.

### Phase 3: Execute Phase (Instruction-Specific)
Each instruction has its own micro-operation sequence in $T_3, T_4, T_5, \ldots$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** When asked "Explain the instruction cycle," you *must* draw the **flowchart of fetch-decode-execute** with the micro-operations $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots$ labeled at each transition. This is a guaranteed 7+ marks in any 14-mark question.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{exec} = N_{inst} \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | Total execution time | Performance analysis |
| $MIPS = f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^{6})$ | Throughput in MIPS | Speed comparison |
| $CPI = \sum_i (CPI_i \times IC_i) / IC_{total}$ | Average CPI | Weighted performance |
| $CPU\ Time = IC \times CPI / f_{clk}$ | Alternate form of exec time | Benchmarks |
| $S = 1 + \sum_{j} F_j$ | Speedup with enhancement $j$ | Amdahl's Law pre-stage |
| $n$-bit bus capacity $= n \times f_{clk}$ | Data throughput (bits/s) | Bus bandwidth |
| Memory address space $= 2^k$ words | Where $k$ = address bus bits | Addressability |
| Memory capacity $= 2^k \times n$ bits | Total memory size | Capacity calculation |
| Control Memory size $= 2^k \times n$ | Microprogram ROM size | Microprogrammed CU |
| Microinstruction size = Control word bits | = Number of control signals | CU design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** A **Horizontal Microinstruction** has one bit per control signal (wide, fast, large CM). A **Vertical Microinstruction** has encoded fields (narrow, slow, small CM). KTU expects you to know the trade-off.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters beyond exams:** Modern processor design at Intel, AMD, ARM, Apple, and Qualcomm is the direct industrial descendant of these principles:
> - **Apple M-series (ARM-based):** Uses microprogrammed boot logic and hardwired decode for speed-critical paths.
> - **Intel x86 (CISC):** Translates complex x86 instructions into RISC-like micro-ops (μops) — a direct application of **microprogrammed control** concepts.
> - **Embedded microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR):** Use **hardwired control** for cost and power efficiency — small instruction set, fixed FSM.
> - **GPU Shader Cores:** Rely on **VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)** and horizontal microinstruction concepts to schedule hundreds of ALU operations in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Fetch Phase Micro-Operation Sequence (Mano's Basic Computer)

The fetch phase is a **3-cycle ($T_0, T_1, T_2$)**, universal sequence executed for *every* instruction. The derivation is rooted in the requirement that we must:
1. Get the address of the next instruction.
2. Read the instruction from memory.
3. Decode the opcode and prepare the operand address.

**Step 1 — Address Transfer ($T_0$):** The address of the current instruction must be presented to memory. The PC holds this address. We transfer it to the AR so memory can latch it.

$$T_0: \quad AR \leftarrow PC$$

**Step 2 — Memory Read & PC Increment ($T_1$):** Memory reads $M[AR]$ (the instruction) into IR. Simultaneously, the PC is incremented to point to the next sequential instruction. (This parallelism is why we use a common bus and ALU.)

$$T_1: \quad IR \leftarrow M[AR], \quad PC \leftarrow PC + 1$$

**Step 3 — Decode & Operand Address Setup ($T_2$):** The 3-bit opcode $IR(12\text{-}14)$ is decoded into $D_0 \ldots D_7$. The 12-bit address field $IR(0\text{-}11)$ (if direct addressing) is transferred to AR for the execute phase.

$$T_2: \quad D_0 \ldots D_7 \leftarrow \text{Decode}(IR(12\text{-}14)), \quad AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The reason $T_1$ combines memory read and PC increment is **resource sharing** — the ALU is free during $T_1$ (not doing instruction-specific work), so we use it to increment PC. This is a classic example of **micro-operation scheduling for hardware efficiency**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Execute Phase for Common Instructions

We derive the micro-operation sequences for the four most-tested KTU instructions.

### 3.2.1 Instruction: ADD (Memory-Reference, Opcode = 001)

**Goal:** $AC \leftarrow AC + M[AR]$

After $T_0, T_1, T_2$ (fetch), the operand is in memory at $AR$. We need to read it into DR, add via ALU, and store back to AC.

$$T_3: \quad DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$
$$T_4: \quad AC \leftarrow AC + DR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0 \text{ (clear sequence counter)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two cycles $T_3, T_4$?** Cycle $T_3$ retrieves the operand into DR (memory read is slow — ~50–100 ns). Cycle $T_4$ performs the actual ALU addition. Separating memory access from ALU operation **avoids bus contention** and **maximizes clock frequency**.

### 3.2.2 Instruction: LDA (Load to AC, Opcode = 010)

**Goal:** $AC \leftarrow M[AR]$

$$T_3: \quad DR \leftarrow M[AR]$$
$$T_4: \quad AC \leftarrow DR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0$$

### 3.2.3 Instruction: STA (Store AC, Opcode = 011)

**Goal:** $M[AR] \leftarrow AC$

$$T_3: \quad M[AR] \leftarrow AC, \quad SC \leftarrow 0$$

Only **one** execute cycle because we write *directly* from AC to memory — no ALU operation needed.

### 3.2.4 Instruction: BUN (Branch Unconditionally, Opcode = 100)

**Goal:** $PC \leftarrow AR$

$$T_3: \quad PC \leftarrow AR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0$$

This is a **single-cycle branch** — the operand address $IR(0\text{-}11)$ is already in AR after $T_2$, so we just transfer it to PC.

### 3.2.5 Instruction: BSA (Branch and Save Return Address, Opcode = 101)

**Goal:** $M[AR] \leftarrow PC, \quad PC \leftarrow AR + 1$

This is a **subroutine call** — we save the return address at the operand location, then jump to $AR+1$.

$$T_3: \quad M[AR] \leftarrow PC, \quad AR \leftarrow AR + 1$$
$$T_4: \quad PC \leftarrow AR, \quad SC \leftarrow 0$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Micro-Operation Timing for All Instruction Types

This is the **complete truth table** Mano expects KTU students to memorize. The Symbol **M[AR]** denotes a memory read/write, **Dᵢ** is the decode output, **SC** is the Sequence Counter.

| Instruction | Opcode ($IR_{12-14}$) | $T_3$ | $T_4$ | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AND** | 000 | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $AC \leftarrow AC \land DR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Bitwise AND |
| **ADD** | 001 | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Add |
| **LDA** | 010 | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $AC \leftarrow DR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Load |
| **STA** | 011 | $M[AR] \leftarrow AC, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Store |
| **BUN** | 100 | $PC \leftarrow AR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Branch |
| **BSA** | 101 | $M[AR] \leftarrow PC, AR \leftarrow AR+1$ | $PC \leftarrow AR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Branch & Save |
| **ISZ** | 110 | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $DR \leftarrow DR+1, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Incr & Skip if Zero |
| **CLA** | 0111 (Reg) | $AC \leftarrow 0, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Clear AC |
| **CLE** | 0101 (Reg) | $E \leftarrow 0, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Clear E (Carry) |
| **CMA** | 0110 (Reg) | $AC \leftarrow \overline{AC}, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Complement AC |
| **CME** | 1001 (Reg) | $E \leftarrow \overline{E}, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Complement E |
| **CIR** | 1010 (Reg) | $AC \leftarrow \text{shr}(AC), AC(15) \leftarrow E, E \leftarrow AC(0)$ | — | Circulate right |
| **CIL** | 1011 (Reg) | $AC \leftarrow \text{shl}(AC), AC(0) \leftarrow E, E \leftarrow AC(15)$ | — | Circulate left |
| **INC** | 1100 (Reg) | $AC \leftarrow AC+1, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Increment |
| **SPA** | 1101 (Reg) | If $AC(15)=0$: $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | — | Skip if AC positive |
| **SNA** | 1110 (Reg) | If $AC(15)=1$: $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | — | Skip if AC negative |
| **SZA** | 1111 (Reg) | If $AC=0$: $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | — | Skip if AC zero |
| **HLT** | 0001 (Reg) | $S \leftarrow 0$ (halt processor) | — | Halt CPU |
| **INP** | 0010 (I/O) | $AC(0\text{-}7) \leftarrow INPR, FGI \leftarrow 0$ | — | Input char |
| **OUT** | 0011 (I/O) | $OUTR \leftarrow AC(0\text{-}7), FGO \leftarrow 0$ | — | Output char |
| **SKI** | 0100 (I/O) | If $FGI=1$: $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | — | Skip on input flag |
| **SKO** | 0101 (I/O) | If $FGO=1$: $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | — | Skip on output flag |
| **ION** | 0110 (I/O) | $IEN \leftarrow 1, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Interrupt enable |
| **IOF** | 0111 (I/O) | $IEN \leftarrow 0, SC \leftarrow 0$ | — | Interrupt disable |

> [!NOTE]
> **ISZ subtlety:** The instruction **ISZ (Increment and Skip if Zero)** does *not* check the zero condition in $T_4$. After $DR \leftarrow DR+1$ and $M[AR] \leftarrow DR$ (write back), the next fetch cycle's $T_0$ checks the zero flag. Students commonly lose marks by claiming ISZ skips within $T_4$ — it doesn't.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hardwired Control Unit Boolean Equations

A **hardwired control unit** is a finite state machine. Each control signal $C_i$ is a Boolean function of:
- The **timing signal** $T_j$ (from sequence counter).
- The **decode signal** $D_i$ (from opcode).
- **Status flags** ($I$, the interrupt status; $E$, the carry flag; $FGI$, $FGO$, etc.).

### Example 1: Control Signal for $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ (Memory Read to DR)

This micro-operation occurs during:
- **$T_3$ of AND, ADD, LDA, ISZ** (any instruction needing $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$).

So:
$$Read = T_3 \cdot (D_0 + D_1 + D_2 + D_6)$$

Where $D_0$ = AND, $D_1$ = ADD, $D_2$ = LDA, $D_6$ = ISZ.

### Example 2: Control Signal for $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$ (ALU Add)

This occurs during:
- **$T_4$ of ADD only.**

$$Add = T_4 \cdot D_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "design the hardwired control for a micro-operation," write the equation in the form:
> $$\text{Control Signal} = (\text{Timing}) \cdot \sum (\text{Relevant Decodes})$$
> and then draw the **AND-OR logic gate circuit** for it. This is worth 7 marks in a 14-mark problem.

### Example 3: Halt Control Signal

$$Halt = T_3 \cdot D_7 \cdot (B'_{11})$$

(For register-reference instruction 1111 = HLT, $B'_{11}$ confirms the bit pattern.)

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Microprogrammed Control Unit — Address Generation

In a **microprogrammed control unit**, each machine instruction is implemented as a **microprogram** stored in the **Control Memory (CM)**. The structure:

$$CAR \leftarrow \text{Next Address Logic}(CAR, CD, BR)$$

Where:
- $CAR$ = **Control Address Register** (the μPC — micro-program counter).
- $CD$ = **Condition / Decode** signals.
- $BR$ = **Branch** field from the current microinstruction.

**Next-Address Logic Formulas:**

1. **Sequential (no branch):** $CAR \leftarrow CAR + 1$
2. **Unconditional branch to address $A$:** $CAR \leftarrow A$
3. **Conditional branch (if condition $C$ true):** $CAR \leftarrow A \text{ if } C, \text{ else } CAR \leftarrow CAR+1$
4. **Mapping from opcode to micro-routine starting address:** $CAR \leftarrow (D_i \cdot \text{CMAP})$ where CMAP is the **CAM (Content Addressable Memory)**-based opcode-to-address mapper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct vs. Indirect Microprogrammed Control:**
> - **Direct:** Each microinstruction explicitly contains the address of the next microinstruction (1-bit branch + 1 address field).
> - **Indirect:** The next-address field is shorter; the high-order bits come from a saved location (μstack or subroutines). Saves control memory space.

---

## 3.6 Performance Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Processor Enhancements

Given a processor enhancement that speeds up a *fraction $F$* of the execution by a factor of $S$, the **overall speedup** is:

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \frac{F}{S}}$$

**Example Calculation:** Suppose a **floating-point unit (FPU)** enhancement makes FP operations run **10× faster**, and FP operations account for **40%** of program execution time. What is the overall speedup?

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- $F = 0.40$ (fraction of time spent in FP)
- $S = 10$ (speedup of FP)
- Unenhanced fraction: $1 - F = 0.60$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Amdahl's Law.

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.40) + \frac{0.40}{10}}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the denominator.

$$S_{overall} = \frac{1}{0.60 + 0.04} = \frac{1}{0.64}$$

**Step 4:** Compute final speedup.

$$S_{overall} = 1.5625$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with an "infinite speedup" of the FP unit ($S \rightarrow \infty$), the overall speedup is bounded by $1 / (1 - F) = 1 / 0.60 \approx 1.667$. **This is Amdahl's curse** — you can never make a program run infinitely fast by speeding up only one part. KTU loves this kind of numerical question.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Parameter | **Hardwired Control** | **Microprogrammed Control** |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational + Sequential gates | ROM / PLA storing microinstructions |
| Speed | **Very fast** (direct logic) | Slower (ROM access ~ 50–100 ns) |
| Flexibility | **Rigid** — redesign requires rewiring | **Flexible** — change microprogram in ROM |
| Complexity for large ISA | Combinatorial explosion | **Scales gracefully** |
| Cost | High for complex CISC | Lower for complex CISC |
| Typical Use | RISC, simple microcontrollers | CISC (e.g., x86, VAX) |
| Control Word Width | 1 bit per signal (horizontal) | Encoded (vertical) — narrower |
| Control Memory Size | 0 (no ROM) | $2^k \times n$ bits |
| Design Time | Long, error-prone | **Short, systematic** |
| Debugging | Difficult | Easier — change microcode |
| Power Consumption | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Best Suited For | ≤ 50 instructions | 100+ instructions |

> [!NOTE]
