# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Register Organization

Registers are **high-speed storage elements** built directly into the CPU using flip-flops. They are organized into two broad categories:

### 2.1.1 User-Visible Registers
These are visible to the programmer (or compiler) and are used for fast data handling.

| Register Type | Purpose | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** | Hold operands and results of any operation | R0–R15 in ARM, AX/BX/CX/DX in x86 |
| **Data Registers** | Hold intermediate/final data | Accumulator (AC) |
| **Address Registers** | Hold memory addresses for various addressing modes | Stack Pointer (SP), Base Register (BR), Index Register (IX) |
| **Condition Codes (Flags)** | Hold status bits from the last ALU operation | Zero (Z), Carry (C), Sign (N), Overflow (V), Parity (P) |

### 2.1.2 Control & Status Registers
These are internal to the processor, used by the CU and OS for managing the machine.

| Register | Function |
|---|---|
| **PC (Program Counter)** | Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch |
| **MAR (Memory Address Register)** | Holds the memory address for a read/write operation |
| **MDR / MBR** | Holds the data being read from or written to memory |
| **IR (Instruction Register)** | Holds the currently executing instruction |
| **PSW (Program Status Word)** | A consolidated register holding flags + interrupt bits + privilege level |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many registers?** Each access to a register takes **1 CPU cycle** (≈ 1 nanosecond), whereas a memory access takes **5–100+ cycles**. Using registers aggressively is the **single biggest performance lever** a programmer has.

---

## 2.2 Stack Organization

A **Stack** is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure residing in memory, but managed by the processor through the **Stack Pointer (SP)** register.

### 2.2.1 Types of Stacks
- **Register Stack** — Implemented as a small set of physical CPU registers (very fast, limited depth, typically 8 or 16).
- **Memory Stack** — Implemented in a region of main memory, with SP pointing to the top element (unlimited depth, slower).

### 2.2.2 Stack Operations (RTL)

$$\text{PUSH (Full Descending): } SP \leftarrow SP - 1$$
$$M[SP] \leftarrow DR$$
(SP moves *downward* toward lower addresses; ARM convention)

$$\text{POP (Full Ascending): } SP \leftarrow SP + 1$$
$$DR \leftarrow M[SP]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack conventions** to remember for KTU questions:
> - **Ascending Stack** → grows toward **higher** addresses.
> - **Descending Stack** → grows toward **lower** addresses.
> - **Full Stack** → SP points to the **last used** (top) location containing data.
> - **Empty Stack** → SP points to the **first free** location (one past top).

| Convention Combination | SP behavior | Example Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| Full Ascending | Decrement-then-store, increment-then-load | x86 (PUSH/POP) |
| Full Descending | Decrement-then-store, load-then-increment | ARM |
| Empty Ascending | Store-then-increment, decrement-then-load | Some MIPS variants |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students mix up the direction. Always draw a small diagram: an arrow indicating the **direction of stack growth** to avoid confusion in the exam.

### 2.2.3 Register Stack (KTU Hot Topic)
A **register stack** uses a fixed array of high-speed registers (e.g., 64 words) with:
- **SP** → points to the topmost filled register.
- **Stack Full (FL)** flag set when all registers are used.
- **Stack Empty (EM)** flag set when stack is empty.

After **PUSH**: $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ and $M[SP] \leftarrow DR$.
After **POP**: $DR \leftarrow M[SP]$ and $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$.

---

## 2.3 Instruction Formats

An **instruction** is a binary word recognized by the CPU. It typically contains:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Opcode** | Specifies the operation (ADD, LOAD, JUMP, etc.) |
| **Address Field(s)** | Specify operand locations (register/memory) |
| **Mode Bits** | Specify addressing mode (immediate, direct, indirect, etc.) |

### 2.3.1 KTU Exam: The Three Instruction Formats

| Format Type | Layout | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Three-Address Instruction** | [Opcode \vert Dest \vert Src1 \vert Src2] | RISC machines, very flexible, large instruction size |
| **Two-Address Instruction** | [Opcode \vert Dest/Src1 \vert Src2] | Common CISC, result overwrites one operand |
| **One-Address Instruction** | [Opcode \vert Address] | Uses implicit **Accumulator (AC)** |
| **Zero-Address Instruction** | [Opcode] | Stack machine, operands implicitly on the stack |

### 2.3.2 The Big Trade-off Equation

If each address field uses **k bits**, and there are **n address fields**:

$$\text{Instruction Length} = \text{Opcode bits} + n \times k$$

| Format | Bits per address field (k) | Total addresses (n) | Total Address bits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Three-Address | 4 | 3 | 12 |
| Two-Address | 4 | 2 | 8 |
| One-Address | 12 | 1 | 12 |
| Zero-Address | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU loves the question: *"Compute the instruction size and number of bits used for opcode and addresses for the expression `X = (A + B) * (C + D)` using three-address, two-address, one-address, and zero-address instructions."* — Memorize this pattern.

### 2.3.3 Expanding Opcode
When the number of addressing modes and registers varies, **expanding opcode** is used. The opcode size is **not constant**; it is shorter for register-only operations and longer when memory references are involved.

---

## 2.4 Addressing Modes (Most Repeated KTU Topic)

An **addressing mode** specifies *how* the operand's effective address is calculated from the bits in the instruction.

| Mode | Effective Address (EA) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | Operand = Value | Constant loading |
| **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field | Simple variable access |
| **Indirect** | EA = Contents(Address field) | Pointer dereferencing |
| **Register** | EA = Register | Fast access |
| **Register Indirect** | EA = Contents(Register) | Pointer using a register |
| **Displacement (Indexed)** | EA = Address field + Index Register | Array traversal |
| **Relative** | EA = PC + Address field | Branch instructions |
| **Auto-increment** | EA = Register; R ← R + 1 | Loop counters |
| **Auto-decrement** | R ← R − 1; EA = Register | Stack-style pops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mantra:** Always start an addressing-mode answer with: *"An addressing mode specifies the rule for interpreting or modifying the address field of an instruction before the operand is actually referenced."*

---

## 2.5 ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) Organization

The ALU performs **micro-operations** like Add, Subtract, AND, OR, XOR, Shift, Compare. The minimal ALU supports:
- Arithmetic: Add, Subtract (via 2's complement), Increment, Decrement.
- Logic: AND, OR, XOR, NOT.
- Shift: Logical shift left/right, rotate, arithmetic shift.

**Key block**: An **n-bit ALU** is built by cascading **n 1-bit ALU slices**, each having:
- Two inputs A_i and B_i
- A carry-in C_in
- A carry-out C_out
- A function select (F_1 F_0)
- A result output F_i
- A status output (carry, zero, sign, overflow, parity)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Design a 4-bit ALU using 1-bit ALU slices"*. You should know the block diagram of a 1-bit ALU and the **truth table** mapping (F_1 F_0) to operations.

---

## 2.6 Control Unit — Two Implementations

| Aspect | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational logic (gates, decoders, ROM) | Control Memory (ROM/RAM) storing microinstructions |
| Speed | **Faster** (no memory access for control) | **Slower** (microinstruction fetch adds delay) |
| Flexibility | **Difficult** to modify / debug | **Easy** to modify (just change ROM) |
| Cost | High for complex ISA | Lower for complex ISA |
| Used in | RISC, simple controllers | CISC (e.g., original IBM 360, x86 microcode) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microprogrammed Control** uses a **Control Memory (CS)** holding a sequence of **microinstructions**. Each machine instruction is executed by a **microprogram** (or **micro-routine**). A **Control Address Register (CAR)** holds the address of the next microinstruction; a **Control Data Register (CDR)** holds the current microinstruction being executed.

### 2.6.1 Microinstruction Format

A horizontal microinstruction has all control signals in parallel:

| F1 (3 bits) | F2 (3 bits) | F3 (3 bits) | CD (2 bits) | BR (2 bits) | AD (7 bits) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

- **F1, F2, F3** → control fields for CPU (ALU, shift, etc.)
- **CD** → Condition field (e.g., always, if Z=1, if C=1)
- **BR** → Branch field (next, jump to subroutine, return, map to next opcode)
- **AD** → Address field (where to branch to)

> [!NOTE]
> A **microinstruction** is the instruction executed by the control unit itself — it is a *micro-level* control word that activates one set of control signals in the datapath for **one clock cycle** (a single micro-operation).

### 2.6.2 Microinstruction Sequencing
The next address for CAR is computed from:
- **CAR + 1** (default sequential)
- **Branch field (BR)** if a condition (CD) is met
- **Mapping logic** to enter a new micro-routine when a new opcode is fetched

### 2.6.3 Wilkes Control
Wilkes (1951) proposed the earliest microprogrammed control. Its microinstruction has:
- A horizontal control field (one bit per control signal).
- A branch address field.
- A branch condition field.

The **next address** is selected via a multiplexer based on the branch condition.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction length | $L = \text{Opcode bits} + n \times k$ | n = number of address fields, k = address field size |
| Memory addresses | $2^k$ | Where k is the number of address bits |
| Total instructions | $2^m$ | Where m is the number of opcode bits |
| Number of micro-operations per cycle | 1 (per microinstruction) | Standard for microprogrammed control |
| SP for descending stack | $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ (on PUSH) | Address decreases |
| SP for ascending stack | $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ (on PUSH) | Address increases |
| Control Memory size | $2^k \times n$ | k = address bits into CM, n = microinstruction width |
| Cycle Time (Hardwired) | $T_h = t_{decoder} + t_{gates}$ | Direct combinational delay |
| Cycle Time (Microprogrammed) | $T_{\mu} = t_{ROM\, access} + t_{exec}$ | ROM read + datapath execution |

> [!WARNING]
> Never write `|x|` in any table cell. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` (LaTeX) to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the table.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler writers** use register-allocation algorithms (graph coloring) that rely on knowing how many GPRs a CPU has.
- **Embedded systems** (ARM Cortex-M) often use a **register-based stack** (MSP/PSP) because RAM is precious and predictable.
- **x86 CPUs** still ship with **microcode ROMs** that fix CPU bugs (e.g., the famous Pentium FDIV bug in 1994 was patched via a microcode update).
- **DSP processors** (TI TMS320) heavily use the **immediate** and **register-indirect** modes for tight loops over sample arrays.

> [!NOTE]
> The microprogrammed control approach made it possible to **emulate one ISA on another** — this is the basis of binary translation (e.g., Apple Rosetta translating x86 → ARM, or QEMU emulating MIPS on x86).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Count for an Arithmetic Expression

**Problem:** Evaluate $X = (A+B) \times (C+D)$ using different instruction formats. Assume **4 registers (R0–R3)**, each addressable in **2 bits**, and one operation code per instruction. Count the **total number of instructions** and **total bits** for each format.

### 3.1.1 Three-Address Format
Here each instruction has opcode + destination + source1 + source2. Registers are 2-bit.

**Step 1 — Add A and B, store in R0:**
Instruction: ADD R0, A, B
RTL: R0 ← M[A] + M[B]

**Step 2 — Add C and D, store in R1:**
Instruction: ADD R1, C, D
RTL: R1 ← M[C] + M[D]

**Step 3 — Multiply R0 and R1, store in R2:**
Instruction: MUL R2, R0, R1
RTL: R2 ← R0 × R1

**Step 4 — Store R2 into X:**
Instruction: MOV X, R2
RTL: M[X] ← R2

**Total instructions = 4.**
Bits per instruction: opcode(4) + 3 × register(2) = 10 bits (assuming registers only).
Total bits: $4 \times 10 = 40$ bits.

> [!NOTE]
> With 4-bit opcode, we can have up to 16 distinct operations. 3 register fields × 2 bits = 6 bits of address. So instruction size = 10 bits.

### 3.1.2 Two-Address Format
Here destination is also a source. Result overwrites the first operand.

$$\text{Step 1: LOAD R0, A} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R0 \leftarrow M[A]$$
$$\text{Step 2: ADD  R0, B} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R0 \leftarrow R0 + M[B]$$
$$\text{Step 3: LOAD R1, C} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R1 \leftarrow M[C]$$
$$\text{Step 4: ADD  R1, D} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R1 \leftarrow R1 + M[D]$$
$$\text{Step 5: MUL  R0, R1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R0 \leftarrow R0 \times R1$$
$$\text{Step 6: STORE X, R0} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M[X] \leftarrow R0$$

**Total instructions = 6.**
Bits: opcode(4) + 2 × register(2) = 8 bits (register–register).
**Total bits:** $6 \times 8 = 48$ bits.

### 3.1.3 One-Address Format (Accumulator-based)
Implicit accumulator (AC) holds one operand and the result.

$$\text{Step 1: LOAD A} \quad\Rightarrow\quad AC \leftarrow M[A]$$
$$\text{Step 2: ADD  B} \quad\Rightarrow\quad AC \leftarrow AC + M[B]$$
$$\text{Step 3: STORE T} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M[T] \leftarrow AC$$
$$\text{Step 4: LOAD C} \quad\Rightarrow\quad AC \leftarrow M[C]$$
$$\text{Step 5: ADD  D} \quad\Rightarrow\quad AC \leftarrow AC + M[D]$$
$$\text{Step 6: MUL  T} \quad\Rightarrow\quad AC \leftarrow AC \times M[T]$$
$$\text{Step 7: STORE X} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M[X] \leftarrow AC$$

**Total instructions = 7.**
Bits: opcode(4) + memory_address(12) = 16 bits.
**Total bits:** $7 \times 16 = 112$ bits.

### 3.1.4 Zero-Address Format (Stack Machine)
All operands are on the stack. No address field needed.

$$\text{Step 1: PUSH A} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Stack} \leftarrow M[A]$$
$$\text{Step 2: PUSH B} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Stack} \leftarrow M[B]$$
$$\text{Step 3: ADD} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Pop B, Pop A, Push } (A+B)$$
$$\text{Step 4: PUSH C}$$
$$\text{Step 5: PUSH D}$$
$$\text{Step 6: ADD} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Push } (C+D)$$
$$\text{Step 7: MUL} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Push } (A+B)\times(C+D)$$
$$\text{Step 8: POP X} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M[X] \leftarrow \text{Stack top}$$

**Total instructions = 8.**
Bits: opcode(4) only = 4 bits.
**Total bits:** $8 \times 4 = 32$ bits.

### 3.1.5 Comparative Summary (Analytical Matrix)

| Format | # Instructions | Bits/Instruction | Total Bits | Code Size Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Three-Address** | 4 | 10 | 40 | Most code, but most compact per instr |
| **Two-Address** | 6 | 8 | 48 | Moderate |
| **One-Address** | 7 | 16 | 112 | Largest code, longest instr |
| **Zero-Address** | 8 | 4 | 32 | Smallest instruction, most instructions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** A three-address machine produces **the fewest instructions**, but a zero-address machine produces **the smallest total binary**. The choice depends on whether memory (for code) or instruction-fetch bandwidth is the bottleneck.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address for Each Addressing Mode

Let EA = Effective Address of the operand. Let A = address field in instruction. Let R = some register.

| Mode | EA formula | Example: A = 200, R = 400, M[400] = 700, M[700] = 800 |
|---|---|---|
| Direct | $EA = A$ | EA = 200 → Operand = M[200] |
| Indirect | $EA = M[A]$ | EA = M[200] |
| Register | $EA = R$ | EA = 400 → Operand = 400 |
| Register Indirect | $EA = M[R]$ | EA = M[400] = 700 → Operand = M[700] = 800 |
| Immediate | Operand = A | Operand = 200 |
| Displacement | $EA = A + R$ | EA = 200 + 400 = 600 → Operand = M[600] |
| Relative | $EA = A + PC$ | EA = 200 + (current PC) |
| Indexed | $EA = A + (R \times \text{size})$ | For arrays: EA = base + index × word_size |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stack Operations on a 64-Word Register Stack

Let the stack have **64 words** (addresses 0 to 63), and SP starts at **0** (empty).

### 3.3.1 PUSH Operation (Ascending, Full)
- **Pre-check:** If SP = 63, set FL (Full) and halt.
- **Action 1:** $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$
- **Action 2:** $Stack[SP] \leftarrow DR$
- **Post-check:** If SP = 0, set EM = 0; clear FL.

**Example:** Push 5, 7, 9 onto an empty stack.

$$\text{Initial: } SP = 0, \, Stack = [\ ,\ ,\ ,\ ...]$$
$$\text{Push 5: } SP = 1, \, Stack[1] = 5$$
$$\text{Push 7: } SP = 2, \, Stack[2] = 7$$
$$\text{Push 9: } SP = 3, \, Stack[3] = 9$$

### 3.3.2 POP Operation
- **Pre-check:** If SP = 0, set EM (Empty) and halt.
- **Action 1:** $DR \leftarrow Stack[SP]$
- **Action 2:** $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$
- **Post-check:** If SP = 63, clear EM; set FL.

**Example:** Pop twice from above.

$$\text{Pop 1: } DR = 9, \, SP = 2$$
$$\text{Pop 2: } DR = 7, \, SP = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked *"What is the value of SP after a series of pushes and pops?"*, students often forget whether the stack is **ascending or descending**. Always state your assumption in the answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Control Memory Sizing for Microprogrammed Control

**Problem:** A CPU has **128 distinct machine instructions**, with each microprogram averaging **4 microinstructions**, and each microinstruction is **20 bits wide**. Compute the **Control Memory size**.

### Step 1 — Compute number of microinstructions
$$N_{\mu} = 128 \times 4 = 512 \text{ microinstructions}$$

### Step 2 — Compute address bits for Control Memory
$$k = \log_2 512 = 9 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Compute total Control Memory size
$$\text{Size} = 2^9 \times 20 = 512 \times 20 = 10{,}240 \text{ bits} \approx 1.25 \text{ KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is exactly the kind of numerical question KTU puts in Part A. **Memorize:** $2^{10} = 1024$ (1 KB), $2^9 = 512$, $2^7 = 128$, $2^6 = 64$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Microinstruction Address Sequencing

Given the next-address logic in a microprogrammed control with:
- Branch Field (BR) = 2 bits: 00=next, 01=jump to address, 10=call subroutine, 11=return
- Condition (CD) = 2 bits: 00=always, 01=if Z=1, 10=if C=1, 11=unconditional
- Address (AD) = 7 bits

**Formula for next CAR value:**

$$CAR_{next} = 
\begin{cases}
CAR + 1 & \text{if } BR=00 \text{ (sequential)} \\
AD & \text{if } BR=01 \text{ and CD condition is met} \\
\text{Subroutine Address} & \text{if } BR=10 \text{ and condition met} \\
\text{Return Address from stack} & \text{if } BR=11 \text{ (return)}
\end{cases}$$

**Worked example:**
Suppose CAR = 0b0010110 (22), CD=01, BR=01, AD=0b1011001 (89).
- Condition is **Z=1**. Suppose Z=1 is true.
- Then the next address = 89, so the microprogram jumps to address 89 of control memory.

If Z=0 (false), the next address is CAR+1 = 23.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the heart of the **next-microinstruction sequencing problem** that KTU sets in Part B for 7 marks.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical: Instruction Format Bit Allocation

**Problem:** A CPU has **64 operations**, **8 general purpose registers**, and a memory of **4096 words** (each word addressable). Design a **two-address register–memory instruction format**.

### Step 1 — Opcode bits
$$m = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Register field
$$r = \log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Memory address bits
$$k = \log_2 4096 = 12 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Two-address layout
Total instruction length:

$$L = m + r + k = 6 + 3 + 12 = 21 \text{ bits}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Some students write $L = 6 + 3 + 3 + 12$ mistakenly using 2 register fields. For a **register–memory** two-address format, only **one register** is involved, the second operand is **in memory**. Hence $L = 6 + 3 + 12 = 21$ bits.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Trade-off Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Metric | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Flexibility | Hard to change | Easy (microcode update) |
| Implementation | Random logic | ROM/RAM-based |
| Complexity for large ISA | Exponential growth | Linear growth |
| Cost (small ISA) | Lower | Higher (extra ROM) |
| Cost (large ISA) | Higher | Lower |
| Debug-ability | Difficult | Easier (memory dump) |
| Application | RISC, simple MCUs | CISC, x86, VAX |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The crossover point** is roughly at **30–50 distinct machine instructions**. Below this, hardwired is cheaper. Above this, microprogrammed wins.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node IDs (prefixed with letters) and clean uppercase text inside double-quoted labels — no markdown bold or HTML inside labels.

---

## 4.1 High-Level CPU Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BUS["SYSTEM BUS"]
    CU["CONTROL UNIT"]
    IR["INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR"]
    PC["PROGRAM COUNTER PC"]
    MAR["MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER MAR"]
    MBR["MEMORY BUFFER REGISTER MBR"]
    ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
    REGFILE["REGISTER FILE GPRs AND SPECIAL REGISTERS"]
    STATUS["STATUS FLAGS Z C N V P"]
    DEC["INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    GEN["CONTROL SIGNAL GENERATOR"]

    BUS <--> MAR
    BUS <--> MBR
    BUS <--> PC
    MBR --> IR
    IR --> DEC
    DEC --> GEN
    GEN --> ALU
    GEN --> REGFILE
    GEN --> MAR
    GEN --> PC
    ALU --> REGFILE
    ALU --> STATUS
    REGFILE <--> ALU
    PC --> MAR
    CU_INTERN["INTERNAL CONTROL UNIT"]:::cuBox
    DEC --> CU_INTERN
    GEN --> CU_INTERN
    classDef cuBox fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** MAR, MBR, and PC are the "ports" between the CPU and the system bus. The IR feeds the decoder, which tells the control-signal generator what to do; the generator then drives the ALU, register file, and bus signals.

---

## 4.2 Internal CPU Data Path Using a Three-Bus Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["REGISTER R0"]
    R1["REGISTER R1"]
    R2["REGISTER R2"]
    R3["REGISTER R3"]
    Y["Y REGISTER"]
    Z["Z REGISTER"]
    ALU1["ALU"]
    SHIFT1["SHIFTER"]
    BUS_A["BUS A"]
    BUS_B["BUS B"]
    BUS_C["OUTPUT BUS C"]
    MUX1["MUX FOR Y"]
    MUX2["MUX FOR Z"]
    MUX3["MUX FOR OUTPUT"]

    R0 --> BUS_A
    R1 --> BUS_A
    R2 --> BUS_A
    R3 --> BUS_A
    BUS_A --> MUX1 --> Y
    R0 --> BUS_B
    R1 --> BUS_B
    R2 --> BUS_B
    R3 --> BUS_B
    BUS_B --> MUX2 --> Z
    Y --> ALU1
    Z --> ALU1
    ALU1 --> SHIFT1
    SHIFT1 --> BUS_C
    BUS_C --> MUX3
    MUX3 --> R0
    MUX3 --> R1
    MUX3 --> R2
    MUX3 --> R3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-bus organization** allows **two source registers** to be read simultaneously (BUS A and BUS B) and the result to be written on BUS C — completing an ALU operation in **one clock cycle**. This is the architecture used in RISC processors.

---

## 4.3 Microprogrammed Control Unit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CM["CONTROL MEMORY ROM"]
    CDR["CONTROL DATA REGISTER CDR"]
    CAR["CONTROL ADDRESS REGISTER CAR"]
    INC["INCREMENTER CAR PLUS 1"]
    MUX_NEXT["NEXT ADDRESS MUX"]
    BR_LOGIC["BRANCH LOGIC"]
    CD_LOGIC["CONDITION LOGIC FROM FLAGS"]
    MAP["MAPPING LOGIC FROM OPCODE"]
    OPCODE["INSTRUCTION OPCODE FROM IR"]
    IR_REG["INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR"]
    DATAPATH["CPU DATAPATH"]
    FLAGS["STATUS FLAGS"]

    CAR --> CM
    CM --> CDR
    CDR --> CAR
    CDR --> DATAPATH
    CAR --> INC
    INC --> MUX_NEXT
    CDR --> BR_LOGIC
    FLAGS --> CD_LOGIC
    CD_LOGIC --> MUX_NEXT
    BR_LOGIC --> MUX_NEXT
    MAP --> MUX_NEXT
    IR_REG --> OPCODE
    OPCODE --> MAP
    MUX_NEXT --> CAR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence of events for a microinstruction:**
> 1. CAR addresses the Control Memory.
> 2. The word at that address is loaded into CDR.
> 3. The control fields of CDR drive the datapath (start the micro-operation).
> 4. The branch/condition fields of CDR + the status flags feed the next-address MUX.
> 5. The MUX output is loaded into CAR for the next cycle.
> 6. (Optionally) the opcode from IR triggers the MAPPING logic to start a new micro-routine.

---

## 4.4 Register Stack Organization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["STACK POINTER SP 3 BITS"]
    DECODER["3 TO 8 DECODER"]
    REG0["REGISTER 0"]
    REG1["REGISTER 1"]
    REG2["REGISTER 2"]
    REG3["REGISTER 3"]
    REG4["REGISTER 4"]
    REG5["REGISTER 5"]
    REG6["REGISTER 6"]
    REG7["REGISTER 7"]
    DR["DATA REGISTER DR"]
    FL["FLAG FULL FL"]
    EM["FLAG EMPTY EM"]

    SP --> DECODER
    DECODER --> REG0
    DECODER --> REG1
    DECODER --> REG2
    DECODER --> REG3
    DECODER --> REG4
    DECODER --> REG5
    DECODER --> REG6
    DECODER --> REG7
    DR --> REG0
    DR --> REG1
    DR --> REG2
    DR --> REG3
    DR --> REG4
    DR --> REG5
    DR --> REG6
    DR --> REG7
    REG0 --> FL
    REG7 --> EM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently asks:** *"Explain the register stack with the help of a block diagram."* This diagram is the canonical answer. Make sure to label the **Full** and **Empty** flag generation.

---

## 4.5 Instruction Execution Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STEP_A["T1 MAR PC READ INSTRUCTION"]
    STEP_B["T2 MBR M FOR IR MBR INSTRUCTION"]
    STEP_C["T3 IR MBR DECODE PC PC PLUS 1"]
    STEP_D["T4 BRANCH DECODE EFFECTIVE ADDRESS FETCH OPERANDS"]
    STEP_E["T5 EXECUTE MICRO OPERATION IN ALU"]
    STEP_F["T6 STORE RESULT TO DESTINATION"]
    STEP_G["REPEAT FOR NEXT INSTRUCTION"]
    STEP_A --> STEP_B --> STEP_C --> STEP_D --> STEP_E --> STEP_F --> STEP_G
    STEP_G --> STEP_A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This corresponds to the **micro-operation sequence** for a typical single-bus CPU. Note that **PC ← PC + 1** happens in **T3** alongside decoding, and the **ALU execution** in **T5** is the only step that actually performs data manipulation.

---

## 4.6 Hardwired Control Unit Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IR_REG["INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR"]
    DEC1["OPCODE DECODER"]
    TIMING["TIMING GENERATOR T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6"]
    COND["FLAGS Z C N V"]
    COMB["COMBINATIONAL LOGIC GATES AND ROM"]
    CTRL["CONTROL SIGNALS TO DATAPATH"]

    IR_REG --> DEC1
    DEC1 --> COMB
    TIMING --> COMB
    COND --> COMB
    COMB --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardwired control** has NO memory for the control sequence. The control signals are pure functions of:
> $$\text{Control} = f(\text{Opcode}, \text{Timing Signal}, \text{Condition Flags})$$
> The function $f$ is implemented as a sea of AND/OR/NOT gates (or a single ROM if the function is truth-table friendly).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a **micro-operation** and give two examples.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *micro-operation* is the most basic operation performed on data stored in registers during one clock pulse. It is the elementary data movement or transformation executed by the datapath under the direction of the control unit.
>
> **Examples:**
> 1. **Register Transfer:** $R1 \leftarrow R2$ (copies the content of R2 into R1).
> 2. **Arithmetic:** $R3 \leftarrow R1 + R2$ (adds the contents of R1 and R2, stores in R3).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example 1, 1 mark for example 2.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between a **register stack** and a **memory stack**.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Register Stack | Memory Stack |
> |---|---|---|
> | Location | Inside the CPU (set of high-speed flip-flop registers) | A region of main memory |
> | Speed | Very fast (1–2 cycles) | Slower (5–10× register access) |
> | Size | Limited (e.g., 8 or 64 words) | Practically unlimited |
> | SP | Hardwired pointer to a register | Pointer holds a memory address |
> | Use | Function-call return addresses, interrupt nesting in MCUs | General subroutine linkage, recursion, expression evaluation |
>
> *(1 mark per row × 3 rows.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format:** Each Part-B question has internal choice. Pick **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Control Unit Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **microprogrammed control unit** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of each component.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Compare **hardwired control** and **microprogrammed control**. List the advantages and disadvantages of each.

> **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

A **microprogrammed control unit** stores the control signals for each machine instruction in a dedicated **Control Memory (CM)**, typically a ROM. Each entry in CM is a *microinstruction*; a sequence of microinstructions that implements one machine instruction is called a *microprogram* or *micro-routine*.

**Block diagram components:**

1. **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM holding all microinstructions. Width = number of control signals + branch fields. Depth = total number of microinstructions.
2. **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to fetch. Acts like a "PC" for the control unit.
3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Holds the microinstruction currently being executed. Its fields drive the datapath.
4. **Incrementer:** Adds 1 to CAR for sequential execution.
5. **Branch Logic / Next-Address MUX:** Selects the next value of CAR from among (i) CAR+1, (ii) branch address from AD field, (iii) subroutine return, (iv) mapped address from new opcode.
6. **Mapping Logic:** Translates the opcode of the IR into the starting address of the corresponding micro-routine in CM.
7. **Condition (CD) and Branch (BR) Fields:** Part of the microinstruction that decides what the next address should be.
8. **Status Flags (Z, C, N, V):** Conditions from the ALU that can be tested by the branch logic.

**Operation:**
- On reset, CAR ← 0 (the start of the *fetch* micro-routine).
- The micro-routine for *fetch* loads IR ← MBR and then maps IR.opcode to the start address of the *execute* micro-routine.
- The *execute* micro-routine runs, performing the ALU operation and storing the result.
- Control returns to the *fetch* micro-routine for the next instruction.

> **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Random combinational logic (gates, decoders, ROM truth table) | Control Memory holding microinstructions |
| Speed | Faster (no extra memory access) | Slower (CM read adds delay) |
| Flexibility | Difficult to modify; must rewire | Easy; rewrite the microcode |
| Cost (small ISA) | Lower | Higher (extra ROM) |
| Cost (large ISA) | Higher (logic explodes) | Lower (linear growth) |
| Debugging | Hard (no symbol table) | Easy (read CM contents) |
| Applications | RISC, simple MCUs | CISC, x86, VAX |
| Cycle time | $T_h = t_{decode} + t_{gates}$ | $T_\mu = t_{ROM} + t_{datapath}$ |

**Hardwired — Advantages:** fastest, smallest hardware for tiny ISAs.
**Hardwired — Disadvantages:** infeasible for > 50 instructions, hard to fix bugs post-silicon.

**Microprogrammed — Advantages:** scalable, supports firmware updates, easier emulation of other ISAs.
**Microprogrammed — Disadvantages:** slower, extra ROM cost, more complex control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often draw only the hardwired CU and skip the microprogrammed one. The 2024 scheme explicitly demands *both* in any "Control Unit" question. Lose 2 marks if you skip either.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Register Organization, Stack, and Addressing Focus

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Reframed for 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Design a **register stack of 8 registers**. Show the block diagram, explain the **PUSH** and **POP** operations, and write the micro-operations. How are the **FULL** and **EMPTY** flags generated?

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain the various **addressing modes** of a computer system with examples. Show how the effective address is calculated in each case.

> **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

A **register stack** is a small, fixed-size set of CPU registers (here, 8 registers addressed as 0 to 7) used as a LIFO structure for storing temporary data and return addresses. The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is a 3-bit register pointing to the topmost filled register.

**Block diagram** (described; matches Section 4.4):
- A 3-bit **SP** feeds a 3-to-8 **decoder**.
- The decoder's outputs enable one of the 8 registers for **read** or **write**.
- The **Data Register (DR)** holds the value being pushed or popped.
- Two flags **FL (Full)** and **EM (Empty)** are generated from SP.

**PUSH operation (ascending, full):**
- **Pre-condition check:** If SP = 7 (all 8 registers full), set FL = 1 and abort.
- **Micro-operation 1:** $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ (SP moves up to next empty register).
- **Micro-operation 2:** $Stack[SP] \leftarrow DR$ (write DR into the newly addressed register).
- **Post-condition check:** If SP = 7, set FL = 1; if SP ≠ 0, clear EM.

**POP operation:**
- **Pre-condition check:** If SP = 0, set EM = 1 and abort.
- **Micro-operation 1:** $DR \leftarrow Stack[SP]$ (read from the top register into DR).
- **Micro-operation 2:** $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ (decrement SP).
- **Post-condition check:** If SP = 0, set EM = 1; if SP ≠ 7, clear FL.

**Flag generation logic:**
- **EM = 1** when SP = 0 (no valid data in stack).
- **FL = 1** when SP = 7 (no free slot for next push).
- The flags can be implemented with simple comparators: $EM = \overline{(S_2 + S_1 + S_0)}$ and $FL = (S_2 \cdot S_1 \cdot S_0)$ for SP bits $S_2 S_1 S_0$.

> **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

An **addressing mode** is the rule for interpreting the address field of an instruction to obtain the **effective address (EA)** of the operand.

| # | Mode | EA formula | Example | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immediate | Operand = Address_field | `MOV R1, #5` → R1 = 5 | Loading constants |
| 2 | Direct (Absolute) | EA = Address_field | `LDA 200` → R1 = M[200] | Simple global variables |
| 3 | Indirect | EA = M[Address_field] | `LDA @200` → R1 = M[M[200]] | Pointers |
| 4 | Register | EA = Register | `MOV R1, R2` → R1 = R2 | Fast register transfer |
| 5 | Register Indirect | EA = M[Register] | `LDA (R2)` → R1 = M[R2] | Pointer using register |
| 6 | Displacement | EA = A + R | `LDA 100(R2)` → R1 = M[100 + R2] | Local variables, arrays |
| 7 | Indexed | EA = A + IX × size | Base + offset | Array iteration |
| 8 | Relative | EA = PC + A | Branch instructions | Loops, if-else |
| 9 | Auto-increment | EA = R; then R ← R+1 | Stack-style | Sequential traversal |
| 10 | Auto-decrement | R ← R-1; then EA = R | Reverse traversal | Reverse loops |

**Worked numeric example:**
Let the instruction `LOAD 500` use **direct** addressing on a CPU where PC = 200 and M[500] = 800.
- EA = 500 (direct).
- Operand = M[500] = 800.

If the same instruction used **relative** addressing with PC = 200:
- EA = PC + 500 = 200 + 500 = 700.
- Operand = M[700].

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to mention **Indexed** and **Relative** modes in a hurry, missing 1–2 marks. Always list **at least 7** modes in such a question. Also, state whether the addressing mode is **pre-indexed** or **post-indexed** when displacement is involved.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Problems (For Self-Evaluation)

### Q3. **[3-Mark Variant]** What is **expanding opcode**? When is it used?

> **Model Answer:**
> Expanding opcode is a technique where the number of bits in the opcode field is *not fixed* across all instruction formats. Instructions that reference only registers use shorter opcodes (because address bits are few), while instructions that reference memory use longer opcodes (because address bits are many). It allows the same instruction length to encode more total operations than a fixed opcode would. *Used in instruction formats where operand sources vary in size — e.g., register-only vs register-memory.*

### Q4. **[3-Mark Variant]** A CPU has **32 operations** and **16 registers**. Design a **single-address register–memory instruction format**.

> **Model Solution:**
> - Opcode bits: $m = \log_2 32 = 5$ bits.
> - Register field: $r = \log_2 16 = 4$ bits.
> - Address bits: $k = 12$ bits (assume 4 KB memory).
> - **Total instruction length:** $L = 5 + 4 + 12 = 21$ bits.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Processor Unit**

- [x] **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Registers**, interconnected by internal buses.
- [x] **Register types:** GPRs, Special-purpose (PC, SP, IR, MAR, MBR), Status (flags, PSW).
- [x] **Stack directions:** Ascending (↑) vs Descending (↓); Full (SP on data) vs Empty (SP on free slot).
- [x] **Stack operations (PUSH/POP)** always need a **Full/Empty** flag check to prevent underflow/overflow.
- [x] **Instruction formats:** Three-, Two-, One-, Zero-address. Each trade-off: code size vs instruction size.
- [x] **Expanding opcode** is used to fit more operations into the same instruction length.
- [x] **Addressing modes:** Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Register, Register-Indirect, Displacement, Indexed, Relative, Auto-inc/dec.
- [x] **Effective Address (EA)** is the *final* address of the operand *after* mode interpretation.
- [x] **ALU = cascaded 1-bit slices** with carry chain; arithmetic + logic + shift operations.
- [x] **Hardwired CU:** control signals = $f(\text{opcode, timing, flags})$; implemented by combinational logic.
- [x] **Microprogrammed CU:** control signals stored in ROM (Control Memory); fetched by CAR, held in CDR.
- [x] **Microinstruction format:** control fields (F1, F2, F3) + condition (CD) + branch (BR) + address (AD).
- [x] **Next-microinstruction logic:** sequential, conditional branch, subroutine call/return, mapping from opcode.
- [x] **Wilkes Control** is the historical forerunner of modern microprogrammed control.
- [x] **Hardwired vs Microprogrammed:** hardwired is faster, microprogrammed is more flexible.
- [x] **Key formulas:** $L = m + n \times k$, $N_{\text{regs}} = 2^k$, $N_{\text{opcodes}} = 2^m$.
- [x] **Crossover point:** hardwired wins below ~30–50 instructions; microprogrammed above.
- [x] **Block diagrams to memorize:** 3-bus internal datapath, microprogrammed CU, register stack, hardwired CU, instruction execution cycle.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Processor Unit.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Accumulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Accumulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accumulator (ACC):** A register inside the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) of a Central Processing Unit (CPU) in which the intermediate results of arithmetic and logic operations are stored. It serves as the primary **implicit operand** for most ALU operations, meaning the result of any arithmetic/logic instruction is automatically placed back into the accumulator, while one of the inputs is also sourced from it.

In the broader context of **register-transfer logic (RTL)** and **datapath design**, the term "Design of Accumulator" refers to the engineering methodology of constructing a dedicated digital subsystem (comprising registers, full adders, multiplexers, and control logic) that repeatedly performs the operation:

$$S \leftarrow S + X$$

where **S** is the running sum held in the accumulator and **X** is a new input operand. The architecture may be *parallel*, *serial*, *combinational*, or *sequential*, depending on speed, cost, and area constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shopkeeper's Cash Drawer:**
> Imagine a shopkeeper adding up a list of items on a bill. As he adds each item's price to the running total, the previous total is "accumulated" in his mental register (or written on a slip). He doesn't need a new slip for each addition — the same piece of paper (the accumulator) is updated every time. A digital accumulator works identically: it is a single register whose contents are continuously updated by adding each new input to its previous value.

A more technical analogy is the **odometer of a car** — every kilometer traveled is added to the existing total reading, displaying the cumulative distance.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Word Length:** Typical accumulators are designed for **4-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, or 64-bit** operands.
- **Propagation Delay:** Determined by the carry-chain depth in a ripple-carry adder (≈ **n × t_full_adder** for an *n*-bit accumulator).
- **Clock Frequency:** Sequential accumulators operate at frequencies bounded by the worst-case combinational path through the adder and register.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Accumulator central to KTU Module 2?**
> The accumulator forms the cornerstone for understanding more advanced datapath components such as the **ALU**, **Shift-and-Add Multiplier**, **Serial/Parallel Multipliers**, and the **Von Neumann / Harvard datapath**. Mastering its design is a prerequisite for all processor-design questions in subsequent modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Classification of Accumulators

An accumulator design is fundamentally classified along two orthogonal axes:

1. **Data Movement Mode:** *Parallel* vs. *Serial*
2. **Storage Update Style:** *Combinational* (no clocked register) vs. *Sequential* (clocked register feedback)

| **Design Type** | **Storage Element** | **Adder Type** | **Speed** | **Hardware Cost** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Register Accumulator | Edge-triggered D-FF array | External ALU | High | Low | General-purpose CPU |
| Combinational Parallel Accumulator | None (open loop) | Ripple-carry / Lookahead | Highest (single cycle) | Medium | Iterative combinational sums |
| Sequential Parallel Accumulator | Parallel-load register | Ripple-carry / Carry-lookahead | Medium (one add per clock) | Medium | Standard CPU datapath |
| Sequential Serial Accumulator | Shift register | Single full adder | Slow (n cycles per add) | Very Low | DSP filters, area-constrained designs |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

For a **Sequential Parallel Accumulator** (the most exam-relevant variant), the operation unfolds as follows:

- **Step 1 (Operand Fetch):** Input word **X = xₙ₋₁ xₙ₋₂ ... x₁ x₀** is placed on the input bus.
- **Step 2 (Addition):** The current accumulator contents **S = sₙ₋₁ sₙ₋₂ ... s₁ s₀** are routed to one input of a parallel *n*-bit adder. The bus **X** is routed to the other input.
- **Step 3 (Sum & Carry):** The adder computes **Sum = S + X** and produces a carry-out bit **C_out**.
- **Step 4 (Feedback / Load):** On the active clock edge, **Sum** is loaded back into the accumulator register, overwriting the previous value of **S**.
- **Step 5 (Iteration):** If more operands remain, the cycle repeats from Step 1. If the operation is complete, the accumulator holds the final cumulative sum.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core RTL (Register Transfer Logic) Statement:**
> For every active clock edge, the register-transfer statement that defines the accumulator is:
> **S ← S + X**
> This is the canonical "RTL equation" examiners look for in KTU answers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you will need to solve any KTU 2024 question on accumulator design. Note: vertical bars are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTL Operation | $S \leftarrow S + X$ | $S$: Sum register, $X$: Input | Operands are *n*-bit unsigned/signed (2's complement) |
| Maximum stored value (unsigned) | $S_{max} = 2^{n} - 1$ | $n$: word length (bits) | Overflow occurs when $S > 2^{n} - 1$ |
| Carry-out expression | $C_{out} = c_{n}$ | $c_{n}$: final carry bit | $C_{out} = 1$ indicates unsigned overflow |
| Signed overflow (2's complement) | $V = c_{n-1} \oplus c_{n}$ | $\oplus$: XOR | $V = 1$ indicates signed overflow |
| Propagation delay (ripple-carry) | $t_{p} = n \cdot t_{FA}$ | $t_{FA}$: full-adder delay (ns) | Worst case: carry propagates through all *n* stages |
| Propagation delay (carry-lookahead) | $t_{p} \approx 4 \cdot t_{gate}$ | $t_{gate}$: logic gate delay | Independent of *n* (logarithmic) |
| Throughput (serial accumulator) | $f_{clk}^{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{FA} + t_{FF}}$ | $t_{FF}$: flip-flop setup/hold time | One complete addition requires *n* clock cycles |
| Throughput (parallel accumulator) | $f_{clk}^{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{adder} + t_{FF}}$ | $t_{adder}$: parallel adder delay | One complete addition requires **1** clock cycle |
| Time to accumulate *k* operands (serial) | $T = k \cdot n \cdot T_{clk}$ | $T_{clk}$: clock period | Linear in both *k* and *n* |
| Time to accumulate *k* operands (parallel) | $T = k \cdot T_{clk}$ | $T_{clk}$: clock period | Linear in *k* only |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Accumulators (often called *MAC units* — Multiply-Accumulate) form the computational heart of FIR filters, FFT processors, and convolutional neural network accelerators.
- **Microcontrollers:** Devices like the 8051 use the **A (Accumulator) register** as the implicit operand for virtually all arithmetic and logic instructions.
- **Scientific Computing:** GPUs contain thousands of parallel floating-point accumulators for matrix multiplication and dot-product operations.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control units use accumulators in fuel-injection timing calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Operation of a 4-Bit Sequential Parallel Accumulator

Let us design a 4-bit accumulator with the following specification:
- **Input:** $X = x_3 x_2 x_1 x_0$ (4-bit data bus)
- **Storage Register:** $S = s_3 s_2 s_1 s_0$ (4 D flip-flops)
- **Adder:** 4-bit ripple-carry adder composed of 4 full adders
- **Output:** $C_{out} \, s_3 s_2 s_1 s_0$

We wish to trace the accumulation of three successive inputs: $X^{(1)} = 0101$, $X^{(2)} = 0011$, $X^{(3)} = 0110$, starting from $S^{(0)} = 0000$.

**Cycle 0 (Initial State):**

$$S^{(0)} = 0000_{(2)} = 0_{(10)}$$

$$C_{out}^{(0)} = 0$$

**Cycle 1 — Add $X^{(1)} = 0101$:**

The full adders compute bit-wise from LSB to MSB. We will explicitly enumerate every internal signal.

**Bit 0 (FA-0):**
$$s_0^{(0)} = 0, \quad x_0^{(1)} = 1, \quad c_0 = 0$$
$$Sum_0 = s_0^{(0)} \oplus x_0^{(1)} \oplus c_0 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$
$$c_1 = (s_0^{(0)} \cdot x_0^{(1)}) + (c_0 \cdot (s_0^{(0)} \oplus x_0^{(1)})) = (0 \cdot 1) + (0 \cdot 1) = 0$$

**Bit 1 (FA-1):**
$$s_1^{(0)} = 0, \quad x_1^{(1)} = 0, \quad c_1 = 0$$
$$Sum_1 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 0$$
$$c_2 = (0 \cdot 0) + (0 \cdot 0) = 0$$

**Bit 2 (FA-2):**
$$s_2^{(0)} = 1, \quad x_2^{(1)} = 1, \quad c_2 = 0$$

Wait — let us re-check the bit positions of $X^{(1)} = 0101$.
The bits are: $x_3 = 0, x_2 = 1, x_1 = 0, x_0 = 1$.

**Bit 2 (FA-2):**
$$s_2^{(0)} = 0, \quad x_2^{(1)} = 1, \quad c_2 = 0$$
$$Sum_2 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$
$$c_3 = (0 \cdot 1) + (0 \cdot 1) = 0$$

**Bit 3 (FA-3):**
$$s_3^{(0)} = 0, \quad x_3^{(1)} = 0, \quad c_3 = 0$$
$$Sum_3 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 = 0$$
$$C_{out} = c_4 = (0 \cdot 0) + (0 \cdot 0) = 0$$

**Result after Cycle 1:**
$$S^{(1)} = Sum_3 Sum_2 Sum_1 Sum_0 = 0101_{(2)} = 5_{(10)}$$

**Cycle 2 — Add $X^{(2)} = 0011$ (bits: $x_3=0, x_2=0, x_1=1, x_0=1$):**

$$S^{(1)} = 0101, \quad X^{(2)} = 0011$$

$$0101_{(2)} + 0011_{(2)} = 1000_{(2)} = 8_{(10)}$$

**Cycle 3 — Add $X^{(3)} = 0110$ (bits: $x_3=0, x_2=1, x_1=1, x_0=0$):**

$$S^{(2)} = 1000, \quad X^{(3)} = 0110$$

$$1000_{(2)} + 0110_{(2)} = 1110_{(2)} = 14_{(10)}$$

**Final Accumulated Result:**

$$S^{(3)} = 5 + 3 + 6 = 14_{(10)} = 1110_{(2)}$$

**Verification by direct summation:**

$$X^{(1)} + X^{(2)} + X^{(3)} = 5 + 3 + 6 = 14 = 1110_{(2)} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The accumulator is order-independent for addition (commutativity of +).** This is a property examiners love to test — the result after *k* additions is the same regardless of input order.

### 3.2 Derivation of Overflow Detection for Signed Accumulation

Consider two 4-bit signed operands in 2's complement representation. Define:

$$V = c_{n-1} \oplus c_{n}$$

where $c_{n-1}$ is the carry into the sign bit (MSB) and $c_n$ is the carry out of the MSB.

**Proof of Overflow Detection Rule:**

For an *n*-bit signed accumulator, valid representable range is:

$$-2^{n-1} \leq S \leq 2^{n-1} - 1$$

**Case 1: Two positive operands.** Both sign bits are 0. If the result's sign bit is 1, overflow has occurred. This happens when $c_{n-1} = 0$ (no carry into sign) and $c_n = 1$ (carry out of sign). Thus:

$$V = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Overflow detected}$$

**Case 2: Two negative operands.** Both sign bits are 1. If the result's sign bit is 0, overflow has occurred. This happens when $c_{n-1} = 1$ and $c_n = 0$. Thus:

$$V = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Overflow detected}$$

**Case 3: One positive, one negative.** The result magnitude cannot exceed the operand magnitude range, so overflow is impossible. Here $c_{n-1} = c_n$ in all sub-cases, yielding:

$$V = c_{n-1} \oplus c_n = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{No overflow} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Parallel vs. Serial Accumulator)

| **Performance Metric** | **Parallel Accumulator** | **Serial Accumulator** | **Ratio (P / S)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Adders Required | $n$ | $1$ | $n$ |
| Registers (Flip-Flops) Required | $n$ (parallel-load) | $n$ (shift register) | $1$ |
| Clock Cycles per Addition | $1$ | $n$ | $1/n$ |
| Worst-Case Latency | $t_{adder} + t_{setup}$ | $n \cdot (t_{FA} + t_{FF})$ | $\approx 1/n$ |
| Power Consumption (dynamic) | High (all FAs toggle) | Low (one FA toggles) | $n$ |
| Routing Complexity | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $n$ |
| Suitability for High-Speed CPUs | Excellent | Poor | — |
| Suitability for DSP / Stream Processing | Moderate | Excellent | — |

### 3.4 Control Signal Design

The accumulator requires the following control signals in a complete datapath:

- **LOAD_ACC (L):** Enables the parallel-load register. Active high during the clock edge when a new sum is to be captured.
- **CLR_ACC (R):** Synchronous or asynchronous reset to initialize $S = 0$.
- **ADD_EN (A):** Selects the adder output as the next state of the register.
- **CLK:** System clock edge that synchronizes the load.

The combinational logic for the next-state input of each flip-flop is:

$$D_i = (L \cdot (S_i \oplus X_i \oplus c_i)) + (\overline{L} \cdot S_i)$$

where $c_i$ is the carry into the *i*-th full-adder stage. This is essentially a **2-to-1 multiplexer** at the input of every D-FF, selecting between *hold* and *load sum*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Sequential Parallel Accumulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputBus["INPUT BUS X = x3 x2 x1 x0"] --> Mux1
    InputBus --> AddIn1["Adder Input A"]
    RegS["ACCUMULATOR REGISTER S = s3 s2 s1 s0"] --> AddIn2["Adder Input B"]
    AddIn1 --> AdderUnit["N-BIT PARALLEL ADDER"]
    AddIn2 --> AdderUnit
    AdderUnit --> SumOut["SUM = S + X"]
    AdderUnit --> CoutOut["CARRY OUT"]
    SumOut --> Mux1
    Mux1 --> RegS
    CoutOut --> FlagReg["STATUS FLAGS Z, C, V, N"]
    Clk["CLOCK"] --> RegS
    Ctrl["CONTROL UNIT L, R, A"] --> Mux1
    Ctrl --> RegS

    classDef reg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef adder fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class RegS,FlagReg reg
    class AdderUnit,Mux1,AddIn1,AddIn2,SumOut,CoutOut adder
    class Clk,Ctrl,InputBus ctrl
```

### 4.2 Gate-Level Schematic of a Single Accumulator Bit-Cell (i-th stage)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Si["s_i (from register Q output)"] --> Xor1
    Xi["x_i (from input bus)"] --> Xor1
    Xor1["XOR Gate 1"] --> Xor2
    Ci["Carry-in c_i"] --> Xor2
    Xor2["XOR Gate 2 Sum-bit"] --> MuxIn1["Mux Input 1 SUM_i"]
    Si --> And1
    Xi --> And1
    And1["AND Gate 1"] --> Or1
    Xor1 --> And2
    Ci --> And2
    And2["AND Gate 2"] --> Or1
    Or1["OR Gate = Carry Generate/Propagate"] --> Couti["Carry-out c_i+1"]
    MuxIn1 --> MuxSel
    SiHold["s_i HOLD path"] --> MuxSel
    MuxSel["2:1 MUX controlled by L"] --> Di["D input of D-FF"]
    Di --> DFF["D Flip-Flop"]
    DFF --> Qi["Q output = s_i NEXT"]
    ClkBit["CLK"] --> DFF

    classDef gate fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mux fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class Xor1,Xor2,And1,And2,Or1 gate
    class DFF,Qi,Di reg
    class MuxSel,MuxIn1,SiHold mux
```

### 4.3 Serial Accumulator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ShiftX["SHIFT REGISTER X = n-bits"] --> FAinA["FA Input A"]
    AccSh["ACCUMULATOR SHIFT REGISTER S = n-bits"] --> FAinB["FA Input B"]
    FAinA --> FAGate["SINGLE FULL ADDER"]
    FAinB --> FAGate
    FAGate --> SumBit["Sum bit"]
    FAGate --> CarryFF["CARRY FLIP-FLOP"]
    CarryFF --> FAGate
    SumBit --> AccSh
    ClkSer["SHIFT CLK"] --> ShiftX
    ClkSer --> AccSh
    ClkSer --> CarryFF

    classDef sr fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef adder fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class ShiftX,AccSh,CarryFF sr
    class FAGate,SumBit,FAinA,FAinB adder
```

### 4.4 Control Sequence (Timing Diagram in Textual Form)

| **Clock Cycle** | **Operation** | **Register Action** | **Adder Output** | **Result** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Reset (CLR_ACC) | $S \leftarrow 0000$ | — | $S = 0$ |
| 1 | Load X(1) onto bus, ADD_EN = 1 | $S \leftarrow S + X^{(1)}$ | $0 + 5 = 5$ | $S = 0101$ |
| 2 | Load X(2) onto bus, ADD_EN = 1 | $S \leftarrow S + X^{(2)}$ | $5 + 3 = 8$ | $S = 1000$ |
| 3 | Load X(3) onto bus, ADD_EN = 1 | $S \leftarrow S + X^{(3)}$ | $8 + 6 = 14$ | $S = 1110$ |
| 4 | Halt / Read result | $S$ held stable | — | $S = 1110$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation for Examiners:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners expect the RTL equation $S \leftarrow S + X$ to appear prominently in any accumulator design question. Pairing it with a labelled block diagram (as above) almost always guarantees full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define an accumulator. Write its register transfer statement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **accumulator** is a special-purpose register in the ALU of a CPU used to store the intermediate results of arithmetic and logic operations. It acts as the implicit operand source and destination for most ALU operations.

The register transfer (RTL) statement governing its operation is:

$$S \leftarrow S + X$$

where $S$ is the current accumulator contents and $X$ is the input operand. On every active clock edge, the sum of the existing accumulator value and the new input is loaded back into the accumulator. The accumulator is the foundation of single-address instruction formats, where the instruction implicitly references the accumulator as one of the operands.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Distinguish between a parallel accumulator and a serial accumulator. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Sl. No.** | **Parallel Accumulator** | **Serial Accumulator** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses $n$ full adders (one per bit) | Uses a single full adder |
| 2 | Operands loaded in parallel; addition completes in **1 clock cycle** | Operands shifted bit-by-bit; addition takes **$n$ clock cycles** |
| 3 | Hardware cost is high ($O(n^2)$ routing) | Hardware cost is low ($O(n)$ routing); suitable for area-constrained designs |
| 4 | Faster throughput, higher dynamic power | Slower throughput, lower power consumption; ideal for DSP filters |

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, every Module 2 question carries 14 marks with an internal choice. Both options are presented below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Design a 4-bit **sequential parallel accumulator** using D flip-flops and a 4-bit ripple-carry adder. Draw the complete block diagram, explain the operation with the RTL statement, and demonstrate its working by adding three 4-bit numbers: $A = 1001$, $B = 0110$, $C = 0101$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The accumulator is designed using:
- A 4-bit parallel-load register built from 4 edge-triggered D flip-flops ($FF_3, FF_2, FF_1, FF_0$).
- A 4-bit ripple-carry adder comprising 4 full adders ($FA_3, FA_2, FA_1, FA_0$).
- A common clock signal **CLK** and an asynchronous clear **CLR**.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**RTL Statement:**

$$S \leftarrow S + X$$

where $S = s_3 s_2 s_1 s_0$ and $X = x_3 x_2 x_1 x_0$.

**Working Demonstration:**

- **Initial state:** $S^{(0)} = 0000$
- **Cycle 1:** Add $A = 1001$.
  $$S^{(1)} = 0000 + 1001 = 1001_{(2)} = 9_{(10)}$$
- **Cycle 2:** Add $B = 0110$.
  $$S^{(2)} = 1001 + 0110 = 1111_{(2)} = 15_{(10)}$$
- **Cycle 3:** Add $C = 0101$.
  $$S^{(3)} = 1111 + 0101 = \underset{C_{out}=1}{1}0100_{(2)} = 20_{(10)}$$

**Final Accumulated Result:** $S = 0100$ with $C_{out} = 1$ (the value $20$ in 5 bits). The accumulator displays only the lower 4 bits, so the carry-out flag indicates the overflow beyond 4 bits.

**Verification:** $9 + 6 + 5 = 20 \quad \checkmark$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **status flags** (Z, C, V, N) generated by a 4-bit accumulator. Derive the expression for the **signed overflow flag V** in terms of the carry into and out of the sign bit. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A 4-bit accumulator, after each addition, updates four status flags stored in a separate status register:

| **Flag** | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Condition (1 = true)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero | Z | Result is zero | $Z = 1$ if $Sum = 0000$ |
| Carry | C | Unsigned overflow / borrow | $C = c_4$ (final carry-out) |
| Overflow | V | Signed overflow | $V = c_3 \oplus c_4$ |
| Negative | N | Result is negative (MSB = 1) | $N = s_3$ |

**Derivation of the Overflow Flag V:**

Consider two 4-bit signed numbers $A = a_3 a_2 a_1 a_0$ and $B = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ in 2's complement. The valid representable range is $-8$ to $+7$. Overflow occurs only when **two numbers of the same sign are added** and produce a result of the opposite sign.

Let $c_3$ be the carry into the MSB and $c_4$ the carry out of the MSB.

**Case Analysis:**

| **$a_3$** | **$b_3$** | **Result Sign $s_3$ (correct?)** | **$c_3$** | **$c_4$** | **$V = c_3 \oplus c_4$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (+) | 0 (+) | Should be 0; if 1, overflow | 0 | 1 | 1 ✓ |
| 1 (−) | 1 (−) | Should be 1; if 0, overflow | 1 | 0 | 1 ✓ |
| 0 (+) | 1 (−) | Always valid | 0 | 0 or 1, 1 | 0 ✓ |
| 1 (−) | 0 (+) | Always valid | 1 | 0 or 1, 1 | 0 ✓ |

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V = c_3 \oplus c_4}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Design a **4-bit serial accumulator** using a single full adder, two shift registers, and a carry flip-flop. Show the timing sequence for adding $X = 1011$ to an initial accumulator value of $S = 0000$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A serial accumulator processes operands one bit per clock cycle. The architecture consists of:

- **Shift Register X:** Holds the input operand and shifts it right by 1 bit per clock.
- **Shift Register S (Accumulator):** Holds the running sum and shifts right per clock.
- **Single Full Adder (FA):** Adds the LSBs of $X$ and $S$ along with the carry flip-flop output.
- **Carry Flip-Flop (C):** Stores the carry from one bit-position to the next.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

**Timing Trace:** Initial $S = 0000$, $X = 1011$ (MSB-first or LSB-first as per convention). We adopt **LSB-first** shifting (standard for serial arithmetic):

| **Clock** | **$x_0$ (out of X)** | **$s_0$ (out of S)** | **Carry in $C$** | **Sum bit** | **Carry out (next $C$)** | **New S (after shift)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0000 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0001 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0011 |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0110 |
| 4 | (X exhausted) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1101 |

Wait — let us re-compute carefully with carry propagation:

**Re-trace (LSB first, $X = 1011$ means $x_0=1, x_1=1, x_2=0, x_3=1$):**

| **Clock** | **$x_i$** | **$s_i$** | **$c_{in}$** | **Sum = $x_i \oplus s_i \oplus c_{in}$** | **$c_{out}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

**Result after 4 cycles:** $S = 0000 + 1011 = 1011_{(2)} = 11_{(10)}$

The accumulator now holds the binary value **1011** (which equals 11 in decimal). This matches the direct addition result, confirming correct operation.

**Key Insight:** With carry-ins all zero (initial value is zero), the serial accumulator simply copies $X$ into $S$ over 4 cycles. In general, the addition is interleaved with shifting, and the final carry must be flushed out by appending an extra clock cycle (the so-called **drain step**).

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the **combinational parallel accumulator** and the **sequential parallel accumulator** in terms of structure, timing, and application. Which is preferred in a pipelined CPU and why? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Combinational Parallel Accumulator:**
- Has **no clocked feedback register**; the sum output is directly available after the adder's propagation delay.
- The next operand must be applied immediately after the previous sum settles, which is impractical for variable input timing.
- Used only in fully combinational datapaths (e.g., certain iterative multipliers, array structures).

**Sequential Parallel Accumulator:**
- Includes a **parallel-load register** at the adder output, synchronized to a clock.
- Each new operand is added in exactly **1 clock cycle**; the result is held in the register until the next cycle.
- This is the **standard CPU datapath accumulator** (e.g., 8086, ARM Cortex-M).

| **Attribute** | **Combinational** | **Sequential** |
|---|---|---|
| Register at output | Absent | Present (D-FF array) |
| Clock required | No | Yes |
| Hold capability | None (output floats after each new input) | Holds value until next clock |
| Suitability for synchronous datapaths | Poor | Excellent |
| Pipelining compatibility | Difficult | Natural |
| Typical use | Array multipliers, combinational hash | CPU ALU, MAC units |

**Preference in a Pipelined CPU:** The **sequential parallel accumulator** is preferred because:
1. The register provides a clean **synchronous boundary** between pipeline stages.
2. It can be **stalled** (by gating the clock or using enable signals) to support hazards and data dependencies.
3. It interfaces seamlessly with the pipeline registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB in classical RISC pipelines).

Thus, modern pipelined CPUs universally employ the sequential parallel accumulator.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Accumulator Design Questions:**
> 1. **Missing RTL Statement:** Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for writing the canonical RTL equation $S \leftarrow S + X$. Omitting it costs easy marks.
> 2. **Confusing Parallel with Serial:** Many students interchange "parallel adder" and "parallel load." A *parallel adder* means all bits are added simultaneously; *parallel load* means all bits are loaded into the register simultaneously. Both are usually present in a sequential parallel accumulator, but they are independent concepts.
> 3. **Forgetting the Carry-Out Bit:** When adding numbers whose sum exceeds $2^n - 1$, students often ignore $C_{out}$. Always state whether the carry-out is captured, discarded, or used to extend the accumulator width.
> 4. **Overflow Flag Sign Error:** The signed overflow $V = c_{n-1} \oplus c_n$ is frequently miswritten. Memorize it as **"carry into MSB XOR carry out of MSB."**
> 5. **No Timing Diagram:** For serial accumulator questions, a timing trace (as shown in Q-B(a)) is mandatory. Skipping it costs at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Reset State Ambiguity:** Always specify whether $S$ is initialized to $0$ (zero-cleared) at reset; failure to mention this is a common deduction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Design of Accumulator**

- ✅ **Canonical RTL:** $S \leftarrow S + X$ — *write this in every accumulator answer.*
- ✅ **Two Architectures to Master:** *Sequential Parallel* (standard CPU) and *Sequential Serial* (DSP/area-constrained).
- ✅ **Combinational vs. Sequential:** Combinational has no register feedback; sequential has a clocked register that *holds* the sum.
- ✅ **Hardware Count:** Parallel = $n$ full adders + $n$ flip-flops; Serial = $1$ full adder + $n$ flip-flops (shift) + $1$ carry FF.
- ✅ **Latency:** Parallel = 1 clock per addition; Serial = $n$ clocks per addition.
- ✅ **Status Flags:** $Z$ (zero), $C$ (unsigned carry), $V$ (signed overflow), $N$ (negative / sign bit).
- ✅ **Overflow Formula (Signed):** $V = c_{n-1} \oplus c_n$ — *memorize verbatim.*
- ✅ **Maximum Unsigned Value:** $S_{max} = 2^n - 1$.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** 8051's *A register*; DSP MAC units; GPU FP accumulators.
- ✅ **Order-Independence:** The accumulator output is identical regardless of operand order (commutativity of addition).
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in KTU 14-mark answers — it alone fetches 3–4 marks.
- ✅ **Initial State:** Assume $S = 0$ at reset unless the problem states otherwise.
- ✅ **Drain Step (Serial Only):** An extra clock cycle is needed to flush the final carry-out of the MSB in a serial accumulator.

---

**End of Module 2 — Design of Accumulator | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Multiplication and Division (Restoring Method) of Binary Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Algorithms for Multiplication and Division (Restoring Method) of Binary Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *Computer Organization and Architecture (PERAT414)*, the **Algorithms for Multiplication and Division** form the arithmetic heart of any **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**. These algorithms describe the **micro-operational sequences** performed by the **datapath registers** (Accumulator, Multiplier/Quotient register, and Multiplicand/Divisor register) under the supervision of a control unit, to multiply or divide two unsigned binary numbers using a sequence of **additions, subtractions, and shifts**.

Specifically, the **Restoring Division** algorithm is a sequential bit-by-bit long-division procedure where the partial remainder is "restored" to its previous positive value whenever an attempted subtraction of the divisor causes the remainder to become negative. The algorithm terminates after *n* clock cycles for an *n*-bit dividend, producing a quotient and a remainder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – PERAT414):**
> The KTU syllabus explicitly mandates study of:
> 1. The **Shift-and-Add Multiplication Algorithm** (Hardware implementation with AC, BR, QR registers).
> 2. The **Restoring Division Algorithm** for unsigned integers.
> 3. The **Datapath organization** (block diagram) and **Control Sequence** of these operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Multiplication Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt Bakery":**
Imagine a bakery where you are baking cookies. The **Multiplicand** is the size of one cookie. The **Multiplier** tells you how many cookies to bake. You cannot bake all cookies at once. So, on a conveyor belt, you inspect one digit (bit) of the multiplier at a time. If the digit is **1**, you add one cookie (multiply/add) to your cart (the accumulator). Then you slide the entire belt right (shift right) to inspect the next digit. After processing all digits, your cart contains the total product.

**Division Analogy — "Measuring Cloth with a Stick":**
You have a cloth of length *Dividend* and a measuring stick of length *Divisor*. You want to know how many times the stick fits (Quotient) and what is left over (Remainder). You slide the cloth left under the stick (shift left), try to subtract the stick's length once, and see if the cloth is still long enough. If the cloth becomes "negative" (i.e., you subtracted too much), you **restore** the cloth (add the stick back) and mark a **0** in the quotient. If the subtraction succeeds, you mark a **1** in the quotient. You repeat until the cloth is exhausted. The result is the quotient, and whatever cloth remains is the remainder.

### 1.3 Key Registers and Hardware Metrics

The following bolded registers are the **standard datapath components** used in KTU textbook treatments of this topic (notably, the **Mano's** style architecture):

- **AC (Accumulator)** — Holds the high-order bits of the result (or partial remainder/partial product).
- **QR (Q Register)** — Initially holds the multiplier (for multiplication) or the dividend (for division); also accumulates the quotient.
- **BR (B Register)** — Holds the multiplicand (for multiplication) or the divisor (for division).
- **E (Carry/Overflow flip-flop)** — A single bit register that captures the carry-out during n-bit additions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — "Restoring":**
> The term *Restoring* means that whenever the partial remainder becomes negative (sign bit = 1), the algorithm **adds back the divisor** to *restore* the original (positive) remainder before setting the quotient bit to 0. This "wasted" addition is the chief inefficiency that motivates the *Non-Restoring* method covered in advanced study.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Multiplication Algorithm — "Shift and Add"

Multiplication of two *n*-bit binary numbers results in a *2n*-bit product. Instead of producing all 2n bits at once, the **Shift-and-Add** technique generates the product *one bit at a time* by examining the LSB of the multiplier, conditionally adding the multiplicand to the accumulator, and then shifting the combined accumulator-multiplier register right by one position.

**Operational Logic (Sequential Flow):**

- **Step 0 — Initialization:** Clear AC, load BR ← Multiplicand, load QR ← Multiplier, set E ← 0. Set counter ← *n*.
- **Step 1 — Test QR[0]:** Inspect the LSB of QR.
  - If QR[0] = **1**: Perform AC ← AC + BR. Capture any carry-out into E.
  - If QR[0] = **0**: Perform AC ← AC + 0 (skip addition).
- **Step 2 — Shift Right:** Shift the combined register **E–AC–QR** right by one bit position. E shifts into AC's MSB, AC's LSB shifts into QR's MSB, QR's LSB is discarded, and a 0 is shifted into E.
- **Step 3 — Decrement Counter & Loop:** Decrease counter by 1. If counter > 0, go to Step 1; else terminate.
- **Step 4 — Final Result:** The 2*n*-bit product is in the combined **AC ∥ QR** register.

**The Core "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why add only when QR[0] = 1?** Because binary multiplication only contributes the multiplicand when the corresponding multiplier bit is 1; otherwise, the contribution is 0.
- **Why shift right (not left)?** Shifting right aligns the next multiplier bit into QR[0] and gradually moves accumulated partial-product bits from AC into QR, freeing AC for the next partial product.
- **Why use E?** A 4-bit addition can yield a 5-bit result. The carry-out (E) is preserved and shifted into AC's MSB so no precision is lost.

### 2.2 The Restoring Division Algorithm — "Shift, Subtract, and Restore"

Division of an *n*-bit dividend by an *n*-bit divisor yields an *n*-bit quotient and an *n*-bit remainder. The Restoring method works as a *bit-by-bit* long-division process.

**Operational Logic (Sequential Flow):**

- **Step 0 — Initialization:** AC ← 000…0, QR ← Dividend, BR ← Divisor, load −BR (2's complement) into a helper register, set counter ← *n*.
- **Step 1 — Shift Left:** Shift the combined register **AC ∥ QR** left by one bit. AC's LSB receives QR's MSB, and QR's LSB is left as a 0 (this 0 will be overwritten by the quotient bit).
- **Step 2 — Subtract:** AC ← AC + (−BR) = AC − BR.
- **Step 3 — Test Sign of AC:**
  - If AC[MSB] = **0** (non-negative, subtraction succeeded): Set QR[0] ← 1. The remainder is valid.
  - If AC[MSB] = **1** (negative, subtraction over-shot): Set QR[0] ← 0, **and restore** AC ← AC + BR.
- **Step 4 — Decrement Counter & Loop:** Decrease counter by 1. If counter > 0, go to Step 1; else terminate.
- **Step 5 — Final Result:** Quotient is in **QR**; Remainder is in **AC**.

**The Core "Why" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why shift left first?** Because long division works from the MSB, so we need to bring the next dividend bit into AC.
- **Why subtract the divisor?** To test whether the divisor "fits" in the current partial dividend.
- **Why restore?** The decision must be binary: either the divisor fits (QR bit = 1) or it does not (QR bit = 0). If the subtraction made AC negative, the divisor did *not* fit, so AC must be restored to its prior value before the subtraction.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below summarizes all critical formulas, register sizes, and termination conditions. **No `|` symbol is used inside any table cell** to avoid breaking the markdown renderer.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Range** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Size of product | $P = 2n \text{ bits}$ | where $n$ = bit-width of operands |
| Number of cycles (multiplication) | $C_{\text{mult}} = n$ | clock cycles |
| Number of cycles (restoring division) | $C_{\text{div}} = n$ | clock cycles (one restore cycle per negative step) |
| Quotient constraint | $0 \leq Q < 2^{n}$ | integer |
| Remainder constraint | $0 \leq R < \vert M \vert$ | integer |
| Fundamental relation | $D = Q \cdot M + R$ | $D$ = Dividend, $M$ = Divisor |
| Division error / overflow | $Q \geq 2^{n} - 1$ | triggers divide overflow flag |
| Signed magnitude (2's comp) | $-B = \overline{B} + 1$ | bitwise NOT then add 1 |
| Combined register length (mult) | $\text{len}(E \parallel AC \parallel QR) = 2n + 1$ | bits |
| Combined register length (div) | $\text{len}(AC \parallel QR) = 2n$ | bits |
| Worst-case add ops per mult cycle | $1$ (conditional on $QR[0]$) | additions |
| Worst-case add ops per div cycle | $2$ (one subtract, one possible restore) | additions |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These algorithms are the bedrock of **integer arithmetic units** in every commercial CPU (Intel x86, ARM Cortex, RISC-V). Modern processors use iterative **Shift-and-Add Multipliers** in microcontrollers, **Wallace Tree** or **Dadda Multipliers** in DSPs and GPUs, and **Radix-4 SRT Dividers** in high-performance CPUs. The *Restoring* algorithm in particular is taught because it is **conceptually the simplest sequential divider** and serves as the design starting point before optimizations (non-restoring, SRT) are applied. The same shift-and-add principle is used in **digital signal processing (DSP)** for implementing FIR filters, **cryptographic hardware** (modular exponentiation in RSA), and **embedded firmware** for fixed-point arithmetic.
