$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{\frac{k}{n} + 1 - \frac{1}{n}} = k$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Throughput

Throughput = number of instructions completed per cycle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \frac{n}{T_{pipe}} \text{ [instr/cycle]} \\
&= \frac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p} \\
&= \frac{1}{T_p} \cdot \frac{n}{k + n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the limit, $TP_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$, which is the **ideal throughput of a pipeline** (one instruction per cycle).

### 3.4 Derivation: Efficiency

Efficiency is defined as the fraction of useful work done per cycle, measured by the speedup divided by the theoretical maximum $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \frac{n}{k + n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expressed as a percentage:
$$\eta\% = \frac{n}{k + n - 1} \times 100\%$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 1 — Balanced Pipeline

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A processor has a 5-stage pipeline with each stage taking 10 ns. Compute the total time, speedup, throughput, and efficiency for executing 100 instructions. Compare with a non-pipelined processor that takes 50 ns per instruction.

**Solution:**

Given: $k = 5$, $T_p = 10$ ns, $n = 100$, $T_1 = 50$ ns.

*Note: For the sequential time, the single-instruction time $T_1$ in a balanced pipeline is $T_1 = k \cdot T_p = 5 \times 10 = 50$ ns. ✓*

**Total time (pipelined):**
$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p = (5 + 100 - 1) \cdot 10 = 104 \cdot 10 = 1040 \text{ ns}$$

**Total time (sequential):**
$$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = 100 \cdot 50 = 5000 \text{ ns}$$

**Speedup:**
$$S_k = \frac{5000}{1040} = \frac{nk}{k + n - 1} = \frac{100 \cdot 5}{5 + 100 - 1} = \frac{500}{104} \approx 4.807$$

**Throughput:**
$$TP = \frac{100}{1040} \approx 0.0962 \text{ instr/ns} = 96.15 \text{ MIPS (if ns)}$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{n}{k + n - 1} = \frac{100}{104} \approx 0.9615 = 96.15\%$$

**Asymptotic (ideal) values for $n = \infty$:** $S_{max} = 5$, $\eta = 100\%$, $TP_{max} = \dfrac{1}{10 \text{ ns}} = 100$ MIPS.

### 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Non-Balanced (Unbalanced) Pipeline

> **Problem:** A 4-stage pipeline has stage delays of 20 ns, 15 ns, 25 ns, and 10 ns. Compute the clock cycle time, total time for 200 instructions, speedup, and efficiency. Sequential time per instruction is 70 ns.

**Solution:**

Given: $k = 4$, $n = 200$, $T_1 = 70$ ns, stage delays $t_1=20, t_2=15, t_3=25, t_4=10$ ns.

**Clock cycle time** (slowest stage wins):
$$T_p = \max(20, 15, 25, 10) = 25 \text{ ns}$$

**Total time (pipelined):**
$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p = (4 + 200 - 1) \cdot 25 = 203 \cdot 25 = 5075 \text{ ns}$$

**Total time (sequential):**
$$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = 200 \cdot 70 = 14000 \text{ ns}$$

**Speedup:**
$$S_k = \frac{14000}{5075} \approx 2.758$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{2.758}{4} = 0.6896 = 68.96\%$$

**Alternate derivation using $\eta = n/(k+n-1)$:**
$$\eta = \frac{200}{203} \approx 0.985 \text{ (this is theoretical max only for balanced pipeline)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students blindly use $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k+n-1}$ for **unbalanced** pipelines. The formula $S_k = \dfrac{n \cdot T_1}{(k+n-1) \cdot T_p}$ works in both cases, but the simplified efficiency $\dfrac{n}{k+n-1}$ **assumes balanced stages**. For unbalanced, compute $\eta = \dfrac{S_k}{k}$ directly.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Metric | Sequential Processor | Balanced Pipeline ($n$ instr) | Unbalanced Pipeline |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| Cycle time | $T_1$ | $T_p = T_1/k$ | $T_p = \max(t_i)$ |
| Total time | $n \cdot T_1$ | $(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$ | $(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$ |
| Speedup $S_k$ | 1 | $\dfrac{nk}{k+n-1}$ | $\dfrac{n \cdot T_1}{(k+n-1) \cdot T_p}$ |
| Throughput | $\dfrac{1}{T_1}$ | $\dfrac{n}{(k+n-1)T_p}$ | $\dfrac{n}{(k+n-1)T_p}$ |
| Efficiency | 100% | $\dfrac{n}{k+n-1} \times 100\%$ | $\dfrac{S_k}{k} \times 100\%$ |
| Asymptotic Speedup | — | $k$ | $\dfrac{k \cdot T_p^{ideal}}{T_p^{actual}}$ |

### 3.8 Worked Example 3 — Amdahl's Law Application

> **Problem:** In a 6-stage pipeline, 80% of the execution is parallelizable. What is the maximum achievable speedup? What if only 20% is parallelizable?

**Solution:**

For $f = 0.8$, $k = 6$:
$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \frac{0.8}{6}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1333} = \frac{1}{0.3333} \approx 3.0$$

For $f = 0.2$, $k = 6$:
$$S = \frac{1}{0.8 + \frac{0.2}{6}} = \frac{1}{0.8 + 0.0333} = \frac{1}{0.8333} \approx 1.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Amdahl's Law demonstrates that even with infinite pipeline stages, the sequential portion of code severely limits overall speedup. This is why modern CPUs combine pipelining with **superscalar execution**, **out-of-order execution**, and **branch prediction** to minimize sequential bottlenecks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic 5-Stage Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S1 [Stage 1: Instruction Fetch]
        S1A[IF: PC addr to MEM]
        S1B[Instruction Register IR]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Stage 2: Instruction Decode]
        S2A[ID: Decode opcode]
        S2B[Read register file]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Stage 3: Execute]
        S3A[ALU operation]
        S3B[Compute branch target]
    end
    subgraph S4 [Stage 4: Memory Access]
        S4A[Read Write data MEM]
    end
    subgraph S5 [Stage 5: Write Back]
        S5A[Write result to register file]
    end
    S1A --> S1B
    S1B --> S2A
    S2A --> S2B
    S2B --> S3A
    S3A --> S3B
    S3B --> S4A
    S4A --> S5A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid block above renders a **block-level functional architecture** of the canonical 5-stage MIPS-style RISC pipeline as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each subgraph represents a dedicated pipeline segment with its own combinational logic and pipeline register at the boundary.

### 4.2 Pipeline Execution Timing Diagram (Reservation Table)

The reservation table shows which stage each instruction occupies in each clock cycle. For 4 instructions ($I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$) in a 5-stage pipeline:

| Cycle → | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_1$ | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — | — |
| $I_2$ | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — |
| $I_3$ | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — |
| $I_4$ | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |

After cycle 5, the pipeline operates in **steady state** with one instruction completing per cycle.

### 4.3 Pipeline Concept Visual (Parallel Sub-Operations)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start of Pipeline] --> Fill[Pipeline Fill k cycles]
    Fill --> Steady[Steady State: 1 instr per cycle]
    Steady --> Drain[Pipeline Drain: remaining instructions complete]
    Drain --> End[End of Pipeline]
    Fill -.-> StageFill[Stage 1 first then Stage 2 ... Stage k]
    Steady -.-> FullPipe[All stages active in parallel]
    Drain -.-> TailEnd[Last few instructions exit one per cycle]
```

### 4.4 Speedup Curve Sketch (Mermaid-style Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Small n: Speedup small due to fill overhead] --> B[Medium n: Speedup grows rapidly]
    B --> C[Large n: Speedup approaches k asymptotically]
    C --> D[Asymptote S = k only in limit]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Unbalanced vs Balanced Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Balanced [Balanced Pipeline: Equal Stage Delays]
        B1[Stage 1: 10 ns] --> B2[Stage 2: 10 ns] --> B3[Stage 3: 10 ns] --> B4[Stage 4: 10 ns]
    end
    subgraph Unbalanced [Unbalanced Pipeline: Varying Stage Delays]
        U1[Stage 1: 20 ns] --> U2[Stage 2: 10 ns] --> U3[Stage 3: 25 ns] --> U4[Stage 4: 8 ns]
        U3 -.Bottleneck.-> U3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** The slowest stage (25 ns in the unbalanced example) becomes the **bottleneck** and dictates the clock period. Stages faster than the bottleneck remain idle for part of every cycle, reducing overall efficiency. This is the **stage imbalance problem** in real CPU pipelines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define pipelining. State two advantages and one disadvantage of pipelining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Pipelining is a technique of decomposing a sequential process into sub-operations, with each sub-operation executed in a dedicated segment that operates concurrently with all other segments. The output of one segment becomes the input of the next, with pipeline registers separating the segments.

> **Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
> 1. **Increased instruction throughput** — one instruction completes per cycle in steady state, dramatically improving MIPS rating.
> 2. **Higher resource utilization** — all hardware units remain busy in steady state instead of sitting idle.
> 3. **Lower average execution time per instruction** in steady state compared to non-pipelined designs.

> **Disadvantage (1 mark):**
> 1. **Pipeline hazards** (structural, data, control) introduce stalls, reducing the ideal speedup.
> 2. Increased hardware complexity and cost due to pipeline registers and inter-stage forwarding logic.
> 3. **No reduction in latency** of individual instructions; in fact, latency may slightly increase.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**
**Explain the terms: pipeline throughput, pipeline efficiency, and pipeline speedup.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Throughput (1 mark):** The number of instructions completed per unit time, typically measured in instructions per cycle (IPC). In a balanced $k$-stage pipeline, the maximum throughput is $\dfrac{1}{T_p}$ instructions per cycle, where $T_p$ is the clock period.

> **Efficiency (1 mark):** The ratio of the actual speedup achieved to the maximum theoretical speedup $k$, given by $\eta = \dfrac{S_k}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k+n-1}$. Expressed as a percentage, it indicates how well the pipeline stages are utilized.

> **Speedup (1 mark):** The factor by which pipelined execution is faster than sequential execution for $n$ instructions, given by $S_k = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$. As $n \to \infty$, $S_k \to k$, the ideal speedup.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice (a) and (b) sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR two complete alternative questions. Below, two complete 14-mark alternatives are provided.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Derivation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A non-pipelined processor takes **400 ns** to process a single instruction. The same processor is redesigned as a **5-stage pipeline** with stage delays of **80 ns, 70 ns, 90 ns, 60 ns, and 100 ns** respectively. Compute the speedup achieved when executing **1000 instructions** on the pipelined version. Comment on the result. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum speedup of a $k$-stage pipeline executing $n$ instructions. Explain why the speedup cannot exceed $k$ even with infinite instructions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Identify the clock cycle time.** Since the pipeline is **unbalanced**, the clock period is determined by the slowest stage:
$$T_p = \max(80, 70, 90, 60, 100) = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2: Compute total time on pipelined processor.**
$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p = (5 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 100 = 1004 \cdot 100 = 100400 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Compute total time on non-pipelined (sequential) processor.**
$$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = 1000 \cdot 400 = 400000 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4: Compute the speedup.**
$$S_k = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{400000}{100400} \approx 3.984$$

**Step 5: Comment.**
The ideal speedup would be $k = 5$ (had the pipeline been perfectly balanced). The achieved speedup of **3.984** is below the ideal due to **stage imbalance** — the slow stages (90 ns, 100 ns) force faster stages to wait, leaving the 60 ns, 70 ns, 80 ns stages idle for portions of each cycle. To improve, the slow stages (90 ns and 100 ns) should be subdivided into smaller sub-stages.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Define variables.** Let $k$ = number of pipeline stages, $n$ = number of instructions, $T_1$ = time per instruction sequentially, and $T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{k}$ = time per stage (assuming balanced pipeline).

**Step 2: Write total times.**
- Sequential: $T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = n \cdot k \cdot T_p$
- Pipelined: $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$

**Step 3: Form the speedup ratio.**
$$S_k = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_p}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

**Step 4: Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_k &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k \cdot n}{n \left(1 + \frac{k-1}{n}\right)} \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{1 + \frac{k-1}{n}} \\
&= \frac{k}{1 + 0} = k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.** The speedup is bounded by $k$ because, in the steady state, the pipeline can issue at most **one instruction per clock cycle** (i.e., the throughput ceiling is $\dfrac{1}{T_p}$). The single-instruction latency is $k \cdot T_p = T_1$, identical to sequential execution. Hence, no matter how many instructions we execute, we cannot complete more than $k$ times as many instructions in the same time window, because each instruction still needs $k$ stages of work — those stages are just overlapped with the stages of other instructions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often forget to subtract 1 in $(k + n - 1)$ and write $(k + n)$, leading to wrong answers. The correct expression has $-1$ because the **first** instruction exits at cycle $k$, not at cycle $k+1$.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alt)]**

**(a)** A processor has a **4-stage pipeline** with stage delays of **15 ns, 20 ns, 10 ns, and 25 ns** respectively. Calculate: (i) clock cycle time, (ii) total time to execute **500 instructions**, (iii) speedup, and (iv) efficiency. Assume sequential time per instruction is **70 ns**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What is Amdahl's Law? A program spends **30%** of its time in a section that can be parallelized using a **10-stage pipeline**, while the remaining **70%** is sequential. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to (a):**

Given: $k = 4$, $n = 500$, $T_1 = 70$ ns, stage delays = 15, 20, 10, 25 ns.

**(i) Clock cycle time:**
$$T_p = \max(15, 20, 10, 25) = 25 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Total time for 500 instructions:**
$$T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p = (4 + 500 - 1) \cdot 25 = 503 \cdot 25 = 12575 \text{ ns}$$

Sequential comparison:
$$T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = 500 \cdot 70 = 35000 \text{ ns}$$

**(iii) Speedup:**
$$S_k = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{35000}{12575} \approx 2.783$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{2.783}{4} \approx 0.696 = 69.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** The ideal speedup for a 4-stage pipeline is 4, but due to stage imbalance (the 25 ns stage dominates), we achieve only 2.78×. The efficiency is 69.6%, meaning roughly 30% of the pipeline's time is wasted on idle stages.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Definition (3 marks):** Amdahl's Law, formulated by Gene Amdahl in 1967, states that the maximum achievable speedup $S$ of a system due to an enhancement is limited by the fraction $f$ of the system that can be improved (parallelized). The formula is:
$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}}$$
where $k$ is the speedup factor of the enhanced portion. It reveals that the **sequential portion $(1-f)$** of a program acts as a hard ceiling on overall speedup.

**Numerical application (4 marks):**
- Parallel fraction: $f = 0.30$
- Sequential fraction: $(1 - f) = 0.70$
- Pipeline depth: $k = 10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \frac{0.30}{10}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.73} \\
&\approx 1.370
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (extra credit):** Even though the 30% parallelizable portion runs 10× faster, the overall program only gets 1.37× faster because the remaining 70% is sequential and unaffected. If the parallel portion were 90%, the speedup would be $\dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.09} = \dfrac{1}{0.19} \approx 5.26$. **Lesson:** To extract maximum benefit from pipelining, minimize sequential bottlenecks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common mistake is to apply Amdahl's Law with $k$ = the **number of pipeline stages** and $f$ = **1.0** (i.e., assuming the entire program is parallelizable). This gives a misleading speedup of $k$ but ignores the sequential portions of real programs (e.g., control flow, memory stalls, I/O).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Most-Deducted Marks in Pipelining Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$** in the formula $T_k = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$. Cost: full marks of the sub-question.
> 2. **Using balanced formulas on unbalanced pipelines.** Always verify if all stages have equal delay. Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing throughput with speedup.** Throughput has units of instructions/time, speedup is dimensionless.
> 4. **Mismatched units.** $T_p$ in ns vs. $\mu$s vs. ms can cause 1000× errors. Always state units.
> 5. **Ignoring Amdahl's Law constraint** when the question mentions only partial parallelization.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Pipeliining Basic Principles**

- **Definition:** Pipelining overlaps instruction execution by dividing each instruction into $k$ sub-operations executed in dedicated hardware stages connected in series.
- **Goal:** Improve instruction **throughput**, not single-instruction latency.
- **Canonical 5-stage MIPS pipeline:** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB.
- **Total time formula (THE master equation):** $T_k = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$
- **Sequential time:** $T_{seq} = n \cdot T_1 = n \cdot k \cdot T_p$ (balanced case).
- **Speedup formula:** $S_k = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$
- **Asymptotic speedup:** $S_{max} = k$ (as $n \to \infty$).
- **Throughput:** $TP = \dfrac{n}{(k+n-1) \cdot T_p}$ instructions/cycle; max = $\dfrac{1}{T_p}$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{S_k}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ (balanced case).
- **Clock period for unbalanced pipeline:** $T_p = \max(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k)$ — slowest stage dictates.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ — sequential portion $(1-f)$ caps the maximum achievable speedup.
- **Golden rule:** Balance the stages for maximum efficiency.
- **Hazards (intro level):** Structural, data, and control hazards (full treatment in next topic) reduce the ideal speedup.
- **MIPS rating formula:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{\text{Clock Rate}}{CPI \times 10^6}$
- **Pipeline registers** are mandatory between stages to hold intermediate results and prevent contamination of concurrent instruction flows.
- **Laundry analogy:** Wash → Dry → Fold → Store = 4-stage pipeline; throughput doubles when overlapped.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Pipelining: Basic Principles | PERAT414 Module 3**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: CLASSIFICATION OF PIPELINE PROCESSORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pipeline Processor?

A **Pipeline Processor** is a digital signal processing or CPU architecture technique in which a sequence of operations (instructions or arithmetic computations) is decomposed into multiple sub-operations (stages), each of which is executed in a dedicated segment that operates concurrently with the other segments. The data/instruction stream flows through these stages like an assembly line, achieving instruction-level parallelism without duplicating the entire processor hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PERAT414):**
> A pipeline is a set of data-processing elements (stages) arranged so that the output of one element is the input of the next. The overall throughput of the processor is increased because multiple instructions can be processed simultaneously in different stages, even though each instruction still passes through the same sequential stages.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Car Assembly Line 🏭

Imagine a **car assembly line** with 4 stations:
1. **Station 1** – Fit the chassis
2. **Station 2** – Install the engine
3. **Station 3** – Paint the body
4. **Station 4** – Attach wheels & quality check

- A **non-pipelined** worker would build one entire car from scratch before starting the next. If each station takes **20 minutes**, one car takes **80 minutes**.
- A **pipelined** assembly line works on **multiple cars simultaneously** – while Car A is being painted, Car B is having its engine installed, and Car C is getting its chassis. Every **20 minutes**, a *new car* rolls out of the factory.

> This is the essence of pipelining: **overlap the execution of multiple tasks** to maximize throughput. The first car still takes 80 minutes, but cars emerge every 20 minutes thereafter.

### 1.3 Why Classify Pipelines?

Not all pipelines are built the same. KTU examiners emphasize classification because it determines:
- The **hardware complexity** required
- The **types of instructions/data** supported
- The **scheduling flexibility** (static vs dynamic)
- The **performance limits** (scalar, super-pipelined, superscalar, vector)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 3 of PERAT414 explicitly asks students to *classify* pipeline processors along multiple axes: **structure (arithmetic vs instruction), function (uni vs multi), configuration (static vs dynamic), control (synchronous vs asynchronous), and order (in-order vs out-of-order)**. Be ready to tabulate and contrast.

---

### 1.4 Top-Level Classification Snapshot

Pipelines are classified on the following primary dimensions (each explored in detail in Section 2):

| # | Classification Axis | Categories |
|---|---------------------|------------|
| 1 | **By Pipeline Level** | Arithmetic Pipeline, Instruction Pipeline, Processor Pipeline |
| 2 | **By Function** | Uni-functional, Multifunctional |
| 3 | **By Configuration** | Static, Dynamic |
| 4 | **By Control Timing** | Synchronous, Asynchronous |
| 5 | **By Pipeline Structure** | Linear, Non-linear (with feed-forward / feedback connections) |
| 6 | **By Execution Order** | In-order, Out-of-order |
| 7 | **By Parallelism Grade** | Scalar, Superpipelined, Superscalar, VLIW, Vector |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Axis 1 — By Pipeline Level (Structure)

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Pipeline
- Used to perform **fixed-point or floating-point arithmetic** operations (e.g., multiply, divide, floating-point add).
- Each stage handles a **sub-operation** of the arithmetic algorithm.
- *Example:* **Floating-Point Adder Pipeline** has stages → Compare exponents → Align mantissas → Add mantissas → Normalize result.

### 2.1.2 Instruction Pipeline
- Used to overlap the **fetch–decode–execute** phases of **instruction processing** in a CPU.
- Classic **5-stage RISC pipeline**: `IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB`
- Each clock cycle, one instruction **enters** the pipeline and one instruction **exits**.

### 2.1.3 Processor Pipeline
- A combination of **instruction pipeline + arithmetic pipeline**; the whole CPU is treated as one large pipeline, where instruction-level stages feed data to arithmetic-level stages.
- Found in high-end modern processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiner tip:** "Distinguish between arithmetic and instruction pipeline" is a **3-mark guaranteed question**.

---

## 2.2 Classification Axis 2 — By Function (Uni-functional vs Multifunctional)

### 2.2.1 Uni-functional Pipeline
- Can perform **only one fixed function** (e.g., a dedicated floating-point multiplier).
- Hardware is **simple**, control is **trivial**.
- Low cost, low flexibility.

### 2.2.2 Multifunctional Pipeline
- Can perform **several different functions** by reconfiguring the inter-stage connections and control signals.
- *Example:* A multifunctional arithmetic pipeline that can act as adder, multiplier, or MAC unit depending on the microprogrammed control word.
- Hardware is **complex**, with multiplexers at each stage boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Multifunctional pipelines are further classified as **static** (one function at a time, but the function can be switched) or **dynamic** (multiple functions simultaneously on different data streams).

---

## 2.3 Classification Axis 3 — By Configuration (Static vs Dynamic)

### 2.3.1 Static Pipeline
- A multifunctional pipeline in which **only one function is active at a time**.
- To switch from function A to function B, the pipeline must be **flushed** (drained) and then reconfigured.
- Easier to design; commonly used in older supercomputers (e.g., **Cray-1** scalar pipelines).

### 2.3.2 Dynamic Pipeline
- A multifunctional pipeline that can perform **more than one function simultaneously** on different data inputs.
- Requires **multiple reservation tables** and sophisticated **scheduling logic** to prevent conflicts.
- Found in **Tomasulo-style** out-of-order processors and modern superscalar CPUs.

### Key Differences Table

| Property | Static Pipeline | Dynamic Pipeline |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Active functions at any instant | One | Many |
| Reconfiguration delay | Yes (must flush) | None |
| Hardware complexity | Low | High |
| Reservation tables | One per function | Multiple, interleaved |
| Throughput | Moderate | Higher |

---

## 2.4 Classification Axis 4 — By Control Timing (Synchronous vs Asynchronous)

### 2.4.1 Synchronous Pipeline
- A **common clock** drives all stage registers. Data advances to the next stage on every clock edge.
- Simpler timing, easier to design.
- **Limitation:** Clock period must accommodate the **slowest stage**, which may cause idle time in faster stages.

### 2.4.2 Asynchronous Pipeline
- Each stage operates with a **handshake protocol** (e.g., request–acknowledge). A stage forwards data to the next as soon as it is ready.
- Better **average-case performance** since fast stages are not held back by the slowest.
- **Drawback:** Handshake overhead and harder timing analysis.

---

## 2.5 Classification Axis 5 — By Pipeline Structure (Linear vs Non-linear)

### 2.5.1 Linear Pipeline
- Stages are connected **strictly in series**: $S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_3 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow S_k$.
- No feedback or feed-forward paths.
- All instructions traverse the stages in the **same fixed order**.

### 2.5.2 Non-linear Pipeline
- Stages have **feed-forward** and/or **feedback** connections to enable **iterative operations** (e.g., $S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_1$ for a multiply-accumulate loop).
- Requires **collision-free scheduling** using **reservation tables** and **latency analysis** to avoid conflicts.
- Common in **multifunctional dynamic** arithmetic pipelines.

### Reservation Table Concepts (Non-linear)
- A **reservation table** is a 2-D grid (rows = pipeline stages, columns = clock cycles) that shows which stage is used in which cycle.
- **Forbidden latency / collision:** A forbidden latency occurs when a stage is used by two different tasks in the same cycle.
- **Average latency / minimal average latency (MAL):** Used to compute the **maximum throughput** of the pipeline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CYCLES FOR COLLISION-FREE SCHEDULING** algorithm and **MAL** are favorite KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.6 Classification Axis 6 — By Execution Order (In-order vs Out-of-order)

| Aspect | In-order Pipeline | Out-of-order Pipeline |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Issue order | Same as program order | May differ from program order |
| Completion order | Same as program order | May differ from program order |
| Hazard handling | Stall on hazard | Execute independent instructions, commit in order |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (Tomasulo, reorder buffer) |
| Example | Classic MIPS 5-stage | Intel Core i-series, AMD Zen |

---

## 2.7 Classification Axis 7 — By Parallelism Grade

This is the **most performance-relevant** classification for KTU Module 3.

### 2.7.1 Scalar Pipeline
- Issues **at most 1 instruction per cycle**.
- Base reference design.

### 2.7.2 Superpipelined
- Clock cycle is **subdivided**; instruction issue rate is **1 per sub-cycle** (effectively > 1 per clock).
- Uses **deep pipelines** (e.g., 14+ stages) running at a high clock rate.
- *Example:* MIPS R4000, early Intel Pentium.

### 2.7.3 Superscalar
- **Multiple execution units** are present, so the CPU can issue **2, 4, or more instructions per cycle**, in parallel.
- Throughput is multiplied by the issue width $n$.
- *Example:* Intel Core i7, AMD Ryzen.

### 2.7.4 VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)
- The **compiler** packs multiple independent operations into one large instruction word; hardware executes them in parallel.
- No dynamic scheduling needed.
- *Example:* Intel Itanium (IA-64), TI TMS320C6x DSP.

### 2.7.5 Vector Pipeline
- Operates on **vectors (arrays)** of data using **vector registers** and **vector functional units**.
- A single vector instruction performs the same operation on many data items.
- *Example:* Cray-1, NEC SX-Aurora, modern SIMD units (SSE, AVX, NEON).

### Performance Summary

| Type | Issue Width (k) | Clock Rate | Peak IPC | Compiler Role |
|------|-----------------|------------|----------|----------------|
| Scalar | 1 | f | 1 | Low |
| Superpipelined | 1 | k·f | 1 | Low |
| Superscalar | k | f | k | Medium |
| VLIW | k | f | k | **High** |
| Vector | 1 (per lane) | f | 32–512 (with lanes) | Medium |

Where **IPC = Instructions Per Cycle**.

---

## 2.8 📐 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas use the following standard notation:
> - $n$ = total number of tasks (instructions)
> - $k$ = number of pipeline stages
> - $t_p$ = clock period (time per stage)
> - $t_n$ = time to execute one task in a non-pipelined unit

$$
T_{\text{non-pipelined}} = n \cdot t_n
$$

$$
T_{\text{pipelined}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot t_p
$$

$$
S_{\text{speedup}} = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipelined}}}{T_{\text{pipelined}}} = \frac{n \cdot t_n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{efficiency}} = \frac{S_{\text{speedup}}}{k} = \frac{n \cdot t_n}{k \cdot (k + n - 1) \cdot t_p}
$$

$$
W_{\text{throughput}} = \frac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p} = \frac{1}{t_p}\left[\frac{n}{k+n-1}\right]
$$

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{\text{speedup}} = \frac{t_n}{t_p}, \qquad \lim_{n \to \infty} \eta_{\text{efficiency}} = \frac{t_n}{k \cdot t_p}
$$

$$
\text{Maximum Speedup (ideal)} = k \quad \text{(when } t_n = t_p \text{ and } n \gg k\text{)}
$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Processor Design:** Guides trade-offs between **pipeline depth (k)** and **clock frequency (1/tp)**.
- **DSP Systems:** Vector pipelines underpin **FIR/FFT** accelerators in 5G base stations and radar.
- **GPU Architecture:** Modern GPUs combine **superscalar + superpipelined + SIMD (vector)** layers — a 3-deep classification in one chip.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Pipelined Execution Time

### Step 1 — Non-Pipelined Baseline
In a non-pipelined (sequential) processor, each task is **fully completed** before the next one begins. If a single task takes time $t_n$ and we have $n$ tasks:

$$
T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot t_n
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* Execute tasks one after another — no overlap, no parallelism.

### Step 2 — Decompose Task Into Stages
The task is split into **k sequential stages**, each of duration $t_p$ (the clock period). For balanced pipelining, $t_n = k \cdot t_p$.

> *Textual interpretation:* The total work per task $t_n$ is distributed evenly over $k$ stages.

### Step 3 — First Task (Pipeline Fill-Up)
The first task must traverse all $k$ stages, so it takes $k$ cycles:

$$
T_{\text{first}} = k \cdot t_p
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* This is the "pipeline fill" time — the assembly line has no output for the first $k$ cycles.

### Step 4 — Remaining Tasks (Steady State)
Once the pipeline is full, a **new task completes every cycle** ($t_p$). For the remaining $(n-1)$ tasks:

$$
T_{\text{remaining}} = (n-1) \cdot t_p
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The assembly line now outputs a car every 20 minutes.

### Step 5 — Total Pipelined Time
Add Step 3 and Step 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{pipe}} &= T_{\text{first}} + T_{\text{remaining}} \\
&= k \cdot t_p + (n-1) \cdot t_p \\
&= (k + n - 1) \cdot t_p
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* $k$ cycles to fill + $(n-1)$ cycles to drain — a classic result.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Speedup Factor

### Step 1 — Definition
Speedup $S$ is the ratio of sequential time to pipelined time:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Expressions

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{n \cdot t_n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Balanced Pipeline Limit
If the pipeline is **balanced** ($t_n = k \cdot t_p$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{n \cdot k \cdot t_p}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Asymptotic Behaviour
As $n \to \infty$ (huge workload):

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = k
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* The **theoretical maximum speedup equals the number of stages**. No more, no less.

### Step 5 — Practical Case With $n=10$, $k=4$
Plug in values to validate:

$$
S = \frac{10 \cdot 4}{4 + 10 - 1} = \frac{40}{13} \approx 3.077
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* With 4 stages and 10 tasks, we get ~3× speedup, close to the ideal 4× only because $n$ is still small.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Efficiency

### Step 1 — Definition
Efficiency $\eta$ is the fraction of the pipeline stages that is *usefully busy*:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Useful work per task}}{\text{Available stage time per task}} = \frac{S}{k}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{n \cdot t_n}{(k+n-1) \cdot t_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Balanced Pipeline Limit

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \frac{n}{k + n - 1}
$$

### Step 4 — Asymptotic Behaviour

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \eta = \frac{n}{n} = 1 \quad (100\%)
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* With an infinite stream of tasks, the pipeline stages are *never* idle — perfect utilization.

### Step 5 — Throughput
Throughput $W$ is the number of tasks completed per unit time:

$$
W = \frac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* For large $n$, throughput $\to 1/t_p$ — one task every clock cycle.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A 4-stage floating-point pipeline processes 100 floating-point additions. Each stage takes 20 ns. A non-pipelined adder would take 80 ns per addition. Compute the **speedup**, **efficiency**, and **throughput**.

**Given:** $k = 4$, $n = 100$, $t_p = 20$ ns, $t_n = 80$ ns.

### (a) Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{n \cdot t_n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p} \\
  &= \frac{100 \cdot 80}{(4 + 100 - 1) \cdot 20} \\
  &= \frac{8000}{103 \cdot 20} \\
  &= \frac{8000}{2060} \\
  &= 3.88
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The pipeline is ~3.88× faster than the sequential design.

### (b) Efficiency

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.88}{4} = 0.970 = 97\%
$$

> *Interpretation:* With 100 tasks and 4 stages, the pipeline is 97 % utilized — close to the ideal 100 %.

### (c) Throughput

$$
W = \frac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p} = \frac{100}{2060 \text{ ns}} = 4.85 \times 10^{7} \text{ additions/sec}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Approximately **48.5 million** additions per second.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Master Classification Summary Table

| Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 | Type 4 | Type 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **By Level** | Arithmetic | Instruction | Processor | – | – |
| **By Function** | Uni-functional | Multifunctional | – | – | – |
| **By Configuration** | Static | Dynamic | – | – | – |
| **By Control** | Synchronous | Asynchronous | – | – | – |
| **By Structure** | Linear | Non-linear | – | – | – |
| **By Order** | In-order | Out-of-order | – | – | – |
| **By Parallelism** | Scalar | Superpipelined | Superscalar | VLIW | Vector |

---
