# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between a Static Pipeline and a Dynamic Pipeline. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Pipeline | Dynamic Pipeline |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Active functions | Only one function at a time | Multiple functions simultaneously |
| Reconfiguration | Pipeline must be flushed | No flushing required |
| Reservation tables | One per function | Multiple reservation tables |
| Hardware complexity | Lower | Higher (needs more interconnection logic) |
| Example | Cray-1 scalar pipeline | Tomasulo-based superscalar out-of-order pipeline |

> A static pipeline is simpler and deterministic, whereas a dynamic pipeline offers higher throughput at the cost of more complex control and scheduling.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]
**Compare Arithmetic Pipeline and Instruction Pipeline. State two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Arithmetic Pipeline:** Decomposes an arithmetic operation (e.g., floating-point add) into sub-operations such as exponent compare, mantissa align, mantissa add, and normalize. *Examples:* Floating-point adder pipeline, fixed-point multiplier pipeline.
- **Instruction Pipeline:** Decomposes the **fetch–decode–execute** cycle of instruction processing. *Examples:* Classic 5-stage RISC pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB), 2-stage fetch-decode pipeline in early Intel 8086.

> The arithmetic pipeline deals with *data sub-operations*; the instruction pipeline deals with *instruction sub-stages*. Modern CPUs combine both.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern:** Part B has internal choice. **Answer either Option A or Option B in full.**

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — Pipeline Classification + Reservation Table
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 SET A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Classify pipeline processors along the following axes with examples: (i) Function (ii) Configuration (iii) Control Timing (iv) Structure.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) By Function**
- *Uni-functional* — performs a single fixed operation. Example: dedicated floating-point multiplier.
- *Multifunctional* — performs multiple operations, reconfigurable. Example: a pipeline that can add, multiply, or do MAC.

**(ii) By Configuration**
- *Static* — one function active at a time. Example: Cray-1 pipeline.
- *Dynamic* — multiple functions active concurrently. Example: modern superscalar out-of-order pipelines.

**(iii) By Control Timing**
- *Synchronous* — common clock drives all stages. Example: MIPS 5-stage pipeline.
- *Asynchronous* — handshake-driven stage transitions. Example: asynchronous DSP pipelines.

**(iv) By Structure**
- *Linear* — strict serial connection of stages. Example: classic RISC pipeline.
- *Non-linear* — feedback/feed-forward paths exist. Example: iterative multiply-accumulate pipeline.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a 4-stage non-linear pipeline, the reservation table is:**

| Stage | t1 | t2 | t3 | t4 | t5 | t6 |
|-------|----|----|----|----|----|-----|
| S1    | X  |    |    |    | X  |     |
| S2    |    | X  |    |    |    | X   |
| S3    |    |    | X  |    |    |     |
| S4    |    |    |    | X  |    |     |

**Find: (i) Forbidden latencies, (ii) Permissible latencies, (iii) Minimal Average Latency (MAL), and (iv) Maximum throughput.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Forbidden latencies** = differences between X's in the same row.
- Row S1: X at t1 and t5 → forbidden latency = 5 − 1 = **4**
- Row S2: X at t2 and t6 → forbidden latency = 6 − 2 = **4**
- Forbidden set = **{4}**

**(ii) Permissible latencies** = {1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7} − forbidden.
- Permissible set = **{1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7}**

**(iii) Minimal Average Latency (MAL):** Find a cycle that uses only permissible latencies and is non-colliding.
- Greedy cycle: latency sequence → {1, 2, 3}
- Average = (1 + 2 + 3) / 3 = **2 cycles per task**
- So **MAL = 2**.

**(iv) Maximum throughput** = 1 / MAL = **1/2 task per cycle** = **0.5 tasks/cycle**.

> *Interpretation:* The pipeline can complete at most one task every 2 clock cycles due to the feedback/iteration.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Speedup, Efficiency, and Parallelism Grade
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 SET B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**A 5-stage instruction pipeline has a clock period of 2 ns. A non-pipelined processor takes 50 ns per instruction. Compute the speedup, efficiency, and throughput for executing 100 instructions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $k = 5$, $t_p = 2$ ns, $t_n = 50$ ns, $n = 100$.

**Pipelined time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{pipe}} &= (k + n - 1) \cdot t_p \\
                &= (5 + 100 - 1) \cdot 2 \\
                &= 104 \cdot 2 = 208 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sequential time:**

$$
T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot t_n = 100 \cdot 50 = 5000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{5000}{208} = 24.04
$$

**Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{24.04}{5} = 4.808 = 480.8\%
$$

> *Note:* Efficiency > 100 % indicates the sequential unit was *unbalanced* — its per-instruction time (50 ns) divided by stages (5) gives 10 ns per stage, so the sequential unit was **5× slower per stage** than the pipelined unit.

**Adjusted efficiency (relative to balanced sequential, $t_n = k \cdot t_p = 10$ ns):**

$$
\eta = \frac{100 \cdot 10}{5 \cdot 208} = \frac{1000}{1040} = 0.9615 = 96.15\%
$$

**Throughput:**

$$
W = \frac{n}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{100}{208 \text{ ns}} = 4.807 \times 10^{8} \text{ instr/s}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Scalar, Superscalar, Superpipelined, VLIW, and Vector pipeline architectures with neat diagrams showing their instruction-issue patterns.**

**Model Solution:**

```
SCALAR:
   Cycle:  1   2   3   4   5   6
   I1:     F D E M W
   I2:         F D E M W
   I3:             F D E M W
   Issued per cycle: 1

SUPERPIPELINED (depth 2 within same cycle):
   Cycle:  1   2   3   4
   I1:     F1 F2 D1 D2 E  W
   I2:         F1 F2 D1 D2 E W
   Issued per cycle: 1 (but at twice the rate per sub-stage)

SUPERSCALAR (issue width 2):
   Cycle:  1   2   3
   I1:     F D E
   I2:     F D E
   I3:         F D E
   I4:         F D E
   Issued per cycle: 2

VLIW (compiler packs 3 ops per long word):
   Cycle:  1   2
   LW1:    [Op1 | Op2 | Op3]
   LW2:                [Op1 | Op2 | Op3]
   Issued per cycle: 3 (programmer/compiler's responsibility)

VECTOR (single vector instruction, length N=8):
   Cycle:  1   2   3 ... N
   V1:     a1 a2 a3 ... a8  (single vector instruction, N data items)
   Issued per cycle: N lane operations
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Type | Issue Width | Compiler Effort | Hardware Complexity | Best For |
|------|-------------|-----------------|--------------------|----------|
| Scalar | 1 | Low | Low | General code |
| Superpipelined | 1 (faster) | Low | Medium | High-frequency CPUs |
| Superscalar | k | Medium | High | Servers, Desktops |
| VLIW | k | **Very High** | Low (HW) | DSP, Embedded |
| Vector | 1 instr, N data | Medium | High | Scientific, Multimedia |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Pipeline Classification Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up Static vs Dynamic:** Static ≠ "no pipeline"; it just means "one function at a time." Many students write "static = no overlap" and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Superpipelined with Superscalar:** Superpipelined = *deeper* sub-stages at higher clock; Superscalar = *multiple* instructions per cycle. Writing them as synonyms is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the +1 in pipelined time:** Many students write $(k + n) \cdot t_p$ instead of $(k + n - 1) \cdot t_p$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Reservation-table forbidden latencies:** A common error is counting *column differences* instead of *stage differences* — the forbidden latency is read from the **same row**, not across rows.
> 5. **Throughput units:** Always quote throughput as `tasks/second`, not just a number. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Pipelining Goal:** Overlap execution of multiple tasks to improve **throughput** (not single-task latency).
- **Top-Level Classification Axes (memorize all 7):**
  1. **By Level:** Arithmetic / Instruction / Processor
  2. **By Function:** Uni-functional / Multifunctional
  3. **By Configuration:** Static / Dynamic
  4. **By Control:** Synchronous / Asynchronous
  5. **By Structure:** Linear / Non-linear
  6. **By Order:** In-order / Out-of-order
  7. **By Parallelism:** Scalar / Superpipelined / Superscalar / VLIW / Vector
- **Key Formula Triplet:**
  - $T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) t_p$
  - $S = n t_n / [(k+n-1)t_p]$, max speedup = $k$
  - $\eta = n/[k(k+n-1)]$ for balanced, $\to 1$ as $n \to \infty$
- **Reservation Table Workflow:** Draw table → read forbidden latencies → find permissible set → compute **MAL** (greedy cycle) → throughput = $1/\text{MAL}$.
- **Static pipeline** = one function at a time (e.g., Cray-1). **Dynamic pipeline** = multiple functions simultaneously (e.g., modern superscalar).
- **Superpipelined** = *faster clock with deeper stages*; **Superscalar** = *wider issue with multiple units*. Never confuse.
- **VLIW** shifts scheduling responsibility to the **compiler**, simplifying hardware.
- **Vector pipeline** is essential for **DPP/scientific workloads** and underlies modern **SIMD (SSE/AVX/NEON)** extensions.
- **Ideal Maximum Speedup = number of stages $k$**, achieved only when $n \gg k$ and stages are perfectly balanced.
- **Speedup vs Efficiency vs Throughput:** Speedup is *relative time gain*; Efficiency is *utilization*; Throughput is *tasks per unit time*. They are distinct and must be quoted separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction and Arithmetic Pipelines (Design Examples Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruction and Arithmetic Pipelines (Design Examples Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the computation into a sequence of independent, concurrent sub-operations called *stages*. Each stage performs a specific function and is connected to the next stage through a *pipeline register*. While one instruction is being processed in stage $i$, another instruction can simultaneously be processed in stage $i-1$, exploiting **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, two principal variants are studied:

1. **Instruction Pipeline** — Decomposes the *fetch–decode–execute* cycle of the CPU into discrete sub-stages so that successive instructions can enter the pipeline before previous ones have completed.
2. **Arithmetic Pipeline** — Decomposes a complex arithmetic operation (such as floating-point addition or multiplication) into a chain of sub-computations, each handled by a dedicated hardware stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The current module explicitly states *"Design Examples Not Required."* This means students must focus on the **principles, performance metrics, hazard classification, and reservation-table interpretation** of existing arithmetic pipelines, but are *not* required to design new pipelines from scratch.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Laundry Pipeline**
> Consider washing four loads of clothes using a *Washer → Dryer → Folder* system. Without pipelining, you would wash all 4 loads, then dry all 4 loads, then fold all 4 loads. Total time = $4 \times (W + D + F)$.
> With pipelining, as soon as *Load 1* finishes washing, it moves to the dryer, and *Load 2* immediately enters the washer. The stages operate **concurrently on different loads**. The first load still takes $W + D + F$ time to complete, but subsequent loads emerge every $(W + D + F)/3$ units of time (if stages are balanced).
> This is the essence of pipelining: **stages work in parallel; throughput is dictated by the slowest stage.**

### 1.3 Classification of Pipeline Types

| Pipeline Type | Function Decomposed | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Pipeline** | Instruction fetch, decode, operand fetch, execute, write-back | General-purpose CPU datapath |
| **Arithmetic Pipeline** | Floating-point addition, multiplication, division, square-root | Vector & scientific processors |
| **Processor Pipeline** | Multiple cooperating processors on a task | Multicore / superscalar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 module focuses on the **first two categories** in this taxonomy.

### 1.4 Canonical Stage Decompositions (for reference)

**A. Generic 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline (MIPS-style):**
$$\text{FI} \rightarrow \text{DI} \rightarrow \text{FO} \rightarrow \text{EX} \rightarrow \text{WB}$$

**B. Canonical 4-Stage Floating-Point Arithmetic Pipeline:**
$$\text{Compare/Align} \rightarrow \text{Add/Sub Mantissas} \rightarrow \text{Normalize} \rightarrow \text{Round}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles of Pipelining

- **Stage Partitioning:** The operation is broken into $k$ sequential *sub-functions* $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_k$. Each sub-function is implemented by a dedicated hardware segment.
- **Pipeline Registers:** Latches placed between consecutive stages that hold intermediate results for one clock cycle. They are the synchronization points of the pipeline.
- **Clock Period Constraint:** The pipeline clock period $T_p$ must be greater than or equal to the delay of the slowest stage plus the setup time of the next pipeline register:
$$T_p \geq \max(\tau_i) + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$
where $\tau_i$ is the propagation delay of stage $i$.
- **Throughput:** Under ideal conditions, one instruction completes per clock cycle, so the steady-state throughput approaches $1/T_p$ instructions per second.

### 2.2 Pipeline Performance Metrics — The Five Core Formulas

Let $k$ be the number of pipeline stages, $T_p$ the pipeline clock period, $n$ the number of tasks (instructions), and $t_i$ the time taken by the $i^{th}$ stage.

| # | Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unpipelined Total Time** $T_1$ | $T_1 = n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$ | Sequential execution of $n$ tasks. |
| 2 | **Pipelined Total Time** $T_k$ | $T_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + (n-1) \cdot T_p$ | Startup latency + steady-state. |
| 3 | **Pipeline Clock Period** $T_p$ | $T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{reg}$ | Set by the slowest stage. |
| 4 | **Speedup** $S_k$ | $S_k = \dfrac{T_1}{T_k}$ | Ratio of unpipelined to pipelined time. |
| 5 | **Efficiency** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{S_k}{k} = \dfrac{T_1}{k \cdot T_k}$ | Fraction of time every stage is busy. |
| 6 | **Throughput** $H$ | $H = \dfrac{n}{T_k} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | Tasks completed per unit time. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Ideal Maximum Speedup** of a $k$-stage pipeline is $S_{\infty} = k$, attained only when $n \to \infty$ and the stages are perfectly balanced ($\max(t_i) = \text{mean}(t_i)$). The closer $S_k$ is to $k$, the better the design.

### 2.3 Hazards in Instruction Pipelines (KTU High-Priority Topic)

Hazards are conditions that prevent the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle, disrupting the ideal "one instruction per cycle" throughput. They are classified into three families:

| Hazard Class | Cause | Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural** | Hardware resource conflict (two instructions need the same unit in the same cycle). | Pipeline stall. |
| **Data Hazard** | An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline. | Read-before-write violation. |
| **Control Hazard** | Branch / jump instruction alters the PC; the next instructions are already fetched. | Wrong-path execution. |

#### 2.3.1 Sub-classification of Data Hazards

1. **RAW (Read After Write)** — *True data dependency.* $I_2$ needs a value that $I_1$ will produce.
2. **WAR (Write After Read)** — *Anti-dependency.* $I_2$ writes a register that $I_1$ must read earlier.
3. **WAW (Write After Write)** — *Output dependency.* Two instructions write the same destination; order must be preserved.

> [!NOTE]
> WAR and WAW can only occur in pipelines that allow **out-of-order execution** (e.g., super-scalar processors). A simple in-order MIPS pipeline experiences only **RAW** data hazards.

#### 2.3.2 Hazard Resolution Techniques

| Technique | Hazard Addressed | Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline Interlock / Stall (Bubble)** | RAW, Structural | Insert one or more NOPs; detected by a hazard detection unit. |
| **Data Forwarding (Bypassing)** | RAW | Route the ALU result directly to the ALU input of the next instruction. |
| **Branch Prediction** | Control | Guess the outcome of a branch and speculatively fetch. |
| **Delay Slot** | Control | Compiler places a useful instruction after every branch. |
| **Scoreboarding / Tomasulo** | WAR, WAW | Track register readiness and issue instructions when operands are available. |

### 2.4 The Arithmetic Pipeline — Floating-Point Addition Pipeline (Reference Example)

A canonical floating-point adder requires the following functional sub-operations:

1. **Compare exponents** of the two operands.
2. **Align mantissa** — shift the smaller mantissa right by the exponent difference.
3. **Add/Subtract mantissas** using a fixed-point adder.
4. **Normalize** the result (shift mantissa, adjust exponent).
5. **Round** the mantissa to the required precision.

The reservation table of a 4-stage FP adder is typically:

| Stage | Step 1 | Step 2 | Step 3 | Step 4 | Step 5 | Step 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1: Compare | A1 |    |    |    |    |    |
| S2: Shift  |    | A1 |    |    |    |    |
| S3: Add    |    |    | A1 |    |    |    |
| S4: Normalize |  |    |    | A1 |    |    |
| **Next Add B** |  |  |  |  | B1 |  |
| S2: Shift  |  |  |  |  |  | B1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum **latency** (delay between two consecutive task initiations) of an arithmetic pipeline is the *minimum average distance (MAD)* between two X's in the same row of the reservation table. The **throughput** is the reciprocal of the *forbidden latency* set.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Design (Intel x86, ARM Cortex, RISC-V):** Deep pipelines (14–20 stages) provide GHz clock rates.
- **GPU SIMT Pipelines:** Hundreds of arithmetic units in lockstep compute floating-point operations.
- **DSP & Signal Processing:** Fixed-function arithmetic pipelines in FFT chips, FIR filters.
- **Aerospace & CFD:** Vector supercomputers rely on arithmetic pipelines to sustain TFLOPs.

### 2.6 KTU Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize for the ESE:**
> - $T_1 = n \cdot \sum t_i$
> - $T_k = \sum t_i + (n-1)T_p$
> - $S_k = n \cdot \sum t_i \,/\, \left[\sum t_i + (n-1)T_p\right]$
> - $H = n / T_k$ ; $\eta = S_k / k$
> - **Hazards:** Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control.
> - **Forwarding** solves RAW; **Stalling** solves structural & unresolved RAW.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pipelined Execution Time $T_k$

Consider $k$ pipeline stages with stage delays $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_k$ and a clock period $T_p$ governing all stages. We process $n$ tasks.

**Step 1 — Time to fill the pipeline (latency).**
During the first $k$ clock cycles, each stage receives its first task in succession. The total fill-time equals the sum of stage delays.

$$\text{Fill Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$$

**Step 2 — Time to drain / steady-state.**
Once the pipeline is full, a new task completes every $T_p$ seconds. For the remaining $(n-1)$ tasks:

$$\text{Drain Time} = (n-1) \cdot T_p$$

**Step 3 — Total Pipelined Time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_k &= \text{Fill Time} + \text{Drain Time} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + (n-1) \cdot T_p
\end{aligned}$$

> Each line above represents a logical transition: fill latency is a one-time cost; the $(n-1)T_p$ term captures the steady-state overlap.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Ideal Speedup

The unpipelined processor executes all $n$ tasks sequentially, one full operation per task:

$$T_1 = n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$$

The pipelined processor uses the formula from §3.1:

$$T_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + (n-1) \cdot T_p$$

Therefore the speedup is:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_k &= \frac{T_1}{T_k} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i + (n-1) \cdot T_p}
\end{aligned}$$

**Limit as $n \to \infty$:** the $\sum t_i$ term in the denominator becomes negligible, and using $T_p = \max(t_i)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_k &= \frac{n \cdot \sum t_i}{(n-1) \cdot \max(t_i)} \\
&\approx \frac{\sum t_i}{\max(t_i)} \quad \text{(if } T_p = \max(t_i)\text{)} \\
&\leq k \quad \text{(with equality when all } t_i \text{ are equal)}
\end{aligned}$$

The inequality $S_k \leq k$ is the **fundamental upper bound** of pipelining.

### 3.3 Derivation of Efficiency

Efficiency is defined as the fraction of time the *entire pipeline* is doing useful work, i.e., the speedup normalized by the number of stages.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_k}{k} \\
&= \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_k} \\
&= \frac{n \cdot \sum t_i}{k \left[\sum t_i + (n-1) T_p\right]}
\end{aligned}$$

As $n \to \infty$ with balanced stages ($\max t_i = \sum t_i / k$):

$$\eta \to 1 \quad \text{(100% efficiency)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** A 4-stage pipeline has stage delays $t_1 = 50\text{ ns}$, $t_2 = 60\text{ ns}$, $t_3 = 50\text{ ns}$, $t_4 = 40\text{ ns}$. Pipeline register delay $t_{reg} = 5\text{ ns}$. Compute $T_p$, $T_1$ for $n=100$ instructions, $T_k$, $S_k$, and $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Compute the pipeline clock period.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_p &= \max(t_i) + t_{reg} \\
&= \max(50, 60, 50, 40) + 5 \\
&= 60 + 5 = 65 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Unpipelined total time for $n=100$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= n \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i \\
&= 100 \cdot (50 + 60 + 50 + 40) \\
&= 100 \cdot 200 = 20{,}000 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pipelined total time.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_k &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i + (n-1) \cdot T_p \\
&= 200 + 99 \cdot 65 \\
&= 200 + 6{,}435 = 6{,}635 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_k &= \frac{T_1}{T_k} = \frac{20{,}000}{6{,}635} \approx 3.014
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{3.014}{4} = 0.7535 = 75.35\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Throughput.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{n}{T_k} = \frac{100}{6{,}635 \text{ ns}} \approx 15.07 \text{ Mips (Million instructions per second)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The theoretical maximum speedup of this 4-stage pipeline is $k = 4$. Our achieved $S_k \approx 3.01$ is reasonable because $n=100$ is finite. For $n \to \infty$, $S_k \to 200/60 \approx 3.33 < 4$ due to **stage imbalance** ($t_2$ is the bottleneck).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How Stage Balance Affects Performance

| Stage Delays (ns) | $\sum t_i$ | $T_p$ | $S_\infty$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (50, 50, 50, 50) | 200 | 55 | $200/50 = 4.00$ | Perfectly balanced — ideal case. |
| (60, 50, 50, 40) | 200 | 65 | $200/60 \approx 3.33$ | Mild imbalance. |
| (90, 50, 40, 20) | 200 | 95 | $200/90 \approx 2.22$ | Severe imbalance — bad design. |
| (10, 10, 10, 170) | 200 | 175 | $200/170 \approx 1.18$ | Catastrophic — no benefit. |

> [!TIP]
> **Design lesson (no design required per syllabus, but conceptual):** Always balance the stages. A slow stage dominates the clock and degrades the entire pipeline.

### 3.6 Reservation-Table Latency Analysis (Conceptual)

For an arithmetic pipeline with reservation table $T$ (an $m \times n$ binary matrix where a '1' in row $i$, column $j$ indicates task uses stage $i$ at time step $j$):

- **Latency** $l$ = any positive integer such that row $l$ of the modified table has no collision with the original.
- **Collision vector** $C = (c_{n-1}, \dots, c_0)$ where $c_j = 1$ if latency $j+1$ causes a collision.
- **Permissible latencies** = positions of 0s in the collision vector.
- **Minimum average latency (MAL)** = minimum steady-state initiation interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus states *"Design Examples Not Required,"* so you will not be asked to *construct* a new collision vector from a given table. You **must**, however, be able to **interpret a given reservation table** and state its permissible latencies, forbidden latencies, MAL, and hence the throughput.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical data flow of a MIPS-style instruction pipeline, complete with forwarding paths for resolving RAW hazards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IF["IF Instruction Fetch"] -->|"IR, PC+4"| ID
    ID["ID Instruction Decode /\nRegister Read"] -->|"Read Regs, Sign-Ext Imm"| EX
    EX["EX Execute /\nALU Op / Branch"] -->|"ALU Result /\nBranch Target"| MEM
    MEM["MEM Data Memory\nAccess"] -->|"Read/Write Data"| WB
    WB["WB Write Back\nto Register File"] -->|WB Data| ID

    EX -.->|"Forward A\nALU Result"| EX
    MEM -.->|"Forward B\nMemory/ALU Result"| EX
    MEM -.->|"Forward C\nLate Result"| EX

    style IF fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style ID fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style EX fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style MEM fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style WB fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows denote the normal forward data path through the pipeline registers.
- Dashed arrows denote **bypassing (forwarding) paths** that send an ALU or MEM result back to the EX stage inputs, eliminating RAW stalls in many cases.
- The reverse arrow from WB to ID indicates the *write-back* of computed results into the integer register file.

### 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram — 3-Instruction Sequence

The following chart shows how three instructions overlap across a 5-stage pipeline over 7 clock cycles.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Instruction Pipeline Timing Diagram (No Hazards)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Instruction I1
    IF for I1   :a1, 0, 1
    ID for I1   :a2, 1, 1
    EX for I1   :a3, 2, 1
    MEM for I1  :a4, 3, 1
    WB for I1   :a5, 4, 1

    section Instruction I2
    IF for I2   :b1, 1, 1
    ID for I2   :b2, 2, 1
    EX for I2   :b3, 3, 1
    MEM for I2  :b4, 4, 1
    WB for I2   :b5, 5, 1

    section Instruction I3
    IF for I3   :c1, 2, 1
    ID for I3   :c2, 3, 1
    EX for I3   :c3, 4, 1
    MEM for I3  :c4, 5, 1
    WB for I3   :c5, 6, 1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each row of the Gantt is one instruction. Each bar is one clock cycle spent in one stage.
- Observe the staircase pattern: in cycle 2, stage IF handles I2 *and* stage ID handles I1 simultaneously — this is the *pipelined overlap*.
- I1 finishes in cycle 5; I2 in cycle 6; I3 in cycle 7. The interval is exactly **one instruction per cycle** — the ideal throughput.

### 4.3 Block Diagram of a 4-Stage Floating-Point Arithmetic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Operand X\nMantissa X, Exponent Xe"] --> S1
    B["Operand Y\nMantissa Y, Exponent Ye"] --> S1
    S1["S1: Compare /\nAlign Exponents"] -->|"Shifted Mantissa\nAligned Exponent"| S2
    S2["S2: Mantissa\nAdd / Subtract"] -->|"Sum /\nDifference"| S3
    S3["S3: Normalize\nShift + Adjust Exponent"] -->|"Normalized\nMantissa / Exp"| S4
    S4["S4: Round\nto Target Precision"] --> R["Floating-Point\nResult Z"]

    style S1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style S2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style S3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style S4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style R fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The exponent comparator determines the alignment shift; the smaller mantissa is right-shifted to match exponents.
- The mantissa adder operates on the aligned significands.
- Normalization removes leading zeros (or adds leading ones for subnormals) by left-shifting and incrementing the exponent.
- Rounding produces the final IEEE 754-style result.

### 4.4 Hazard Stall Insertion — Bubble in the Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["Hazard Detected\n(RAW on register R)"] --> ST["Insert Bubble\n(NOP in EX)"]
    ST --> CS["Stall PC &\nIF/ID Register"]
    CS --> H2["Next Cycle:\nResolve and Resume"]
    style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style ST fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style CS fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style H2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A hazard detection unit (typically in the ID stage) flags a RAW conflict.
- The control logic freezes the PC and the IF/ID pipeline register, effectively inserting a NOP into the EX stage.
- The next cycle, the source operand is available and the stalled instruction proceeds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define pipelining. With a suitable example, explain the concept of instruction pipelining.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**

Pipelining is an implementation technique that overlaps the execution of multiple instructions by partitioning the datapath into a sequence of independent, concurrent stages separated by pipeline registers. Each stage performs a portion of the instruction processing while the next instruction enters the previous stage.

**Example — 4-stage Instruction Pipeline:**
Consider a 4-stage instruction pipeline with stages *Fetch (F), Decode (D), Execute (E), Write-back (W)*. While instruction $I_1$ is in stage *E*, $I_2$ is in *D*, and $I_3$ is in *F*. Thus three instructions are processed in parallel. The key benefit is **increased instruction throughput** (≈ one instruction per clock cycle in steady state) without increasing the clock frequency.

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three types of pipeline hazards.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Structural Hazards** — Occur when two instructions in the pipeline need the same hardware resource (e.g., a single memory port serving both IF and MEM). Resolved by *duplicating the resource* or by *stalling* one instruction.
2. **Data Hazards** — Occur when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline (RAW, WAR, WAW). Resolved by *forwarding*, *stalling*, or *register renaming*.
3. **Control Hazards** — Caused by branch and jump instructions; the next instruction to fetch is unknown until the branch is resolved. Mitigated by *branch prediction*, *delay slots*, or *speculative execution*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1 — Speedup ≠ Always k.**
> A common mistake is to assume ideal speedup equals the number of stages. The correct ideal speedup is $S_\infty = (\sum t_i)/(\max t_i)$, which is $\leq k$. A balanced pipeline achieves equality; an imbalanced one does **not**.
> **Pitfall #2 — Pipeline Time Formula Mistake.**
> Many students write $T_k = (n+k-1) \cdot T_p$, which is only valid when **all stages have equal delay equal to $T_p$**. The general formula is $T_k = \sum t_i + (n-1)T_p$. Use the general form unless explicitly told the stages are balanced.
> **Pitfall #3 — Confusing Throughput and Latency.**
> Throughput is the *rate* of completion (tasks/second). Latency is the *time per individual task*. Pipelining improves throughput but does **not** reduce the latency of a single instruction — it actually slightly *increases* it due to register overhead.

---

#### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a 4-stage floating-point arithmetic pipeline for addition. List the sub-operations performed in each stage and show the timing diagram for two successive additions.

**Model Answer:**

**Sub-operations of the FP adder pipeline:**

1. **S1 — Compare/Align:** Compare exponents $X_e$ and $Y_e$. Compute the shift amount $d = \lvert X_e - Y_e \rvert$. Shift the mantissa of the operand with the smaller exponent right by $d$ bits.
2. **S2 — Add Mantissas:** Use a fixed-point adder to compute $X_m' \pm Y_m'$.
3. **S3 — Normalize:** Left-shift the result mantissa until the leading 1 is in the MSB; decrement the exponent accordingly.
4. **S4 — Round:** Apply the rounding mode (e.g., round-to-nearest-even) and produce the final IEEE 754 result.

**Block diagram** — refer to §4.3 above.

**Timing diagram for two successive additions A and B:**

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1: Compare/Align** | A | B |   |   |   |   |   |
| **S2: Add**          |   | A | B |   |   |   |   |
| **S3: Normalize**    |   |   | A | B |   |   |   |
| **S4: Round**        |   |   |   | A | B |   |   |

- Task **A** completes at the end of cycle 4.
- Task **B** completes at the end of cycle 5.
- The **initiation interval (latency)** between A and B is **1 cycle**, since no two tasks use the same stage in the same cycle. Therefore, the pipeline supports a throughput of one addition per cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A non-pipelined processor takes 250 ns to execute one instruction. A pipelined version of the same processor uses 5 stages with stage delays 50, 45, 60, 40, 55 ns. Pipeline register delay is 5 ns. Compute (i) the clock period, (ii) the speedup for 100 instructions, (iii) the efficiency, and (iv) the throughput in MIPS.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Clock period:**

$$T_p = \max(50, 45, 60, 40, 55) + 5 = 60 + 5 = 65 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Speedup for $n = 100$:**

Unpipelined time per instruction = 250 ns ⇒ sum of stage delays = $50+45+60+40+55 = 250$ ns ✓

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 100 \times 250 = 25{,}000 \text{ ns} \\
T_k &= 250 + (100-1) \times 65 \\
&= 250 + 6{,}435 = 6{,}685 \text{ ns} \\
S_k &= \frac{25{,}000}{6{,}685} \approx 3.74
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{3.74}{5} = 0.748 = 74.8\%$$

**(iv) Throughput in MIPS:**

$$H = \frac{n}{T_k} = \frac{100}{6{,}685 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 14.96 \times 10^{6} \text{ instr/s} \approx 14.96 \text{ MIPS}$$

Equivalently, $H \approx 1/T_p = 1/65\text{ns} \approx 15.38$ MIPS (asymptotic, $n \to \infty$).

---

#### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three types of data hazards in instruction pipelines with examples. How are they typically resolved?

**Model Answer:**

The three data hazards are:

1. **RAW — Read After Write (True Dependency)**
   *Example:*
   ```
   I1: ADD  R1, R2, R3    ; R1 = R2 + R3
   I2: SUB  R4, R1, R5    ; needs R1 written by I1
   ```
   $I_2$ tries to *read* R1 in its EX stage, but $I_1$ *writes* R1 only in its WB stage. If $I_2$ reads too early, it gets a stale value.
   **Resolution:** *Data forwarding* from EX/MEM or MEM/WB pipeline registers to the EX stage input of $I_2$; or *stalling* $I_2$ for 2 cycles.

2. **WAR — Write After Read (Anti-dependency)**
   *Example:*
   ```
   I1: MUL  R2, R1, R3    ; reads R2
   I2: ADD  R2, R4, R5    ; writes R2
   ```
   $I_2$ writes R2 that $I_1$ still needs to read. In an *in-order* pipeline with register read in ID and write in WB, this cannot happen — it is only an issue in *out-of-order* execution.
   **Resolution:** *Register renaming* (rename R2 to a fresh physical register in $I_2$).

3. **WAW — Write After Write (Output Dependency)**
   *Example:*
   ```
   I1: MUL  R2, R1, R3
   I2: ADD  R2, R4, R5
   ```
   Both write R2. If $I_2$ completes first, R2 will have the wrong value.
   **Resolution:** *Stall* the later instruction until the earlier one reaches WB, or use *register renaming*.

> [!TIP]
> In a classic 5-stage in-order MIPS pipeline, **only RAW hazards are possible**. WAR and WAW appear when multiple instructions can be *in-flight simultaneously in the same functional unit* (super-scalar / VLIW / out-of-order processors).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-stage pipeline has stage delays (in ns): 20, 25, 30, 18, 22, 28. Pipeline register delay is 4 ns. Compute the ideal maximum speedup, the speedup for 500 tasks, efficiency, and comment on the result.

**Model Answer:**

**Stage delay sum:**

$$\sum t_i = 20+25+30+18+22+28 = 143 \text{ ns}$$

**Clock period:**

$$T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{reg} = 30 + 4 = 34 \text{ ns}$$

**Ideal maximum speedup (as $n \to \infty$):**

$$S_\infty = \frac{\sum t_i}{T_p} = \frac{143}{34} \approx 4.21$$

> Note: $S_\infty < k = 6$ because the stages are **unbalanced**. The slowest stage (30 ns) is the bottleneck.

**Speedup for $n = 500$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= 500 \times 143 = 71{,}500 \text{ ns} \\
T_k &= 143 + (500-1) \times 34 = 143 + 16{,}966 = 17{,}109 \text{ ns} \\
S_k &= \frac{71{,}500}{17{,}109} \approx 4.18
\end{aligned}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{S_k}{k} = \frac{4.18}{6} \approx 0.697 = 69.7\%$$

**Comment:**
The achieved speedup (4.18) is close to the ideal (4.21) because $n=500$ is large. However, both are well below $k=6$ because of **stage imbalance** — the 30 ns stage dominates. To approach the theoretical maximum of 6, the stages would need to be rebalanced to roughly $143/6 \approx 23.83$ ns each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pipelines (Module 3)**

- **Pipelining** = overlapping execution by partitioning work into $k$ sequential, concurrent stages separated by pipeline registers.
- The two flavours in the KTU syllabus are **Instruction Pipelines** (FI–DI–FO–EX–WB) and **Arithmetic Pipelines** (e.g., FP addition: Compare → Align → Add → Normalize → Round).
- **Five key metrics to memorize:** $T_1$, $T_k$, $S_k$, $\eta$, $H$.
- **Master the formulas:**
  - $T_1 = n \sum t_i$
  - $T_k = \sum t_i + (n-1) T_p$
  - $T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{reg}$
  - $S_k = T_1 / T_k$
  - $\eta = S_k / k$
  - $H = n / T_k \approx 1/T_p$ (asymptotic).
- **Ideal speedup** is $S_\infty = (\sum t_i) / \max(t_i) \leq k$. Equality only if all stages are balanced.
- **Hazards — three families:**
  - *Structural* → hardware resource conflict.
  - *Data* → RAW (true), WAR (anti), WAW (output). Only RAW is relevant in a simple in-order pipeline.
  - *Control* → branches and jumps.
- **Resolutions:** forwarding (RAW), stalling / interlocks, branch prediction, delay slots, register renaming (WAR/WAW).
- **Reservation tables & collision vectors** are for *arithmetic* pipelines: latency = minimum average distance between task initiations; throughput = reciprocal of the minimum permissible latency.
- **Pipelining improves throughput, not the latency of a single instruction** — note this distinction in any 14-mark answer.
- **Amdahl-style intuition:** the slowest stage limits the clock; balancing stages is the key to high efficiency.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always show: (i) the $T_p$ computation, (ii) the explicit $T_1$ and $T_k$ formulas with numbers substituted, (iii) the final numeric answer, and (iv) a one-line interpretive comment. Examiners award method marks generously when intermediate steps are shown.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Detection and Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazard Detection and Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Hazard:** A condition in a pipelined processor where the *next instruction cannot execute in the designated clock cycle* due to a dependency on a previous instruction, an in-use resource, or a pending control flow decision.

A hazard is essentially a **"wait condition"** that breaks the smooth, one-instruction-per-clock-cycle (IPC = 1) ideal flow of a pipelined datapath. The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes these under three primary heads:

| # | Hazard Type | Formal KTU Term | Root Cause |
|---|------------|-----------------|------------|
| 1 | **Structural Hazard** | Resource Conflict | Hardware cannot support all combinations of instructions in the overlapping pipeline stages |
| 2 | **Data Hazard** | Data Dependency Conflict | An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline |
| 3 | **Control Hazard** | Branch / Jump Conflict | The pipeline must fetch a new instruction before the outcome of a branch is resolved |

> [!NOTE]
> The golden rule of pipelining — *one instruction per cycle throughput* — is **violated** the moment a hazard appears. Hazard detection and resolution are the **mechanisms that restore this ideal throughput**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Platform

Imagine a **railway station with five sequential platforms** (Boarding → Ticketing → Security → Waiting → Train). Five passengers (instructions) line up, each moving from one platform to the next every minute.

- **Normal flow (no hazard):** Passenger 1 leaves "Boarding" the same minute Passenger 5 enters it. Perfect overlap.
- **Structural hazard:** Only **one security scanner** exists. When Passenger 1 is at Security, Passenger 2 cannot enter it — everyone waits.
- **Data hazard:** Passenger 2 needs **Passenger 1's ticket** before reaching Ticketing, but Passenger 1 has not yet printed it. Passenger 2 must stall.
- **Control hazard:** A signpost at the end of "Boarding" says *"If you are going to Trivandrum, divert to Platform 6."* But Passenger 1 has not yet announced their destination. The next 4 passengers must wait until the decision is clear.

**Hazard detection** is the station master *identifying* which kind of jam has occurred. **Hazard resolution** is the corrective action — adding a backup scanner (extra hardware), passing a ticket forward (data forwarding), or guessing the destination early (branch prediction).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Bold-List (High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Pipeline Stall (Bubble / NOP):** Inserting one or more `nop` (no-operation) instructions to delay the issue of a dependent instruction.
- **Forwarding (Bypassing):** Routing the result of an ALU operation *directly* from a producing pipeline stage back to a consuming ALU input, without waiting for write-back to the register file.
- **Pipeline Interlock:** A hardware mechanism that detects hazards and dynamically inserts stalls or activates forwarding paths.
- **Load-Use Delay (One-Cycle Stall):** A specific bubble required when an arithmetic instruction immediately follows a **load** that supplies its operand.
- **Branch Delay Slot:** The instruction position immediately following a branch; historically filled by an instruction that is *always* executed regardless of branch outcome.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Hazard Classes — Granular Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Structural Hazards (Resource Conflicts)

- **Definition:** Two pipeline stages attempt to use the *same physical hardware resource* in the same clock cycle.
- **Canonical KTU Example:** A **single memory port** serving both the *Instruction Fetch (IF)* stage and the *Memory Access (MEM)* stage. A `lw` instruction in MEM would collide with the next `add` instruction's IF.
- **Why it happens:** Designers sometimes *share* functional units to save silicon area (area vs. throughput trade-off).
- **Resolution Strategy:**
  1. **Duplication of resources** — e.g., separate Instruction Cache (I-cache) and Data Cache (D-cache), known as the **Harvard Architecture** split.
  2. **Pipeline stall** — freeze the upstream stage for one cycle (insert bubble).

### 2.1.2 Data Hazards (Data Dependencies)

Data hazards occur when instructions overlap but share data. There are **three** named dependencies following Bernstein's conditions:

1. **RAW — Read After Write (True Data Dependency):**
   - Instruction $j$ tries to *read* a register/source before instruction $i$ has *written* it.
   - **Most common** and only "true" hazard that reflects real program semantics.
   - Example: `add $t0, $t1, $t2` followed by `sub $t4, $t0, $t5`.

2. **WAR — Write After Read (Anti-dependency):**
   - Instruction $j$ writes to a register that instruction $i$ is *about to read*.
   - Cannot occur in simple 5-stage pipelines because **reads happen in ID, writes happen in WB**, and WB is *after* ID chronologically. Emerges only with **out-of-order execution** or **register renaming** failures.

3. **WAW — Write After Write (Output Dependency):**
   - Two instructions write to the *same* destination register; the order of writes must be preserved.
   - Also absent in in-order pipelines; appears in pipelines with *variable-latency* functional units (e.g., a multiply unit that takes 3 cycles while add takes 1).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module places **primary emphasis on RAW hazards** in the 5-stage MIPS pipeline. WAR and WAW are introduced as *conceptual completeness* and revisited in advanced modules on **out-of-order execution**.

### 2.1.3 Control Hazards (Branch Hazards)

- **Definition:** The pipeline must decide *which instruction to fetch next* while the branch outcome is still being computed.
- **The 5-Stage MIPS Latency:** In the classic MIPS pipeline, the branch target is known only at the **end of the EX stage** (cycle 3 for a branch issued in cycle 1). Thus, the next 2 instructions fetched are *speculative*.
- **Resolution Strategies** (in increasing hardware complexity):

| Strategy | Mechanism | Mis-prediction Penalty |
|----------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Stall until resolved** | Freeze IF/ID until EX completes | **2 bubbles per branch** |
| **Branch Prediction — Always Not Taken** | Continue sequential fetch, flush if wrong | 0 cycles if correct; 2 if wrong |
| **Delayed Branch (compiler)** | Fill delay slot with a *useful* instruction | 0 cycles if optimized |
| **Dynamic Branch Prediction (1-bit / 2-bit)** | BHT (Branch History Table) with saturating counter | Varies, typically < 1 cycle average |
| **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** | Cache of recent branch target PC addresses | Often 0 cycles |

## 2.2 Resolution Mechanisms — The Engineering Toolkit

### 2.2.1 Pipeline Interlock Hardware

The **Hazard Detection Unit (HDU)** is a combinational logic block in the ID stage. It examines:

- The destination register of instructions in EX/MEM and MEM/WB pipeline registers.
- The source registers of the instruction currently in ID.

If a match is found and the matched instruction is a **load** (whose data is not yet available), the HDU asserts a **Stall** signal that:
- Holds the PC and IF/ID register (the instruction does not advance).
- Inserts a `nop` into the ID/EX register.

### 2.2.2 Forwarding (Bypassing) Paths

Forwarding eliminates most RAW stalls by routing ALU results from **later stages back to the ALU inputs** in the EX stage. There are **three canonical forwarding paths** in a 5-stage MIPS:

| Path Name | Source Stage | Destination | When Used |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **EX/MEM → EX** | End of EX | ALU input A or B | `add` followed by dependent `add` |
| **MEM/WB → EX** | End of MEM | ALU input A or B | Result of a 2-cycle-back instruction |
| **MEM/WB → EX (load data)** | End of MEM | ALU input | Handles the lone unforwardable case |

The Load-Use case (load in EX, dependent instruction needs data) **cannot be solved by forwarding alone** — it requires a **1-cycle stall + forwarding from MEM/WB**.

### 2.2.3 Branch Resolution Acceleration

Moving the branch decision earlier (to the **ID stage**) eliminates the 2-cycle penalty. This requires:
- A separate **adder** in the ID stage to compute the branch target.
- Comparison logic in ID (the register file read ports already exist).
- Result: branch penalty drops to **0 cycles** (effectively) if done well.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation & Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Pipeline Speedup** | $S_{\text{ideal}} = k$ | $k$ = number of pipeline stages; dimensionless |
| 2 | **Actual Speedup (with stalls)** | $S = \dfrac{k \cdot n}{k + n - 1 + \text{Total Stall Cycles}}$ | $n$ = instruction count |
| 3 | **Pipeline Throughput (IPC)** | $\text{IPC} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1 + \text{stalls}}$ | Instructions per cycle |
| 4 | **Branch Penalty (no prediction)** | $P_{\text{branch}} = b \cdot p$ | $b$ = branch frequency; $p$ = penalty in cycles |
| 5 | **Speedup with forwarding (RAW only)** | $S_{\text{fwd}} = \dfrac{n}{1 + \text{stalls}_{\text{load-use}}}$ | Assumes only load-use requires stall |
| 6 | **Load-Use Stall Frequency** | $f_{\text{LU}} = \dfrac{\text{load-use pairs}}{n}$ | Fraction of instructions; $0 \le f_{\text{LU}} \le 1$ |
| 7 | **Amdahl's Pipeline Limit** | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction of pipeline-friendly code |
| 8 | **Clock Period Constraint (pipeline)** | $T_{\text{clk}} \ge \max(T_{\text{stage}}) + t_{\text{setup}} + t_{\text{skew}}$ | Seconds; $T_{\text{stage}}$ per stage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On a KTU 2024 question paper, you will be **expected to substitute numerical values** into formulas 1–6. Memorize the **denominator structure** of the actual-speedup formula — it appears in nearly every Part B question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hazard handling is not academic — it is the **heart of every commercial CPU**:

- **Intel / AMD Out-of-Order Cores** use a **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** and **Register Alias Table (RAT)** to resolve WAR/WAW hazards via *register renaming* — a technique every senior SoC engineer must master.
- **ARM Cortex-A series** pipelines (e.g., 13-stage in Cortex-A77) include **speculative execution** with **branch target prediction** accuracy above 95%, validated by hazard simulation.
- **RISC-V Vector Processors** depend critically on **structural hazard-free chaining** of vector functional units.
- **Safety-critical systems** (automotive, medical) use **lockstep dual-pipeline execution** — a direct anti-hazard redundancy technique.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pipeline Speedup with and without Hazards

We start with the **sequential execution time** of $n$ instructions on a non-pipelined processor with cycle time $T$ and $k$ stages per instruction:

$$T_{\text{seq}} = n \cdot k \cdot T$$

### Step 1 — Ideal Pipelined Time (No Hazards)

The first instruction takes $k$ cycles to complete; each subsequent instruction finishes one cycle later. Total time:

$$T_{\text{ideal}} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$$

### Step 2 — Speedup of Ideal Pipeline

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T}$$

Cancel $T$:

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Add Hazard Stalls

Let $S_{\text{cycles}}$ denote the **total stall cycles** introduced by all hazards. Then:

$$T_{\text{actual}} = (k + n - 1 + S_{\text{cycles}}) \cdot T$$

### Step 4 — Actual Speedup

$$S_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1 + S_{\text{cycles}}) \cdot T}$$

Cancel $T$:

$$S_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1 + S_{\text{cycles}}}$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: As $n \to \infty$

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{\text{actual}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1 + S_{\text{cycles}}}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{\text{actual}} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{\dfrac{k - 1}{n} + 1 + \dfrac{S_{\text{cycles}}}{n}}$$

As $n \to \infty$, the fractions $(k-1)/n$ and $S_{\text{cycles}}/n$ tend to zero (provided stalls scale sub-linearly with $n$). Hence:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{\text{actual}} = k$$

> This proves that **ideal pipeline speedup = number of stages**, but only when stalls grow *slower* than linearly with the instruction count. Excessive hazards destroy this property.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivation — MIPS 5-Stage Pipeline with Forwarding

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
- 5-stage MIPS pipeline ($k = 5$).
- Total instructions $n = 1000$.
- Branch frequency $b = 20\%$ (i.e., 200 branches).
- Each branch is resolved in the **EX stage** → penalty $p = 2$ cycles if not taken, $p = 2$ cycles if mispredicted.
- A static **Predict-Not-Taken** policy is used. 60% of branches are actually not-taken.
- Load-use pairs: 50 instances → $S_{\text{LU}} = 50$ stall cycles.
- No structural hazards.

### Step 1 — Compute Branch Stalls

For predict-not-taken, a stall occurs **only when the branch is taken** (misprediction). 

Number of taken branches: $200 \times 0.40 = 80$.

Wait, re-reading: 60% are *not-taken*. Therefore 40% are *taken*. With predict-not-taken, every *taken* branch causes a flush.

Number of mispredictions: $200 \times 0.40 = 80$ taken branches × 2-cycle penalty.

$$S_{\text{branch}} = 80 \times 2 = 160 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 2 — Load-Use Stalls

$$S_{\text{LU}} = 50 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 3 — Total Stall Cycles

$$S_{\text{cycles}} = S_{\text{branch}} + S_{\text{LU}} = 160 + 50 = 210 \text{ cycles}$$

### Step 4 — Actual Total Time

$$T_{\text{actual}} = (5 + 1000 - 1 + 210) \cdot T = 1214 \, T$$

### Step 5 — Sequential (Non-Pipelined) Time

$$T_{\text{seq}} = 1000 \times 5 \times T = 5000 \, T$$

### Step 6 — Actual Speedup

$$S_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{5000 \, T}{1214 \, T} = \dfrac{5000}{1214} \approx 4.12$$

### Step 7 — Compare with Ideal

$$S_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 5}{5 + 1000 - 1} = \dfrac{5000}{1004} \approx 4.98$$

### Step 8 — Efficiency Loss

$$\eta_{\text{loss}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{ideal}} - S_{\text{actual}}}{S_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100\%$$

Substitute:

$$\eta_{\text{loss}} = \dfrac{4.98 - 4.12}{4.98} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.86}{4.98} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{loss}} \approx 17.27\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner will give **full marks** only if the student explicitly writes each substitution. **Do not skip arithmetic** — even if the numbers are clean, every step must be shown.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard × Resolution Cross-Reference

| Hazard Type | Detection Stage | Resolution Hardware | Stall Cycles Saved | Hardware Cost |
|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Structural (single mem) | IF vs MEM | Separate I-cache / D-cache | All | High (duplication) |
| RAW (ALU-ALU) | ID | Forwarding from EX/MEM, MEM/WB | All except load-use | Low (mux + control) |
| RAW (Load-Use) | ID | Stall (1 cycle) + Forward | Forward eliminates 1 of 2 | Very Low |
| WAR / WAW | Issue (OoO only) | Register renaming | All | Very High (ROB) |
| Control (conditional) | EX | Predict-Not-Taken / BTB / BHT | 0–2 per branch | Low to High |
| Control (unconditional jump) | ID | Direct target compute | All | Low (adder in ID) |

## 3.4 Branch Penalty Computation — Detailed Derivation

For a pipeline with a branch resolved in stage $X$ (numbered $1 \ldots k$), the *always-stall* penalty is:

$$p_{\text{stall}} = X - 1$$

For 5-stage MIPS, $X = 3$ (EX), so $p_{\text{stall}} = 2$.

With **predict-not-taken** and flush-on-mispredict:

$$p_{\text{avg}} = p_{\text{stall}} \times P(\text{branch is taken})$$

Substituting $p_{\text{stall}} = 2$ and $P(\text{taken}) = 0.40$ from the previous example:

$$p_{\text{avg}} = 2 \times 0.40 = 0.8 \text{ cycles per branch}$$

Total branch penalty for the program:

$$P_{\text{total}} = n \times b \times p_{\text{avg}} = 1000 \times 0.20 \times 0.8 = 160 \text{ cycles}$$

This matches $S_{\text{branch}}$ derived in Step 1, confirming the two derivation methods are consistent.

## 3.5 Forwarding Path Decision Logic

The forwarding control signals are derived from the instruction fields in the EX/MEM and MEM/WB pipeline registers. For a destination register $R_d$ and source registers $R_{s1}, R_{s2}$:

**Forward-A Source (to ALU input A):**

$$
\text{FwdA} = \begin{cases}
00 & \text{if no hazard} \\
01 & \text{if EX/MEM.Rd} = R_{s1} \text{ and EX/MEM.RegWrite} \\
10 & \text{if MEM/WB.Rd} = R_{s1} \text{ and MEM/WB.RegWrite} \\
\end{cases}
$$

**Special exclusion:** When $R_d = \$zero$ (register 0), forwarding is disabled because register 0 is hardwired to zero in MIPS.

> The HDU must perform this comparison **combinationally** in the ID stage of the dependent instruction, with the instruction fields of the two upstream pipeline stages latched in EX/MEM and MEM/WB registers.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MIPS 5-Stage Pipeline with Hazard Detection Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stage fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef hd fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef fwd fill:#15803d,stroke:#052e16,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#a16207,stroke:#451a03,color:#ffffff

    instr_in([Instruction Stream]) --> ifStage[IF: Instruction Fetch]
    ifStage --> idStage[ID: Instruction Decode / Register Read]
    idStage --> exStage[EX: Execute / Address Calc]
    exStage --> memStage[MEM: Memory Access]
    memStage --> wbStage[WB: Write Back]
    wbStage --> result([Result / Register File Update])

    exmemReg[EX/MEM Pipeline Register]:::reg
    memwbReg[MEM/WB Pipeline Register]:::reg
    ifidReg[IF/ID Pipeline Register]:::reg
    idexReg[ID/EX Pipeline Register]:::reg

    ifStage -.latches.-> ifidReg
    ifidReg -.feeds.-> idStage
    idStage -.latches.-> idexReg
    idexReg -.feeds.-> exStage
    exStage -.latches.-> exmemReg
    exmemReg -.feeds.-> memStage
    memStage -.latches.-> memwbReg
    memwbReg -.feeds.-> wbStage

    fwdA1[/Forward A from EX MEM/]:::fwd
    fwdA2[/Forward A from MEM WB/]:::fwd
    fwdB1[/Forward B from EX MEM/]:::fwd
    fwdB2[/Forward B from MEM WB/]:::fwd

    exmemReg -.-> fwdA1
    memwbReg -.-> fwdA2
    exmemReg -.-> fwdB1
    memwbReg -.-> fwdB2

    fwdA1 --> exStage
    fwdA2 --> exStage
    fwdB1 --> exStage
    fwdB2 --> exStage

    hdUnit{Hazard Detection Unit}:::hd
    hdUnit -.Stall.-> ifStage
    hdUnit -.Stall.-> idStage
    hdUnit -.Bubble NOP.-> idexReg

    exmemReg -.inputs.-> hdUnit
    memwbReg -.inputs.-> hdUnit
    ifidReg -.reads.-> hdUnit

    class ifStage,idStage,exStage,memStage,wbStage stage
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Forwarding Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef decision fill:#facc15,stroke:#713f12,color:#000000
    classDef action fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef hazard fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff

    start([Instruction in ID stage]) --> readRegs[Read Source Registers Rs and Rt]
    readRegs --> checkEX{EX MEM Rd equals Rs or Rt?}
    readRegs --> checkMEM{MEM WB Rd equals Rs or Rt?}
    readRegs --> checkLoad{EX MEM is a Load?}

    checkEX -- Yes, Rd matches Rs --> fwdA01[Set ForwardA equals 01]:::action
    checkEX -- Yes, Rd matches Rt --> fwdB01[Set ForwardB equals 01]:::action
    checkEX -- No --> next1[Continue to next check]

    checkLoad -- Yes --> stallAction[Stall one cycle - insert NOP]:::hazard
    checkLoad -- No --> noStall[No stall required]:::action

    checkMEM -- Yes, Rd matches Rs --> fwdA10[Set ForwardA equals 10]:::action
    checkMEM -- Yes, Rd matches Rt --> fwdB10[Set ForwardB equals 10]:::action
    checkMEM -- No --> noForward[Set Forward equals 00 - from register file]:::action

    class checkEX,checkMEM,checkLoad decision
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Load-Use Stall Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IF as IF Stage
    participant ID as ID Stage
    participant EX as EX Stage
    participant MEM as MEM Stage
    participant HDU as Hazard Detection Unit

    Note over IF,ID: Cycle 1 - LW instruction fetched
    IF->>ID: Forward LW for decode

    Note over ID,EX: Cycle 2 - LW reads base register
    ID->>EX: LW advances to EX
    Note right of HDU: HDU sees LW in EX/EX and dependent ADD in ID/ID

    Note over IF,ID: Cycle 3 - Stall bubble inserted
    HDU-->>IF: Stall signal asserted
    HDU-->>ID: Hold PC and IF/ID register
    HDU-->>EX: Inject NOP into ID/EX
    Note right of MEM: LW accesses memory, data ready at end of cycle

    Note over EX,MEM: Cycle 4 - ADD uses forwarded data
    EX->>MEM: ADD with forwarded value from MEM/WB

    Note over MEM: Cycle 5 - Normal completion
```
