3. **Control Data Register (CDR):** Holds the microinstruction currently being executed. Functions like the IR at the micro-level.
4. **Next Address Logic (Sequencer):** Decides the next value of CAR. It supports: sequential addressing ($CAR+1$), conditional branches (if a flag is set, branch to the address field), unconditional branches, and **opcode mapping** (dispatching to the micro-routine of the fetched machine instruction).
5. **Microinstruction Decoder:** Decodes the control field of the CDR to generate the actual control signals for the datapath.

The block diagram (refer Section 4.2) shows the flow: $CAR \to CM \to CDR \to \text{Decoder} \to \text{Control Signals}$ and the feedback $CDR \to \text{Sequencer} \to CAR$.

**(b) Numerical and Conceptual (7 marks)**

**(i) Control Memory Size:**

$$\text{Size} = 2^k \times n = 2^7 \times 16\ \text{bits} = 128 \times 16\ \text{bits} = 2048\ \text{bits} = 256\ \text{bytes}$$

**(ii) Fetch Micro-routine (assume it starts at address `0x00`):**

| CAR (Hex) | Micro-operation | Next CAR |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ | `0x01` (sequential) |
| `0x01` | $READ$, $MBR \leftarrow M[MAR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | `0x02` (sequential) |
| `0x02` | $IR \leftarrow MBR$ | `0x03` (sequential) |
| `0x03` | Dispatch: $CAR \leftarrow$ opcode-mapped address | Decoded opcode |

**(iii) Dispatch Mechanism:**

After fetching the instruction into the IR, the microprogram at address `0x03` performs the **mapping operation**. The sequencer concatenates (or ORs) the upper bits of CAR with the lower bits of the IR's opcode field. For example, if CAR's upper 3 bits are `001` and the opcode is `000` (ADD), the next CAR becomes `001000` (binary) = `0x08`. The control memory is pre-organised so that this calculated address is the **start of the micro-routine** for the $ADD$ instruction. Thus, the control unit "jumps" to the correct routine corresponding to the fetched machine instruction without any explicit comparison — it is a hardware-level jump table.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake in hardwired design:** Students often **skip the K-map simplification** and present unsimplified Boolean expressions. Examiners **deduct up to 2 marks** for not simplifying.
> 2. **Common mistake in microprogram design:** Students confuse **CAR with PC** and **CDR with IR** at the micro-level. Remember: the micro-program has its own PC (CAR) and IR (CDR) that operate on the *micro-program*, not on the *user/machine program*.
> 3. **In fetch cycle:** The PC is incremented at $T_1$ (or $T_2$), not at $T_0$. $T_0$ is the **address phase** (MAR ← PC), $T_1$ is the **data phase** (memory read into MBR), and $T_2$ is the **decode/load IR** phase. Markers penalise swapped timing.
> 4. **Address field width:** When asked to calculate control memory size, students sometimes use the *number of microinstructions actually used* instead of $2^k$. Always use the **CAR width $k$** to compute the maximum capacity $2^k \times n$.
> 5. **In microprogrammed control:** Forgetting the **MAP/Dispatch** microinstruction at the end of the fetch routine is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control**

- [x] The **Control Unit** generates control signals to coordinate datapath activities.
- [x] **Hardwired CU** = fixed logic gates + sequence counter + instruction decoder.
- [x] **Microprogrammed CU** = Control Memory + CAR + CDR + Sequencer + Decoder.
- [x] General equation for any control signal in hardwired CU: $C_i = \sum D_j \cdot T_m \cdot F_n$.
- [x] **Microinstruction** = (Control Field) + (Next Address Field).
- [x] **Horizontal microinstruction** = one bit per control signal (fast, wide).
- [x] **Vertical microinstruction** = encoded control field (compact, slower).
- [x] Control memory size = $2^k \times n$ bits ($k$ = CAR bits, $n$ = microinstruction width).
- [x] The **fetch micro-routine** is universal and runs for every instruction.
- [x] **Dispatch** is the micro-instruction that maps the IR opcode to the start address of the corresponding micro-routine.
- [x] The **Next Address Logic / Sequencer** supports 4 operations: sequential, conditional branch, unconditional branch, and opcode mapping.
- [x] **Hardwired** is fast but inflexible; **Microprogrammed** is slow but easily modifiable.
- [x] **RISC** → predominantly hardwired; **CISC** → predominantly microprogrammed.
- [x] **Microcode updates** are how modern x86 vendors (Intel, AMD) fix CPU bugs in the field.
- [x] For KTU design problems: always present the **control matrix first**, then the **Boolean expressions**, then the **simplification (K-map)**, then the **gate-level implementation**.
- [x] **Wilkes Control (1951)** = first formal microprogrammed design, using a diode matrix.
- [x] The control field's number of bits is equal to (or less than, if encoded as vertical) the number of distinct control signals required by the datapath.
- [x] Condition flags ($Z$, $N$, $C$, $V$) from the ALU gate the activation of branch control signals in both hardwired and microprogrammed schemes.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Hardwired & Microprogrammed Control**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Processor Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of Processor Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Control Unit (CU)** is the central nervous system of the Central Processing Unit (CPU) that orchestrates, regulates, and coordinates the execution of instructions by generating precisely timed **control signals**. It is responsible for fetching, decoding, and executing machine instructions by directing the data flow between the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **registers**, **memory**, and **I/O subsystems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Control Unit is defined as the hardware component that interprets machine instructions and generates the appropriate sequence of control signals to execute them. It transforms the *instruction register* content and the *machine state* (flags, timing) into physical electrical signals that activate the CPU datapath components.

The **Microprogrammed Control Unit** stores the binary control values (micro-orders) for every instruction in a dedicated **Control Memory (CM)**, where each word in this memory is called a **Control Word (CW)** or **Microinstruction**. Each control word contains the bits that directly drive the datapath signals for a single micro-operation (clock sub-cycle).

The **Hardwired Control Unit**, in contrast, implements the control logic using **fixed combinational logic gates, decoders, and flip-flops** that derive each control signal as a Boolean function of the instruction opcode, timing signals (T-states), and condition flags.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Railway Signal Controller**
> Imagine a giant railway junction where multiple trains (data) must travel to their destination platforms (registers/ALU/memory). The **Control Unit** is the *station master* standing in the signal cabin.
> - The **train schedule** = the machine instruction fetched from memory.
> - The **signal levers** = control signals that switch tracks.
> - **Hardwired Control** is like an *electromechanical relay logic cabin* — the wires are physically soldered such that pulling a specific lever automatically triggers a fixed chain of switches.
> - **Microprogrammed Control** is like a *modern computer-assisted signal box* — the master consults a printed **micro-program booklet** (control memory) for each scheduled train, reads the next line (microinstruction), and pulls the levers accordingly. The booklet can be reprinted to handle new routes (firmware upgradable).
> - The **timing pulses (T1, T2, T3...)** are the ticking of the station master’s clock — each step in the journey happens on one tick.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Physical Quantities

- **Control Word Length (m):** Total number of bits in one microinstruction.
- **Control Memory Size:** Number of microinstructions × word length = $2^k \times m$ bits, where $k$ is the microinstruction address width.
- **Microinstruction Cycle Time (Tμ):** Time to fetch and execute one microinstruction. Must be ≤ **1 clock cycle** of the host CPU.
- **Standard T-States for a Fetch Cycle:** **T1, T2, T3, T4** (4 T-states per memory access on classic 8085-style timing).
- **Sequencer:** The hardware block that determines the *next* microinstruction address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** The students are required to design both **Hardwired** and **Microprogrammed** control units, derive the Boolean equations of control signals, construct **microinstruction formats** (Horizontal and Vertical), and explain **microinstruction sequencing techniques** (Next Address Field, CAR increment, subroutine stacks).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functions of the Control Unit (Structured Logic)

The CU performs **three primary duties**, each decomposable into measurable sub-functions:

- **Sequencing:** Generate the correct **time-ordered** sequence of control signals for every instruction.
- **Execution:** Activate the specific datapath components (ALU, registers, buses) for each micro-operation.
- **Interpretation:** Decode the *opcode* bits of the IR and use them to select the proper micro-routine.

### 2.2 Inputs and Outputs of the Control Unit

**Inputs (to CU):**
- **Instruction Register (IR, 16/32 bits)** — holds the current opcode to be decoded.
- **Clock (CLK)** — primary timing reference (T1, T2, T3, …).
- **Condition Flags** — Zero (Z), Carry (CY), Sign (S), Overflow (V) from the status register.
- **Control Bus Signals** — READY, BUS REQUEST, INTERRUPT ACKNOWLEDGE from external sources.
- **Sequencer State** — current CAR (Control Address Register) value for the next address.

**Outputs (from CU):**
- **Internal CPU Signals:** `PC_out`, `PC_in`, `IR_in`, `READ`, `WRITE`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`.
- **ALU Control Lines:** `ALU_op_select[3:0]`.
- **Bus Control Signals:** `Bus_Select[2:0]` (tri-state buffer enables).
- **Memory/IO Strobes:** `MEM_RD`, `MEM_WR`, `IO_RD`, `IO_WR`.

### 2.3 Hardwired Control Unit — Operational Theory

The hardwired CU is built as a **finite state machine (FSM)**. Each T-state represents one *state* of the FSM. For each state, the next state is determined by the current opcode, flags, and timer.

- **Step 1:** Decode the opcode using a decoder.
- **Step 2:** For each required control signal, write a **sum-of-products (SOP)** Boolean equation.
- **Step 3:** Combine these equations into fixed AND-OR-NOT gate arrays.
- **Step 4:** A *sequencer counter* steps through the T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4) for every instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Hardwired"?** Because the Boolean logic is *burned into silicon*. Changing it requires physical redesign. It is the **fastest** control method (used in RISC pipelines) but is **inflexible**.

### 2.4 Microprogrammed Control Unit — Operational Theory

The microprogrammed CU uses **stored-program logic** at the micro-level.

- **Step 1:** Each machine instruction is implemented as a **micro-routine** — a sequence of microinstructions stored in the **Control Memory (CM)**.
- **Step 2:** The **Control Address Register (CAR)** holds the address of the next microinstruction to fetch.
- **Step 3:** The **Control Data Register (CDR)** holds the microinstruction currently being executed (i.e., the Control Word).
- **Step 4:** Each Control Word has two logical fields:
  - **Control Field (CF):** Bits that directly drive the datapath signals.
  - **Next Address Field (NAF):** Specifies where the next microinstruction resides.
- **Step 5:** The **Sequencing Logic** computes the next CAR value from: *(a)* NAF of current microinstruction, *(b)* branch conditions, *(c)* subroutine returns.

### 2.5 Microinstruction Formats: Horizontal vs Vertical

| Property | Horizontal Microinstruction | Vertical Microinstruction |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Field Width** | Very wide (one bit per signal) | Narrow (decoded fields) |
| **Encoding** | **Unencoded (1 bit per control signal)** | **Highly encoded (opcodes for actions)** |
| **Parallelism** | High (many signals active at once) | Low (one action at a time) |
| **Speed** | Fast (no decoding) | Slower (must decode field) |
| **Microprogram Size** | **Short** (fewer microinstructions) | Long (more microinstructions) |
| **Use Case** | CISC machines, Wilkes Control | RISC microcontrollers |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for the Control of Processor Unit topic are tabulated below. Memorize these for direct 3-mark and 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i$ | **Control Word Length** = sum of bits per control field | bits |
| 2 | $N_{\mu} = 2^k$ | **Number of microinstructions** in Control Memory, where $k$ = address width | microinstructions |
| 3 | $S_{CM} = N_{\mu} \times m$ | **Control Memory Size** in bits | bits |
| 4 | $S_{CM} = 2^k \times m$ | Combined form of (2) and (3) | bits |
| 5 | $T_{\mu} \leq T_{CLK}$ | **Microinstruction cycle ≤ CPU clock cycle** (critical timing constraint) | seconds |
| 6 | $F_{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\mu}}$ | **Microinstruction execution frequency** | Hz |
| 7 | $C_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left( \prod_{i=1}^{q_j} M_i \right)$ | **Control signal SOP** = sum of minterms (opcode × T-state × flags) | Boolean |
| 8 | $A_{next} = f(NAF, \text{branch}, Z, C, \text{CS})$ | **Next Address Function** for microsequencer | address bits |
| 9 | $P_{mux} = 2^{n}$ | **Mux input ports** to drive $n$ next-address sources | ports |
| 10 | $N_{\text{branch}} = 2^{b}$ | **Number of branch targets** from a $b$-bit branch field | targets |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardwired Control** is used in **Intel Core i-series** RISC-style execution pipelines and **ARM Cortex** cores where nanosecond latency is mandatory.
- **Microprogrammed Control** is used in **Intel x86 CISC** CPUs (since the 8086), **IBM System/360**, and modern **FPGA soft-cores** (MicroBlaze, Nios-II) where flexibility and patchability are critical.
- **Control Memory** is analogous to firmware — it can be updated in-field to fix microcode bugs (e.g., the famous Intel Pentium FDIV bug fix of 1994).
- The **microsequencer** is the direct analog of a CPU's program counter — making the Control Unit a "CPU inside the CPU."

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Control Word Length for a Hypothetical CPU

> **Problem:** A 16-bit CPU has 12 distinct register transfer operations, 8 ALU function codes, 7 memory read/write signals, and 4 sequencer branch conditions. Each group is encoded with the minimum number of bits. Compute the **Control Word Length (m)** and the **Control Memory Size** if the microprogram has 256 microinstructions.

**Step 1 — Determine bits per control field.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{RTO} &= \lceil \log_2(12) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits} \\
b_{ALU} &= \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
b_{MEM} &= \lceil \log_2(7) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
b_{BR}  &= \lceil \log_2(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** — the smallest integer ≥ the given value. This ensures enough binary codes to represent every operation in the group.

**Step 2 — Sum the bits to get the control word length.**

$$
m = b_{RTO} + b_{ALU} + b_{MEM} + b_{BR} = 4 + 3 + 3 + 2 = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Control Memory Size.**

Given $N_{\mu} = 256$ microinstructions, the address width is:

$$
k = \log_2(256) = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
S_{CM} = N_{\mu} \times m = 256 \times 12 = 3072 \text{ bits} = 384 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Answer:** $m = 12$ bits; $S_{CM} = 3072$ bits ($\approx 384$ bytes).

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Boolean Control Signal for `PC_out`

> **Problem:** In a single-bus CPU, derive the Boolean expression for the control signal `PC_out` (which enables the tri-state buffer of the Program Counter onto the common bus) for the machine instruction **ADD R1, R2** (opcode = `0001`). Assume the bus transfer happens in T2 of the fetch cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify all activation conditions.**

The signal `PC_out` is asserted when:
- The current T-state is **T2** (timing condition), AND
- The opcode in IR is `0001` (ADD), AND
- The bus transfer is required during the **instruction fetch** phase (always true at T2).

**Step 2 — Write the SOP Boolean equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC\_out} &= T_2 \cdot \overline{IR_3} \cdot \overline{IR_2} \cdot \overline{IR_1} \cdot IR_0 \\
&= T_2 \cdot \overline{IR_3 IR_2 IR_1} \cdot IR_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with the decoder approach.**

A 4-to-16 instruction decoder produces 16 active-high outputs $D_0, D_1, \ldots, D_{15}$, where $D_i$ is high only when the opcode equals binary $i$. The opcode `0001` activates $D_1$. So:

$$
\text{PC\_out} = T_2 \cdot D_1
$$

This single line implements the entire Boolean equation using one AND gate. **Hardware reduction: 4 gates vs 1 gate.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** When asked to "design a hardwired control signal," always show **two** forms: (1) raw SOP from opcode bits, and (2) simplified form using the instruction decoder output $D_i$. This typically scores the full 7 marks in a Part B sub-question.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Horizontal vs Vertical Control Word Comparison

> **Problem:** A CPU has 32 distinct control signals. Compare the control word length of a **horizontal** vs **vertical** (fully encoded) microinstruction scheme.

**Horizontal Scheme — 1 bit per signal:**

$$
m_H = 32 \text{ bits}
$$

**Vertical Scheme — fully encoded (like a sub-opcode):**

$$
m_V = \lceil \log_2(32) \rceil = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Trade-off analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compression Ratio } \eta &= \frac{m_V}{m_H} = \frac{5}{32} = 0.15625 = 15.625\% \\
\text{Speed Penalty} &: \text{Vertical requires an } 32\text{-to-}5 \text{ decoder; } T_{decode} \approx 3 \text{ gate delays}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering conclusion:** Vertical saves $84.4\%$ of control memory, but each microinstruction takes longer to execute. In pipelined superscalar CPUs, the *horizontal* form is preferred because it allows **wide-issue** micro-operations per cycle.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed CU

| Performance Metric | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | **Very Fast** (single gate delay) | Slower (CM access + decode) |
| **Flexibility** | None — fixed at fabrication | **High** — microcode can be patched |
| **Hardware Cost (gates)** | **High** for complex ISAs (grows as $O(2^n)$) | Lower for complex ISAs (memory grows linearly) |
| **Design Time** | Long (manual Boolean minimization) | Shorter (microcode is software-like) |
| **Best Suited For** | **RISC**, real-time, pipelined cores | **CISC**, firmware-upgradable, emulation |
| **Examples** | ARM Cortex-A, MIPS R4000 | Intel x86 (microcode ROM), IBM z/Architecture |
| **Failure Mode** | Stuck-at faults in PLA | Microcode bit-flips (rare, but patchable) |

---

### 3.5 Worked Derivation 4: Wilkes Control Microinstruction Format

> **Problem:** For the classic **Wilkes Control Unit (1951)**, the microinstruction has 16 horizontal control bits and 2 branch bits driving a 4-way branch. Compute the **total microinstruction width** and the **branch address space**.

**Step 1 — Microinstruction width.**

$$
m_{Wilkes} = m_{control} + m_{branch} = 16 + 2 = 18 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Branch address space.**

$$
N_{branch} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ possible branch targets}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** With 2 branch bits, the microsequencer can implement a 4-way conditional branch: branch to one of 4 microinstruction addresses based on the current condition flags (Z, C, S, V).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hardwired Control Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Register IR] --> B[Opcode Decoder]
    B --> C[Decoder Outputs D0 to D15]
    D[Clock Generator] --> E[Timing Sequencer]
    E --> F[T1 T2 T3 T4 State Pulses]
    G[Status Flags Z C S V] --> H[Combinational AND OR Gates]
    C --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Control Signals Bus]
    I --> J[CPU Datapath ALU Registers Memory]
    K[Bus Request Interrupt Ready] --> H
```

**Functional description of the diagram:**

- The **IR opcode** is decoded into one of $2^n$ active decoder outputs ($D_0 \ldots D_{15}$ for a 4-bit opcode).
- The **clock generator** produces T-state pulses, which the **timing sequencer** shapes into discrete timing windows (T1, T2, T3, T4).
- The **AND-OR logic array** combines three inputs: opcode-decoder outputs, timing pulses, and condition flags.
- The output is the bundle of **control signals** that drive the datapath.

---

### 4.2 Microprogrammed Control Unit — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CM[Control Memory CM] -->|microinstruction| CDR[Control Data Register CDR]
    CDR -->|Control Field CF| DEC[Decoders and Signal Generators]
    DEC --> SIG[Control Signals to Datapath]
    CDR -->|Next Address Field NAF| SEQ[Microsequencer]
    SEQ --> CAR[Control Address Register CAR]
    CAR --> CM
    EXT[External Start Address and Subroutine Stack] --> SEQ
    FLG[Status Flags Z C S V] --> SEQ
```

**Block descriptions:**

- **Control Memory (CM):** Read-only memory (ROM) holding the micro-routines for every machine instruction. Each location stores one microinstruction.
- **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction just read from CM, splits it into CF and NAF.
- **Decoders and Signal Generators:** Convert the encoded CF bits into individual control signals (e.g., `PC_out`, `ALU_ADD`).
- **Microsequencer:** Computes the next CAR value using the NAF, condition flags, subroutine stack, and external start address (for instruction dispatch).
- **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the current CM address; analogous to the PC at the micro-level.

---

### 4.3 Microinstruction Sequencing Flowchart (Sequencer Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Microinstruction Cycle] --> B{Is it Branch Microinstruction}
    B -- No --> C[Next Address = CAR plus 1 Sequential]
    B -- Yes --> D{Evaluate Condition Flag}
    D -- Flag is Set --> E[Next Address = NAF from Microinstruction]
    D -- Flag is Reset --> F[Next Address = CAR plus 1]
    E --> G[Load into CAR]
    F --> G
    C --> G
    G --> H[Fetch Next Microinstruction from CM]
    H --> I[End of Cycle]
```

**Sequencing logic explanation:**

The flowchart captures the three primary next-address selection strategies:
1. **Sequential increment** (default for non-branch microinstructions).
2. **Conditional branch** (uses condition flags like Z or C to select NAF or CAR+1).
3. **Unconditional jump** (always loads NAF into CAR).

---

### 4.4 Horizontal vs Vertical Microinstruction Format (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Horizontal Format] --> H2[Bit 0 PC out]
    H1 --> H3[Bit 1 PC in]
    H1 --> H4[Bit 2 IR out]
    H1 --> H5[Bit 3 IR in]
    H1 --> H6[Bit 4 ALU ADD]
    H1 --> H7[Bit 5 ALU SUB]
    H1 --> H8[Bit 31 NAF plus flags]
    V1[Vertical Format] --> V2[Field 0 Operation Code 3 bits]
    V1 --> V3[Field 1 Register Select 2 bits]
    V1 --> V4[Field 2 Operand Mode 2 bits]
    V1 --> V5[Field 3 Next Address 5 bits]
    V1 --> V6[Field 4 Condition 1 bit]
```

**Schematic interpretation:**

- **Horizontal:** Each bit is dedicated to a specific control signal. The microinstruction is **wide** (e.g., 32+ bits), but decoding is trivial.
- **Vertical:** Fields are encoded. A 3-bit `OPCODE` field can select one of 8 pre-defined micro-actions; decoding is needed at runtime.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Control Unit. List its three primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Control Unit (CU)** is the hardware component of the CPU that orchestrates the execution of machine instructions by generating timed control signals for the datapath, memory, and I/O units.

Its three primary functions are:
1. **Instruction Sequencing** — producing the correct time-ordered series of control signals for each instruction.
2. **Instruction Execution** — activating the appropriate datapath components (ALU, registers, buses) for each micro-operation.
3. **Instruction Interpretation** — decoding the opcode bits of the Instruction Register (IR) and selecting the corresponding micro-routine or hardwired logic.

*(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for the three functions listed, 0.5 mark for any one-line elaboration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a Hardwired Control Unit and a Microprogrammed Control Unit. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Fixed combinational logic (gates, PLA) | Stored micro-routines in Control Memory |
| Flexibility | Not easily modified | Microcode can be patched in-field |

**Advantage of Hardwired CU:** Faster execution (single gate delay; no CM access).

**Advantage of Microprogrammed CU:** Easy to design and modify for complex ISAs (e.g., x86 emulation).

*(1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for advantages.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the design of a **Hardwired Control Unit** with a neat block diagram. Derive the Boolean control signal expression for the `MEM_RD` (memory read) signal in a single-bus CPU executing the instruction **LDA 2050H** (opcode `3A`, 3-byte instruction). Assume the memory read occurs at T3 of the fetch cycle and the operand fetch is at T4 and T5. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Microprogrammed Control Unit**. Define the terms *Control Word, Control Memory, Microinstruction,* and *Next Address Field*. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Hardwired Control Unit**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1)

**Step 1 — Instruction format.**
LDA 2050H is a 3-byte instruction:
- Byte 1 (opcode `3A` = `0011 1010`)
- Byte 2 (low address `50H`)
- Byte 3 (high address `20H`)

**Step 2 — T-state timing for LDA.**

| T-State | Action | Control Signals |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | `PC_out`, `MEM_RD`, `IR_in` | Bus ← PC, IR ← Memory[PC] |
| T2 | `PC_out`, `ADD`, `PC_in` | PC ← PC + 1 |
| T3 | `PC_out`, `MEM_RD`, `IR_in` (low byte) | IR ← Memory[PC] |
| T4 | `PC_out`, `ADD`, `PC_in` | PC ← PC + 1 |
| T5 | `PC_out`, `MEM_RD`, `AR_in` (high byte) | AR ← Memory[PC] |
| T6–T8 | Execution: `AR_out`, `MEM_RD`, `ACC_in` | ACC ← Memory[AR] |

**Step 3 — Derive `MEM_RD` Boolean expression.**

The signal `MEM_RD` is asserted at T1, T3, and T5 for the LDA instruction. Letting the decoder output for opcode `3A` be $D_{3A}$:

$$
\text{MEM\_RD} = D_{3A} \cdot (T_1 + T_3 + T_5)
$$

**Expanded SOP form using opcode bits** (with $D_0 \ldots D_7$ for the 8-bit opcode $IR_7 \ldots IR_0$):

$$
\text{MEM\_RD} = D_{3A} \cdot T_1 + D_{3A} \cdot T_3 + D_{3A} \cdot T_5
$$

**Step 4 — Hardware reduction.**

Factor out $D_{3A}$:

$$
\text{MEM\_RD} = D_{3A} \cdot (T_1 + T_3 + T_5)
$$

This requires **1 OR gate (3 inputs) + 1 AND gate (2 inputs) = 2 gates total.** Without factoring, it would need 3 AND + 1 OR = 4 gates. **Marks are awarded for the simplification step.** ✅

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Microprogrammed Control Unit**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2)

**Definitions:**

- **Control Word (CW):** A single microinstruction containing all control bits that drive the datapath signals for one micro-operation. Its width is $m$ bits.
- **Control Memory (CM):** A read-only memory (ROM) that stores the complete set of control words (microprogram) for all machine instructions. Typical size: $2^k \times m$ bits.
- **Microinstruction:** Each row/word in the Control Memory, equivalent to one Control Word.
- **Next Address Field (NAF):** A sub-field of the microinstruction that specifies the address of the next microinstruction to be executed, used by the microsequencer to drive the CAR.

**Architecture flow:**

1. The IR opcode is used to generate a *starting CM address* (via a mapping ROM).
2. The CAR fetches the microinstruction at that address from CM.
3. The CDR latches it; the **Control Field** drives the datapath; the **Next Address Field** feeds the sequencer.
4. The sequencer computes the next CAR value (using branch conditions).
5. Cycle repeats until the micro-routine ends.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **microinstruction**? Compare **Horizontal** and **Vertical** microinstruction formats. A CPU requires **48 control signals** for its datapath. Calculate the control word length for both horizontal and vertical encoding schemes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Wilkes Control** with a neat block diagram. List the advantages and disadvantages of the **microprogrammed control approach**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a)**

**Definition:** A **microinstruction** is the smallest executable unit within the Control Memory of a microprogrammed Control Unit. It specifies a single micro-operation (e.g., `PC_out`, `ADD`, `PC_in`) that completes within one micro-cycle.

**Comparison Table (Horizontal vs Vertical):**

| Parameter | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| Bit per signal | 1 | Encoded |
| Word length | Wide (e.g., 48 bits for 48 signals) | Narrow (e.g., 6 bits) |
| Decoding | None | Required (decoder) |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Microprogram length | Short | Long |

**Calculation for 48 control signals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_H &= 48 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(one bit per signal)} \\
m_V &= \lceil \log_2(48) \rceil = 6 \text{ bits} \quad \text{(fully encoded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compression achieved:** $\dfrac{48 - 6}{48} = 87.5\%$ memory reduction in vertical form.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Wilkes Control**

**Block Diagram Description:**

The **Wilkes Control Unit (M. V. Wilkes, 1951)** has these key features:
- A **diode matrix** (early form of ROM) acts as the Control Memory.
- Each row of the matrix is one microinstruction.
- A **counter** and a **branch address register** jointly supply the next CM address.
- **Condition flags** (Z, C) select between the counter-incremented address and the branch target.

**Microinstruction structure (Wilkes format):**
- **Control field** (e.g., 16 bits) — directly drives the datapath.
- **Branch field** (e.g., 2 bits) — selects one of 4 next-address sources.

**Advantages of Microprogrammed Control:**
1. **Easier to design** for complex instruction sets.
2. **Flexible** — microcode can be patched in firmware updates.
3. **Lower hardware cost** for large ISAs (memory grows linearly, not exponentially).
4. **Easier debugging** — microcode is essentially a "program."

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Slower** than hardwired control (CM access adds latency).
2. **Microcode bugs** can cause widespread functional issues.
3. **Control Memory** is a non-trivial overhead in low-end MCUs.
4. **Sequencing logic** must be carefully designed to avoid pipeline hazards.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Control Unit Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing NAF (Next Address Field) with the address bus** — NAF is internal to the microsequencer; it does NOT go to external memory.
> 2. **Forgetting T-state timing** — In hardwired control derivations, students often write the SOP equation WITHOUT the $T_i$ timing terms, losing 2 marks per such omission.
> 3. **Mixing horizontal and vertical** — A control field of 3 bits labeled "PC_out, PC_in, IR_out" is HORIZONTAL; if the 3 bits are encoded, it becomes VERTICAL. The bit-encoding decides the type, NOT the field name.
> 4. **Skipping the simplification step** — Always show the Boolean factorization (e.g., $D_{3A} \cdot T_1 + D_{3A} \cdot T_3 = D_{3A}(T_1 + T_3)$) — examiners specifically look for it.
> 5. **No mention of "Wilkes"** in microprogrammed CU answers — naming the original 1951 design by M. V. Wilkes shows depth and earns a bonus mark.
> 6. **Control Memory units** — Always express in *bits* first, then convert to bytes/words. Mixing units is the #1 silent marks-killer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Control of Processor Unit**

- ✅ **Control Unit = Traffic Police** of the CPU. Inputs: IR, T-states, Flags. Outputs: control signals.
- ✅ **Two design philosophies:** Hardwired (gates, fast, fixed) vs Microprogrammed (ROM-based, flexible, slower).
- ✅ **Hardwired CU** = combinational AND-OR-NOT logic array driven by **opcode decoder outputs** $D_i$, **T-state pulses** $T_j$, and **condition flags**.
- ✅ **Microprogrammed CU** stores the control logic in **Control Memory** as a sequence of **microinstructions** (Control Words).
- ✅ **Key components of a microprogrammed CU:** CM, CAR, CDR, Microsequencer, Decoders.
- ✅ **Control Word** = Control Field (CF) + Next Address Field (NAF). Width = $m$ bits.
- ✅ **Control Memory Size formula:** $S_{CM} = 2^k \times m$ bits, where $k$ = micro-address width.
- ✅ **Horizontal microinstruction:** 1 bit per control signal → wide, fast, short microprograms.
- ✅ **Vertical microinstruction:** encoded fields → narrow, slower, long microprograms.
- ✅ **Wilkes Control (1951):** The original microprogrammed design using a diode matrix and branch fields.
- ✅ **Microsequencer** computes next address from NAF, flags, subroutine stack, and starting address from IR.
- ✅ **Trade-off formula to memorize:** $m_V = \lceil \log_2(m_H) \rceil$ (vertical bits from horizontal count).
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Hardwired in **ARM/RISC**, Microprogrammed in **x86/IBM z-Series**.
- ✅ **Hardwired grows $O(2^n)$** with opcode bits — bad for large ISAs.
- ✅ **Microprogrammed grows $O(N)$** with microinstruction count — good for large ISAs.
- ✅ **Pentium FDIV bug (1994)** — fixed via a microcode patch; classic case of microprogrammed CU flexibility.
- ✅ **Always include T-state terms** in hardwired Boolean derivations.
- ✅ **Always show both raw SOP and decoded form** in exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprogram Sequencer, Microprogrammed CPU Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microprogram Sequencer, Microprogrammed CPU Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microprogrammed Control Unit (CU)

A **Microprogrammed Control Unit** is a control unit whose binary control variables (control words) are stored as a memory of binary words called **microinstructions** in a special memory called **Control Memory (CM)**. The collection of microinstructions is termed a **microprogram**, which is typically stored in Read-Only Memory (ROM) or Programmable Logic Array (PLA).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT414 - Module 4):**
> *Microprogrammed control is a control implementation technique in which the control logic is implemented as a microprogram stored in a control memory. The control memory holds the set of microinstructions that drive the datapath operations of the CPU.*

## 1.2 Microprogram Sequencer

The **Microprogram Sequencer** is the heart of the microprogrammed control unit. It is a combinational/sequential circuit that determines the **next microinstruction address** to be fetched from the Control Memory. It accepts inputs like the current address, external status/condition flags, and the opcode of the macro-instruction, and produces the **Next Address** for the Control Address Register (CAR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functions of a Microprogram Sequencer:**
> 1. Increment the CAR by 1 (sequential execution).
> 2. Branch to a specific address based on status flags (conditional branching).
> 3. Load a new address from the microinstruction's address field (unconditional jump).
> 4. Map an opcode of the macro-instruction to a starting microaddress.
> 5. Handle subroutine calls and returns using a micro-stack.

## 1.3 Microprogrammed CPU Organization

A **Microprogrammed CPU Organization** refers to the structural design of a CPU in which the control unit is implemented using microprogrammed logic. It separates the **datapath** (registers, ALU, buses) from the **control logic** (microsequencer + control memory), enabling systematic and modifiable control design.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Orchestra Conductor & Sheet Music**
>
> Imagine a CPU as a **large orchestra**:
> - The **musicians** (violin, trumpet, drums) are like the **datapath components** (ALU, registers, buses).
> - The **conductor** raising his baton is the **microprogrammed control unit**.
> - The **sheet music** in front of the conductor is the **Control Memory** containing **microinstructions**.
> - The **conductor's hand movements** (left hand turning pages, right hand waving for crescendo) is the **microprogram sequencer** deciding **which line of music to play next**.
> - A **crescendo signal** from the lead violinist is like a **status flag** (e.g., Zero flag, Carry flag) that tells the conductor to take a different path.
> - When the conductor wants the strings section to start, he **jumps** to a different page — this is a **branch microoperation**.

In short:
- **Control Memory** = the *sheet music* (fixed sequence of operations)
- **Microprogram Sequencer** = the *conductor's logic* (decides next page/line)
- **Macro-instruction opcode** = *audience's request* (which song to play — maps to first page)
- **Status flags** = *real-time feedback* (loud/soft, fast/slow — affects dynamics)

This analogy explains why microprogrammed control is **flexible, modular, and easy to modify** — just rewrite the sheet music (control memory) rather than rewiring the conductor's brain (hardwired logic).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Microprogrammed Control Unit

A microprogrammed control unit consists of the following primary components:

1. **Control Address Register (CAR)** — Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched. Equivalent to the **Program Counter (PC)** for microinstructions.
2. **Control Memory (CM)** — A ROM that stores all the microinstructions. Each location contains a microinstruction.
3. **Control Data Register (CDR)** — Holds the microinstruction currently being executed (analogous to Instruction Register). Often called the **Microinstruction Register (MIR)** or **Pipeline Register**.
4. **Next Address Generator (NAG)** / **Microprogram Sequencer** — Combinational circuit that computes the next address for the CAR.
5. **Subroutine Register (SBR)** / **Microstack** — Used to store return addresses during micro-subroutine calls.
6. **Mapping Logic (Decoders/PLA)** — Converts the macro-instruction opcode into the starting microaddress of its microprogram.

## 2.2 Microinstruction Format

A typical microinstruction contains two major fields:

$$
\boxed{\text{Microinstruction} = \underbrace{\text{Control Field}}_{\text{DataPath Signals}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{\text{Condition Field}}_{\text{Branch Control}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{\text{Branch Address Field}}_{\text{Next Address}}}
$$

| Field | Width (Example) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Field** | 20–40 bits | Activates datapath control signals (e.g., $ADD$, $LD\_A$, $PC_{out}$) |
| **Condition Field (CD)** | 3–4 bits | Selects a status flag/condition for branching (e.g., $Z$, $C$, $N$, $V$) |
| **Branch Address Field (BA)** | $k$ bits | Provides the target address for a branch/jump |
| **Mapping Bits (MAP)** | 1–2 bits | Indicates address source (incrementer, MAP ROM, SBR, BA field) |

## 2.3 Address Sequencing Operations

The microprogram sequencer must support the following next-address generation operations:

### Operation 1: Sequential Addressing
$$
\text{Next Address} = \text{CAR} + 1
$$
Used for the default case of executing the next consecutive microinstruction.

### Operation 2: Unconditional Branch
$$
\text{Next Address} = \text{BA (Branch Address Field)}
$$
The microinstruction itself specifies the next address, overriding the incrementer.

### Operation 3: Conditional Branch
$$
\text{Next Address} = \begin{cases} \text{BA} & \text{if Condition} = 1 \\ \text{CAR} + 1 & \text{if Condition} = 0 \end{cases}
$$
A status flag (Zero, Carry, Sign, etc.) decides between two paths.

### Operation 4: Mapping (Dispatch) from Opcode
$$
\text{Next Address} = \text{MAP ROM}(\text{Opcode})
$$
A hardware mapping ROM translates the macro-instruction opcode into the starting microaddress of its microprogram. This is the **initial dispatch** for any new instruction.

### Operation 5: Micro-Subroutine Call
$$
\text{Next Address} = \text{BA (subroutine entry)} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Stack.push(CAR+1)}
$$

### Operation 6: Micro-Subroutine Return
$$
\text{Next Address} = \text{Stack.pop()}, \quad \text{Stack.pop()} \rightarrow \text{SBR}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula & Logic Cheat Sheet (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Formula / Logic | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sequential Next Address | $N+1$ | $N$ = current CAR value |
| 2 | Control Memory Size | $CM_{size} = 2^k \times n$ | $k$ = address bits, $n$ = microinstruction word length |
| 3 | Branch Decision | $N \leftarrow BA$ if $CD = 1$ | $CD$ = condition flag |
| 4 | Mapping Logic | $M(\text{Opcode}) = \text{Start Address}$ | $M$ = Mapping ROM/PLA |
| 5 | Microinstruction Word Length | $n = CF + CD + BA + MAP$ | sum of all fields |
| 6 | Status Flag Test | $CD \in \{Z, NZ, C, NC, S, NS, V, NV, P, N\}$ | standard 4-bit $CD$ |
| 7 | Fetch Routine Length | fixed (typically 3–6 microinstructions) | common to all opcodes |
| 8 | Mapping Bits Encoding | 00 = Inc, 01 = MAP, 10 = SBR, 11 = BA | 2-bit MUX select |
| 9 | Next Address Function | $f(N, IR, CD, SBR) \rightarrow N_{new}$ | combinational |
| 10 | Stack Operations | $PUSH$ on call, $POP$ on return | LIFO microstack |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microprogrammed control is foundational in:
- **CISC processors** (e.g., Intel x86 family) where complex instructions need flexible microcode.
- **Emulation and virtualization** — different microcode versions can mimic older instruction sets.
- **Microcode patching** for CPU bug fixes (e.g., the famous Intel Pentium FDIV bug was fixed via microcode update).
- **Bit-slice processors** (AMD 2900 series) — the design is inherently microprogrammed.
- **RISC Exception Handling** — even modern RISC cores use microcode for complex multi-cycle operations like interrupts and traps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Microprogram Sequencer Next-Address Logic

Let us derive the boolean logic of a typical microprogram sequencer with **3 mapping bits** (M1, M2, M3) used as MUX select lines, and a single **condition bit** derived from a 4-to-1 MUX of status flags.

### Step 1: Define the Inputs

The sequencer receives four candidate sources for the next address:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= N+1 \quad \text{(incremented CAR)} \\
A_1 &= \text{MAP(IR[opcode])} \quad \text{(mapped from opcode)} \\
A_2 &= \text{SBR} \quad \text{(subroutine return address)} \\
A_3 &= \text{BA} \quad \text{(branch address from microinstruction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 4-to-1 MUX selects between these four sources.

### Step 2: Define the MUX Select Lines (Mapping Bits)

The 2-bit field $(M_1, M_2)$ selects the MUX input:

| $M_1$ | $M_2$ | Selected Source |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $A_0 = N+1$ |
| 0 | 1 | $A_1 = \text{MAP(IR)}$ |
| 1 | 0 | $A_2 = \text{SBR}$ |
| 1 | 1 | $A_3 = \text{BA}$ |

### Step 3: Introduce the Condition Bit

For conditional branching, an extra logic gate is added:

$$
N_{\text{new}} = \begin{cases}
\text{MUX}(A_0, A_1, A_2, A_3; M_1, M_2) & \text{if } (M_1, M_2) = (0,0) \text{ AND } CD = 0 \\
A_3 = \text{BA} & \text{if } (M_1, M_2) = (0,0) \text{ AND } CD = 1 \\
\text{MUX} & \text{otherwise (override condition)}
\end{cases}
$$

This is the **conditional branch override**: when the mapping bits indicate "increment" but the condition $CD$ is true, force the output to BA.

### Step 4: Derive the Boolean Expression

Let $M_1, M_2, CD$ be the three binary inputs. The next-address output $N_{\text{new}}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{new}} = \;& \overline{M_1}\overline{M_2}\,CD \cdot BA \\
&+ \overline{M_1}\overline{M_2}\,\overline{CD} \cdot (N+1) \\
&+ \overline{M_1} M_2 \cdot \text{MAP}(IR) \\
&+ M_1 \overline{M_2} \cdot SBR \\
&+ M_1 M_2 \cdot BA
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verification by Truth Table

| $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $CD$ | $N_{\text{new}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | $N+1$ (sequencer proceeds sequentially) |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | $BA$ (conditional branch satisfied) |
| 0 | 1 | x | $\text{MAP}(IR)$ (dispatch new opcode) |
| 1 | 0 | x | $SBR$ (return from sub-microprogram) |
| 1 | 1 | x | $BA$ (unconditional jump / call) |

This matches the canonical **Am2909 / Am2910 microsequencer** behavior used as a textbook example.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Logic for an 8-Instruction CPU

Consider a CPU with a 3-bit opcode (8 instructions). The mapping logic is a ROM/PLA that converts the opcode into an 8-bit starting microaddress. The microprogram for each instruction resides in control memory.

| Macro-Opcode | Instruction | Starting Microaddress (in CM) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 000 | ADD | `0x20` | Begin ADD microprogram |
| 001 | SUB | `0x30` | Begin SUB microprogram |
| 010 | MUL | `0x40` | Begin MUL microprogram |
| 011 | DIV | `0x50` | Begin DIV microprogram |
| 100 | LDA | `0x60` | Begin LDA microprogram |
| 101 | STA | `0x70` | Begin STA microprogram |
| 110 | JMP | `0x80` | Begin JMP microprogram |
| 111 | BZ | `0x90` | Begin BZ microprogram |

> [!NOTE]
> The mapping ROM has $2^3 = 8$ rows and produces an 8-bit microaddress, so its size is $8 \times 8 = 64$ bits.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Conditional Branch Microoperation

**Problem:** A microprogram is at CAR = `0x14`. The next microinstruction has $(M_1, M_2) = 00$ and $CD = Z$ (test the Zero flag) with $BA = 0x20$. If the Zero flag is currently $1$, what is the next address?

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} &\quad (M_1, M_2) = 00 \implies \text{default to incremental, but check condition.} \\
\text{Step 2:} &\quad CD = Z = 1 \implies \text{condition TRUE}. \\
\text{Step 3:} &\quad N_{\text{new}} = BA = 0x20. \\
\text{Step 4:} &\quad \text{The sequencer loads } 0x20 \text{ into the CAR.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the Zero flag had been $0$:
$$
N_{\text{new}} = N + 1 = 0x14 + 1 = 0x15
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Hardwired vs Microprogrammed Control

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Combinational logic gates, decoders, FFs | ROM/PLA + Microsequencer |
| Speed | **Faster** (no ROM access delay) | **Slower** (ROM access in critical path) |
| Flexibility | **Low** — requires rewiring | **High** — change microprogram |
| Design Effort | High for complex instructions | Systematic, modular |
| Cost for Complex CISC | Very High | Moderate |
| Used in | RISC, simple controllers | CISC, complex controllers |
| Example CPUs | ARM Cortex-A series | Intel x86, VAX, IBM 360 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Microprogrammed CPU Organization

The following diagram illustrates the high-level microprogrammed CPU organization, showing how the control memory, microsequencer, and datapath interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["Microprogrammed CPU"]
        direction TB
        subgraph CU["Control Unit"]
            CM["Control Memory ROM\n(holds microprogram)"]
            CAR["Control Address Register\n(CAR / uPC)"]
            CDR["Control Data Register\n(CDR / MIR)"]
            MS["Microprogram Sequencer\n+ Mapping Logic"]
            SBR["Subroutine Stack\n(SBR)"]
        end
        subgraph DP["Datapath"]
            PC["Program Counter"]
            IR["Instruction Register\n(IR)"]
            ALU["ALU"]
            REG["Register File\n(A, B, TEMP)"]
            DR["Data Register\n(DR)"]
        end
    end
    MEM["Main Memory"]
    EXT["Status Flags\n(Z, C, N, V)"]

    PC --> IR
    IR -- "Opcode" --> MS
    MS -- "Next Address" --> CAR
    CAR -- "Address Bus" --> CM
    CM -- "Microinstruction" --> CDR
    CDR -- "Control Field" --> DP
    CDR -- "BA + CD + MAP" --> MS
    MS -- "Push/Pop" --> SBR
    EXT --> MS
    DP --> MEM
    MEM --> DR
    DP --> EXT
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The **opcode** of the macro-instruction is sent to the **Microprogram Sequencer**, which uses the **Mapping ROM** to derive the starting address of that instruction's microprogram.
- This starting address is loaded into the **CAR**.
- The **CM** is read at this address, producing a **microinstruction** in the **CDR**.
- The microinstruction's **control field** activates datapath signals (e.g., $LD\_A$, $ADD$, $PC_{out}$).
- The microinstruction's **BA, CD, and MAP bits** are fed back to the sequencer to determine the next address.

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram: Microprogram Sequencer (Next Address Generator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN_CAR["Current CAR (N)"]
    IN_IR["IR Opcode"]
    IN_CD["Condition Bit\n(from CD field + Status)"]
    IN_BA["BA Field\n(from microinstruction)"]
    IN_SBR["SBR\n(Stack Top)"]
    IN_M1["M1"]
    IN_M2["M2"]

    INC["Incrementer\n(N + 1)"]
    MAP["Mapping ROM/PLA\n(Opcode -> Start Addr)"]
    MUX1["4-to-1 MUX\n(Select Next Address)"]
    OR["OR Gate for\nConditional Override"]

    OUT_CAR["New CAR (N_new)"]
    STK["Subroutine Stack"]
    STK --> IN_SBR

    IN_CAR --> INC
    INC --> MUX1
    IN_IR --> MAP
    MAP --> MUX1
    IN_SBR --> MUX1
    IN_BA --> MUX1
    IN_BA --> OR
    IN_CD --> OR
    OR --> MUX1
    IN_M1 --> MUX1
    IN_M2 --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> OUT_CAR
```

**Key Operations of the Sequencer:**
- **Incrementer** produces $N+1$ for sequential execution.
- **Mapping ROM** translates opcode to start address.
- **SBR** holds the return address for micro-subroutines.
- **BA Field** is the explicit branch target.
- **OR gate** with $CD$ allows the condition bit to override the incrementer when $M_1 M_2 = 00$.

## 4.3 Flowchart: Microprogram Execution Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Power ON / Reset\nCAR <- 0 (Fetch Routine)"]
    B["CAR -> CM[Address]"]
    C["CM -> CDR (Microinstruction)"]
    D["Decode Microinstruction\nin CDR"]
    E["Execute Control Field\n(activate datapath signals)"]
    F{"Check MAP field\n(M1, M2)"}
    G["Sequencer: N+1"]
    H["Sequencer: MAP(IR)"]
    I["Sequencer: SBR (return)"]
    J["Sequencer: BA (jump/call)"]
    K{"Condition\nsatisfied?"}
    L["N+1"]
    M["Load BA into CAR"]
    N["Update CAR -> Loop back to B"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -- "M1M2 = 00" --> K
    F -- "M1M2 = 01" --> H
    F -- "M1M2 = 10" --> I
    F -- "M1M2 = 11" --> J
    K -- "No" --> L
    K -- "Yes" --> M
    H --> N
    I --> N
    J --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> B
```

## 4.4 Microinstruction Memory Map (Sample Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CM["Control Memory (CM)"]
        direction TB
        A0["0x00 - 0x05\nFetch Routine (common)"]
        A1["0x06 - 0x0F\nDecode & Dispatch Logic"]
        A2["0x10 - 0x1F\nArithmetic (ADD, SUB)"]
        A3["0x20 - 0x2F\nLogic (AND, OR, XOR)"]
        A4["0x30 - 0x3F\nMemory (LDA, STA)"]
        A5["0x40 - 0x4F\nBranch (JMP, BZ, BNZ)"]
        A6["0x50 - 0x5F\nShared Micro-Subroutines\n(E.g., Add from Memory)"]
        A7["0xF0 - 0xFF\nInterrupt & Trap Handlers"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Memory Allocation in Control Store:**
> - The **Fetch Routine** is a common entry point for all macro-instructions.
> - After fetching, the **Mapping ROM** dispatches execution to the instruction-specific microprogram.
> - **Shared subroutines** (e.g., fetch operand from memory) reduce microcode size.
> - **Interrupt/Trap handlers** are usually placed at high addresses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a microprogrammed control unit? Differentiate between hardwired and microprogrammed control.**

**Model Answer:**

A **microprogrammed control unit** is one in which the control logic is implemented as a microprogram stored in a **Control Memory (CM)**. Each macro-instruction's control sequence is a sequence of **microinstructions** retrieved from CM. The sequencer generates the next microinstruction address based on status flags, opcode, and branch fields.

| Parameter | Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation | Gates, FFs, decoders | ROM + sequencer |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (ROM delay) |
| Flexibility | Hard to modify | Easily modified by rewriting microprogram |
| Cost | High for complex CISC | Moderate |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation table)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Explain the role of the mapping logic in a microprogrammed control unit.**

**Model Answer:**

The **mapping logic** (also called the **mapping ROM** or **mapping PLA**) is a hardware decoder that converts the **opcode** of a macro-instruction into the **starting microaddress** of its corresponding microprogram in the control memory.

**Role:**
1. **Dispatch Function:** When a new macro-instruction is fetched, the sequencer uses the mapping logic to jump from the common fetch routine to the specific execution microprogram.
2. **Decoding:** It decodes the $n$-bit opcode into a $k$-bit microaddress, where $k \geq \log_2(\text{number of microinstructions})$.
3. **Centralization:** Centralizes the instruction-to-microcode mapping, making the microprogrammed control modular and maintainable.

**Example:** A 3-bit opcode (8 instructions) is mapped by an $8 \times k$-bit ROM to microaddresses `0x20, 0x30, ..., 0x90`.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for role and example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **organization of a microprogrammed control unit**. Describe the functions of CAR, CDR, and Control Memory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **address sequencing operations** performed by the microprogram sequencer. Discuss conditional and unconditional branching with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit Organization (7 marks)**

The microprogrammed control unit consists of the following interconnected blocks (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

**1. Control Address Register (CAR):**
- Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be read.
- Functionally similar to the **Program Counter (PC)** of macro-instructions.
- It is loaded by the **microsequencer** at the end of each microinstruction cycle.

**2. Control Memory (CM):**
- A **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** that stores all the **microinstructions** (the microprogram).
- Addressed by the CAR. Each location contains one microinstruction of $n$ bits.
- **Size formula:** $CM = 2^k \times n$ bits, where $k$ = address bits.

**3. Control Data Register (CDR) / Microinstruction Register:**
- Holds the microinstruction **currently being executed**.
- Splits the microinstruction into:
  - **Control field** → drives the datapath.
  - **Address/condition fields** → feed the sequencer for the next address.

**4. Microprogram Sequencer (Next Address Generator):**
- Computes the next address for the CAR using inputs from CDR, IR (opcode), and status flags.
- Supports sequential, branch, jump, and mapping operations.

**5. Mapping ROM:**
- Maps the macro-instruction opcode to the starting microaddress of its microprogram.

**Execution Flow:**
1. Power-on: CAR is reset to 0 (fetch routine address).
2. CM[CAR] → CDR (microinstruction fetched).
3. Control field executes on datapath.
4. Sequencer computes next address → loads into CAR.
5. Cycle repeats.

---

**(b) Address Sequencing Operations (7 marks)**

The microprogram sequencer supports **six** next-address operations:

**1. Increment (Sequential):**
$$N_{\text{new}} = N + 1$$
Used by default for executing consecutive microinstructions. The **incrementer** adds 1 to CAR each cycle.

**2. Unconditional Branch (Jump):**
$$N_{\text{new}} = BA$$
The microinstruction's **BA field** specifies the next address. Used for jumps and subroutine calls. **Example:** A microinstruction with $M_1M_2 = 11$ forces the next address to be $BA = 0x40$.

**3. Conditional Branch:**
$$N_{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} BA & \text{if Condition} = 1 \\ N+1 & \text{if Condition} = 0 \end{cases}$$
**Example:** Test the Zero flag. If $Z = 1$, jump to `0x30`; else proceed to `N+1 = 0x12`.

**4. Mapping (Dispatch):**
$$N_{\text{new}} = \text{MAP ROM}(\text{Opcode})$$
Used after the fetch routine to jump to the specific microprogram for the instruction. **Example:** Opcode `001` (SUB) → microaddress `0x30`.

**5. Micro-Subroutine Call:**
$$N_{\text{new}} = BA_{\text{subroutine}}, \quad SBR \leftarrow N+1$$
The current return address is pushed onto the stack.

**6. Micro-Subroutine Return:**
$$N_{\text{new}} = SBR \quad (\text{from stack})$$
The stack-popped value is loaded into CAR.

**Truth Table Summary:**

| $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $CD$ | $N_{\text{new}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | $N+1$ |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | $BA$ (conditional branch) |
| 0 | 1 | x | $\text{MAP}(IR)$ |
| 1 | 0 | x | $SBR$ (return) |
| 1 | 1 | x | $BA$ (unconditional) |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022 / Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain **Wilkes control unit** in detail with a block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **microprogram for a 4-instruction CPU** (ADD, LDA, STA, BZ) showing the microinstruction sequence, control memory layout, and mapping logic. Use any standard microinstruction format. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wilkes Control Unit (7 marks)**

The **Wilkes Control Unit**, proposed by **M.V. Wilkes in 1951**, was the first systematic microprogrammed control design. It uses a **diode matrix** (essentially a ROM) to store microinstructions and a **microsequencer** with conditional branching.

**Block Diagram (in text form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IR (opcode)} &\rightarrow \text{Mapping Diode Matrix} \rightarrow \text{CAR}_k \\
\text{CAR} &\rightarrow \text{Diode Matrix (Control Store)} \rightarrow \text{CDR} \\
\text{CDR} &\rightarrow \text{Combinational Logic} \rightarrow \text{Datapath Control Signals} \\
\text{Status Flags} &\rightarrow \text{Next Address Logic} \rightarrow \text{CAR} \\
\text{Microinstruction CD field} &\rightarrow \text{Next Address Logic}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Features:**
1. **Diode Matrix for Control Store:** Each row corresponds to one microinstruction; columns are control signals.
2. **Branching Matrix:** A second matrix computes the next address based on the current microinstruction's address bits and status flags.
3. **Direct Microinstruction Decoding:** No intermediate decoder — diodes directly drive control lines.

**Advantages:**
- **Systematic design** — eliminates ad-hoc logic of hardwired control.
- **Easier to debug and modify** — change microprogram, not wiring.
- **Encouraged standardization** of control design.

**Limitations:**
- **Slow** — diode matrix read is in the critical path.
- **Limited microinstruction width** — number of diodes grows rapidly.
- **Sequential** — pipelining and parallelism not exploited.
- **Replaced** by modern ROM/PLA-based designs.

**Wilkes' Contribution:** He established the fundamental principle that **control logic is just another program** — a microprogram — and his design directly inspired modern microprogrammed CPUs like the IBM 360.

---

**(b) Microprogram Design for 4-Instruction CPU (7 marks)**

**Assumptions:**
- 2-bit opcode: `00=ADD, 01=LDA, 10=STA, 11=BZ`
- 8-bit microaddress; CM has 256 locations.
- Microinstruction format: `[Control Field (16 bits) | CD (2 bits) | BA (8 bits) | MAP (2 bits)]`

**Step 1: Mapping ROM (4×8 bits):**

| Opcode | Start Microaddress |
|---|---|
| `00` (ADD) | `0x20` |
| `01` (LDA) | `0x40` |
| `10` (STA) | `0x60` |
| `11` (BZ)  | `0x80` |

**Step 2: Control Memory Layout:**

| Address | Microinstruction (CF / CD / BA / MAP) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | `FETCH_PC` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $PC_{out}$, $AR_{in}$, $READ$, $INC\_PC$ |
| `0x01` | `FETCH_IR` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $DR_{out}$, $IR_{in}$ |
| `0x02` | `DECODE` / `xx` / `--` / `01` | Dispatch via MAP(IR) |
| `0x20` | `ADD_F1` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $IR_{addr}$, $DR_{out}$, $B_{in}$ |
| `0x21` | `ADD_F2` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $A_{out}$, $ADD$, $A_{in}$ |
| `0x22` | `ADD_END` / `xx` / `--` / `11` | Jump to `0x00` (fetch next) |
| `0x40` | `LDA_F1` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $IR_{addr}$, $DR_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ |
| `0x41` | `LDA_F2` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $READ$, $DR_{out}$, $A_{in}$ |
| `0x42` | `LDA_END` / `xx` / `--` / `11` | Jump to `0x00` |
| `0x60` | `STA_F1` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $IR_{addr}$, $DR_{out}$, $AR_{in}$ |
| `0x61` | `STA_F2` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $A_{out}$, $DR_{in}$, $WRITE$ |
| `0x62` | `STA_END` / `xx` / `--` / `11` | Jump to `0x00` |
| `0x80` | `BZ_TEST` / `Z` / `0x82` / `00` | If $Z=1$, branch to `0x82` (take branch) |
| `0x81` | `BZ_NEXT` / `xx` / `--` / `11` | Jump to `0x00` (no branch) |
| `0x82` | `BZ_TAKE` / `xx` / `--` / `00` | $IR_{addr}$ → $PC$ |
| `0x83` | `BZ_END` / `xx` / `--` / `11` | Jump to `0x00` |

**Step 3: Sample Microinstruction Bit Pattern (in Hex):**
For address `0x20` (ADD_F1): Control Field activates $IR_{addr}$, $DR_{out}$, $B_{in}$, $Read=0$, $Write=0$. $CD = 00$, $BA$ = don't care, $MAP = 00$.

**Execution Trace for ADD instruction (Opcode = 00):**
1. Fetch cycle: `0x00 → 0x01 → 0x02` (decode dispatches to `0x20`).
2. ADD: `0x20 → 0x21 → 0x22` (then jumps to `0x00` for next instruction).

This systematic design allows easy modification of the control logic by simply changing the CM contents.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the Mapping ROM:** Many students describe the sequencer but omit the **Mapping Logic** that maps opcode to start address. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Conflating CAR and CDR:** CAR = address pointer; CDR = holds the actual microinstruction. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **Not labeling the microinstruction fields:** Always label **Control Field (CF)**, **Condition (CD)**, **Branch Address (BA)**, and **Mapping (M1, M2)** clearly in diagrams.
> 4. **Missing the SBR/Stack for subroutines:** When describing micro-subroutines, always mention the **SBR** and the stack push/pop operations.
> 5. **Hardwired vs Microprogrammed table:** In 3-mark questions, the comparison table is a must. A vague verbal comparison loses marks.
> 6. **Truth Table Errors:** Students often write $M_1M_2 = 00$ as "unconditional branch." The correct behavior is "incremental OR conditional branch based on CD."
> 7. **Wilkes Diagram:** Don't skip the branching matrix — it's the most important part of Wilkes' design and a frequent 7-mark sub-question.
> 8. **Confusing $N$ and $N+1$:** The current address is $N$; the next sequential address is $N+1$. Mixing them up leads to wrong sequencer logic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Microprogram Sequencer & Microprogrammed CPU Organization**
>
> **Key Definitions:**
> - **Microinstruction:** A binary word in the control memory specifying datapath control signals and next-address info.
> - **Microprogram:** A sequence of microinstructions implementing the control logic for a macro-instruction.
> - **Control Memory (CM):** ROM storing the microprogram.
> - **CAR (Control Address Register):** Holds the next microaddress to fetch.
> - **CDR (Control Data Register / MIR):** Holds the currently executing microinstruction.
> - **Microsequencer / Next Address Generator:** Combinational circuit producing the next CAR value.
> - **SBR (Subroutine Register):** Stores return address for micro-subroutines.
> - **Mapping ROM/PLA:** Translates macro-instruction opcode to starting microaddress.
> - **Wilkes Control Unit:** Earliest microprogrammed design using diode matrices.
>
> **Six Next-Address Operations:**
> 1. Increment ($N+1$)
> 2. Unconditional Branch (BA)
> 3. Conditional Branch (BA if condition true, else $N+1$)
> 4. Mapping (MAP(IR))
> 5. Subroutine Call (BA, push return)
> 6. Subroutine Return (SBR, pop)
>
> **Microinstruction Fields:**
> - **Control Field (CF):** Activates datapath signals (largest part).
> - **Condition Field (CD):** Selects status flag for conditional branch.
> - **Branch Address (BA):** Target address for jump/call.
> - **Mapping (M1, M2):** Selects source for next address.
>
> **Important Formulas:**
> - $CM_{size} = 2^k \times n$ bits
> - $CD \in \{Z, NZ, C, NC, S, NS, V, NV, P, N\}$
> - Mapping Bits: `00 = Inc, 01 = MAP, 10 = SBR, 11 = BA`
>
> **Advantages of Microprogrammed Control:**
> - Flexible, easy to modify (rewrite microprogram)
> - Systematic design (good for CISC)
> - Emulation & microcode patching support
> - Centralized control
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> - Slower than hardwired (ROM access delay)
> - Microprogram development complexity
> - Requires careful optimization to avoid microcode bloat
>
> **Real-World Usage:**
> - Intel x86, IBM 360, VAX (CISC) — heavily microprogrammed
> - Modern RISC cores use microcode only for exception/interrupt handling
> - Microcode updates fixed the Pentium FDIV bug
> - Bit-slice processors (AMD 2900) are inherently microprogrammed
>
> **Memory Layout Tip:**
> - **Low addresses:** Fetch + Decode routine (common entry point)
> - **Mid addresses:** Instruction-specific microprograms
> - **High addresses:** Interrupt/Trap handlers & shared subroutines
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Micro Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Horizontal and Vertical Micro Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Microprogrammed Control Unit** architecture, the **Control Word (CW)** is the binary bit pattern stored in the Control Memory (CM) that activates the precise set of control signals required to execute one micro-operation. The structure and encoding strategy of this Control Word fundamentally classifies microinstructions into two philosophical categories: **Horizontal Microinstructions** and **Vertical Microinstructions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme — PERAT414):**
> A **Horizontal Microinstruction** is a *long-formatted, unencoded* binary control word in which each bit directly corresponds to a specific control signal in the datapath. Conversely, a **Vertical Microinstruction** is a *short-formatted, encoded* binary control word in which a group of bits represents a coded operation that must be decoded before activating the physical control lines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Light Controller" Model

Imagine you are managing a busy traffic intersection. You have two methods to give instructions to the traffic lights:

**Method 1 — Horizontal (The Light Switch Panel):**
You have a giant physical switchboard with **50 individual switches**, one switch for every single light bulb (Red East, Green North, Yellow South, etc.). You can simultaneously flip switch #1, switch #5, and switch #12 all at once to create complex multi-light patterns. This is powerful and fast, but the panel is huge, wiring is complex, and you must remember which switch does what.

**Method 2 — Vertical (The Remote Control with Buttons):**
You hold a small remote with **8 buttons** labeled `N-S-Green`, `E-W-Red`, `Flash-Amber`, `All-Red`, `Resume-Normal`, etc. You press one or two buttons, and an internal decoder in the traffic light cabinet turns on the correct bulbs. The remote is small, compact, and easy to use — but you cannot directly control an individual bulb, and there is a tiny delay while the decoder interprets the button press.

This perfectly captures the engineering trade-off between the two microinstruction formats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Remember:**
> - Horizontal Control Word Length = **N bits**, where N = total number of control signals.
> - Vertical Control Word Length = **⌈log₂ K⌉ bits per encoded field**, where K = number of mutually exclusive operations in a group.
> - Microinstruction Word size is measured in **bits per CW (Control Word)**.

---

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind the Distinction

A single processor datapath may require anywhere from **20 to 200+ control signals** simultaneously. If every microinstruction stored a literal bit for every signal, the Control Memory (ROM) would become enormous, the access time would degrade, and the microprogram would consume thousands of words. To balance **speed vs. storage**, computer architects devised these two opposing design philosophies.

| Aspect | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| **Format** | Long | Short |
| **Encoding** | None (Direct) | Heavy (Field-encoded) |
| **Parallelism** | Very High | Limited |
| **Decoder Hardware** | Not required | Required |
| **CW Size** | Wide (≈ 50–200 bits) | Narrow (≈ 16–32 bits) |
| **Speed** | Fast (no decode delay) | Slower (1–2 gate delays for decode) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Horizontal Microinstructions — Detailed Anatomy

In a **pure horizontal** microinstruction, the Control Word is essentially a direct replica of the processor's **Control Bus**. Each bit `i` of the microinstruction, when set to `1`, activates control line `i` of the datapath.

**Structure of a Horizontal Control Word:**

$$CW_{horizontal} = [F_0, F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_{N-1}, CD, BR, AD]$$

Where:
- $F_0, F_1, \ldots, F_{N-1}$ → Individual function/control bits (one per signal)
- $CD$ → Condition bit (for conditional branching in microprogram)
- $BR$ → Branch field
- $AD$ → Address field (location of next microinstruction)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality:** A truly horizontal microinstruction sets multiple control signals in parallel, enabling a single micro-step to perform many concurrent micro-operations (e.g., simultaneously read two registers, route them through the ALU, and write back to a third register).

### 2.1.1 Classification — "Purely Horizontal" vs "Hybrid Horizontal"

Modern microprogrammed designs (e.g., the classic **Mano's Basic Computer**) often use a **Field-encoded Horizontal** format. The control signals are grouped by function:
- **F1, F2, F3** → Encode ALU operations
- **CD** → Condition select
- **BR** → Branch type
- **AD** → Address of next microinstruction

This is technically *horizontally-oriented* but with some vertical encoding for compactness.

---

## 2.2 Vertical Microinstructions — Detailed Anatomy

In a **vertical** microinstruction, the control word is heavily encoded and looks much like a traditional **machine instruction** — it has an opcode and operand fields.

**Structure of a Vertical Control Word:**

$$CW_{vertical} = [OPCODE \mid FIELD_1 \mid FIELD_2 \mid \ldots \mid NEXT\_ADDRESS]$$

Each field must pass through a **decoder network** before reaching the physical control lines. For example, a 4-bit ALU field must be decoded into 16 possible operations via a **4-to-16 line decoder**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Decoding is Necessary:** The encoded bits are meaningless to the hardware until they pass through combinational decoders (AND-OR logic or ROM-based lookup tables). This adds **propagation delay** but dramatically reduces Control Word width.

### 2.2.1 Key Limitation of Vertical Format

Because the decoder can only activate **one operation per field per cycle**, vertical microinstructions are inherently **sequential within a field**. You cannot ask the ALU to "add AND subtract" in the same cycle because the opcode field can only hold one value at a time.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal CW Width | $W_H = N$ | $N$ = total number of control signals |
| Vertical Field Width | $W_{field} = \lceil \log_2 K \rceil$ | $K$ = number of operations in the encoded group |
| Total Vertical CW Width | $W_V = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \lceil \log_2 K_j \rceil + \text{AddrBits}$ | $m$ = number of fields |
| Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{W_H}{W_V}$ | Higher CR = greater storage savings |
| Address Field Width | $A = \lceil \log_2 (\text{Control Memory Size}) \rceil$ | Standard: $2^k$ words need $k$ address bits |
| Next Address Logic (Conditional) | $NA = \text{Base\_Addr} \mid (\text{Condition} \cdot \text{Branch\_Offset})$ | Used in microprogram sequencing |
| Microprogram Size | $S = W \times I$ | $W$ = word width (bits), $I$ = number of microinstructions |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU examinations, students frequently forget to add the **Next Address (AD)** and **Condition (CD)** bits to the total Control Word size calculation. Always include them in your word-width calculations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Preferred Format | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **High-speed RISC pipelines** | Horizontal | Minimal decode delay, critical for clock cycle time |
| **CISC microcode (e.g., x86 firmware)** | Vertical | Control Memory is expensive; compactness is essential |
| **Embedded microcontrollers** | Hybrid | Balance between speed and silicon cost |
| **Educational / Simulation (Mano's Computer)** | Field-encoded Horizontal | Pedagogically illustrates micro-operation control |
| **FPGA-based soft-core processors** | Horizontal | Direct mapping to FPGA control logic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Control Word Width Calculation

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style):**
> A processor datapath has the following control signal groups:
> - **ALU operations:** 16 distinct functions
> - **Register selection:** 8 registers (2 read ports, 1 write port)
> - **Memory operations:** 4 distinct memory control signals
> - **Shift operations:** 2 signals (shift left, shift right)
> - **Next address field:** 256 control memory words
>
> Calculate the Control Word width for **(a) Pure Horizontal format** and **(b) Pure Vertical format**.

### 3.1.1 Solution — Part (a): Pure Horizontal

In Pure Horizontal, every control signal gets its own dedicated bit.

**Step 1 — Count total individual control signals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALU signals} &= 16 \\
\text{Register read port 1} &= 3 \text{ bits (to select 1 of 8)} \\
\text{Register read port 2} &= 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Register write port} &= 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Memory signals} &= 4 \\
\text{Shift signals} &= 2 \\
\text{Total control signals} &= 16 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 31
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Add Next Address field bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \lceil \log_2(256) \rceil = 8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Horizontal Control Word Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_H &= 31 + 8 = 39 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $W_H = \mathbf{39 \text{ bits}}$

---

### 3.1.2 Solution — Part (b): Pure Vertical

In Pure Vertical, each functional group is encoded into the minimum number of bits.

**Step 1 — Calculate bits for each encoded field:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALU field} &= \lceil \log_2(16) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Register field (per port)} &= \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Memory field} &= \lceil \log_2(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Shift field} &= \lceil \log_2(2) \rceil = 1 \text{ bit} \\
\text{Next Address field} &= \lceil \log_2(256) \rceil = 8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all field widths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_V &= 4 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 8 = 24 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** $W_V = \mathbf{24 \text{ bits}}$

**Step 3 — Compute Compression Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{W_H}{W_V} = \dfrac{39}{24} = 1.625
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The vertical format saves **38.5%** of Control Memory storage compared to pure horizontal, at the cost of decoder hardware and a small propagation delay.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Microprogram Storage Cost Comparison

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style):**
> A microprogram contains **512 microinstructions**. Calculate the total Control Memory (ROM) size required in bytes for both horizontal and vertical formats, using the word widths from Example 3.1.

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Total bits for Horizontal format:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits}_H &= W_H \times I = 39 \times 512 = 19{,}968 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM size}_H &= \dfrac{19{,}968}{8} = 2{,}496 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.44 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total bits for Vertical format:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits}_V &= W_V \times I = 24 \times 512 = 12{,}288 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM size}_V &= \dfrac{12{,}288}{8} = 1{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 1.5 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Storage savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \dfrac{2{,}496 - 1{,}536}{2{,}496} \times 100\% = 38.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Horizontal | Vertical | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Word Width (Example 3.1) | 39 bits | 24 bits | **Vertical** (compact) |
| Control Memory (Example 3.2) | 2,496 bytes | 1,536 bytes | **Vertical** (compact) |
| Decoder Hardware | None | Multiple decoders | **Horizontal** (simple) |
| Cycle Time | Short | Longer (decode delay) | **Horizontal** (fast) |
| Parallelism per micro-step | Unlimited | One per field | **Horizontal** (powerful) |
