| Microprogrammer Effort | Difficult (manage many bits) | Easier (opcodes) | **Vertical** (easier) |
| Flexibility for new ops | Bit-level | Requires re-decoding | **Horizontal** (flexible) |
| Power Consumption | Higher (more bits toggle) | Lower | **Vertical** (efficient) |

> [!TIP]
> **Hybrid (Field-Encoded) Microinstruction:** In actual KTU examinations, most questions refer to Mano's Basic Computer, which uses a **field-encoded horizontal** format. The ALU operations are encoded into 3 bits (F1, F2, F3 — even though there are only ~7 distinct operations, using 3 bits allows unused codes for "don't care"). This is the **"Goldilocks"** format — a pragmatic balance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Horizontal vs Vertical Decoding Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Control Memory ROM] --> B[Mapped Microinstruction Word]
    B --> C{Encoding Type?}

    C -- Pure Horizontal --> D[Bit i of CW directly drives Control Line i]
    D --> D1[D0 -> Signal 0]
    D --> D2[D1 -> Signal 1]
    D --> D3[Dn-1 -> Signal n-1]
    D1 --> E1[Datapath Control Bus]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1

    C -- Pure Vertical --> F[Field Extraction Unit]
    F --> F1[ALU Opcode Field]
    F --> F2[Register Select Field]
    F --> F3[Memory Field]
    F1 --> G1[ALU Decoder 4-to-16]
    F2 --> G2[Register Decoder 3-to-8]
    F3 --> G3[Memory Decoder 2-to-4]
    G1 --> E2[Datapath Control Bus]
    G2 --> E2
    G3 --> E2

    E1 --> H[Datapath executes micro-operations]
    E2 --> H
```

**Caption:** The diagram illustrates the critical architectural divergence. **Horizontal** microinstructions directly tap the control bus (faster), while **Vertical** microinstructions must traverse a decoder network (slower but compact).

---

## 4.2 Control Word Structure Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HORIZ["HORIZONTAL CONTROL WORD (39 bits)"]
        H1[ALU bit0] --> H2[ALU bit1] --> H3[ALU bit14] --> H4[ALU bit15] --> H5[RegA2] --> H6[RegA1] --> H7[RegA0] --> H8[RegB2] --> H9[RegB1] --> H10[RegB0] --> H11[RegC2] --> H12[RegC1] --> H13[RegC0] --> H14[Mem3] --> H15[Mem2] --> H16[Mem1] --> H17[Mem0] --> H18[Shift1] --> H19[Shift0] --> H20[AD7..AD0]
    end

    subgraph VERT["VERTICAL CONTROL WORD (24 bits)"]
        V1[ALU 4b] --> V2[RegA 3b] --> V3[RegB 3b] --> V4[RegC 3b] --> V5[Mem 2b] --> V6[Shift 1b] --> V7[AD 8b]
    end
```

**Caption:** Visual contrast of bit allocation. Notice how the 16 separate ALU bits in the horizontal word collapse into a single 4-bit opcode field in the vertical word, which must subsequently be decoded.

---

## 4.3 Microinstruction Sequencing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Microprogram Start]) --> Fetch[Fetch Microinstruction from CM]
    Fetch --> DecodeCheck{Format Type?}
    DecodeCheck -- Horizontal --> DriveSignals[Directly drive control bus]
    DriveSignals --> ExecuteOp[Execute micro-operation]
    ExecuteOp --> ComputeNext[Compute next address]
    DecodeCheck -- Vertical --> RunDecoders[Pass fields through decoders]
    RunDecoders --> DriveSignals
    ComputeNext --> BranchCheck{Condition Met?}
    BranchCheck -- Yes --> BranchAddr[Load branch target address]
    BranchCheck -- No --> SeqAddr[Sequential next address]
    BranchAddr --> Fetch
    SeqAddr --> Fetch
```

**Caption:** The execution flow for both formats is identical at the macro level. The difference lies *inside* the **DriveSignals** block, where horizontal achieves it directly while vertical requires intermediate decoding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between Horizontal and Vertical microinstruction formats. List any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Horizontal | Vertical |
|---|---|---|
| Word Length | Long (one bit per control signal) | Short (encoded fields) |
| Encoding | No encoding | Fields are encoded |
| Decoding | Not required | Required (decoder logic) |
| Speed of execution | Fast | Slower (decode delay) |
| Parallelism | High (multiple signals per micro-step) | Low (one operation per field) |

**Advantages:**
- **Horizontal:** (i) Higher degree of parallelism, (ii) No decoder hardware, hence faster execution.
- **Vertical:** (i) Compact Control Word reduces Control Memory size, (ii) Easier to write and understand microprograms (resembles machine instructions).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "Horizontal is always better" or "Vertical is always better." Examiners look for **balanced trade-off analysis** — the answer must explicitly mention the *cost* of each advantage.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the purpose of the Next Address (AD) field in a microinstruction? Why is it necessary in both horizontal and vertical formats?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Next Address (AD)** field specifies the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched from the Control Memory after the current microinstruction completes its micro-operations. It is essential because:

1. **Sequential flow:** In the absence of branches, the next address is `current_address + 1`.
2. **Conditional branching:** When a condition is met (e.g., zero flag = 1), the AD field can be loaded into the Microprogram Counter (µPC) to enable jumps in the microprogram.
3. **Subroutine support:** Allows re-entry points and shared micro-routines for common operations.

The AD field is required in **both formats** because microprograms — regardless of their encoding strategy — still need to specify control flow, not just data operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the design of a Horizontal and Vertical microinstruction format. Consider a system with 64 control signals, 32 micro-operations, and a control memory of 4096 words. Calculate the microinstruction size for both formats and the percentage of storage saved by using vertical format. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks)

**Horizontal Microinstruction Format:**

In the **horizontal** microinstruction, every one of the 64 control signals is assigned a unique bit position. The control word directly maps to the control bus. The microinstruction is read from the Control Memory, and each bit is routed to its corresponding control line. This means we need **64 bits** just for the function signals.

Adding the next address field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \lceil \log_2(4096) \rceil = 12 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total horizontal control word:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_H &= 64 \text{ (function bits)} + 12 \text{ (address bits)} = 76 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vertical Microinstruction Format:**

In the **vertical** microinstruction, the 32 micro-operations are encoded using an opcode field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opcode width} &= \lceil \log_2(32) \rceil = 5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the next address field:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_V &= 5 \text{ (opcode)} + 12 \text{ (address)} = 17 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram Description (Examiner's Note):** The student should draw two control word layouts — one showing 64 individual function bits laid out horizontally, and another showing the compact 5-bit opcode + 12-bit address layout, with a decoder block connecting the opcode to the 32 control lines.

#### Part (b) — Storage Savings Calculation (7 Marks)

Assume the microprogram has $I$ microinstructions. The total Control Memory size is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_H &= W_H \times I = 76I \text{ bits} \\
S_V &= W_V \times I = 17I \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings \%} &= \dfrac{S_H - S_V}{S_H} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{76I - 17I}{76I} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{59}{76} \times 100 \\
&\approx 77.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vertical format saves approximately **77.6%** of Control Memory, but requires decoder hardware and incurs a small propagation delay per microinstruction fetch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to include the **Next Address (AD) field** in the total bit count. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for this omission. Always check whether the question specifies the control memory size — if yes, the address field is mandatory.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Alternative]

**Q: A microprogrammed control unit has the following fields: ALU operations (8 functions), Register selection (2 ports, 8 registers each), Memory operations (4 functions), and a control memory of 1024 words. (a) Design a vertical microinstruction format and calculate the total bit width. (b) If the same system were implemented in a purely horizontal format, what would be the bit width? Compare the formats. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Vertical Format Design (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate each field's width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALU field} &= \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Register A field} &= \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Register B field} &= \lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Memory field} &= \lceil \log_2(4) \rceil = 2 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Next Address field} &= \lceil \log_2(1024) \rceil = 10 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Vertical Control Word Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_V &= 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 + 10 = 21 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Layout of Vertical Microinstruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CW}_V = [\underbrace{ALU_2 ALU_1 ALU_0}_{3b} \mid \underbrace{RA_2 RA_1 RA_0}_{3b} \mid \underbrace{RB_2 RB_1 RB_0}_{3b} \mid \underbrace{M_1 M_0}_{2b} \mid \underbrace{AD_9 \ldots AD_0}_{10b}]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Horizontal Format & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Count individual control signals:**

In pure horizontal, each distinct control signal is represented as a separate bit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALU signals} &= 8 \\
\text{Register A select lines} &= 3 \\
\text{Register B select lines} &= 3 \\
\text{Memory signals} &= 4 \\
\text{Total control signals} &= 8 + 3 + 3 + 4 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Memory signals (4) are treated individually because they may need to be activated concurrently in some micro-operations.

**Step 2 — Total Horizontal Control Word Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_H &= 18 + 10 \text{ (address)} = 28 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

| Parameter | Vertical | Horizontal |
|---|---|---|
| Total Width | 21 bits | 28 bits |
| Decoder | Required | Not required |
| Parallelism | One operation per field | Multiple signals per cycle |
| Storage | Compact | Larger |

**Compression Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{W_H}{W_V} = \dfrac{28}{21} \approx 1.33
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In horizontal format questions, students often count only the *number of operations* rather than the *number of individual control lines*. For example, "8 ALU operations" requires 8 *individual signal lines* (each can be activated independently) — not 3 bits. This conceptual confusion costs 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Horizontal vs Vertical Micro Instructions**

- **Definition Anchor:** Horizontal = unencoded, long CW; Vertical = encoded, short CW. Memorize this distinction first.
- **Bit Count Rule:** Horizontal CW width = (Number of individual control signals) + (Address field bits).
- **Encoding Rule:** Vertical CW width = Σ ⌈log₂(Kⱼ)⌉ for all fields + Address bits.
- **Address Field Width:** Always ⌈log₂(Control Memory Size)⌉. Never omit this in KTU numerical problems.
- **The Decoding Trade-off:** Vertical format *always* requires decoder logic; horizontal *never* does. This is the single most important exam point.
- **Parallelism:** Horizontal allows multiple concurrent micro-operations per cycle; Vertical is limited to one operation per encoded field.
- **Mano's Computer:** Uses **field-encoded horizontal** format — a hybrid approach using 3 bits for ALU (F1, F2, F3), a CD field for condition, a BR field for branch type, and an AD field for next address.
- **Speed vs Storage:** Horizontal optimizes for **speed**; Vertical optimizes for **storage efficiency**. The hybrid format optimizes for **balance**.
- **Compression Ratio Formula:** CR = W_H / W_V. A higher CR means greater storage savings when using vertical.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing "number of operations" with "number of control signals." In horizontal format, you count *signals*, not *operations*.
- **Key Acronyms to Memorize:**
  - **CW** = Control Word
  - **CM** = Control Memory
  - **µPC** = Microprogram Counter
  - **CD** = Condition (select) bit
  - **BR** = Branch field
  - **AD** = Address field
- **Engineering Insight:** Most real-world microprogrammed designs (Intel x86, AMD microcode) use a **vertical or hybrid** format because control memory silicon is expensive. Pure horizontal is rare in production but excellent in FPGA implementations.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Horizontal and Vertical Micro Instructions**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — PERAT414*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Organization: Accessing of I/O Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** I/O Organization: Accessing of I/O Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **I/O Organization** refers to the structured methodology, hardware interfaces, and control strategies employed by a computer system to facilitate the transfer of data between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Main Memory**, and the diverse set of peripheral **Input/Output (I/O) devices** (e.g., keyboards, disk drives, printers, sensors).

**Accessing I/O Devices** is the specific sub-topic that deals with the **mechanisms and protocols** through which the CPU identifies, communicates with, and exchanges data with external peripheral hardware. In KTU parlance, this is broadly classified into two orthogonal dimensions:
1. **I/O Addressing Schemes** — *How* the CPU logically locates a device (Isolated vs Memory-Mapped I/O).
2. **Data Transfer Mechanisms** — *How* data actually moves between device and memory/CPU (Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, DMA).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Mapping:** This topic directly contributes to Course Outcome **CO3** — *"Design and analyze I/O subsystems, memory hierarchies, and pipeline architectures."* The cognitive Bloom level targeted is **Apply / Analyze (L3–L4).**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office Model" 🏤

Imagine the **CPU** is a busy office clerk sitting at a desk, the **Main Memory** is a giant filing cabinet in the same room, and the **I/O Devices** are external delivery trucks parked outside the building.

- **Programmed I/O (Polling):** The clerk repeatedly walks to the window, peers out, and asks *"Is the truck here yet? Is it here yet?"* — wasting enormous time on the check itself.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** The truck driver honks a loud horn the moment he arrives. The clerk continues his filing work and only stops when he hears the horn. Much more efficient!
- **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** A trusted assistant is given the filing cabinet keys and a copy of the delivery manifest. The truck driver and the assistant handle the entire transfer without the clerk's involvement. The clerk only gets a final "delivery complete" notification.

This single analogy maps directly onto the three core transfer mechanisms in KTU's Module 4 syllabus.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard hardware metrics are essential for KTU board numericals:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| System Bus Width | $n$ | **8, 16, 32, 64 bits** |
| I/O Port Address Space | $A_{IO}$ | **64K (16-bit) ports** (Isolated) |
| Memory-Mapped I/O Range | $A_{MMIO}$ | Subset of physical address space |
| Data Transfer Rate | $R_{IO}$ | **Bytes/sec** or **bits/sec** |
| Polling Overhead | $T_{poll}$ | **microseconds (μs)** per check |
| Interrupt Latency | $T_{lat}$ | **nanoseconds (ns)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical View of Accessing I/O Devices

The complete process of accessing an I/O device can be decomposed into **three logical layers**:

### 🔹 Layer 1 — I/O Addressing (Device Selection)
The CPU must uniquely identify *which* peripheral it wants to talk to. Two architectural choices exist:

**(A) Isolated I/O (Port-Mapped I/O)**
- Uses a **separate address space** exclusively for I/O devices.
- Requires **dedicated control signals** on the system bus: $\overline{IOR}$ (I/O Read) and $\overline{IOW}$ (I/O Write).
- The CPU has specialized instructions: `IN` and `OUT` (e.g., x86 `IN AL, DX`).
- **Advantage:** Full memory address space remains available for RAM.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires extra hardware decoding logic and more powerful instruction set.

**(B) Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)**
- I/O device registers are mapped into the **same address space** as main memory.
- Uses the same control signals as memory: $\overline{MEMR}$ and $\overline{MEMW}$.
- Any standard data-movement instruction (`MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`) can be used.
- **Advantage:** No special instructions needed; full flexibility.
- **Disadvantage:** Consumes a portion of the valuable memory address space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** *"Differentiate between Isolated I/O and Memory-Mapped I/O"* — this is a guaranteed 7-mark question. Always draw a comparison table and mention the **control signal distinction** ($\overline{IOR}/\overline{IOW}$ vs $\overline{MEMR}/\overline{MEMW}$).

---

### 🔹 Layer 2 — Data Transfer Mechanism (How Data Moves)
Once a device is selected, the actual data transfer can occur in one of three fundamental modes:

**(A) Programmed I/O (Polling / Busy-Wait)**
- CPU **actively and continuously** checks a **Status Register** of the I/O device.
- If `READY = 1`, CPU executes a data read/write instruction. Else, it loops (spins).
- **CPU utilization** is wasted on the polling loop. Suitable only for **single, dedicated, fast devices**.

**(B) Interrupt-Driven I/O**
- CPU initiates the I/O, then **continues other work**.
- When the I/O device is ready, it raises an **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** line.
- CPU completes the current instruction, saves context (PC, PSW) on stack, jumps to the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, transfers the data, then **returns from interrupt (RETI)**.
- **Overhead:** Context save/restore is non-trivial but still vastly better than polling.

**(C) Direct Memory Access (DMA)**
- A specialized hardware controller — the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** — takes over the bus.
- CPU sets up the DMAC with: **Source Address, Destination Address, Count, and Mode**.
- DMAC performs the entire block transfer *bypassing the CPU* using **bus arbitration** (HOLD / HLDA protocol in classic 8086; modern systems use PCIe mastering).
- CPU gets a single interrupt only when the entire block is done.
- **Best for:** High-speed block transfers (disk, network, video).

---

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Handshaking & Synchronization
For asynchronous devices, two synchronization protocols are used:

- **Strobed Data Transfer:** A single control signal (the *strobe*) is pulsed by source/sink to indicate data validity. Simple but error-prone with slow devices.
- **Handshaking Data Transfer:** Uses **two control signals** — *Data Valid* (from source) and *Data Accepted* (from sink). Forms a request-acknowledge interlock. **KTU standard** for most peripherals.

---

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M):** Use primarily **memory-mapped I/O** because it requires fewer instruction cycles in tight loops.
- **x86 PCs:** Use **isolated I/O** for legacy ports + **memory-mapped I/O** for high-speed devices (GPUs, NICs).
- **Disk Controllers & SSDs:** Universally use **DMA + Interrupt** hybrid for peak throughput.
- **Real-Time OS (RTOS):** Heavy reliance on **interrupt-driven I/O** with bounded latency guarantees.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **one-stop revision sheet** for all KTU board numericals on this topic. All boundary conditions and units are stated explicitly.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **I/O Address Space (Isolated)** | $A_{IO} = 2^n$ | $n$ = address lines on I/O bus (typically **16**, giving **64K ports**) |
| 2 | **Memory-Mapped I/O Size** | $A_{MMIO} = 2^m - 2^p$ | $m$ = total address bits, $p$ = bits reserved for RAM |
| 3 | **Polling Transfer Time (per byte)** | $T_{prog} = T_{poll} + T_{transfer}$ | seconds, includes the wasted polling overhead |
| 4 | **Programmed I/O Total Time (N bytes)** | $T_{PIO} = N \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{exec})$ | $T_{exec}$ = instruction execution time |
| 5 | **Interrupt-Driven I/O Total Time** | $T_{INT} = N \cdot T_{service} + (N-1) \cdot T_{cpu\_other}$ | $T_{service}$ = ISR execution time |
| 6 | **DMA Block Transfer Time** | $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \frac{B}{R_{DMA}} + T_{ack}$ | $B$ = block size in bytes, $R_{DMA}$ = DMA transfer rate (bytes/sec) |
| 7 | **CPU Utilization in Polling** | $U_{poll} = 1.0$ (i.e., **100%**) | CPU is always busy in the loop |
| 8 | **CPU Utilization in Interrupt** | $U_{int} = \frac{T_{service}}{T_{int\_period}}$ | $T_{int\_period}$ = time between consecutive interrupts |
| 9 | **CPU Utilization in DMA** | $U_{DMA} \approx \frac{T_{setup} + T_{ack}}{T_{DMA}}$ | Approaches 0 for large blocks |
| 10 | **Throughput of I/O Subsystem** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{B}{T_{total}}$ | bytes/sec; $T_{total}$ is total elapsed time |
| 11 | **Handshake Cycle Time** | $T_{hs} = t_{prop} + t_{valid} + t_{resp}$ | Sum of propagation + validity + response delays |
| 12 | **Bus Arbitration Latency** | $T_{arb} = T_{req} + T_{grant}$ | For DMA HOLD/HLDA handshaking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Many students confuse $T_{poll}$ (a small fixed overhead per check) with $T_{exec}$ (the actual data-move instruction). They are **different** and must be summed in Programmed I/O calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are exhaustive. **No step is skipped.** Every algebraic transition is justified with a plain-English reasoning line, in strict compliance with the KTU 2024 Scheme "Show Full Working" valuation rubric.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right I/O Method

This decision matrix is a **standard KTU exam question** ("*Given a scenario, justify your choice of I/O method*"). The conditions for selection are derived below:

| Scenario | Best Method | Justification (The "Why") |
|---|---|---|
| Single character echo from keyboard | **Programmed I/O** | Latency-critical, only 1 byte, no setup cost worthwhile |
| Continuous data logging from a sensor at 1 kHz | **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | CPU must do other processing; polling would starve it |
| Transferring 4 MB file from SSD to RAM | **DMA** | Block transfer; offloading saves massive CPU cycles |
| Reading one byte from a status register repeatedly | **Programmed I/O** | Overhead of interrupt far exceeds data size benefit |
| High-speed network packet reception | **DMA + Interrupt** | DMA for bulk payload, interrupt to signal packet arrival |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Time to Transfer N Bytes using Programmed I/O

**Problem Setup:**
A CPU polls an I/O device. Each poll check takes $T_{poll} = 2\,\mu s$. Each data transfer instruction (when the device is ready) takes $T_{exec} = 1\,\mu s$. The device produces data at a rate of one byte every $T_{dev} = 10\,\mu s$. Derive the total time to transfer $N$ bytes.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

For a single byte, the CPU must perform the polling check **until** the device indicates readiness. On average, the device is found ready halfway through a polling cycle. Therefore the **average poll overhead per byte** is the time spent spinning:

$$
\overline{T}_{poll,byte} \approx \frac{T_{dev}}{2}
$$

This is because, under uniform random arrival, the average waiting time in a busy-wait loop of period $T_{dev}$ is half the period. The CPU performs an average of $\frac{T_{dev}}{2 \cdot T_{poll}}$ poll instructions before finding data ready.

Once ready, the CPU executes exactly **one** data transfer instruction. So the **total CPU time per byte** is:

$$
T_{cpu,byte} = \overline{T}_{poll,byte} + T_{exec}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{cpu,byte} = \frac{10\,\mu s}{2} + 1\,\mu s = 5\,\mu s + 1\,\mu s = 6\,\mu s
$$

For $N$ bytes, since the operations are sequential and non-overlapping:

$$
T_{total} = N \cdot T_{cpu,byte}
$$

$$
T_{total} = N \cdot 6\,\mu s
$$

**Final General Formula:**

$$
\boxed{T_{PIO} = N \cdot \left( \frac{T_{dev}}{2} + T_{exec} \right)}
$$

**Real-Time Sanity Check:** If $N = 1000$ bytes, $T_{total} = 6000\,\mu s = 6\,ms$. This is the time the CPU is **100% busy**, which demonstrates the key disadvantage of Programmed I/O.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Transfer N Bytes using Interrupt-Driven I/O

**Problem Setup:**
Same device as above ($T_{dev} = 10\,\mu s$ between bytes). The CPU runs other useful work with an average instruction time of $T_{other} = 0.5\,\mu s$. Each Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) takes $T_{service} = 4\,\mu s$ (including context save/restore). Derive the time to transfer $N$ bytes and compare with Programmed I/O.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

For the **first byte**, the device is initially not ready, so CPU does other work for approximately $T_{dev}$ before the first interrupt arrives:

$$
T_{first} = T_{dev} + T_{service} = 10\,\mu s + 4\,\mu s = 14\,\mu s
$$

For each subsequent byte (bytes 2 through N), the device produces a new byte every $T_{dev}$. The CPU does other useful work for one full $T_{dev}$ period, then is interrupted. So the time per subsequent byte is:

$$
T_{subsequent} = T_{dev} + T_{service} = 14\,\mu s
$$

**Total time** is the first byte's time plus $(N-1)$ subsequent bytes' times:

$$
T_{total} = T_{first} + (N-1) \cdot T_{subsequent}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 14\,\mu s + (N-1) \cdot 14\,\mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{INT} = N \cdot 14\,\mu s}
$$

**CPU Utilization Calculation (Analytical Insight):**
In interrupt-driven I/O, the CPU is busy with the ISR for $T_{service} = 4\,\mu s$ out of every $14\,\mu s$ period. So:

$$
U_{int} = \frac{T_{service}}{T_{period}} = \frac{4\,\mu s}{14\,\mu s} = 0.286
$$

This means the CPU is **only 28.6% busy** with I/O and has **71.4% free** for other work. This is a vast improvement over Programmed I/O's 100% CPU lockup.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DMA Block Transfer Time and Effective Throughput

**Problem Setup:**
A disk drive has a transfer rate of $R_{disk} = 100$ MB/s. To read $B = 1$ MB into RAM, the system uses a DMA controller. The CPU takes $T_{setup} = 5\,\mu s$ to program the DMAC registers, and the final interrupt acknowledgment takes $T_{ack} = 1\,\mu s$. The DMA controller can sustain $R_{DMA} = 50$ MB/s (the bottleneck is the slower of disk vs DMA). Derive the total time and effective throughput.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The DMA transfer is a pipeline of two stages operating in parallel (kind of), but the *wall-clock* time is governed by the slower stage. The **effective transfer rate** is the minimum of the two:

$$
R_{eff} = \min(R_{disk}, R_{DMA}) = \min(100, 50) = 50\,MB/s
$$

The time to actually move the data block is:

$$
T_{move} = \frac{B}{R_{eff}} = \frac{1\,MB}{50\,MB/s} = 0.02\,s = 20\,ms
$$

The total time includes the CPU's setup overhead and the final acknowledgment:

$$
T_{total} = T_{setup} + T_{move} + T_{ack}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 5\,\mu s + 20\,000\,\mu s + 1\,\mu s = 20\,006\,\mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{total} \approx 20.006\,ms}
$$

**CPU Utilization in DMA:**

$$
U_{DMA} = \frac{T_{setup} + T_{ack}}{T_{total}} = \frac{5 + 1}{20\,006} = 3 \times 10^{-4}
$$

That is only **0.03%** of CPU time! For all practical purposes, the CPU is free.

**Effective Throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{B}{T_{total}} = \frac{1\,MB}{20.006\,ms} \approx 49.985\,MB/s
$$

This is **slightly less** than the peak $R_{eff} = 50\,MB/s$ due to the tiny setup overhead — an important nuance for KTU numericals.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Table — Quantitative Summary

For transferring $N = 1000$ bytes under the conditions above:

| Method | Total Time | CPU Utilization | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Programmed I/O | $1000 \times 6 = 6000\,\mu s$ | **100%** | Single dedicated device |
| Interrupt-Driven I/O | $1000 \times 14 = 14\,000\,\mu s$ | **28.6%** | Moderate data, CPU must multitask |
| DMA | $\approx T_{setup} + \frac{N}{R_{DMA}}$ | **$\approx 0\%$** | Large block transfers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Although DMA is **slower in wall-clock time per byte** for small $N$ (due to setup overhead), for large $N$ (e.g., megabytes), it is **vastly more efficient** in CPU utilization and overall system throughput. This trade-off is a frequent KTU essay question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are Mermaid-rendered. They are designed for maximum compatibility — no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels, purely alphanumeric node IDs.

---

## 4.1 System-Level I/O Organization Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU["CPU"] -->|Address Bus| DEC["Address Decoder"]
    CPU -->|Control Bus| DEC
    DEC -->|Select Signal| DEV1["Keyboard Port"]
    DEC -->|Select Signal| DEV2["Disk Controller"]
    DEC -->|Select Signal| DEV3["Printer Port"]
    CPU -->|Data Bus| DEV1
    CPU -->|Data Bus| DEV2
    CPU -->|Data Bus| DEV3
    DEV1 -->|IRQ Line| PIC["Programmable Interrupt Controller"]
    DEV2 -->|IRQ Line| PIC
    DEV3 -->|IRQ Line| PIC
    PIC -->|INTR| CPU
    DMAC["DMA Controller"] -.->|HOLD| CPU
    CPU -.->|HLDA| DMAC
    DMAC -->|Bus Master| MEM["Main Memory"]
    DMAC -->|Bus Master| DEV2
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — CPU Decision Logic for I/O Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["I/O Request Initiated"] --> Q1{"Block Size N"}
    Q1 -->|N is small and 1 device| Q2{"CPU has other work"}
    Q1 -->|N is large block| DMA["Use DMA Transfer"]
    Q2 -->|No, single task| POLL["Use Programmed I/O Polling"]
    Q2 -->|Yes, multitasking| INT["Use Interrupt Driven I/O"]
    DMA --> DONE["Block Transfer Complete Interrupt"]
    INT --> ISR["Execute ISR Transfer 1 Unit"]
    ISR --> MORE{"More Data Pending"}
    MORE -->|Yes| INT
    MORE -->|No| DONE
    POLL --> DONE
    DONE --> END["Return to Main Program"]
```

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — DMA Block Transfer

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CPU
    participant D as DMA Controller
    participant M as Main Memory
    participant X as I/O Device
    C->>D: Write Source Addr
    C->>D: Write Destination Addr
    C->>D: Write Byte Count B
    C->>D: Write Mode Start Transfer
    C-->>M: CPU Continues Other Work
    D->>X: Request Data Bus Arbitration
    X-->>D: Bus Granted
    loop For Each Byte in Block
        D->>X: Read Data Byte
        D->>M: Write Data Byte to Memory
    end
    D->>C: Raise Interrupt Transfer Complete
    C->>D: Acknowledge and Reset
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Architecture — Isolated I/O vs Memory-Mapped I/O

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ISO["ISOLATED I/O ARCHITECTURE"]
        ISO_CPU["CPU"]
        ISO_IN["IN Instruction"]
        ISO_OUT["OUT Instruction"]
        ISO_BUS["Separate I/O Bus"]
        ISO_DEC["I/O Address Decoder"]
        ISO_DEV["I/O Device Registers"]
        ISO_MEM["Main Memory"]
        ISO_CPU --> ISO_IN
        ISO_CPU --> ISO_OUT
        ISO_IN --> ISO_BUS
        ISO_OUT --> ISO_BUS
        ISO_BUS --> ISO_DEC
        ISO_DEC --> ISO_DEV
        ISO_CPU -.Separate Path.-> ISO_MEM
    end
    subgraph MM["MEMORY MAPPED I/O ARCHITECTURE"]
        MM_CPU["CPU"]
        MM_MOV["MOV or LDR or STR Instruction"]
        MM_BUS["Unified System Bus"]
        MM_DEC["Combined Address Decoder"]
        MM_DEV["I/O Device Registers"]
        MM_MEM["Main Memory"]
        MM_CPU --> MM_MOV
        MM_MOV --> MM_BUS
        MM_BUS --> MM_DEC
        MM_DEC --> MM_DEV
        MM_DEC --> MM_MEM
    end
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. The mark distribution (**3 marks for Part A, 14 marks for Part B**) and the module-internal choice structure are reproduced exactly.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Isolated I/O and Memory-Mapped I/O in terms of address space and control signals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Isolated I/O:** Uses a **separate, dedicated address space** for I/O devices. Requires special CPU instructions (`IN`, `OUT`) and **separate control signals** $\overline{IOR}$ and $\overline{IOW}$. Does not consume memory address space. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Memory-Mapped I/O:** I/O device registers are treated as **memory locations** within the same address space. Uses standard data-move instructions (`MOV`, `LDR`) and the same control signals ($\overline{MEMR}$, $\overline{MEMW}$). **Consumes part of the memory address space.** **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of a DMA controller in an I/O subsystem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller** is a specialized hardware unit that performs **block data transfers between I/O devices and main memory autonomously, without CPU intervention.** The CPU only sets up the DMA registers (source, destination, count) and is interrupted upon completion. This **frees the CPU** for other tasks and dramatically improves I/O throughput, especially for high-speed peripherals like disks and network cards. **(3 marks — full credit for mentioning "block transfer," "without CPU," and "interrupt on completion")**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer **ONE** of two choices per question. Each 14-mark question is split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three primary methods of data transfer between CPU and I/O devices: **Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and DMA**. Compare them with respect to CPU involvement, transfer speed, and suitable use cases. Use a comparison table.

**(b) [7 marks]** A system uses a DMA controller to transfer a block of $64\,KB$ from a disk to memory. The disk's data rate is $8\,MB/s$. The DMA controller's setup time is $10\,\mu s$ and the final interrupt acknowledgment takes $2\,\mu s$. Calculate: (i) the total transfer time, and (ii) the CPU utilization. Take $1\,MB = 1024\,KB$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation and Comparison:**

| Feature | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Involvement** | CPU polls continuously, 100% busy | CPU interrupted only on readiness | CPU sets up and gets one final interrupt |
| **Transfer Speed (Small Data)** | Fastest (no overhead) | Slower (context switch) | Slowest (setup dominates) |
| **Transfer Speed (Large Block)** | Wastes CPU; throughput low | Moderate | Highest sustained throughput |
| **Hardware Cost** | Lowest | Needs interrupt controller | Needs DMAC + bus arbiter |
| **Suitable For** | Single dedicated, fast device | Moderate-speed, multitasking | High-speed block transfers |

**Justification lines (each sub-point 1 mark, total 7 marks as per KTU key):**
1. Programmed I/O — CPU executes a *busy-wait loop* checking a status register. *(1 mark)*
2. Interrupt-Driven I/O — Device raises IRQ; CPU vectors to ISR, transfers data, returns. *(1 mark)*
3. DMA — DMAC takes bus control via HOLD/HLDA; performs block transfer. *(1 mark)*
4. CPU involvement comparison with the 100% vs intermittent vs near-zero framing. *(1 mark)*
5. Transfer speed comparison. *(1 mark)*
6. Hardware cost discussion. *(1 mark)*
7. Suitable use cases. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Block size: $B = 64\,KB = 65\,536\,bytes$
- Disk rate: $R_{disk} = 8\,MB/s = 8 \times 1024\,KB/s = 8192\,KB/s$
- DMA setup: $T_{setup} = 10\,\mu s$
- Interrupt ack: $T_{ack} = 2\,\mu s$

**Step 1 — Compute the actual data transfer time:**
The bottleneck transfer rate is the disk's rate (DMA is typically faster than the device), so $R_{eff} = 8\,MB/s$.

$$
T_{move} = \frac{B}{R_{disk}} = \frac{64\,KB}{8\,MB/s}
$$

Convert units consistently: $8\,MB/s = 8 \times 1024\,KB/s = 8192\,KB/s$.

$$
T_{move} = \frac{64\,KB}{8192\,KB/s} = 0.0078125\,s = 7812.5\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Total transfer time:**
$$
T_{total} = T_{setup} + T_{move} + T_{ack}
$$
$$
T_{total} = 10\,\mu s + 7812.5\,\mu s + 2\,\mu s = 7824.5\,\mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{total} \approx 7824.5\,\mu s \approx 7.825\,ms}
$$

**Step 3 — CPU utilization:**

$$
U_{DMA} = \frac{T_{setup} + T_{ack}}{T_{total}} = \frac{10 + 2}{7824.5}
$$

$$
U_{DMA} = \frac{12}{7824.5} = 0.001534
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{DMA} \approx 0.153\%}
$$

This confirms the CPU is virtually free for other work.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **handshaking method of asynchronous data transfer** between a source and a destination. Why is handshaking preferred over simple strobing for variable-speed peripherals?

**(b) [7 marks]** A CPU using **Programmed I/O** must read 500 bytes from a sensor. The polling check takes $T_{poll} = 1.5\,\mu s$ and the data-read instruction takes $T_{exec} = 0.5\,\mu s$. The sensor produces a new byte every $T_{dev} = 8\,\mu s$. Calculate: (i) the average time per byte, (ii) the total transfer time, and (iii) the effective throughput in bytes per second.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Handshaking Explanation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (to be drawn by student):**
- Show **Source** with `Data Valid` line and data bus.
- Show **Destination** with `Data Accepted` line and data bus.
- Two control lines interconnect them in a request-acknowledge interlock.

**Key Points (each 1 mark):**
1. Source places data on the bus and asserts *Data Valid* (request). *(1 mark)*
2. Destination reads the data and asserts *Data Accepted* (acknowledge). *(1 mark)*
3. Source sees the acknowledge and de-asserts *Data Valid*. *(1 mark)*
4. Destination de-asserts *Data Accepted*, completing one cycle. *(1 mark)*
5. Each side **waits for the other** — guarantees data integrity regardless of speed. *(1 mark)*
6. Preferred over strobing because strobe-based transfer assumes a **fixed timing**, which fails with variable-speed devices. Handshaking **adapts dynamically**. *(1 mark)*
7. Real-world example: UART flow control (RTS/CTS), printer `Busy` handshake. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 500$ bytes, $T_{poll} = 1.5\,\mu s$, $T_{exec} = 0.5\,\mu s$, $T_{dev} = 8\,\mu s$.

**(i) Average time per byte:**
$$
T_{avg,byte} = \frac{T_{dev}}{2} + T_{exec}
$$

The CPU polls an average of $\frac{T_{dev}}{2 \cdot T_{poll}} = \frac{8}{2 \times 1.5} \approx 2.67$ times before data is ready. The time wasted spinning is $\frac{T_{dev}}{2} = 4\,\mu s$. Then one data-read instruction executes.

$$
T_{avg,byte} = 4\,\mu s + 0.5\,\mu s = 4.5\,\mu s
$$

**(ii) Total transfer time:**
$$
T_{total} = N \cdot T_{avg,byte} = 500 \times 4.5\,\mu s = 2250\,\mu s = 2.25\,ms
$$

**(iii) Effective throughput:**
$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{N}{T_{total}} = \frac{500\,bytes}{2250\,\mu s}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{500}{2250 \times 10^{-6}\,s} = \frac{500}{0.00225\,s} = 222\,222\,bytes/s
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Throughput} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{5}\,bytes/sec \approx 222.2\,KB/s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Conflating **$1\,MB = 10^6\,bytes$** with **$1\,MB = 1024 \times 1024\,bytes$**. KTU uses the **binary definition** ($1\,MB = 1024\,KB = 1\,048\,576\,bytes$) unless explicitly stated otherwise. **Loses 2 marks** if wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting $T_{setup}$ and $T_{ack}$ in DMA totals:** Many students compute only $\frac{B}{R}$ and skip the setup/acknowledge overhead. **Loses 1–2 marks** depending on the question.
> 3. **Mixing Polling and Interrupt formulas:** Using $T_{poll}$ in an interrupt-driven calculation is a fatal error. Always identify the method *first* from the question wording.
> 4. **Missing the comparison table:** In 7-mark "Explain" questions, the comparison table is often worth **3 of the 7 marks** by itself.
> 5. **Not stating control signals:** For Isolated vs Memory-Mapped I/O, you **must** write $\overline{IOR}/\overline{IOW}$ vs $\overline{MEMR}/\overline{MEMW}$ to get full credit.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **I/O Organization** is the *bridge* between the CPU/Memory world and the external peripheral world.
- ✅ **Two addressing schemes:** *Isolated I/O* (separate bus, `IN`/`OUT`, $\overline{IOR}/\overline{IOW}$) and *Memory-Mapped I/O* (unified bus, `MOV`/`LDR`, $\overline{MEMR}/\overline{MEMW}$).
- ✅ **Three transfer methods:** *Programmed I/O* (CPU polls — 100% busy), *Interrupt-Driven I/O* (device signals CPU — partial busy), *DMA* (DMAC takes bus — near-zero CPU).
- ✅ **Programmed I/O Total Time formula:** $T_{PIO} = N \cdot \left(\frac{T_{dev}}{2} + T_{exec}\right)$.
- ✅ **DMA Total Time formula:** $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + \frac{B}{R_{eff}} + T_{ack}$.
- ✅ **CPU Utilization order:** Programmed (100%) > Interrupt (depends) > DMA (≈ 0%).
- ✅ **Handshaking** uses two control signals (Data Valid + Data Accepted) — best for variable-speed devices.
- ✅ **Strobing** uses one control signal — simple but assumes fixed timing.
- ✅ **DMA setup** requires: source address, destination address, byte count, and mode register.
- ✅ **Bus arbitration** for DMA uses HOLD/HLDA signals (legacy) or PCIe bus mastering (modern).
- ✅ **KTU mark-weight tip:** Always draw a **block diagram or flowchart** for 7-mark "Explain" questions — diagrams earn 1–2 free marks.
- ✅ **Numerical tip:** Always identify the **bottleneck rate** as $R_{eff} = \min(R_{device}, R_{DMA}, R_{bus})$ before computing transfer time.
- ✅ **Memory-Mapped I/O reduces instruction count** but **steals address space** — a classic trade-off.
- ✅ **Isolated I/O preserves full memory space** but requires **extra hardware and special instructions**.

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — I/O Organization: Accessing of I/O Devices*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech ESE pattern*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts, Interrupt Hardware - Direct Memory Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT414 - COMPUTER ORGANIZATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Interrupts, Interrupt Hardware - Direct Memory Access # 📘 MODULE 4 — INTERRUPTS & DIRECT MEMORY ACCESS (DMA) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Interrupt? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Interrupt):** > An **Interrupt** is a hardware or software-generated signal (event) that temporarily suspends the normal program execution of the CPU, forcing it to transfer control to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes execution of the original program from the point where it was interrupted. **Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:** Imagine you are deeply cooking dinner in the kitchen. Suddenly, the **doorbell rings** (an interrupt request). You *pause* your cooking (save context), walk to the door, attend to the visitor (execute the ISR), and then *return* to your cooking, continuing from exactly where you left off. The doorbell is asynchronous — you never know when it will ring. The CPU behaves the same way: it reacts to asynchronous external events (I/O completion, hardware errors, timer ticks) without having to constantly "poll" or check each device. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Polling:** > - **Polling:** CPU repeatedly checks each device → wastes CPU cycles. > - **Interrupts:** Device signals CPU only when it needs attention → CPU is free to do other work. --- ### 1.2 What is Direct Memory Access (DMA)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (DMA):** > **Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a data transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware controller (**DMA Controller / DMAC**) takes over the system buses from the CPU to transfer blocks of data directly between I/O devices and main memory, **without continuous CPU intervention**. The CPU is involved only at the beginning (to set up the DMA controller) and at the end (via an interrupt signaling completion). **Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition:** Think of the CPU as a *librarian* who manually carries every book from the delivery van to the shelf. This is inefficient because the librarian is **busy** and cannot help students. Now, install an **automated conveyor belt** (the DMA controller) that moves books directly from the van to the shelf. The librarian (CPU) only sets up the conveyor at the start and is interrupted when the entire job is done. This frees the CPU for higher-value tasks. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics for DMA:** > - **Burst Transfer Rate:** Up to the bus bandwidth (e.g., **133 MB/s** for PCI, **up to several GB/s** for modern systems). > - **Setup Overhead:** Only a few bus cycles to initialize the DMAC. > - **Word/Block Size:** 1 byte to several kilobytes per transfer request. --- ### 1.3 Why Do We Need Interrupts and DMA? | Problem Without Interrupt/DMA | Solution Provided | |-------------------------------|-------------------| | CPU wastes cycles in busy-wait polling | Interrupt-based I/O frees CPU | | CPU is overloaded moving data | DMA offloads bulk data movement | | Slow response to urgent events | Interrupts provide immediate, event-driven response | | Poor system throughput | Both techniques drastically improve throughput | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Interrupts (KTU 2024 Syllabus — High Priority Topic) Interrupts are classified along several orthogonal axes. The KTU board frequently asks 3-mark questions on this classification. ### 2.1.1 Based on Source / Origin | Type | Source | Example | |------|--------|---------| | **Hardware (External) Interrupts** | External I/O devices via interrupt request lines | Keyboard key press, I/O device ready signal | | **Software (Internal) Interrupts** | Exceptional conditions or special instructions inside the CPU | Division by zero, illegal opcode, `INT n` (software interrupt instruction) | | **Traps** | Synchronous exceptions generated by the program | System call, breakpoint, overflow | > [!NOTE] > **Synchronous vs Asynchronous:** > - *Synchronous:* Triggered at the **same instruction** every time the same condition occurs (e.g., divide-by-zero). > - *Asynchronous:* Triggered at **random times** with respect to the executing program (e.g., I/O device ready). ### 2.1.2 Based on Maskability | Type | Maskable? | Example | |------|-----------|---------| | **Maskable Interrupt** | Yes — can be ignored (disabled) via the Interrupt Enable (IE) flag | I/O interrupts | | **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** | No — cannot be ignored; highest priority | Power failure, critical hardware failure | ### 2.1.3 Based on Vectoring | Type | How ISR address is found | Speed | |------|--------------------------|-------| | **Vectored Interrupt** | Device provides an interrupt vector (address) directly | Fast | | **Non-Vectored Interrupt** | CPU must poll devices to find the source | Slow | --- ## 2.2 Interrupt Hardware — The Physical Mechanism The CPU and external devices are connected through dedicated **interrupt hardware** consisting of: 1. **Interrupt Request (IRQ) Lines** — One or more physical lines (`INTR`, `NMI`, `INT0`, `INT1`, etc.) on which devices signal the CPU. 2. **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA) Line** — CPU pulses this line to inform the device that the request has been accepted. 3. **Interrupt Controller** — A separate chip (e.g., Intel 8259A PIC) that manages multiple IRQ lines, prioritizes them, and presents a single signal to the CPU. 4. **Priority Resolver** — Hardware logic that decides which interrupt is served first when multiple arrive simultaneously. ### 2.2.1 The Interrupt Handling Sequence (Step-by-Step) 1. **Device raises an interrupt** by asserting the IRQ line. 2. **CPU finishes the current instruction** (atomic boundary). 3. **CPU checks the Interrupt Enable flag** — if disabled, the interrupt is ignored (except NMI). 4. **CPU saves the current state** — pushes **Program Counter (PC)**, **Status Register (PSW)**, and possibly other registers onto the **system stack**. 5. **CPU acknowledges** by sending an INTA pulse to the interrupt controller. 6. **Interrupt controller supplies the vector** (address of the ISR) — for vectored systems. 7. **CPU loads the ISR address** into the PC and begins execution. 8. **ISR executes** — services the device, clears the interrupt flag of the device. 9. **ISR ends with an `IRET` (Interrupt Return) instruction** — restores saved PC and PSW, resuming the main program. --- ## 2.3 Priority Interrupt Schemes (KTU High-Yield ⭐) When **multiple interrupts arrive simultaneously**, a deterministic way to choose which to service first is essential. KTU frequently tests the two classical hardware schemes below. ### 2.3.1 Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt - Devices are connected in a **serial chain** (daisy chain) along a **priority line**. - The device **closest to the CPU has the highest priority**. - When multiple devices assert IRQ simultaneously, the **priority line propagates** through the chain; the first device to receive an *active priority in* and *active interrupt request* blocks the signal from propagating further down the chain. - **Disadvantage:** Priority is **fixed by physical position** — not flexible. ### 2.3.2 Parallel Priority Interrupt (Using a Priority Encoder) - All devices are connected in **parallel** to a **priority encoder** (e.g., 8-to-3 encoder like 74LS148). - The priority encoder **simultaneously** examines all active requests and produces the **binary code of the highest-priority active request**. - This binary code is used as an **index into a vector table** to fetch the ISR address. - **Advantage:** Fast (combinational logic), fixed priority is well-defined, easily extendable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these two schemes matter for KTU:** > The 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on hardware-level interrupt arbitration. Expect either a 3-mark direct question (e.g., "Explain daisy-chain priority interrupt") or a 7-mark sub-part comparison. --- ## 2.4 Direct Memory Access (DMA) — Deep Theory ### 2.4.1 Why DMA? In **programmed I/O**, the CPU executes every data move. For a block of $N$ words: - Time consumed = $N \times T_{cycle}$ (where $T_{cycle}$ is one I/O instruction cycle). - This blocks the CPU from doing other useful work. In **interrupt-driven I/O**, the CPU is interrupted per word or per buffer — still involves CPU effort for every transfer. **DMA** removes the CPU from the bulk transfer path entirely, using a dedicated controller. ### 2.4.2 The DMA Controller (DMAC) The DMA Controller is a separate processor-like chip that contains: - **Memory Address Register (MAR)** — holds the current memory address for transfer. - **Word Count Register (WCR)** — holds the number of words yet to be transferred. - **Control Register** — direction (read/write), transfer mode, start/stop bits. - **Data Register (DR)** — temporary buffer for the data being moved. - **Bus Request / Bus Grant logic** — to take control of the system bus from the CPU. ### 2.4.3 DMA Transfer Modes (KTU 2024 High-Yield ⭐) | Mode | CPU Activity During Transfer | Speed | Use Case | |------|------------------------------|-------|----------| | **Burst Mode (Block Transfer)** | CPU is **completely blocked** for the entire transfer | Fastest | Large block transfers, e.g., disk-to-memory | | **Cycle Stealing Mode** | DMAC transfers **one word per "steal"**; CPU waits for that one memory cycle | Slower than burst, but CPU can interleave | Real-time systems, moderate bandwidth | | **Transparent Mode** | DMAC transfers **only when CPU is NOT using the bus** | Slowest, but CPU never waits | Time-critical CPU tasks | > [!NOTE] > **Cycle Stealing in detail:** > The DMAC requests the bus for **one memory cycle**, transfers one word, then releases the bus. The CPU effectively loses one memory cycle per word transferred. The term "steal" comes from the DMAC *stealing* memory cycles from the CPU. > [!NOTE] > **Transparent Mode in detail:** > The DMAC monitors the CPU's bus signals. It only performs a transfer when it detects the CPU is **not** using the bus (e.g., during instruction decode). CPU sees **zero performance loss** but throughput is lowest. ### 2.4.4 DMA Operation Sequence 1. **CPU programs the DMAC**: sets MAR (starting memory address), WCR (count), and Control Register (direction, mode). 2. **CPU issues a DMA command** (e.g., `READ` or `WRITE` I/O instruction with a special DMA signal). 3. **DMAC requests the bus** by asserting `HRQ` (Hold Request) to the CPU. 4. **CPU completes the current bus cycle**, then asserts `HLDA` (Hold Acknowledge) and **tri-states** its bus drivers. 5. **DMAC takes control of the bus** and performs the data transfer: - Increments MAR - Decrements WCR - Transfers one word per bus cycle (or per burst, depending on mode) 6. **When WCR = 0**, the DMAC de-asserts HRQ and raises an **interrupt** to the CPU. 7. **CPU takes back the bus** (HLDA de-asserted) and resumes normal operation, possibly servicing the DMA-completion interrupt. ### 2.4.5 DMA Transfer Methods — Fly-By vs Fetch-Store - **Fly-By (Single-Cycle Transfer):** Data moves from I/O device to memory (or vice versa) in a **single bus cycle**. The DMAC only supplies the address; data flows directly. Very fast. **Limitation:** Cannot perform I/O-to-I/O or memory-to-memory transfer. - **Fetch-Store (Two-Cycle Transfer):** DMAC first reads data into its internal register, then writes it to the destination in a second bus cycle. More flexible (memory-to-memory possible) but slower. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Critical ⭐) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions asked in KTU ESE on this module.** | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Notes | |---|---------|---------------------|-------|-------| | 1 | CPU idle time in **Programmed I/O** for $N$ words | $T_{PIO} = N \cdot T_{io}$ | seconds | $T_{io}$ = time per I/O instruction | | 2 | CPU idle time in **Interrupt-driven I/O** | $T_{INT} = N \cdot (T_{isr} + T_{overhead})$ | seconds | ISR still runs per buffer/word | | 3 | CPU free time with **DMA (Burst Mode)** | $T_{CPU\_free} = T_{transfer} - T_{setup}$ | seconds | $T_{setup}$ typically 1–10 µs | | 4 | Total DMA transfer time (Burst) | $T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{mem\_cycle}$ | seconds | DMAC owns bus continuously | | 5 | Total DMA transfer time (Cycle Stealing) | $T_{CS} = T_{setup} + N \cdot (T_{mem\_cycle} + T_{steal\_overhead})$ | seconds | One word per steal | | 6 | Speedup of DMA over Programmed I/O | $S = \dfrac{T_{PIO}}{T_{DMA}}$ | dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ | | 7 | CPU cycles saved by DMA | $C_{saved} = N \cdot (T_{io} - T_{steal})$ | cycles | If $T_{steal} \ll T_{io}$ | | 8 | Effective data rate | $R = \dfrac{N \cdot W}{T_{total}}$ | bytes/sec | $W$ = word width in bytes | | 9 | Bus utilization by DMAC | $U = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{mem\_cycle}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | For cycle-stealing: $U \to \frac{1}{1+T_{steal}/T_{mem}}$ | | 10 | Interrupt latency | $L = T_{finish\_current} + T_{save\_context} + T_{fetch\_ISR}$ | seconds | Lower bound is finite, non-zero | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions / Limits:** > - DMA is **only beneficial** if $T_{setup} \ll N \cdot T_{io}$. For small $N$ (e.g., 1–4 words), programmed I/O may be faster. > - Cycle-stealing mode: bus utilization $\leq 50\%$ when $T_{steal} = T_{mem\_cycle}$. > - Burst mode: bus utilization = **100%** during transfer. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Disk controllers** (SATA, NVMe) use DMA heavily. - **Network interface cards (NICs)** use DMA to move packets into host memory. - **GPUs** use DMA-like engines to transfer frame buffers. - **Audio/Video streaming** subsystems rely on DMA for continuous, glitch-free playback. - **Embedded systems** (ARM Cortex-M, AVR) use DMA for sensor data acquisition without waking the CPU. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Problem 1 — DMA vs Programmed I/O Time Comparison **Problem Statement (Model KTU Numercal):** A system needs to transfer a block of **$N = 1024$ words** from an I/O device to memory. - Programmed I/O takes **$T_{io} = 10\,\mu s$ per word**. - DMA setup time: **$T_{setup} = 5\,\mu s$**. - DMA burst transfer time per word: **$T_{mem\_cycle} = 1\,\mu s$**. **Find:** (a) Total time with Programmed I/O, (b) Total time with DMA, (c) Speedup, (d) % CPU time saved. --- ### 3.1.1 (a) Programmed I/O Time By definition: $$ T_{PIO} = N \cdot T_{io} $$ Substitute $N = 1024$ and $T_{io} = 10\,\mu s$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{PIO} &= 1024 \times 10\,\mu s \\ &= 10{,}240\,\mu s \\ &= 10.240\,ms \end{aligned} $$ > The CPU is **fully occupied** for 10.24 ms — no useful work possible in parallel. --- ### 3.1.2 (b) DMA Burst Transfer Time Total DMA time = setup time + transfer time: $$ T_{DMA} = T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{mem\_cycle} $$ Substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{DMA} &= 5\,\mu s + 1024 \times 1\,\mu s \\ &= 5 + 1024\,\mu s \\ &= 1029\,\mu s \\ &= 1.029\,ms \end{aligned} $$ > DMA is **~10× faster** in wall-clock time, and the CPU is free for 1.024 ms of useful work. --- ### 3.1.3 (c) Speedup $$ S = \frac{T_{PIO}}{T_{DMA}} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{10{,}240\,\mu s}{1029\,\mu s} \\ &\approx 9.95 \end{aligned} $$ > **Speedup ≈ 9.95×** — nearly an order of magnitude improvement. --- ### 3.1.4 (d) % CPU Time Saved CPU time spent in programmed I/O = $T_{PIO}$. CPU time spent with DMA = $T_{setup}$ (only). $$ \text{CPU}_{\text{PIO}} = 10{,}240\,\mu s $$ $$ \text{CPU}_{\text{DMA}} = 5\,\mu s $$ $$ \text{CPU}_{\text{saved}} = \text{CPU}_{\text{PIO}} - \text{CPU}_{\text{DMA}} = 10{,}240 - 5 = 10{,}235\,\mu s $$ Percentage saved: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{saved} &= \frac{\text{CPU}_{\text{saved}}}{\text{CPU}_{\text{PIO}}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{10{,}235}{10{,}240} \times 100 \\ &\approx 99.95\% \end{aligned} $$ > The CPU is freed for **99.95%** of the transfer duration — an enormous efficiency gain. --- ## 3.2 Problem 2 — Cycle-Stealing DMA Bus Utilization **Problem Statement:** A system uses cycle-stealing DMA. Each memory cycle is $T_{mem} = 100\,ns$. Each "steal" (bus arbitration overhead) costs $T_{steal} = 20\,ns$. The CPU needs a memory cycle every $T_{CPU} = 200\,ns$ on average. **Find:** (a) Effective bus time per transfer, (b) Maximum bus utilization by DMAC, (c) CPU's effective access rate. --- ### 3.2.1 (a) Effective Bus Time per Transfer For one word transfer, the DMAC must: - Spend $T_{steal}$ arbitrating for the bus. - Spend $T_{mem}$ performing the actual transfer. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{transfer} &= T_{steal} + T_{mem} \\ &= 20\,ns + 100\,ns \\ &= 120\,ns \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.2.2 (b) Maximum Bus Utilization If the DMAC is constantly stealing: $$ \begin{aligned} U_{DMAC} &= \frac{T_{mem}}{T_{transfer}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{100}{120} \times 100 \\ &\approx 83.33\% \end{aligned} $$ > Bus utilization by DMAC is **83.33%** — leaving 16.67% for the CPU. --- ### 3.2.3 (c) CPU's Effective Access Rate Fraction of bus time available to CPU: $$ f_{CPU} = 1 - U_{DMAC} = 1 - 0.8333 = 0.1667 $$ Time between effective CPU memory accesses: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{eff\_CPU} &= \frac{T_{CPU}}{f_{CPU}} \\ &= \frac{200\,ns}{0.1667} \\ &\approx 1200\,ns \end{aligned} $$ > The CPU's effective access interval **increases 6×** — a critical consideration in real-time systems. --- ## 3.3 Problem 3 — Interrupt Latency Calculation **Problem Statement:** A CPU has the following interrupt handling parameters: - Time to finish current instruction: $T_{inst} = 400\,ns$ (worst case) - Time to save context (PC + PSW): $T_{save} = 50\,ns$ - Time to fetch ISR address from vector: $T_{vector} = 100\,ns$ **Find:** (a) Interrupt latency, (b) Maximum interrupt frequency sustainable. --- ### 3.3.1 (a) Interrupt Latency $$ L = T_{inst} + T_{save} + T_{vector} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} L &= 400 + 50 + 100 \\ &= 550\,ns \end{aligned} $$ > Interrupt latency = **550 ns** — the CPU takes at most 550 ns to react. --- ### 3.3.2 (b) Maximum Interrupt Frequency $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{1}{550 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.818\,MHz $$ > CPU can respond to at most **1.818 million interrupts per second** without queuing. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which I/O Method to Use? | Criterion | Programmed I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA | |-----------|----------------|---------------|-----| | Small data volume (1–4 words) | ✅ Best | ❌ Overkill | ❌ Setup overhead dominates | | Moderate volume, infrequent | ❌ Wastes CPU | ✅ Best | ❌ Setup not worth it | | Large volume (1 KB+) | ❌ Wastes CPU | ⚠️ Acceptable | ✅ Best | | Real-time, low-latency requirement | ❌ CPU blocked | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Burst mode blocks CPU | | Multiple concurrent devices | ❌ Polling overhead | ✅ Priority handling | ✅ + Interrupt for completion | | Memory-to-memory copy | ❌ Slow | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Best (Fly-By) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Interrupt Handling Sequence — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD devA[Device A raises IRQ] --> chkA{CPU Interrupt
Enabled?} chkA -->|No| chkA chkA -->|Yes| fini[Finish current
instruction] fini --> save[Save PC and PSW
on system stack] save --> ack[CPU sends INTA
to interrupt controller] ack --> vect[Controller supplies
interrupt vector] vect --> load[Load ISR address
into PC] load --> isr[Execute ISR
service the device] isr --> clr[Clear device
interrupt flag] clr --> iret[IRET instruction
restores PC and PSW] iret --> resume[Resume main
program execution] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from top to bottom. Each box represents one hardware/software step. The CPU is engaged throughout — DMA, in contrast, would branch off at `ack` to a separate controller path. --- ## 4.2 Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt — Hardware Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR cpu[CPU] -->|IRQ line| dev1[Device 1
Highest Priority] dev1 -->|Carry prop| dev2[Device 2] dev2 -->|Carry prop| dev3[Device 3] dev3 -->|Carry prop| devN[Device N
Lowest Priority] cpu -.->|Acknowledge| dev1 dev1 -.->|Acknowledge| dev2 dev2 -.->|Acknowledge| dev3 dev3 -.->|Acknowledge| devN ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = IRQ propagation (priority-in, request). Dotted arrows = Acknowledge propagation. The device nearest the CPU has the highest priority; it can block the chain by failing to assert the carry-propagate line. --- ## 4.3 Parallel Priority Interrupt — Using 8-to-3 Priority Encoder ```mermaid flowchart TD int0[IRQ0
Highest] --> enc[8-to-3 Priority
Encoder 74LS148] int1[IRQ1] --> enc int2[IRQ2] --> enc int3[IRQ3] --> enc int4[IRQ4] --> enc int5[IRQ5] --> enc int6[IRQ6] --> enc int7[IRQ7
Lowest] --> enc enc -->|Binary code A2 A1 A0| vec[Vector Address
Generator] vec --> isr[ISR Address
fetched from table] isr --> cpu[CPU jumps to ISR] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** All IRQs feed the encoder in parallel. The encoder outputs the binary code of the **highest-priority active request**. The code indexes into a vector table to fetch the ISR address. Combinational → very fast. --- ## 4.4 DMA Operation — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD cpu[CPU] -->|1. Program DMAC
Set MAR, WCR, Control| dmac[DMA Controller] dmac -->|2. HRQ signal| cpu cpu -->|3. HLDA acknowledge
tri-state buses| dmac dmac -->|4. Take control
of address and data bus| mem[Main Memory] mem -->|5. Data word| io[I/O Device] io -->|6. Data word| mem dmac -->|7. Increment MAR
Decrement WCR| dmac dmac -->|8. WCR = 0 ?| check{Transfer
Complete?} check -->|No| mem check -->|Yes| intr[Raise interrupt
to CPU] intr --> cpu cpu -->|9. Resume normal
operation| cpu ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Steps 1–3 are CPU-initiated setup. Steps 4–7 are DMAC-owned bulk transfer (no CPU involvement). Step 8–9 is completion notification. **Notice the CPU is completely free during steps 4–7.** --- ## 4.5 DMA Transfer Modes — Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start[DMA Transfer
Requested] --> mode{Transfer
Mode?} mode -->|Burst| burst[DMAC holds bus
until all N words
transferred] mode -->|Cycle Steal| steal[DMAC transfers
1 word, releases bus
repeats N times] mode -->|Transparent| trans[DMAC waits for
CPU bus idle
transfers opportunistically] burst --> done[All words
transferred] steal --> done trans --> done done --> int[DMA completion
interrupt to CPU] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** All three modes end with an interrupt to the CPU. The difference is the bus occupancy pattern during transfer. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between a maskable and a non-maskable interrupt. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme):** | Feature | Maskable Interrupt | Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) | |---------|--------------------|------------------------------| | **Can be ignored?** | Yes, by clearing the Interrupt Enable flag | No, cannot be ignored | | **Priority** | Lower than NMI | Highest priority in the system | | **Use Case** | Normal I/O devices | Critical hardware events | | **Example** | Keyboard interrupt (IRQ1) | Power failure, memory parity error | | **Pin in 8086** | `INTR` | `NMI` | > *(Expected answer length: 3 short paragraphs or a 2-column table. Award full marks for at least 2 distinguishing points and one example each.)* --- ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three advantages of using DMA for data transfer over programmed I/O.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme):** 1. **CPU Offload:** The DMA controller handles bulk data transfer, freeing the CPU to perform computation in parallel. *(1 mark)* 2. **Higher Throughput:** Transfer rate is limited by memory bandwidth, not by CPU instruction execution speed. *(1 mark)* 3. **Reduced Bus Traffic for I/O Instructions:** No need to fetch and execute I/O instructions per word, reducing instruction memory traffic. *(1 mark)* *(Optional 4th point for bonus clarity: Lower power consumption in embedded systems.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q: (a)** Explain the working of a **Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt** system with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the operation of **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**. Compare the **three DMA transfer modes**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to B-A(a) — Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Daisy-chain priority is a hardware-based interrupt arbitration scheme in which multiple devices are connected in series along a priority line. The device physically closest to the CPU has the highest priority. **Working (3 marks):** 1. Each device in the chain has three signals: **Interrupt Request (IR)**, **Priority In (PI)**, and **Priority Out (PO)**. 2. When a device has a pending request, it asserts **IR = 1**. 3. The CPU asserts an **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)** pulse that propagates through the chain. 4. If a device sees **PI = 1** (priority is available to it) and **IR = 1** (it has a request), it **intercepts** the INTA, sets **PO = 0** to block downstream devices, and places its **interrupt vector** on the data bus. 5. The CPU reads the vector and jumps to the corresponding ISR. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 — Daisy-Chain Block Diagram above.)* **Advantages (1 mark):** - Simple hardware; only one INTA line needed for the whole chain. - Easy to extend: just add more devices to the chain. - No extra encoder/decoder logic required. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - **Fixed priority** — cannot be changed without rewiring. - **Propagation delay** accumulates as more devices are added (chain length limited). - **Single point of failure** — a faulty device can break the entire chain. --- #### **Solution to B-A(b) — DMA Operation and Transfer Modes (7 Marks)** **DMA Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.4 — DMA Block Diagram above.)* **Operation Steps (2 marks):** 1. CPU programs DMAC: sets MAR (start address), WCR (count), Control Register. 2. CPU issues DMA command → DMAC asserts HRQ. 3. CPU finishes current bus cycle, asserts HLDA, tri-states its buses. 4. DMAC performs the transfer word-by-word, incrementing MAR and decrementing WCR. 5. When WCR = 0, DMAC releases HRQ and raises an interrupt to the CPU. 6. CPU takes back the bus and processes the completion interrupt. **Comparison of DMA Transfer Modes (3 marks):** | Aspect | Burst Mode | Cycle Stealing | Transparent Mode | |--------|------------|----------------|------------------| | **Bus Ownership** | DMAC owns bus for entire block | DMAC owns bus for 1 word at a time | DMAC only uses bus when CPU is idle | | **CPU Activity** | Fully blocked | Slight delay per cycle | Zero delay | | **Throughput** | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | | **Use Case** | Large block transfer | Mixed workload | Time-critical CPU work | --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain **Parallel Priority Interrupt** using a priority encoder. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A system needs to transfer **4096 words** from a disk to memory. Compare the time taken using: - (i) Programmed I/O with $T_{io} = 5\,\mu s$ per word - (ii) Cycle-Stealing DMA with $T_{setup} = 8\,\mu s$ and $T_{cycle} = 0.5\,\mu s$ per word Calculate the **percentage of CPU time saved** by using DMA. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to B-B(a) — Parallel Priority Interrupt (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 mark):** Parallel priority interrupt uses a **priority encoder** to examine all interrupt requests simultaneously and generate the binary code of the highest-priority active request in a single combinational step. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 — Parallel Priority Diagram above.)* **Working (3 marks):** 1. All $N$ devices are connected in **parallel** to the inputs of an $N$-to-$\log_2 N$ priority encoder (e.g., 74LS148 for 8 lines). 2. The encoder outputs the **binary index** of the highest-priority active request (e.g., line 5 active and higher active → output `101` = 5). 3. This binary code is used as an **offset into an interrupt vector table** stored in memory. 4. The CPU reads the corresponding entry to obtain the **ISR address** and transfers control. **Advantages (1 mark):** - **Fixed, well-defined priority** (line 0 = highest, line N−1 = lowest). - **Fast** — purely combinational, no propagation chain delay. - **Easily extended** using cascaded encoders. --- #### **Solution to B-B(b) — Numerical Comparison (7 Marks)** **Given:** $N = 4096$ words. **Step 1: Programmed I/O Time (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{PIO} &= N \cdot T_{io} \\ &= 4096 \times 5\,\mu s \\ &= 20{,}480\,\mu s \\ &= 20.48\,ms \end{aligned} $$ CPU is fully occupied for **20.48 ms**. **Step 2: Cycle-Stealing DMA Time (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{DMA} &= T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{cycle} \\ &= 8\,\mu s + 4096 \times 0.5\,\mu s \\ &= 8 + 2048 \\ &= 2056\,\mu s \\ &= 2.056\,ms \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: CPU Time Comparison (2 marks)** - CPU time in Programmed I/O: $T_{CPU,PIO} = 20{,}480\,\mu s$ - CPU time in DMA: only setup → $T_{CPU,DMA} = 8\,\mu s$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPU}_{\text{saved}} &= T_{CPU,PIO} - T_{CPU,DMA} \\ &= 20{,}480 - 8 \\ &= 20{,}472\,\mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Percentage CPU Time Saved (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{saved} &= \frac{20{,}472}{20{,}480} \times 100 \\ &= 99.96\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** DMA saves **99.96% of CPU time** and is **~10× faster** in wall-clock terms. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the **setup time** in DMA total time. Always include $T_{setup}$ in $T_{DMA}$. > - **Common Mistake #2:** For "CPU time saved" in DMA, students erroneously compare **total wall-clock time** instead of **CPU-busy time**. The CPU is only busy for $T_{setup}$ in DMA; the DMAC does the rest. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up **Cycle Stealing** and **Burst Mode** formulas. Cycle stealing = $N \times T_{cycle}$ of memory access time, not $N \times T_{io}$. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Not writing the **unit** (µs / ms). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers. > - **Common Mistake #5:** In Part (a) of interrupt questions, students draw the diagram but don't **label the signals** (PI, PO, IR, INTA). Always label signal names — KTU awards 1 mark specifically for labeled diagram. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this section and review 30 minutes before the exam. ⭐ = KTU hot topic.** ### 🔑 Interrupt Fundamentals - ⭐ Interrupt = asynchronous event that suspends CPU → executes ISR → resumes. - **Three classes:** Hardware (external), Software (internal/traps), Non-maskable (NMI). - **Maskable vs Non-maskable:** Maskable respects the IE flag; NMI does not. - **Vectored vs Non-vectored:** Vectored = device supplies ISR address; non-vectored = CPU polls. ### 🔑 Interrupt Hardware - ⭐ **Daisy-Chain Priority:** Serial chain, fixed priority by position, simple but slow. - ⭐ **Parallel Priority:** Uses priority encoder, fast combinational logic, fixed priority. - **Interrupt Controller** (e.g., 8259A PIC): Manages multiple IRQs, presents single INTR to CPU. - **INTA line:** CPU's response signal acknowledging the interrupt. - **Interrupt latency** = time from IRQ asserted to ISR first instruction executed. ### 🔑 DMA Fundamentals - ⭐ **DMA** = hardware controller (DMAC) transfers data between I/O and memory without CPU. - **DMAC registers:** MAR, WCR, Control Register, Data Register. - ⭐ **Three transfer modes:** Burst, Cycle Stealing, Transparent. - **Fly-By vs Fetch-Store:** Fly-By is single-cycle (faster); Fetch-Store is two-cycle (more flexible). ### 🔑 Numerical Formulas (Must Memorize) - $T_{PIO} = N \cdot T_{io}$ - $T_{DMA,burst} = T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{mem}$ - $T_{DMA,cycle-steal} = T_{setup} + N \cdot T_{cycle}$ - $S = T_{PIO} / T_{DMA}$ - $\% \text{CPU saved} = (1 - T_{CPU,DMA}/T_{CPU,PIO}) \times 100$ ### 🔑 Decision Rules - Small $N$ (1–4 words) → Programmed I/O. - Large $N$ with parallel CPU work needed → DMA. - Latency-critical, low data volume → Interrupt-driven I/O. - Memory-to-memory copy → DMA (Fetch-Store mode). ### 🔑 Common KTU Traps - Forgetting $T_{setup}$ in DMA time. - Confusing **bus utilization** with **transfer rate**. - Mixing up **synchronous** (traps) and **asynchronous** (I/O) interrupts. - Drawing interrupt diagrams without labeling signals (PI, PO, IR, INTA). ---
